he Institute of East Asian Studies was established at the University of California,
erkeley, in the fall of 1978 to promote research and teaching on the cultures and
ocieties of China, Japan, and Korea. The institute unites several research centers
nd programs, including the Center for Buddhist Studies, the Center for Chinese
tudies, the Center for Japanese Studies, the Center for Korean Studies, the
Group in Asian Studies, the East Asia National Resource Center, and the Inter-
University Program for Chinese Language Studies.

acceptance of manuscripts in this series, responsibility for the opinions
expressed and for the accuracy of statements rests with their authors.
The China Research Monograph series is one of several publication series
sponsored by the Institute of East Asian Studies in conjunction with its
constituent units. The others include the Japan Research Monograph series,
the Korea Research Monograph series, and the Research Papers and Policy

488Preface
Egyptian, by contrast, ultimately gave rise to all historically known
scripts of the modern world, save those of East Asia.... China is, in other
words, one of only two civilizations in the history of mankind to have
invented ex nihilo a script that has endured down to the present day,
and thus to have influenced the course of human history in regard to the
most fundamental feature of historical civilization itself, the use of writ-
ing. (Boltz 1999:123)
The present work deals with the Shang dynasty (ca. 1200-1045 B.C.) in
North China, the first to leave written records, and its efforts-evidently
with great success-which focused on the artisan corps, labor mobiliza-
tion, farming, warfare, hunting, building, leadership, and culture that
made it all possible. My introduction to the subject of Shang labor mobi-
lization-apart from the obligatory graduate student brush with Karl
August Wittfogel's work-came about quite accidentally in a Tokyo
bookstore in mid-December 1967. My wife, Vannie, and I* had just spent
two-plus years in Taipei and were then spending six weeks in Japan
before I returned to Columbia University to write my dissertation on labor
mobilization in the Eastern Zhou (770-221 B.C.). When I asked what
the bookstores had on ancient China, one of the clerks showed me Shima
Kunio's concordance, Inkyo bokuji sōrui
(A compre-
hensive compilation of divinations from the waste of Yin), whose first edi-
tion had been published in November 1967. I was not then working in the
Shang. I didn't read oracle-bone inscriptions. So I merely flipped it open,
glanced at the first page, and moved on to other volumes.
The memory of that first page, however, remained lodged in my mind.
I had evidently had the good sense to realize that it transcribed a series of
inscriptions that dealt with the "raising" of men. Indeed, had I been able
to read the first inscription that Shima transcribed (presented below at
inscription number [52AB]) I would have found: "In the present season,
*. The name Keightley is pronounced "Keetly," not "Kitely": Yorkshire, not Germanic.xii
Working for His Majesty
Preface
xiii
His Majesty should raise men, five thousand (of them), and march to regu-
late the Tufang, (for We) will receive abundant assistance." Almost a year
passed. I thought I should write a preface on the Shang background. The
moment I began to study the oracle-bone inscriptions I realized they con-
tained a mine of information. And before I knew it, the focus of my disser-
tation was shifting from the Eastern Zhou to the Shang and Western Zhou
(1045-771 B.C.) (Keightley 1969). I immediately contacted the Tokyo
bookstore and ordered a copy of Shima's invaluable concordance, which
arrived in New York in October 1968, and which opened up the Shang
inscriptions to me, as it did to all scholars, in ways that permitted Shang
history to be studied in a systematic way.
To Chao Lin, a student of the Academia Sinica in 1968, who first
guided my early steps in the oracle bones, I owe a substantial debt. My
first publication (Keightley 1969a) was a review of Shima Kunio's contri-
bution, and I dedicated my first book (Keightley 1978) to him.¹ Since I was
largely self-taught in oracle-bone studies, I suspect that I might never have
been encouraged to focus on labor mobilization in the Shang had I not
both stumbled across Shima's volume in that Tokyo bookstore, and had
I not flipped it open to that crucial first page. As the Shang would have
appreciated, luck, or perhaps ancestral guidance, plays a role.
I have devoted works to the study of the sources (Keightley 1978, 1994,
1999b, 1999e); the origins of Chinese civilization (1983, 1987a); the Shang
environment (1999a); the Shang in general (1999b, 2000); Shang religion
(1978a, 1984, 1985, 1998, 2004) and divination (1988, 1999d, 2001, 2006a,
2008); the origins of Chinese writing (1989a, 2006); comparisons with the
Greeks (1993); and numerous reviews (e.g., 1973, 1982, 1982a, 1990, 1997).
(I cite only works that bear upon the present endeavor.) But I have kept
my 1969 dissertation-never before published-much in mind over the
intervening forty-plus years. My dissertation focused in part on the activi-
ties of the zhong and ren; they still play a central role in the pages that
follow. But I had subsequently found it necessary to treat the dependent
laborers of Shang in their full administrative and cultural context.² I had
translated 102 Shang oracle bones in the dissertation; the present work
has 341 of them, often with several charges on the bone or shell (I estimate
about 535 charges).
This book deals with oracle-bone inscriptions of the Late Shang dynasty,
covering the period from Wu Ding (ca. ?-1189 B.C.) to Di Xin *
(ca. 1086-1045 B.C.). Wu Ding presumably reigned for more than twenty
years (Keightley 1978:175-76), but we do not have a firm grip on the year
1. One appreciation of Shima's book is at Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan 1999:404-06.
2. For an introduction, in English, to the oracle bones, see, among many others: Creel 1937:
21-26, 185-96; 1938:1-16; Keightley 1978; 1990; 1997; 1997a; 1999e; 2001; Rawson 1980:55-57;
Takashima 1988-89; Wilkinson 2000:395–406.
he succeeded to the throne.³ It thus spans a period of about one and a half
centuries. It is―as, I believe, any good book based on the Shang inscrip-
tions must be-a pointilliste enterprise, in which the picture of the Late
Shang that emerges is built up through the assembling and ordering of a
series of individual oracle-bone inscriptions and their contexts. There are
bound to be gaps in that painting that we see. And it must never be forgot-
ten that much of our knowledge is limited to what the diviners recorded,
what topics they found it important to divine. We are presented, in short,
with a "diviner's eye" view of the Shang situation; that limitation must
always be kept in mind. The account of Late Shang labor mobilization that
follows is based primarily upon information that can be gleaned from the
oracle-bone inscriptions, whose dynastic biases and divinatory impulses
must also be taken into account.4 The complexity and belief systems of the
rest of Shang life must never be ignored. The oracle bones contain a great
deal of information about the world of the living, scattered though it is,
and the world of the ancestors (see, e.g., Keightley 2004). And the assump-
tions of the diviner's world were quite probably shared by many other
levels of society.
I am no archaeologist. Archaeological finds provide vital information
about the organization and practices of Shang labor, and a variety of new
studies is throwing important light on this topic. I refer to such evidence
in what follows, particularly in chapter 2, but the primary focus of this
study is on what the oracle-bone inscriptions can tell us about the ideo-
logical assumptions and administrative practices that permitted the Late
Shang rulers to create a dynastic state and its legacy. This book, building
upon the oracle bones, is certainly narrow, but it is also deep (see Notes
about the Sources). We can actually handle the bones and shells that the
diviner, and even the king, had handled more than three thousand years
ago. The materials are there, at least in part, and the topic is important-a
central cultural dimension of the Bronze Age in North China.
3. A variety of scholars have dated the reign of Wu Ding to fifty-nine years (e.g., Cao
Dingyun 1999), but this rests entirely on Zhou evidence. The passage exists in the "Wu Yi
" of the Shu (Legge 1865:467), but this is suspect as to the date (Shaughnessy 1993:379).
Dating for lunar elipse records is likewise problematic. Liu Xueshun (1998:23) dates, for
example, the lunar eclipse of Yingcang 885b and 886b to 14 August 1166 B.C.; I would date the
eclipse to 27-28 December 1192 B.C. (Keightley 1997b:5-8; Zhang Peiyu 1999; 1999a; 1999b).
Chang Yuzhi (1998:19-65) and Zhang Peiyu (1999:194, table 1), for example, provide a wide
sample of the dating of the lunar eclipse records; also see Peng Yushang 2003.
4. See the discussion of the historiographical issues at Keightley 1978:152-56. See too Boltz
1999:88-89; Keightley 2006a.
5. See, e.g., Underhill (1996; 2002) for craft production in the Neolithic; Liu Li and Chen
Xingcan (2003; 2006) for the development of the early state; Yung-ti Li (2003) for what the
archaeological evidence can and cannot tell us about the organization of the Anyang
bronze foundries; Haapanen (2005) for what the archaeology can tell us about the commu-
nity at Miaopu Locus North 苗圃北地.xiv
Working for His Majesty
Preface
XV
I visited Anyang in 1975-as part of the Paleoanthropology Delega-
tion (Howells and Tsuchitani 1977)—and in 1981 and 1984. In 1981 I wrote,
Physiognomists and palm readers still flourish in the street of Anyang-
should I try to do it? A certain reluctance, an awareness that the fortune
teller might be right, or might, by his very words, influence the future. A
serious business in short. (Keightley 1981:35)
I couldn't find a fortune teller on the Anyang streets. "The police lieu-
tenant told me: 'I move them on. Did I believe in such superstitions!""
(Keightley 1981:46). Still, historians are grateful for such beliefs.
Building as it does upon the first half of a doctoral dissertation that I
wrote in 1969, this book has taken me some forty years to finalize; I have
always had it in mind, and it certainly shows, at times, its pedigree. Some
sections of the work, particularly those dealing with the workshops and
their products, not only cite the more recent scholarship, but make use of
some of the earliest. For I find particular pleasure in citing, for example,
the passage from Liu Ein 1903, from Li Chi
Zuobin Fin 1929, and from Guo Baojun
in 1928, from Dong
in 1933. Indeed, the
invocation of their words is a reminder of how much of the present work
and the latest scholarship itself depends upon the earlier labors of others.
To repeat their words is partly a form of ritual gratitude, letting them and
ourselves know that they are not forgotten.
I cannot help observing, both ruefully and with gratitude, that I too
am partly a dependent laborer of the Shang, still carrying out-in ways
that Wu Ding would not have imagined-"His Majesty's affairs." I owe a
debt to the Shang diviners themselves, for, in some ways at least, we have
shared the same rice bowl. I hope that I have understood them in part and
that my work will in some small way help others to do so more fully. But I
hope at the same time that I have kept a more critical distance than would
have been either safe or possible under the reign of Wu Ding and his suc-
cessor kings. "The inscriptions are admittedly a biased source, but they
are the only written ones available, and given the time and energy which
the court devoted to their production, they should not be taken lightly"
(Lewis 1993:327). There is still work to do, however; other inscriptions, I
hope, to come out of the ground.
6. I have approximately 285 items in the bibliography of the dissertation, dealing with
both the Shang and Western Zhou; I have approximately 1,300 items in the book, dealing
with the Shang alone. We have learned much in the past forty years.
7. See gu, "strengthen," in "Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases."
8. At Yinxu, Huayuan zhuang E, 1,583 new oracle bones were found in 1991. See
YXFX:475-76; Kaogu 1998b:31-32; Keightley 1999c:185-86; Liu Yiman and Cao Dingyun
1999; Ye Xiangkui and Liu Yiman 2001; Zhongguo shehui kexueyuan Kaogu yanjiusuo 2003;
Smith 2008; Cao Dingyun 2009. I stop, generally, in 2009 (with the exception of Takashima
2010); new finds must await a further study.
The list of those colleagues and scholars to whom I am indebted for con-
structive advice, criticism, and guidance, is legion. I would include those
pioneers whom I never met but whose labors did much to establish oracle-
bone studies on a modern footing. There are generations of those who
built upon the shoulders of the giants and were giants themselves: Liu E,
Luo Zhenyu, W. Pervial Yetts, Frank H. Chalfant, Roswell S. Britton, Wang
Guowei, Dong Zuobin, Guo Moruo, Chen Mengjia, Yu Xingwu, Shira-
kawa Shizuka, Itō Michiharu, Qu Wanli, Yan Yiping, Matsumaru Michio,
and Zhang Bingquan.' And those I have actually met include Kaizuka
Shigeki, Xia Nai, Shi Zhangru, Yan Yiping, Hu Houxuan, Zhang Zheng-
lang, Li Xueqin, Rao Zongyi, Gao Ming, Wang Yuxin, Zhang Changshou,
Yang Xizhang, Yang Baocheng, Qiu Xigui, An Jinhuai, Fan Yuzhou, Huang
Shenzhang, Lian Shaoming, Su Bingqi, Yang Shengnan, Zhao Cheng,
Zhang Zhongpei, Pang Pu, Tang Jigen, and Chen Xingcan, among others.
Turning to scholars in the West from whom I have also learned much, I
must acknowledge the influence of Hans A. Bielenstein (who directed the
original Ph.D. thesis)- and, in roughly chronological order, Noel Barnard,
Karl A. Wittfogel, Paul L-M. Serruys, Virginia C. Kane, Ken-ichi Takashima,
Stanley L. Mickel, Peter A. Boodberg, David S. Nivison, Edwin G. Pulley-
blank, John W. Olsen, Jean A. Lefeuvre, Henry Rosemont, Barbara Stephen,
Hsü Chin-hsiung, Joseph Needham, Jessica Rawson, Edward L. Shaugh-
nessy, Louisa G. Fitzgerald Huber, William G. Boltz, Robin Yates, Robert
L. Thorp, Lothar von Falkenhausen, Mark Edward Lewis, Roger L. Olesen,
Cheng Te-k'un, Li Liu, Oliver Venture, and Magnus Fiskesjö. I particu-
larly enjoyed the oracle-bone seminar that was sponsored by the Senter
for Høyere Studier, Norwegian Academy of Sciences, Oslo, from April to
June 2000. Under Christoph Harbsmeier's direction, we translated many
inscriptions, with Ken-ichi Takashima, Li Ling, Qiu Xigui, and others in
attendance. I owe a special debt of thanks to Harbsmeier for the title of this
book, Working for His Majesty (see Wang E in Glossary). And I owe a spe-
cial debt of thanks to K. C. Chang, whom I first met in Beijing in 1975, for
putting Shang studies squarely on the map.
I owe a great debt to my teachers of both Chinese-Loretta Pan, Wu P'ei-
yi, Hans Bielenstein, and Yü Ta-ch'eng-and Japanese-Okamoto Shun-
pei and Chuang Ch'i-ch'ang-among others. I am indebted to a variety of
research assistants, including Chen Yuan, Dong Tiezhu, He Yuming, Seung-
joon Lee, Teresa Sun, and Xu Yamin, when at last I devoted full attention
to the manuscript. I am also indebted to David Goodrich, of the Birdtrack

Press, and to Patrick Davidson, who kept my faulty word processor alive
and well. I am grateful to my three readers, particularly the last one. I am
grateful to Kate Lawn Chouta for copyediting the final manuscript with clar-
ity and efficiency. I thank the indexer, Susan Stone. And finally, more than
fifty years ago, to my English teachers: Donald Mehew Parren at Aldenham

I should like to acknowledge the support of the NDFL Fellow (Chinese),
Foreign Area Fellowship Foundation, and the Department of Health, Edu-
cation, and Welfare, whose fellowships enabled me to write the original
dissertation. I have also been supported by a variety of grants during the
course of my career, from the Committee of Research at Berkeley and as
an ACLS, Guggenheim, Wang, Center for Chinese Studies, Humanities
Research Institute, and MacArthur Fellow. Service with the Committee on
Scholarly Communication with the People's Republic of China has also
made it possible for me to visit scholars and sites in China. I should like to
thank all of them for making this scholarship possible. And finally, I should
like to thank all the doctors in Kaiser Permanente. I have experienced two
heart attacks in 1992 and in 2003- and underwent a quadruple bypass. I
should not have finished the book without their assistance.
And my final thanks must go to the East Asian Library at Berkeley and
its staff.10 I have now toured its shelves for forty-plus years (much of it in
Durant Hall), and I owe a particular debt of thanks to Donald H. Shively,
Pai Y. Chang, Jean Han, Yuki Ishimatsu, Hideyuki Morimoto, Peter Zhou,
Bruce Williams, and Susan Xue, among many others. I worked in both the
Old EAL, in Durant Hall, and the New, with its own building, the C. V.
Starr East Asian Library. My own first contact with Berkeley's East Asian
Library holdings goes back to just before I was hired there as a green
Assistant Professor in 1969. I was finishing my doctorate at Columbia in
the spring of that year when I received a job offer from Berkeley. In those
days, Berkeley was far away from the East Coast, and I was greatly con-
cerned that its library holdings might not match those I had benefitted
from at Columbia. So I wrote Dr. Elizabeth Huff, then the Director of the
EAL, and sent her a list of all the Shang oracle-bone collections I had been
working with at Columbia and planned to work with for the foreseeable
future. Did Berkeley have these books?, I asked. Back came the reply:
Berkeley had all of them! And I took the job.
David N. Keightley
10. I discover that I have cited over ten unpublished papers, including two books, in the
body of my text. If anyone would like to consult any of these, I plan to put one copy of each
in the East Asian Library (marked with an asterisk in Bibliography B).
Notes about the Sources, Citation, and
Transcription Conventions
One copy of the oracle-bone script and its modern transcription may be
found in Yao Xiaosui and Xiao Ding #T, eds., Yinxu jiagu keci
moshi zongji #####(1988), abbreviated as MZ (reviewed
by Keightley 1997:507-08).
The main source for the oracle bones is Guo Moruo, ed., Hu
Houxuan, ed. in chief, Jiaguwen heji (13 vols., 1978-82,
abbreviated as Heji (reviewed in Keightley 1990:39-51). A consultation of
Hu Houxuan et al., Jiaguwen Heji cailiao laiyuanbiao XRD
(vol. 2, 1999), will refer you to the original publication sources-from
1903 to 1979-with more potential glosses. I also refer frequently to Hu
Houxuan et al., Jiaguwen Heji shiwen XX (1999) for the mod-
ern characters, abbreviating it as Heshi. In addition to the Heji, which con-
tains 41,956 oracle-bone inscriptions, Peng Bangjiong et al., Jiagu-
wen Heji: Bubian TXA: (1999), contains 13,450 more pieces; I
will cite this as Hebu I also refer to Cao Jinyan
and Shen
Jianhua 沈建華, Jiaguwen xiaoshi zongji 甲骨文校釋總集(2006; abbrevi-
ated as JGXS) and to varied computer databases, in particular CHANT
(Chinese Ancient Texts) (and see Shen Jianhua and Cao Jinyan 2001). All
of these must be considered.²
I also cite Hsü Chin-hsiung, Oracle Bones from the White and Other Collec-
tions (1979) and Xiaotun nandi jiagu
(1980, 1983) (reviewed
in Keightley 1990:51-59), both of which appear in MZ and Yao Xiaosui
and Xiao Ding, eds., Yinxu jiagu keci leizuan
(abbrevi-
ated as Y; reviewed in Keightley 1997:507-13). I also cite, but rarely, a vari-
ety of other collections in Bibliography A.
1. "Since the first discovery of oracle bone inscriptions in 1899, more than 160,000
inscribed pieces have been unearthed" (Lu Liancheng and Yan Wenming 2005:166).

appear twice in Heji. Also see Hu Houxuan (1991); Song Zhenhao (ed., 1999)-which lists

Working for His Majesty
Notes about the Sources, Citation, and Transcription Conventions
xix
A substantial portion of the book is devoted to "Inscription Glosses"
(Appendix 1) and "Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases" (Appendix 2).
The meaning of the Shang words is by no means always clear-our first
Chinese dictionaries, the Er Ya and Shuowen jiezi, have been
dated to a millennium or more later-and scholars have frequently dis-
agreed about their interpretation (see Serruys 1974:12-13). The "Glosses"
and "Glossary" provide an introduction to the interpretative issues and
alert the reader to the uncertain nature of some of the readings that I have
adopted. They are intended to be honest accounts of my thinking, rather
than assertive refutation of the thought of others. They leave grounds for
disagreement and invite readers to form their own conclusions.
A word should be said about the periodization.³ Archaeologically, I
assign the finds from Erlitou and Yanshi to the pre-Shang
period; I assign Erligang and Zhengzhou to the Early Shang;5
I assign Huanbei to the Middle Shang;6 and I assign Xiaotun (or
Anyang) to the Late Shang."
I divide the finds from the sites into "Yinxu I" (Pan Geng [ca. 1300
B.C.], Xiao Xin, Xiao Yi/Z, and early Wu Ding iT); "Yinxu II”
(late Wu Ding, Zu Geng, Zu Jia ); "Yinxu III" (Lin Xin to
Wen Wu Ding XT); and "Yinxu IV" (Di Yi Z and Di Xin [to

of Dong Zuobin (1945), dating the periods from Period "I" (Wu Ding)―
the diviner Bin, Que, etc.-till Period "V" (Di Xin). But I do not fol-
low Dong in seeing the re-emergence of the "Old School" in Period IV,
a dating that was also followed by Heji. I place, rather, the Shi E-group
and Li-group in the two Periods of I and II.10 Among other problems,

"One of the uses of the oracle-bone divinations was to create a consen-
sus as well as to validate a decision" (Yates 1997:81), but Shang divination
evolved with time.
The last nine Shang kings were served by over 120 diviners whose
names are known.... Modern scholars have identified some ten major
diviner groups on the basis of their periods of activity, writing styles,
divination forms, and divination topics. They have also conveniently,
if sometimes imperfectly, divided these groups chronologically into
five periods.13 By the end of the dynasty, however, the king himself had
become virtually the only diviner recorded in the inscriptions, monop-
olizing the mantic role that Wu Ding had earlier shared with other
notables.
The diviners from the Bin group (Period I), in particular, favored
a form of divination in which they treated the topic at issue in com-
plementary fashion. Rather than offer a direct question to the Powers,
they formulated a pair of "charges" (the modern term is mingci ),
one positive and one negative, the "charge" being best understood as
the statement of a prediction, a hope, or a fear.14 These paired formu-
lations of the issue were generally cracked and inscribed on the right
and left sides of a turtle shell.... Wu Ding's diviners treated charges
about a large variety of topics in this dualistic way: harvests, bad omens,
the capture of prisoners, childbearing..., the activities of Di (the
high God) and other Powers, dreams, floods, military strategy, the issu-
ing of orders, rain and sunshine, royal tours of inspection, sickness,
sending men on a mission, settlement building, sorties and other trips,
spiritual approval, assistance, harm, tribute payments, and so on. That

Shengnan (eds., 1999:124-93) and Shen Zhiyu (2001:195-258) for an account of the disputes.
And see in English: Keightley 1978:32, n. 18 (for the early scholarship); 1990:43-45, 55-59;

xxi
much Shang art was also characterized by the symmetrical opposition
of images further suggests the value that the Shang elites attached to
such binary oppositions.15
As divinatory practice and its underlying religious assumptions
evolved, the content and form of Shang divination changed consider-
ably.16 Not only did diviners use complementary, positive and negative
charge pairs increasingly rarely, but they reduced the whole scope of
the enterprise. Most Period V divinations focused on the performance,
according to strict schedule, of ancestral cult, apotropaic wishes for "no
harm" in the next ten-day week, and queries about the royal hunts, which
were now divined on the same five days-the second, fourth, fifth, eighth,
and ninth-in every ten-day week (Matsumaru 1963:70). Charges about
what the Powers might do to the Shang, about receiving or not receiving
millet harvest..., and many of the other topics that Wu Ding's diviners
had addressed-such as the hopes for the birth of a male child..., the
meaning of dreams, the cause and ritual cure for sickness, the choice of
allies in war and of officers to carry out the king's affairs, the mobiliza-
tion of conscripts, the building of settlements, and the will of Di-had
vanished from the record.
Calligraphy, moreover, once large and bold in Period I... had become
minuscule.... Crack notations, that, in Period I, had identified the aus-
piciousness of particular cracks..., were almost completely discarded.
The number of cracks per charge, which might, under Wu Ding, have
involved up to twenty for a charge pair..., had generally been reduced
to no more than three cracks per unpaired charge...." Royal prognos-
tications, that under Wu Ding had forecast both good fortune... and
bad..., and could on occasion be lengthy..
..., were now uniformly aus-
picious and brief...
Shang divination, in short, had, during the century and half from the
reign of Wu Ding to that of Di Xin (1086-1045 B.C.), become more system-
atic, less spontaneous, and less comprehensive; it had also become rou-
tinely optimistic. . . . Given the reduction in the scope of Period V divina-
tion, however, modern scholars are, in many areas, less informed about
the activities, hopes, and fears of Di Yi and Di Xin than they are about
those of Wu Ding.18
This change in the divination record makes it harder, though not impossi-
ble, to pursue diachronic analysis. Probably, the last Shang kings failed to

I translate the inscriptions as declarative in mode, rather than interrog-
ative. I prefer: [160AB] : ±, "Divined: "The western lands will
receive harvest,'" and, "Divined: 'The western lands
might not receive harvest"" rather than "Divined: 'Will the western lands
receive harvest?"" and "Divined: 'Will the western lands might not receive
harvest?"" See the Glossary under zhen.

I have no certainty that the Shang assigned a length to a typical luna-
tion or precisely numbered the moons in advance. They simply noted the
number of the moons as they passed, without assigning specific length to
the typical lunation. Accordingly, I have assigned "moon" -"first moon,"
"second moon," etc.-rather than "month" in the translations given.20 The
ganzhi, at least for divination, rather than the month, was generally
more important.21
I must also confess to some hesitation about the month number at the
start of the
year.
Chinese tradition holds that the Zhou began the year with the zi
month-that is, the lunar month during which the winter solstice occurs.
This is as opposed to the Shang calendar, which supposedly began in the
chou month, or the lunar month beginning after the winter solstice; or
to the Xia calendar, subsequently the standard calendar, which begins
with the yin month, two months after the winter solstice. Despite the
strict belief in this tradition by the time of the Han dynasty, careful calen-
drical studies of the eclipse notations in the Spring and Autumn Annals
reveal that only around the year 600 did the Zhou calendar regularly
begin with the zi month.22 In the more than one hundred years chronicled
before that time, the first month of the year vacillated between the zi and
chou months. It can probably be assumed, therefore, that the calendar of
the Western Zhou was also irregular, beginning sometimes with the zi
month and sometimes with the chou month.23
19. E.g., Zhang Zhenglang 1980 (tr. as Chang Cheng-lang 1980-81); Zhang Yachu and Liu
Yu 1981-82; Liu Liang 1999; Vandermeersch 2001; Li Xueqin 2002.
20. Xueshun Liu (2005:99-179) reviews the evidence, with "the conclusion: the Yin month
can be either 30 or 29 days long" (p. 142).
21. E.g., Keightley 2000:47-49; 2004:12-16.
22. Shinjō 1928, facing p. 315.
23. Shaughnessy 1991:145. "Among the various Warring States calendars, some set the
first month of the civil year at zi [7], corresponding to the beginning of the tropical yearxxii
Working for His Majesty
Notes about the Sources, Citation, and Transcription Conventions
xxiii
I have assumed that the Shang calendar started with the chou month, as
many others have proposed. 24 But considerable opposition has been raised.
The scholars listed in Keightley (2000:43-44) and Chang Yuzhi (1998:422),
for example, see the stars, the climate, and the divinations about agricul-
ture as supporting the view that the Shang calendar started in the fifth
month (June). By contrast, Xueshun Liu (2005:200-39, who also gives a
calendar of the main contenders) thinks that the year started in "the sec-
ond month before the month of the winter solstice."
I suspect, in fact, that "the start of the year” could have involved more
than one kind of year.
Just as modern academics treat January as the first month of the calendar
and tax year but start their academic and fiscal year in the seventh, so too
the Shang may have pegged the first month of their luni-solar calendar
to the first lunation after the winter solstice, while tying their agricultural
calendar to the observation of stars and constellations. It would have
been the first system, not the second, that gave rise to the moon numbers.
(Keightley 1997b:4; cf. 2000:44)
And perhaps different diviners or diviner groups practiced different
systems. More study is required (Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan, eds.,
1999:686).
I always use fanti zi, "complex forms," even in publications from
the People's Republic of China, which generally use the jianti zi 17,
"simplified forms." I transpose the Wade-Giles of the original text cited
into pinyin where appropriate-so Yinxu rather then Yin-hsü.
But the published names remain in the original Wade-Giles-so An Jin-
huai and An Chin-huai. And I generally, but not invariably, do not
cite phonetic reconstructions.
The present extent of our knowledge, or rather ignorance-particularly
in the field of phonology-makes this ideal unattainable for some time
to come. In phonology, for instance, there is a substantial lack of schol-
arly consensus among the small number of specialists reconstructing Old
Chinese....25

I identify individual inscriptions by the piece. "Bingbian 32.1" refers not to
p. 32 but to the plastron. I refer to page number as follows: "Bingbian 98,
pp. 142-44."
The letters "f" and "b" after an inscription number indicate "front"
(zheng E) and "back" (fan ) where appropriate. The use of A, B, C,
etc., in my reference numbers, as in [145A] and [145B], indicates that the
inscription units appear in sequence on the same bone or shell. The letters
A, B, C, etc., attached to the Heji inscription number-as in the case of Heji
14222fA or Heji 14222fC (= [339A] or [339B])-refer to the fragments, jia
, bing, etc., of an "exploded" rejoining.
I have also attempted to identify each quoted translation as bone (8)
or shell (). In a few cases I have had to guess.
Ellipses in a transcription or translation indicate that I have omitted
some of an inscription's characters, mainly because they are not relevant
to the point being made. The symbol ☐ indicates that one graph is miss-
ing in the original inscription, and the symbol ☐ indicates that one or
more graphs may be missing. A "(?)" after a character or translation indi-
cates that the modern transcription I have provided is close or is uncer-
tain. An represents a graph that is present but illegible, or for which no
useful modern transcription exists; 26 translated passages are given as ☑ or
as X, Y, and Z, etc., when no romanization is available. Readers can con-
sult the inscriptions in Y, MZ, Hebu, and JGXS.
Parentheses in a translation indicate that I supply the meaning. Square
brackets mean that I restore a character or word that is now missing but
that would, in my view, have been present in the original, undamaged
inscription.
I give for the first time, in a group of inscriptions, the romanized version
of characters that I deal with in the Glossary but for the second or third
occurrence, etc., in the same section, I do not. The presence of a roman-
ized term in parentheses placed after a word that appears in a translated
inscription, as in [24], "... Qin (Qin) might (qi) lose (sang) zhong," indi-
cates that I discuss the term, in this case Qin, qi, and sang, in the "Glossary
of Shang Terms and Phrases," arranged by alphabetical order.
An asterisk before the inscription citation, such as *Heji 57, indicates
that I discuss the translation problems in the "Inscription Glosses"; this is
also indicated by the asterisk in the "Key to the Inscriptions Translated":
[25*].
26. "Of the total of 4,000 or more characters that appear in the texts of this period, iden-
tification of the word for which the character stands is certain for no more than half. The
remainder fall into two groups, the uncertain and the unknown" (Boltz 1999:88-89). Qiu
Xigui (2000:49) estimates the total at some 4,500 to 5,000 characters.xxiv
Working for His Majesty
A question mark by the graph or romanization-"ji (?)," for
example-means that I only indicate the word's semantic range.
An asterisk in front of a Chinese character or a translation of an inscrip-
tion indicates that the character was not present or the translation is wrong
or hypothetical (for example, Heji 5628 and 27826b [see glosses]).
I occasionally give "N" for "Noun" and "PN" for “Place-Name"; so
"N" translates to "Noun 4," etc.
I occasionally shorten the translation where much of the inscription is
repeated in the positive and negative versions of the charge: "If Di orders
it to rain we might not/will (receive) a regular (good) harvest."
ONE
Introduction: The Setting
The social conditions and cultural dimensions of work in early China were
deeply involved in the genesis of its elite dynastic culture. Neolithic men
and women in China probably worked harder, and were less handsomely
rewarded, than their hunter-gatherer forebears.
The prehistoric cultures that developed in China from ca. 8000 to 2000
B.C. are traditionally described as "Neolithic," meaning that farming was
the main method of subsistence for a community, there was sedentism
rather than seasonal mobility, and people relied upon pottery vessels for
the preparation and consumption of food, as well as on ground stone
tools. (Underhill and Habu 2006:122)
At the same time, a division of labor was occurring along gender lines
as early as the sixth millennium B.C., a division that was associated with
the general decline in the social and economic status of women and chil-
dren. As early as the Neolithic, the ability to enjoy and display the labor of
others contributed to social status, and this ability was increasingly dem-
onstrated by the appearance of tools, more finely made and polished than
mere utility would require, whose function as grave goods was to delin-
eate enduring status (Keightley 1999:7-10). That some of the earliest vic-
tims of human sacrifice were buried with their tools and alongside their
masters further testifies to the way in which dependent labor relations in
this life were expected to continue in the next. It may also be suggested that
the ancestors were exalted by the living in both the Neolithic and Bronze
Ages because the living could, wittingly or unwittingly, put them to use.
The dead, in this culture, were not simply grieved; they were put to work.
The trends involving the differentiation of sex and status and the sym-
bolization of authority by means of emblematic work tools continued to
develop in the Bronze Age.¹ This survey of dependent labor in Late Shang
1. On the Neolithic background of the Shang, see, e.g., Kwang-chih Chang 1986:71-294;
Li Liu 1996; 2000; 2004; Underhill 2002; Underhill, Feinman, Nicholas, et al., 2002; Li Liu and
Xingcan Chen 2003; 2006.2
Working for His Majesty
1: Introduction
3
considers the zhong, the ren, the officers who led them, the nature
of their mobilizations, and their roles in agriculture, warfare, hunting,
and construction (see chs. 12-15). Study of the handicraft industries, also
directed by the royal house, confirms that the Shang elites presided over
a sophisticated and complex economy. An examination of who the state
laborers were leads to the hypothesis that skilled handicraft production
was in the hands of occupational lineages (see ch. 8). Where no legal con-
cept of individual rights existed, furthermore, it is more useful to charac-
terize Shang labor in terms of degrees of dependency and privilege than
in terms of slavery and freedom (see pp. 53 ff.).
Some work and service practices in Late Shang China appear to have
been, in part, highly ritualized.2 Warfare, to take one example of state ser-
vice, involved a series of formalized, religiously sanctioned procedures.
Study of the diviners' work schedules increasingly indicates that the ritu-
alists at the royal Shang court had created a highly ordered situation in
which sacrifices and announcements to the ancestors were divined and
performed according to a strictly controlled schedule and in strictly con-
trolled places (see, e.g., Keightley 2004:20-26).
There was an acceptance of the responsibility, shared generally by
the king's subjects and diffused downward through the patrimonial and
patriarchal political and social system, to serve the king and lineage heads;
the word shi, it is worth recalling, meant both "work" and "service" in
Zhou texts.3 The Shang kings' service ethic helps to explain the strength
of the state-service ethic of later times. These cultural expectations do
much to explain the remarkable successes of early Chinese culture and its
political institutions.
Efficient labor conscription in early China was both a sign of, and pre-
condition for, a strong state; it made power possible and it made power
desirable. The way in which Late Shang divination and kingship were
linked is nowhere better demonstrated than in the mobilization and
employment of the dynasty's manpower. Labor conscripts were the fun-
damental source of state power in ancient China. They served in armies,
built city walls and temple-palaces, excavated tombs, hauled supplies,
dug ditches, cleared and farmed the land in part, and worked at the sun-
dry tasks of production and manufacture that their lord required.5
2. "I will restrict the term 'ritual' to formalized, communicative action that relates its per-
former in some way to a superhuman power" (Lewis 1997:73); cf. Rappaport 1967:1-2, 4;
1999:24-26; Parkin 2001; Robbins 2001.
3. Karlgren 1964, no. 871; see too Schuessler 1987:548.
4. "The Late Shang state probably controlled at most much of Henan and some parts of
Hebei, Shandong, and Shanxi, all within the North China macroregion" (Thorp 2006:237).
5. On the Shang use of "organized large-scale exploitation of one group of people by
another," see K. C. Chang (1976b:56-57).
The appearance in Neolithic burials of tools-such as perforated, rect-
angular or oblong axes-finely made and polished, increasingly made of
jade, and revealing no traces of edge wear, suggests that their function
in graves, as in life, had been symbolic, emblematic of status and worth,
rather than merely utilitarian.6 These objects, more frequently found in
Eastern than in Heartland sites, and developing, if not originating, in the
Liangzhu culture area of the Yangzi delta during the fourth
to the third millennium, were idealized and symbolic tools rather than
tools themselves. It seems likely that the relatively large diameter of their
perforations was not functionally required to haft the blade, but symbol-
ized the extra work required to produce such impressive holes (Loehr
1975:8). Consider too the finely made stone axes, also showing no traces
of use, found in the five large wooden-coffin tombs, all with male grave
lords, excavated at the late Neolithic site (ca. 2200-1700 B.C.) of Taosi
in Shanxi. The tendency for these finely made tools to appear in high-
status Neolithic burials, and the assumption that they were emblems of
the authority to direct the work of others, tallies well with Guo Moruo's
hypothesis that the oracle-bone and bronze-inscription graph for fu X,
"father," was drawn with a hand holding an axe. A father could command
the service of others. "Working hardstones and jade, which seems to have
been negligible at Erlitou and Zhengzhou, reappears with considerable

graves" (Flad 2001:38). Childs-Johnson (1998a:40) stated that "amidst ritual yue-axes in buri-

with red lines painted to signify the position of hafting cords."

rigor," at Anyang, as "large blades and tokens of putative ritual use"
(Thorp 2006:153; cf. p. 162).
By the Late Shang state, the oracle-bone inscriptions permit us to delin-
eate the existence of a strategically significant system of labor mobiliza-
tion that was generalized and flexible in its personnel and applications.10
The inscribed oracle bone, one could argue, had come to supplement the
symbolic axe of the Neolithic chiefs. It was a new tool, primarily in reli-
gious mediation by privileged practitioners, to validate the ruler's ability
and authority to exploit the labor of others."
By the Eastern Zhou, the attraction and employment of the people was
to become one of the great concerns of statesmen and political thinkers.12
The Zhouli 周禮-a work compiled in the late Chunqiu 春秋 (722-479
B.C.) and the Zhanguo (453–221 B.C.) periods, but with some possi-
ble roots in the Western Zhou (1056-771 B.C.), 13-implicitly acknowledged
the importance of labor mobilization to the administration of the state;
the employment of the people was a major goal of the book's nostalgic,
utopian administration. For the Da Sima, the "Grand Supervisor
of the Horse," of the Zhou armies, "If there is a major expedition... at
the time for convoking them, he sets up the grand banner, assembles the
army's zhong, and punishes those who arrive late”(若大師...及至建大
#).4 The Simin E, the "Supervisor of the People," was
“in charge of submitting the numbers of the myriad people” (#\IEŻ
) to the ruler, who received them with respect and in turn "submitted
them to the Heavenly Treasury" (T).5 This fusion of administra-
tive and religious concerns was entirely characteristic of early Chinese
forms of labor mobilization. "If the state has a large-scale labor service
and assembles the multitude.”(若邦有大役聚眾庶 ... )16 was indeed

The upper classes used goods that were made by highly skilled, full-time artisans, often from
valuable, imported materials. The rural population continued to use goods that part-time

5
one of the major contingencies that the Zhouli's bureaucratic symmetries
addressed. Although, in the early transmitted texts, explicit comment
about labor mobilization is found chiefly in the clichéd remonstrances of
moralists, warning their rulers not to exhaust the people's strength," the
frequency of these warnings suggests that the work, though not system-
atically recorded in our sources, was being imposed. The urban centers of
Chunqiu China could not have been walled without it.18
The routine and large-scale employment of labor by a central bureau-
cracy and local elites was to become a typical characteristic of Chinese
civilization in the imperial age. Such labor, particularly in the Late Shang,
was not devoted to "public work" in the modern sense, with connotations
of "shared in common" or "for the public good," for, at the dynastic level,
there was no distinction between the private and public sectors. To a large
extent, the dynasty was the state, and the dynasty consisted of the ruling
elites and their dependents. There would have been few if any state inter-
ests opposed to the ruler's personal ones. Public work in the Late Shang
was primarily service under the king or his immediate retainers-at least
in the Shang oracle-bone inscriptions.19
The early state was a patrimonial, dynastic institution, the domain of
the ruler and his extended family.20 I generally follow Max Weber's treat-
ment of patrimonialism:21
Under patrimonialism the ruler treats all political administration as his
personal affair.... He empowers his officials from case to case, selecting
them and assigning them specific tasks on the basis of his personal confi-
dence in them and without establishing any consistent division of labor
among them.... The officials in turn treat their administrative work
for the ruler as a personal service based on their duty of obedience and
respect. (Bendix 1962:345)
I do not, however, accept the Weberian view that the patrimonial Shang
polity was characterized by a "pervasive combination of traditionalism
and arbitrariness."22 Shang "traditionalism" was not, I would suggest,
entirely "arbitrary," but would have been rational in cultural terms. "The
actions of the Shang kings," in particular,

1: Introduction
7
were restricted by the wishes of the ancestors, just as the powers of the
ancestors may have been restricted in turn by the quasi-contractual
sacrifices offered by the kings below. Ancestral worship served as a kind
of Shang "constitutionalism."23
Underhill notes that "the term 'ancestral rituals' is used here, in prefer-
ence to the term 'ancestor worship' commonly applied to cultures of East
Asia."24 "Scholars who research more than one area note that the central
focus is ritual relations with the dead, not worship of the dead." I con-
tinue to use "ancestor worship," and it is clear that a ritual exchange is
involved (Keightley 1978a:212-14). "Shang religious practice rested upon
the do ut des ('I give, in order that thou shouldst give') belief that correct
ritual procedure by the Shang kings would result in favors conferred by
Di" (Keightley 1978a:214-15)-or the ancestors.25
Sanctity especially takes the form of invoking, honoring, and placating
ancestral spirits who stand for the local group and its subgroups. Cer-
emonies honor the ancestor for their benevolent contributions to fertil-
ity, food production, and success in battle. Ceremonies serve to define
the local group and its corporate entities. (Johnson and Earle 2000:126,
emphasis in the original)
"Sacrifice presupposes communion (a reciprocal relationship) rather than
a 'one-sided mode of propitiation."'" 26 Sacrifice was a form of the commu-
nion, tying the living to the Powers, to the dead, to one another.
The Shang king used his authority and the power of his ancestors to
mobilize his officers and his population to improve the economy. There
is a great deal we can perceive only dimly about the nature of the Shang
economy itself-particularly the ties that bound the various participants.
But we still can document a great deal about the nuts and bolts of the
exchanges that took place.27
First and foremost, the Shang depended on agriculture, sending out
peasants and labor gangs to render the land fruitful.28 For example:

own people. And they also relied upon tribute payments in labor and pris-

29. Yong (?) was a Period I diviner, the name of a person or the name of a place; Y873.2-
74.1; JGWD:1235-36; Sōran, no. 1352. Unlike qi, which has been the subject of extensive
and continuing debate (see Glossary), the words bu and zhen, whose nature is dis-
cussed briefly in the Glossary, are so common that, after this first occurrence, I do not signal
the existence of their appendix entries by inserting the romanized terms in my translations
(see "Notes about the Sources"). I treat the terms for zhong, zhongren, and renin
the same way; they are also given in the Glossary.
30. I use "officers" loosely, to refer to members of the Shang administrative hierarchy.
Given the patrimonial nature of Shang administration, however, it is often impossible to sep-
arate official status from customary and kinship status; undoubtedly many Shang "officers"
were also members of the king's extended lineage, either by blood or by marriage.

TWO
The Work and the Workshops
33. Yang Shengnan 1992:88-90; 1992a. See further ch. 2, n. 109.
Groups of highly skilled artisans labored in the workshops of Middle and
Late Shang.
"Specialized production" has been defined at its most essential level
as "the production of substantial quantities of goods and services well
beyond local or personal need, and whose production is generally orga-
nized, standardized and carried out by persons freed in part from substi-
nence pursuits."¹1
The excavation of vast numbers of articles in bone, stone, shell, and jade,
often intricately carved; of high-grade pottery; of patterned textiles; of
ornate ritual bronzes; of light, elegant chariots and ornamental horse trap-
pings; of a wide range of axes, halberds, arrowheads, bows, and other
stone, bone, and bronze weapons and tools-all these finds indicate the
existence not only of a skilled artisan corps serving the needs of the rul-
ing elite but also of an efficient central management. A survey of the Late
Shang handicraft industries will suggest the range of activities that the
dynastic elites supported and coordinated.²
Bone Working
The Shang period was the golden age of Chinese bone working.³ Far more
arrowheads were made of bone than of bronze or stone, and although bone

hierarchal social formations known as complex or state-level societies to anthropologists and
political scientists and as civilizations to historians and art historians."

Working for His Majesty
did not compete with these materials in the production of heavy weapons
or agricultural implements, it was used extensively for objects of daily use
such as awls, spoons, needles, and hairpins, and even for the readying of
the oracle bones themselves.5 Workers employed harder diabase and lime-
stone to make edged tools employed in chopping and cutting; they made
single-edge harvesting tools out of slate. Further, the Shang stone workers
were masters of a variety of techniques such as sawing, chipping, pressure
flaking, hammering, pecking, polishing, and perforating (Cheng Te-k'un
1960:95). Another paper, also by Cheng Te-k'un (1957),
supports the idea that the Shang people had already invented a rotary
apparatus for stone cutting and carving, and that some metal tools were
used together with the neolithic saw. In recent archaeological finds, we
note that the smooth and regular concentric grooves on the sides of a
jade collared disc (pl. LXV:2) were probably carved by a bronze blade
with fine sand as abrasive while the disc was rotating. (Yeung Kin-fong
1987:189)
The remains of a bone workshop have been excavated ca. 150 m north of the
Middle Shang city wall at Zhengzhou. The workshop, which lay only
50 m north of the Zijingshan bronze foundry (see p. 21 herein), cov-
ered an area 19 m long and 1 m wide and contained over a thousand pieces
of worked and unworked bone, together with eleven grinding stones. The
dimensions of the workshop and the number of grinding stones suggest
a workforce of some twenty artisans. The remains show that arrowheads
and hairpins were both made here, so that military and civilian manufac-
ture took place side by side. In this workshop, half the bones worked were
human, but in general the most common sources of raw material were the
leg bones of cows, sheep, pigs, deer, dogs, chickens, and horses. Since the
Shang had domesticated all but one of these animals, 10 one may assume a
link between the dynastic herds and dynastic bone working.

2: The Work and the Workshops
11
Two Shang bone workshops have been found at Xiaotun, the site
of the Late Shang sacro-administrative center." As of the early 1990s, some
20,400 bone arrowheads had been found at Yinxu 12 compared to only
some 980 arrowheads made of bronze. Hairpins were second to arrow-
heads; ca. 1,590 have been found. 13 And some ca. 1,100 objects of ivory,
which was valued, have also been excavated (YXFX:314, 389, 391, 398).
The Dasikong cun
workshop was situated on the north bank
of the Huan River, its southwest edge close to the water. 14 The work-
shop area covered some 1,380 sq m, making it the largest workshop yet
found at Xiaotun. One pit dwelling (H410), which contained a large quan-
tity of bones and several bone-working tools, presumably served as one
of the workshops; its contents indicate that it came into operation during
the Late Shang period. That over 35,000 fragments of bone, half-finished
bone items, and bone refuse have been found in the twelve pits that sur-
rounded the workshop itself provides some sense of the activity that must
have taken place at the site.15
All told, 3,640 bone sickles and other artifacts were found between
1929 and 1932 alone (An Zhimin 1955:43), and over 24,000 bone tools were
excavated by 1994 (YXFX:383). An Zhimin comments that these Xiaotun
sickles were rather crudely made, perhaps because of the large scale on
which they were manufactured. It is generally supposed that these large
quantities of harvest tools had some connection to the agricultural labor-
ers of the royal house. Li Ji provides details: "only 237 of the tools were
finished. The rest were in various stages of production, with 244 nearly
complete."16 The finds do provide "evidence for some elite management
of agricultural production at Anyang" (Underhill and Fang 2004:134).
Bone-working tools found at the site included three bronze saws, four
bronze drills, and the remains of ten-plus grinding stones. The animals
that provided the bones (mainly leg bones, with a small number of rib
and pelvic bones) included cows, pigs, dogs, sheep, and deer, with cows
being the most numerous source. It is of interest that a measurement of

Working for His Majesty
the various bone blanks-strips (most commonly 2-2.4 cm wide, 4.6–9 cm
long) and squares (most commonly 2.5 cm to a side) 17-found at the site
suggests that this workshop mainly produced hairpins and that the arti-
sans were working to specific, predetermined size requirements. Mensu-
ration was at times critical to the Shang at Anyang.18
Another bone-working area also existed three kilometers west of Xiao-
tun, in the region south of Beixin zhuang (Kwang-chih Chang
1980:127). A semi-subterranean house (H5) and numerous pits that con-
tained bone materials were found in a 247 sq m area. In just one pit, over
20 m to the southwest of the house, there were over five thousand pieces of
bone, together with several bone-working tools, bone objects, and pottery
sherds. The excavations, carried out in 1959 and 1973, were incomplete,
but as at Dasikong cun, the finds included raw bones, half-completed bone
objects, and fairly complete bone-working tools. Cow and pig bones were
the most common materials; no human bones were used (An Zhimin, Jiang
Bingxin, and Chen Zhida 1961:69). Bone strips and awl shapes constituted
40 percent of the bone pieces found, small broken fragments 35 percent.
The former would have been suitable, it is thought, for making hair pins,
the latter for making hairpin caps.19 The several bronze tools found in the
house, which presumably served as a workshop, included two kinds of
saws, four bronze drills, three stone knives, one stone drill, and a good
number of grinding stones, some rough, some fine. 20 Once again one has
the sense of a flourishing and sophisticated bone industry at Xiaotun.21
The fact that additional raw bones and half-formed bone objects have
been found in the residence areas is one indication that the common peo-
ple also engaged in their own production of bone items.22 Finds included

2: The Work and the Workshops

many raw bone materials, half-formed and fully formed bone objects, and
various kinds of bone-working tools. The types of bone items were fairly
complete. They covered a wide range of categories: tools, utensils, weap-
ons, musical instruments, ornaments, carved art objects, and others. This
reflects the wide use of bone items in Late Shang daily life, warfare, and
hunting. In general, the tools, weapons, ornaments, and art objects came
from the large- and middle-sized tombs. The tools also came from resi-
dence areas (YXFX:383).
Stone and Jade Working
The skill of the Shang artisans, working in jade and stone, is indicated by
the variety of objects they manufactured, and by the fact that they regu-
larly made different tools out of different types of stone.23
Various types of materials—including jades, lacquer, shell, horn, and
even hides—were found.24 But as Kwang-chih Chang remarks:
The Shang lithic, bone, and shell inventory, consisting basically of pro-
duction implements used by the ordinary villagers, is again a Longshan
[] carryover. The only stone item that distinguished the Shang from
their Neolithic predecessors is the slate sickle, which was found in far
greater numbers at Shang sites than at Longshan sites. This indicates the
Shang's greater productivity rather than a new technology.25
The most numerous types of stone objects made were large stores of
semi-lunar and rectangular knives (referred to by the modern term, dao
JJ) and sickles (modern lian ).26 Pit E181 at Xiaotun, for example, con-
tained 444 stone knives. Pit B14 and the region immediately north of it
contained approximately 1,000 stone knives, together with carved stone
and jade, suggesting that this was the site of a stone workshop.27

single pit within the temple/palace complex contained a huge number of sickles and as such
constitutes evidence for state control of subsistence production."14
Working for His Majesty
Marble and jade carving also flourished in the Late Shang. As stone was
replaced by bone or bronze, implements made of stone tended to be used
only for the ceremonial or grave objects of the elites. Shang jade work-
ing also depended upon highly skilled craftsmen, who appear to have
developed a rotary disk for cutting, a drill point of extreme hardness, and
perhaps even a lathe.28 In 1975, in Xiaotun North, two small houses were
found for making jade and stone items. The houses contained large quan-
tities of whetstones, half-finished objects, broken jades, jade material, and
models of jade fish, soft-shell turtles, etc. 29 The skills involved in creat-
ing jade objects were challenging: selecting the raw materials, planning
for the carving, the carving itself, and then polishing of the final object;
this was a complicated process that only specialists could perform. Anal-
ysis shows the source of the jades was not in the vicinity of Anyang. It
is widely believed that the jades were, for example, from Liaoning F
and Xinjiang. Only the dynastic elites would have had the power to
transport these local products to Anyang and to convert them to various
kinds of jade objects in the royal workshops.30 As with the stone imple-
ments, early Shang jades served some practical purpose, while later Shang
jades were primarily ornamental and ceremonial.31 Some Shang stone and
jade objects were incised with one or two characters, usually to indicate
ownership.32
The Shang lithic industry did not become obsolescent with the devel-
opment of rival materials such as bronze, but concentrated instead on the
production of luxury items with high symbolic value for the ruling elite,
including ritual vessels, statues, and other implements made of stone and
jade, including jade representation of dragons, rings, cong tubes, scep-
ters, axes, combs, birds, fishes, tigers, turtles, water buffaloes, rabbits,
horses, statues, and other artifacts.33 They may be divided into (1) tools,
(2) ceremonial weapons, (3) objects of everyday life, (4) ritual objects,
(5) ornaments, (6) round scultures with flat bases, and (7) miscellaneous
items (Yeung Kin-fong 1987:189). Perhaps the artisans were left to design
the final forms, but we cannot tell.34
28. Cheng Te-k'un 1960:118–19, 120, 125; Chen Zhida 1986a; YXFX:323-54; 380-83; Lu
Liancheng 1996:146-50; Childs-Johnson, ed.: 2001:58-69; 2002:76-109.
29. Kaogu 1976:264-70; YXFX:440.
30. YXFX:440, 443; Yeung Kin-fong 1987:191.

"scepters" from the Neolithic till Middle Shang.

2: The Work and the Workshops
Pottery
15
15
The site of a pottery workshop has been excavated near Zhengzhou,
about 1,200 m to the west of the Middle Shang city wall: fourteen kiln
sites were found in an area of 1,250 sq m, together with numerous nearby
pits containing unfired and misfired pottery and pottery-making paddles
and anvils. 35 Assuming a minimum of five men to a kiln to form the clay
objects and feed the flames, a workforce of seventy or more is a reasonable
estimate for the workshop itself; a still larger force of clay and fuel gath-
erers would have been required. Rammed-earth foundations were found
near the kilns, and the houses were probably lived in by the potters; if so,
they enjoyed better housing than the common people who lived in pits
(Satō 1962:6). Further, the workers appear to have been highly special-
ized. Kwang-chih Chang (1963:152) concluded that, "since all of the pot-
tery found at this site is of fine clay texture, there were presumably other
kiln quarters for the manufacture of sand-tempered culinary ware, hard
pottery, glazed pottery, and/or white pottery."36
Other kilns have been excavated near Zhengzhou and among the Xiao-
tun and Xingtai settlements. 37 The most common type of Shang pottery
was the litripod, a cooking vessel whose form spread out of central
Shaanxi and southern Shanxi to both east and west during the late Long-
shan (Li Liu 1994:320) and in the Shang. 38 The comparative lack of deco-
ration-except on the white ware, which was decorated and appears to
have been developed to imitate the more valuable bronzes (Cheng Te-k'un
1960:154)-indicates that the pottery making was not a luxury industry
devoted only to the needs of the ruling elites. At Erlitou, Erligang, and
Xiaotun,
Local clays were used for potting. Some clay was refined to small parti-
cles (for example, for serving vessels), while other wares used for cooking
or storage required clay mixed with sand temper to resist heat and add
strength.

A variety of jars and crocks... are distinguished by differences in
shoulders, necks, and mouths, and sometimes lids.39
The Shang potters also made the earthenware molds for the casting of
bronzes.40 One of the triumphs of the Shang potters was the kanguo
(modern term) crucible, sometimes double-walled, that was probably
used for handling molten bronze.41 Thus, part of the potters' work was
geared to the dynastic bronze-casting industry.
Textiles
In textile manufacture, the Shang farmers cultivated both silk and hemp.42
The common people undoubtedly wove their own cloth, but the quality
textiles were produced in royal workshops.43 Fragments of patterned silk
have been found wrapped around buried grave goods, showing that silk
was used by the elite.
Fifty-plus ritual bronzes from the royal burial, M5-with the corpse of
Fu Hao, one of Wu Ding's consorts-have textile fragments stuck to
their surfaces, some single-layered, some multi-layered, with the area not
exceeding a few centimeters. Some seem to have been used to wrap the
vessels. Analysis showed five varieties. 45 And preserved fragments have
shown that Shang silk workers

remarks (1961:59), "foundry practice in China was a direct offspring of a pottery culture, a
highly advanced one in pre-bronze times."

were highly skilled in the art of reeling off the 800-1000 m. of long silk
from the cocoon after its immersion in hot water or steam. The method is
laborious. It requires patience and care. (Sylvan 1937:122)
Such silks, some with patterns woven into them,46 were presumably the
work of specialized craftsmen.
Bone inscriptions suggest that the Shang cultivated mulberries, and the
characters for silk and silkworm are thought by some to occur in the divi-
nations (Satō 1962:113). Many spindle whorls have also been found. It is
clear that mulberry cultivation, silkworm rearing, silk spinning, and the
actual weaving would have required coordination, some of which proba-
bly came from the king or his officers.47 "It seems safe to say that the graph
si or mi] originally meant silk thread and that the graph si showed
two skeins of silk thread."48
Wood Working
Wood working was also highly developed in the Late Shang.49 "In the
royal tombs, wood structures like coffins, pedestals for drums, racks for
musical stones, beams for coffin chambers and chariot parts, which have
decayed and disappeared, have nevertheless left their imprints in the
soil." Some of these items were skillfully inlaid with shell and colored
stones (Cheng Te-k'un 1960:238).
The impressions left in the soil show that skilled carpenters provided
luxury and ceremonial items that, since they found a place in the royal
tombs, may also have served the king. The wooden outer and inner cof-
fins served the industry of death; they would have absorbed a part of the
artisans' energies in the services of the ancestral cult (YXFX:409-10). At
the same time, we know that the many stone sickles unearthed at Xiaotun
originally had handles made of wood, although these have long since dis-
integrated (Cheng Te-k'un 1960:99). Since a significant number of the stone
blades were probably made by royal workers, the same may also have been
46. Sylvan 1937:123. See too Chen Juanjuan 1979; Gao Hanyu, Wang Rencao, and Chen

true of the handles, indicating large-scale production of a standardized
wooden item. Bronze was not mainly used for agricultural implements.50
The triumph of the Shang woodworkers was undoubtedly the great war
machine of the age- the light, two-horse chariot. 51 The selection and sea-
soning of the right types of wood-the building of the spoked wheels, the
necessity for bronze chariot fittings such as axle caps, cross-bar finials, rain
frontals and terminals, and decorative plaques, the application of coats of
lacquer, and the making of leather and rope harness-show how the build-
ing of these vehicles, though probably not in large numbers, 52 required the
cooperation of a considerable number of skilled workers under the direc-
tion of a central manager. And the royal chariot burials suggest that the
economic power to coordinate and support the chariot makers and related
artisans was concentrated in and around the royal court.

Bronze Casting
Many hundreds of inscribed Shang ritual vessels have been unearthed.56
Already, in 1960, Cheng Te-k'un (1960:186) had arrived at the 2,000-plus
estimate of inscribed bronzes. 57 "It is estimated there probably are over ten
thousand inscribed bronzes dating from the pre-Qin period which have
been recorded. . . . Possibly around a quarter of them date from the Shang
period" (Qiu Xigui 2000:62)- about 2,500 of them. Many more are con-
stantly coming out of the ground. "The earliest inscriptions in bronze ves-
sels seem to date from the first century of the Anyang period."58 The pres-
ence of inscriptions cast into the vessels, of the form,

indicates they were manufactured at the command of the ruling elites, who
honored their ancestors-and occasionally their ancestresses (Keightley

a large fangding [], one might infer royal patronage or control of this
foundry" (Thorp 2006:142). Nor was bronze making restricted to ritual
vessels.
A still greater number of bronze secular objects were in use; tools such
as socketed axes, adzes and chisels, chariot fittings in many forms, horse
harnesses and trappings, ornaments and jingles, graded musical knock-
ers and bells, armour such as helmets, shield bosses or buttons for mili-
tary garb, and weapons including spear points, lances, bows and arrows,
broad axes, war axes, and the ge [*].61
Among the bronzes actually found from Shang times, weapons were very
numerous. This shows there were probably other workshops for weapons
production, but perhaps such workshops were not needed for weapons.
Weapons production was more "local" (YXFX:440).
The search for the copper, tin, and lead sources may have contributed
to the Shang expansion and contraction. 62 In Shanxi (at Dahangou
#), Hubei (Tonglushan), and Jiangxi (Tongling) mining sites
have been found, probably for the Shang.63

The hypothesis of a shift of copper sources from the north to the south
is supported by the results of lead isotopic analyses of Shang bronzes,
because a considerable number of bronzes unearthed from major cen-
tres in the north were made with alloys obtained from the south. For
examples, the lead isotopic samples taken from several Erligang bronzes
from Yanshi and Zhengzhou derived from the southern region of the
distribution chart. Likewise, the results of the isotopic analysis on the
twelve bronzes from the Fu Hao tomb at Yinxu of the late Shang period

indicates that the metal sources were mainly from the Tonglingshan
area. 64
One can divide the bronze into six major categories: ritual vessels,
musical instruments, tools and daily utensils, weapons, ornaments and
artworks, and miscellaneous. And the ritual vessels can be further divided
into cooking vessels, food vessels, ale vessels (see the entry for you in
the Glossary), water vessels, and vessels for pouring ale. 65 At Miaopu
North (Chen Zhida 1986:273), for example, the great number of
molds were for ritual vessels-like ding, gu, zhi, gui, gong,
etc.--with a relatively small number of molds for weapons and tools.
Two important foundry sites have been found in the Zhengzhou region,
one north of Zijingshan, about 100 m north of the Middle Shang city wall,
the other about 500 m south of it, below the South Gate, Nanguan .66
The fragments of earthenware molds were found at first site, together
with a complete mold for a knife, and actual bronze arrowheads and hal-
berds (Zhao Quangu et al. 1957:56). The second, southern site covered an
area of about 1,050 square meters and included the remains of three kinds
of clay or pottery crucibles, together with bronze vessels, bronze slag, and
charcoal fragments. Over 1,000 pieces of broken earthenware molds for
making knives, hoes, or picks, and various vessels such as li, jia, and
jue, have been found. Some molds were designed to produce from five
to seven arrowheads at one pouring.67 The molds for the arrowheads were
most numerous, followed by those for hoes or picks (Zhao Quangu et al.
1957:56). This bronze-casting site shows that the same workshop-and,
presumably, the same group of craftsmen-produced bronzes for both
military and civilian use. The large number of molds for hoes or picks,
in fact, suggests that these implements, like the stone sickles of Xiaotun
(p. 13 above), were also to be used by agricultural or construction workers
under, directly or indirectly, the ruler's direction.

SK22
222
Working for His Majesty
By Middle and Late Shang, numerous bronze-casting centers, asso-
ciated with powerful regional polities, had developed in many parts of
China.
In the time of the Anyang kings the manufacture of bronze vessels was
spread over a wider region than ever before, but nowhere within that
region was there any very large area of uniform style. There was no
Anyang horizon; on the contrary, this was a time of flourishing local
industries.68
The Shang were not the only elites who mobilized their workers on this
large-scale, industrial basis.69 But as Li Liu and Xingcan Chen (2006:165)
note, that in casting ritual bronze vessels, "among its contemporary sites
Erlitou is the only locale that yielded evidence for the manufacture of
ritual vessels with multi-piece mold techniques." The evidence suggests
that many Shang settlements possessed a small number of workshops. But
we find only in the major urban complexes a cluster of all workshop types.
This suggests that the corporations of workers attached to the Shang rul-
ing house may also have been separated into villages.70 Whether the local
workshop at Shili miao in Henan (Kwang-chih Chang 1980:59)
and those elsewhere were controlled by the royal house, or, more prob-
ably by the local Shang lords, cannot at present be known, but given the
difficulties of communication and travel in North China at this time, it
seems likely that Shang control would frequently have been nominal and
symbolic. It is clear, however, that the management of these workshops-
especially the bronze foundries-required a degree of centralized control
over both resources and workers."
Remains of bronze foundries have been found near Xiaotun. The indus-
try was a flourishing and important one. 72 The area around pit B15 has

351-59).

Barnard (1963:252-55) and Tan Derui (1999) give an account of bronze-casting and metal-

2: The Work and the Workshops
23
yielded over a hundred bronze molds and several dozen crucibles.73 One
kilometer southeast of Xiaotun in the area known as Miaopu North, exten-
sive remains of Late Shang bronze casting, together with an oblong house
foundation, have been found in a 10,000 square meter area; the remains
include pieces of crucible, inner and outer molds, clay models, and slag.
Another area containing pottery molds and casting remains was found
only 400 meters away.74 Foundry remains have also been found at Shilipu
+in Nanyangxian in southern Henan, some 300 k south-south-
west of Xiaotun, and also at other places.75
The relatively large size of the labor force is further indicated, for the
Late Shang, by relating the size of the bronze vessels to the capacity of the
crucibles found. For example, the Si Mu Wu fang ding, which
weighed some 875 kg, the largest bronze of the Shang dynasty, would
have required a particularly large foundry force." The Si Mu Xin fang ding
, excavated from M5, the tomb of Fu Hao, weighs about 128
kg; it has been dated to the reign of Wu Ding." Since the large crucibles
that have been found have a capacity of about 25 kilograms, over 35 such
crucibles would have been required for the almost simultaneous pouring
of the molten metal of the Si Mu Wu fang ding; crucibles of smaller capac-
ity would have been used in still larger numbers. Assuming three to four
men for each crucible, it is easy to envisage a workforce of ca. 150 men in
the foundry alone.78
As Bagley has noted:
The sheer weight of bronze which survives from the Shang and Zhou
periods demonstrates that economy of raw material was not a pressing
consideration in Chinese foundries.
