Working for His Majesty
2: The Work and the Workshops
25
25
The weight of the Si Mu Wu fang ding... testifies also that Shang work-
shops were organized on a scale exceedingly large by the standards of the
rest of the ancient world. In Shang China there is no trace of the indepen-
dent artisan who in the West might supply all the metal goods required
by a typical Bronze Age community."⁹
The founding, moreover, was only part of a more extensive effort.
Noel Barnard, among others, has described in detail the steps involved
in bronze making in the Anyang region; a summary of the procedures is
given here. (1) Workers extracted copper ore from mines which lay at a
considerable distance from the cult center; (2) they also collected tin ore
(mainly alluvial); (3) they also mined lead ore; (4) workers collected wood
and turned it into charcoal for the primary and secondary smelting; (5)
workers freed the copper ore from the gangue at the mine site and then (6)
smelted it at the mine; (7) workers smelted the stream tin to produce a rela-
tively pure metal; (8) workers then transported the copper ore to Anyang, a
journey that would have required twenty to thirty days or more; (9) work-
ers also transported the tin ore and (10) the lead ore and (11) the charcoal
to Xiaotun; (12) workers then refined the ores still further at the foundry;
(13) at the same time, the artisans who worked in the pottery industry
had molded and baked carefully polished clay models of the vessels to be
cast,80 (14) they built sectional molds around the model and produced a
core by whittling down a clay cast of the vessel; (15) the bronze casters now
alloyed the molten ores to form bronze by placing them in crucibles that
were in turn placed in furnaces provided with bellows to provide drafts of
forced air; (16) the bronze casters then poured the molten bronze from one
or more crucibles into the prepared mold; (17) finally, the cast-bronze ves-
sel, after it had cooled, was finished by hand tooling and polishing.81
It is obvious that some central control must have directed this large
force of miners, wood gatherers, charcoal burners, porters, model makers,
79. Bagley 1987:17, 18; see too 47, n. 29.
80. "Preparing clay for bronze casting molds is not too different from preparing clay for
pottery. Both have to go through steps such as levigation, kneading, aging, souring, and tem-
pering" (Yung-ti Li 2003:74); also Liu Yu et al. 2008:82–86.
81. Barnard 1961:48-59, 65, 87. See too Barnard and Satō 1975:27-68; Franklin 1983; 1983a;
Bagley 1987:15-64; 1999:136-58; 2009; Ledderose (2000:1; cf. 25-49): "the Chinese devised
production systems to assemble objects from standardized parts. These parts were pre-
fabricated in great quantity and could be put together quickly in different combinations,
creating an extensive variety of units from a limited repertoire of components." Yung-ti Li
(2003:60), in his brief account of the replication experiment of Wan Jiabao (Li Ji and Wan Jia-
bao 1972:21), described the following bronze casting steps: model carving; firing of model;
preparing clay slabs for mold sections; applying clay slabs onto the model; trimming mold
sections and preparing mortises and tennons; withdrawing mold sections; firing mold sec-
tions; scraping down the model to be used as core; assembling mold sections and the core;
casting; retrieving the bronze by breaking open the mold; and retouching and finishing the
bronze. See also Liu Yu et al. 2008.
and smelters, working in widely separated locations at a series of tasks
that required careful scheduling. If we arbitrarily assign 100 men each to
mining, fuel collecting, and transport, we arrive at a regular force of no
less than 450-plus men serving each foundry. "Managing huge quanti-
ties of bronze, clay, and firewood involved logistical problems of great
magnitude. No less demanding was the organization of the workforce"
(Ledderose 2000:49).
There is as yet little archaeological evidence to indicate who these
laborers were, how they worked, or even how they lived.82 Some of the
unskilled ones, such as the fuel gatherers and porters, may well have been
recruited from the ren A. Some of the more skilled craftsmen, such as
those employed in the making of the clay models and in the bronze found-
ries, may have been drawn from, or directed by, the zhong, the depen-
dents permanently attached to the royal house (see ch. 4). The fact that
a goodly number of Shang bronzes had inscriptions cast or incised into
them indicates that certain foundry workers may have been semi-literate.
That is to say, they must have at least understood the words of the dedi-
cation sentences commonly used in Shang bronze inscriptions, such as
"father," "grandfather," "made," the vessel names, and the gan + cyclical
characters of the temple names. A good proportion of burials had a vessel
with inscriptions.83 It was evidently important for a man (or a woman) to
be labelled or identified when going into the ground, becoming an ances-
tor, perhaps with one to four characters (with two to three most common),
with lineage symbols, statelet names, and personal names. 84 It also seems
probable that the laborers, both skilled and unskilled, were the king's or
the high elites' workers who performed these tasks throughout much of
the year as their assigned and dependent function, and that they enjoyed
some status benefits as a result.
The great advances made in the skill and precision of casting can be
seen in the complex and ornate ritual vessels of Late Shang. 85 Here again
we find advances in industrial technique linked to the needs of the rul-
ing elites, the only group that could afford to manufacture and use these
prestigious, luxury articles.86 The M5 evidence for ternary alloy vessels,
composed of copper, lead, and tin, indicates that the bronze casters delib-
82. Yung-ti Li 2003 and Haapanen 2005 provide an admirably detailed account of the
archaeological data, its strengths and its weaknesses.

Working for His Majesty
erately controlled the alloy content of their vessels; this shows that the
technology had already been developed under Wu Ding (YXFX:257). But
the technological skill of the craftsmen was presumably matched by the
administrative skill of the controlling, directing elites.
Cowries: Bone and Bronze
Since practically all agricultural and domestic tools and vessels were
made of wood, stone, clay, and bone, it is clear that the use of bronze arti-
facts was a monopoly of the ruling elites.87 Bronze was a semi-precious
metal, its intrinsic value much dependent on the labor input required to
mine and work it. The value of bronze is also indicated by the bronze cow-
ries found in various Shang tombs; it is possible that these were cast as
some form of currency.88 It is worth noting that the burials containing the
bronze cowries were all of the Yinxu late period, suggesting an advance
in the commercial economy by that time. The items found at Xiaotun were
not all local products; some were tribute payments from statelets, some
were booty, some were exchange items.89
Nobles had large stores of natural cowries; there were close to 7,000
cowrie shells in Fu Hao's tomb; 3,000 cowrie shells had been found at
tomb M1 in Sufutun, Shandong; 112 real cowries and 109 bronze
ones had been found at Baode (xian in Shanxi. One inscription found
at Hougang records a gift of twenty cowries, in a temple, which the
recipient used to make a ding for Fu Gui. Various finds of natural
cowries were also observed in more "common" burials, but bronze cow-
ries were very rare at Xiaotun.90

2: The Work and the Workshops
The Emergence of Writing
27
There is certainly some association between the products of early work-
shops and the emergence of what may have been early forms of notation,
if not writing, but it is not yet possible to demonstrate any particular link
between labor organization and written records."
Several signs have been found at the so-called "Early Shang" site of Erli-
tou (ca. 16th century B.C.) in north Central Henan, generally incised on
various pots (Xiaoneng Yang 1999:84-87).... A few more symbols and
graphs have been found on "Middle Shang" artifacts, from sites like Erli-
gang (16th and 15th centuries B.C.); some of them may have been precur-
sors of oracle-bone graph forms-inscriptions have been found on three
bone fragments-but different systems of signs appear to have been
involved at different sites (Kwang-chih Chang 1980:268–269, 305-306).
And few if any of the Erligang ritual bronzes-the new symbols of pres-
tige-were being cast with inscriptions (Bagley 1999:182, n. 92). As Qiu
Xigui (2000:42) has concluded, "all the written material from the early
Shang discovered thus far is both scant and fragmentary... and is of
minor value to our study of the formative process of Chinese writing."92
A number of pieces of Shang pottery found at Xiaotun were marked
with symbols, most of which may be identified with characters found in
the oracle-bone inscriptions. Some were incised after firing, probably by
the user, but others were made prior to firing and must have been written
by the potters. Some of the symbols-such as numerals, position words,
or "right," "center," "left"-relate perhaps to the number of pieces in a
set or to the position they should occupy in a ritual performance (see,
for example, Moriga 2003). Characters were also found on pots, generally
close to the lip and mainly only a single graph or symbol.94 They may be

divided into numbers, positions (left, center, right), names of people, pic-
tographs, ganzhi, hexagrams, and others (YXFX:250-55).
Very probably the names were often a mark of ownership or the nature
of the contents of a vessel.95 Characters were inscribed on certain bone
objects and ornaments. A hairpin, to cite an example, was inscribed with
three characters,, "Xi sent in two (pins)." Although it is possible
that the donors, anxious to receive credit for their donation, had inscribed
the characters prior to sending in the pins, the general Shang practice of
recording such tribute payments on their oracle bones (Keightley 1978:11–
12) makes it possible that the characters would have been inscribed after
the pins had been received at Xiaotun. In any event, making a record of
their provenance evidently mattered, either to the donors or the recipi-
ents. Other marks, however, were pictures of a fish, turtle, dog, dragon,
snake, and chariot (Li Ji 1956:123-25, pls. 61-63). They might well have
served as "emblems" or "clan insignia."97 The workers were in at least an
early stage of functional literacy and would certainly have been more lit-
erate than the peasantry below them.98
The Workshops
Bronze, pottery, and bone artifacts were being produced in Erlitou Phase
I. In Phases II to IV, "thick deposits of slag, remains of crucibles, clay
molds, and remains of casting" have been found to the south of the site,
with bone workshops to both the north and the east (Li Liu and Xingcan
Chen 2003:58, 59, 60).
Bronze-casting activities were situated in high concentration and in a
close proximity to the palatial area, and Erlitou bronze vessels are pri-
marily found within the Erlitou site. This suggests that the production
and distribution of ritual bronzes were clearly controlled by the high elite
of the Erlitou state.⁹9
95. Cao Jiandun 2006; Keightley 2006:184-85.
96. Hu Houxuan 1944b:10b; Chen Mengjia 1956:45; YXFX:383; cf. Bagley 2004:214-16.

2000:98-102, 115-18 (Yang Xiaoneng 2008:182-89, 219-23); Keightley 2006:184-85.
98. Some early writing, of the same general character as that on the oracle bones, has been
found inscribed on two sheep or goat scapula from the Neolithic site of Shijia in Huan-

fragmentary, most of the cracks now lost. The fragments remaining have traces of man-made
incised graphs or symbols. Among the characters that might be present are da or bu; the
incisions were fairly deep, the strokes straight and well made. The second bone has another

A series of bone, pottery, and bronze workshops have also been exca-
vated near Zhengzhou. They all fall within what is known as the Lower
and Upper Erligang phases, 100 that is to say, within the Early and Middle
Shang periods. This suggested to K. C. Chang that "the bronzesmiths of
the Shang dynasty lived in stamped-earth houses and thus seem to have
enjoyed a higher privilege than the common folk who... [lived]... in
subterranean houses" (1963:152). We have already seen how the potters
of Zhengzhou lived—or at least worked-in similarly privileged housing.
The four houses associated with the foundry each had two rooms, each
room with an earth platform by the door and a hearth; the houses were
arranged according to a definite plan, separated by a distance of ten to
eleven meters. Chang speculated
that the bronzesmith's families were of the extended type, and that their
households belonged to one and the same patrilineage, a conclusion in
complete agreement with the historic records concerning the lineage-
occupation linkage.101
A significant number of bronze foundry and bronze workshop remains
have now been excavated in the Xiaotun area. These would include the
foundries at Miaopu North 苗圃北,102 Xiaomintun West 孝民屯西,103 Xuejia
zhuang 104 and Xiaotun Northeast.105 The Miaopu North
finds suggest the kinds of information that archaeology can currently pro-
vide about such workshops. Representing one of the largest workshop
remains found to date, the materials cover an area of over 10,000 sq m at
Miaopu North. The production area appears to have been situated in the
western sector, while houses (living quarters) were found in the eastern
sector, about 25 sq m apart, where twenty-plus house foundations have
been found. It is thought that these houses, with mold fragments found
in some, might have been workshops for mold making or bronze casting.
The early houses averaged some 10 m square in area, the later were larger,
many with an area of ca. 20 m square. The scale of the workshop activi-
ties was small in Yinxu 1 and grew larger in Yinxu 2, 3, and 4. Miaopu
North was the remains of a large bronze-casting workshop that focused

I am frustated by my inability to link the oracle-bone worker, like the Duo
Gong, to the handcraft evidence; "the data from the Anyang foundries
do not provide definite answers about production organization" (Yung-ti
Li 2003:299). But evidently such a topic was not much in need of divina-

sels was designed for the cult of the ancestors; there was no misfortune.
The Products
The evolution from plain items of practical use in the Early Shang to
highly decorated and skillfully worked ceremonial and ornamental items
in the Late Shang, which may be found in the bone, stone, jade, and bronze

industries, indicates both growing technical skills on the part of the arti-

the part of the consuming class of warrior and other elites. Dong Zuobin
had characterized the remarkable wealth of the Shang rulers:
In their handicrafts they used such materials as gold, tin, cinnabar and
turquoise, brought from southern countries across the Yangzi River; they
had beautiful gems from Khotan in Central Asia, cowries from the South
Seas, whale bone from the Pacific, large tortoise from the Malay Penin-

And the contents of the storage pits reveal the way in which wealth was
accumulated by the ruling class. Pit E181 near Xiaotun, for example, con-
tained 57 types of objects, including 1,927 pottery fragments, 760 pieces of
animal bone, 4 animal skulls, the remains of 44 bone objects, 16 bone awls,
18 bone arrowheads, 254 pieces of shell, the remains of 78 shell objects,

1 musical stone, 1,179 turtle shells, 163 cowries, the remains of 30 bronze
vessels, 14 bronze arrowheads, 11 pieces of jade, 4 deer antlers, and so

known, M5, the relatively small but unlooted tomb of Fu Hao, one of Wu
Ding's consorts, contained "enormous quantities of grave goods," includ-
ing 468 bronzes, which weighed more than one-and-a-half metric tons.111

"112
Working for His Majesty
second-largest group consists of 40 jue []." The objects from E181, com-
ing from a storage pit, may represent a fairer estimate of the normal liv-
ing standards of the elite in the cult center. The ruling class of Late Shang,
in any event, due to its ability to accumulate raw materials and transform
them into manufactured articles, was rich.
This wealth involved both the authority to conscript labor gangs
staffed by the peasantry and the ability to mobilize and maintain a large
group of dependent laborers and conscripts, and to coordinate their activ-
ities. The complexity of Shang labor organization may be indicated by
listing some of the ways in which the royal industries inter-locked. In the
first place, there was a large demand by different industries for the same
raw materials. Wood was required by woodworkers such as the carpen-
ters, toolmakers, chariot makers, coffin makers, and bell-stand makers;113
potters needed it to fire their kilns; miners needed it for their pit props
and to make the charcoal used in refining the ores and the final pour-
ings. Clay was needed by the potters, but also by the bronze makers for
their models, molds, and crucibles. In the second place, the various indus-
tries depended heavily on each other. Wood gatherers and woodwork-
ers depended on stone masons to provide them with their tools. Stone
masons and woodworkers cooperated to produce the sickle blades and
handles used by the laborers that farmed the royal lands (see ch. 12). Pot-
ters not only produced wares for daily use but had to provide crucibles
for the bronzesmiths. Bronze casters had to provide chariot fittings for
the chariot makers, weapons for the warriors. It was, in short, a relatively
sophisticated and complex economy, centered on the royal house and rul-
ing elites. And just as the oracle-bone inscriptions will indicate the fac-
tors that were involved in the king's decision-making, this résumé of the
economic linkages between the various working groups provides further
evidence for the complex management in which the king's officers must
themselves have been engaged.
112. Ledderose 2000:45; see too Rawson 1993:811.
113. Kong Deming (2007) observes that the main category was carpenter's tools.
THREE
The Artisan Corps
The Status of the Artisans
The archaeological data indicate the existence of groups of highly skilled
artisans, clustered around the royal house and ruling class. Their resi-
dences and workshops appear to have been of higher quality than the pit
dwellings of other members of the society, and their high degree of techni-
cal skill must also have set them above the common people.¹ Presumably,
the artisans or craftsmen were organized in such a way—either as occu-
pational corporations, clans, or village units (see ch. 8)-that their skills
could be handed on without loss. The archaeological evidence also sug-
gests that some of the artisanate was at least semi-literate, another sign of
high, "official," status (see p. 25).
The status of the artisans was varied. As Li Liu has noted (2003:8), the
traditional view in which "a sharp distinction" existed
between consumers and producers of prestige goods, represented by
elite patrons and commoner artisans who were categorized as attached
craftsmen (Costin 1991; Earle 1987),... has been challenged in recent
years.
In the Neolithic, at least, she proposes (Li Liu 2003:14) that
it may have been the same elite groups who held the cosmological knowl-
edge, had access to the raw material, and controlled the production and
distribution of finished products.
The increasing specialization and even industrialization of Late Shang man-
ufacturing, however, together with the increasing stratification of Bronze
1. For an early, and now dated, review of the evidence, see Keightley 1969:39-65. One may
now consult Kwang-chih Chang 1980:95, 98; YXFX:439-41; Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan,
eds., 1999:579-82; Shen Zhiyu 2001:283-93; Li Liu 2003; Yung-ti Li 2003; Underhill and Hui
Fang 2004; Haapanen 2005.34
Working for His Majesty
3:
The Artisan Corps
[7]
癸未卜: 又回百工
35
Age society, would have encouraged among the artisans a wider range of
statuses than would have existed in the Neolithic.
The archaeological evidence from Erlitou Phases II-III (ca. 1800-1600
B.C.) has suggested to Li Liu and Xingcan Chen (2003:81) the emergence
of "a specialized, full-time labour-force with a centralized system of
organization." And by the Anyang stage,
skilled workers who made prestige goods such as bronze vessels and
jade objects probably had higher status than others. A house interpreted
as a workshop for stone and jade objects at Xiaotun had rammed-earth
floors and painted murals and contained bronze projectile points and
turquoise inlaid hairpins.³
In short, in all forms of labor by the dynastic state, "Service with the
nobles... by these urban dwellers and 'suburban' peasants constituted
them as a 'privileged' minority vis-à-vis their rural fellows" (Lewis 1993:
329). The Duo Gong and the zhong, for example, were probably part of the
"urban" population.
The Duo Gong I
I start the discussion of particular Shang officers with a group of officers
whose name, tantalizingly, may link them to the administration and the
practice of artisanal activities.4 The term Gong was not recorded in the
Shang bronze inscriptions (Li Lingpu et al. 2001: vol. 1, p. 115), with one
possible exception. 5 The Gong I or Duo Gong I of Late Shang were evi-
dently the forerunners of the Gong I and Bai Gong I of Western Zhou.6
The term Bai Gong does in fact occur once in Shang inscriptions:
2. Cf. Li Liu (2006a:189; cf. 169-76): "Erlitou was a political, economic, and ritual center."

that we cannot be certain of the meaning of the verb; this is a problem inscription. Yong Ji,
Making cracks on guiwei (day 20): "Will there be something
ominous (huo) for the Bai Gong." (I-II) (Tunnan 2525)
The title, Bai Gong, is usually translated as "the Hundred Artisans" or
"The Many Officials" (see Gong in the Glossary). Hence the Duo Gong
would provisionally be understood as "The Many Artisans" or "The Many
Officials." These two groups should have belonged to the handicraft
workers of the royal house. And the many fine bronzes, jades, and ivory
objects, found in the burials of the royal cemetery and those of the royal
lineage in Xiaotun, may have been produced by these Duo Gong and Bai
Gong (YXFX:440). This is plausible.⁹
Since the earliest known form of the gong graph was gong = (see the
Glossary entry), it is worth asking if the graph and the contexts in which it
was used can reveal anything about the function-and hence the name-
of the officers who bore it.
The Shang apparently used the graph in three ways: (1) equivalent to
the later form gong I, "artisan, work, worker, official"; (2) to write the
words gong IX, "attack," and/or gong J, "achievement";9 (3) in the com-
pound gong dian I, a ritual phrase that might mean "present the tab-
lets." (See the Glossary entries for these three terms.)
Unfortunately, I do not believe that we can yet determine the Late
Shang meaning of gong I with certainty. Here, I would propose as a
working hypothesis that the character 4 had a core meaning of "strike";
that the graph depicted some sort of implement for striking, chipping, or
hitting; that much artisanal work of Shang times did involve striking-
either the chipping of stone or bone or the hewing and shaping of wood;
that since, as we shall see, there is reason to believe that the Gong-officers
of Shang may have been associated with the music of the temple-palace,10
according to later sources, was the younger brother of Xiao Jia (K6) (Shima 1958:70–71;
Zhou Hongxiang 1958:92-93: Chavannes, tr., 1967:1:189-90; JGWD:1557-58; Sōran, no. 2046;
Nienhauser 1994:46). It would seem strange that a sacrifice be addressed to the junior mem-
ber of an ancestral line, seven or more generations distant.
7. Or following the view of later commentators that Gong, guan ye I, t, "Gong is an
official, officer," we might arrive at "the Hundred Officials" (ZWDZ, p. 4493, no. 8911.10).

the striking may also have involved the striking with a mallet of musi-

stones made of jade or marble, wooden drums, and bells made of pottery
or bronze, have been found."
Eighteen different musical instruments are mentioned in oracle bones
inscriptions, including wind, stringed and percussion instruments.
Stone chimes were of two types in the Shang dynasty: teqing (single
chimes) and bianging (chime sets).
Xun could be played in pairs and larger combinations... three xun
were discovered in the tomb of Lady Fu Hao. These range in size and
all have five holes. They were unearthed together with five bianging, five
biannao [] and 18 tongling [], suggesting that these instruments
performed together as a group.
Shang dynasty musical instruments and dance props have been exca-
vated in Henan, Hebei, Shandong, Hubei, Jiangxi, Sichuan and Inner
Mongolia.12
They were widespread.

(see Y1118.1-2). The title of Gong itself, however, appears rather more fre-
quently.13 Since the translation of gong as "strike" is far from certain, how-
ever, I shall not refer to them as "The Many Strikers" or "The Strikers" but
will simply continue to romanize their title.

3: The Artisan Corps
37
There is, unfortunately, no certain way to link the Duo Gong of the
oracle-bone inscriptions with the artisans who produced the sophisticated
artifacts of Late Shang. Whether the Duo Gong were themselves artisans,
or whether they were the officers, the artisan-masters who directed the
artisans, or whether they were unconnected with any kind of artisanal
activity is unclear. The traditional connotation of gong as "worker" or
"artisan," together with the fact that the Duo Gong of Shang were the fore-
runners of the Bai Gong, "The Hundred Artisans," of Zhou, does suggest
some role for the Duo Gong in the handicraft industries of Shang. The bone
inscriptions, however, indicate a more generalist, non-specialized role for
the gong, from which the more limited artisanal role of later times may
have emerged.
Groups of Late Shang officers such as the Ma, "Horse(-chariot offi-
cers)" (see Glossary for Ma), the She, "Archers," and the Quan, "Dog
(officers)," also appear in the inscriptions as the Duo Ma, "Many
Horse(-chariot officers)" (Y642.1-2), the Duo She , "Many Archers"
(Y1010.2), and the Duo Quan, "Many Dog (officers)" (Y606.2).14 Duo
Gong, therefore, was similarly a collective title, "The Many Gong (offi-
cers)," for the Gong. A natural and important question is: how many was
many? No figures are given for the Duo Gong in the bone inscriptions. The
Ma and the She, however, who served in the army, were divided into units
of left, right, and center, with one hundred Horse-chariot officers or one
hundred Archers to a unit or three hundred personnel to a full comple-
ment (Chen Mengjia 1956:513), as in [73AB],15 [222], and the following:
[8A]
以三百射
"... to take three hundred Archers."
[8B]
貞:勿令以三百射
Divined: "(We) should not order Qin (Qin) to take three
hundred Archers." (I. Bin) (*Heji 5769f)
It is possible, accordingly, that the Gong were similarly structured, but this
is not certain since, unlike the Horse-chariot officers and the Archers, they
do not seem to have been primarily a military group.16
It is reasonable to suppose that the titles of many Shang officers, at least
in their origin, were functional. Thus, the Duo Ma were presumably

involved with the horse chariots, the Duo Quan were involved with

It seems likely, therefore, that the Gong officers took their name from a
tool or instrument, be it a striker or a carpenter's square (see Glossary),
with which they were associated, and this would once again indicate an
artisanal or technical role. But even if such a function were at the cen-
ter of their activities, or at the genesis of their titular role, the divinations
reveal that, like so many officers of Shang, there was no precise correlation
between function and title, and that the Duo Gong were general-purpose
officers serving the king and the royal temple-palaces.
Divinations indicate, in the first place, that the king valued his Gong-
officers and was concerned about their activities:

Such divinations about calamity afflicting the Gong-officers tell us
that they operated within the general universe of Shang religious con-
cerns; unfortunately, however, they tell us little about the Gong's specific
activities, about whether they were involved in the bronze-casting activi-
ties, for example, or involved in enemy attacks, or exposed to danger of
another sort. They tell us only that the Gong formed part of the polit-
ico-religious universe which directly affected the well-being of the Shang
kings and about which the Shang king felt he could legitimately question
the Powers.18 The Gong, in other words, were an object of the king's con-

20. The notation "(K12)" indicates that Zu Yi, the king in question, was the twelfth king to

21. Hsü Chin-hsiung and Ward (1984:154) believe it is possible that the gong referred to
the playing of musical instruments.40
"The Many Strikers"
Working for His Majesty
Inscription [12A], however, does lend some support to the hypothesis that
one of the Duo Gong's roles, as "The Many Strikers," may have been musi-
cal. In this view, the Gong-officers of Shang would originally have been
concerned with the manufacture and playing of musical instruments-in
particular with the chiming stones and drums whose remains have been
found in Shang sites, or whose existence can be inferred from the pedes-
tals and racks discovered (YXFX:361-62). Percussion music was an inte-
gral part of ancient Chinese religious ceremonies, and a musical role for
the Gong would suit their association with the temple-palace and its cer-
emonies (as in [12A] and [13]). From the manufacture and playing of such
musical instruments in stone and bronze, the Gong might have progressed
to the manufacture and tending of other items such as bronze vessels, also
associated with ritual and sacrifice.
The following considerations provide support for such a hypothesis:
1. According to Dong Zuobin (1965:113), of the five major Shang rituals,
the rong ritual employed drum music, and the yiritual involved
a dance with feathers. Large numbers of musical instruments have been
found (see pp. 35-36 above).
2. Passages from the Shu indicate that the authors of these texts felt that
the Western Zhou conquerors had valued, or ought to have valued, the
Gong of Shang and had continued to give them employment. That early
Zhou bronze styles were close to those of the Shang for more than a cen-
tury after the conquest suggests continuity in the personnel who manufac-
tured these ritual objects.²²
3. Gong-officers do appear in Eastern Zhou contexts in the role of musi-
cians or music masters. There is at least one reference to the beating of
drums at the time of solar eclipse (cf. [12A]).23 There are bells, big drums,
lutes, guitars, reed organs, and musical stones being struck in the Zhou
text Shi, "Xiaoya, Guzhong" (Karlgren, tr., 1950a:160, paras.
3-4), but the Gong are not mentioned. Eastern Zhou references to the beat-
ing of drums at times of eclipse (Zhao Gong 17) suggest that the Duo Gong
[12A] might also have reacted to the lunar eclipse in some musical role.
4. The graph itself, as we have seen, may suggest a striking device, like
the mallet used to sound bells or gongs, or, alternatively, may depict a
hanging percussion instrument.
5. The sound of the word itself, *kung (GSR, no. 1172; cf. Schuessler
1987:196) may be an onomatopoetic rendering of the sound of a struck
gong or bell.
22. Keightley 1969:155-97; Falkenhausen 1993:205; Rawson 1999:414-23
23. See Legge, tr., 1872:667; Zhao Gong A17 (Takezoe, ed., 1912: vol. 1, Zhao 17, p.
52). Falkenhausen (1993:215) refers to the Gong as "officiating singers." See also n. 10 in this
chapter.
3: The Artisan Corps
41
None of these arguments can be conclusive, and the hypothesis link-
ing the Gong to music may never be provable. But this inconclusiveness
once again suggests the way in which the oracle-bone inscriptions fail to
provide specific details about how the naming and the role of particular
officers was conceived. The diviners were not recording administrative
manuals; they were engaged with particular officers in a variety of par-
ticular situations whose administrative features were of less concern than
their spiritual dimensions (see too p. 45 below on the Gong as generalists).
A third series of divinations suggests that the Gong sometimes played a
military role, thus reinforcing the cognate relationship proposed between
gong I and gong IX as "strike, attack" (see p. 35 above and the discussion of
[305A-F]). For example-but the translation is tentative-

[16C] 庚子卜亘貞:呼取古芻以
Making cracks on gengzi (day 37), Xuan divined: "(We) call
out (hu), 'Take (qu) the Gong to cut fodder (chu) and take
(foragers?).'" (I. Bin) (*Heji 39483)
乙
The mention of loss, and the very fact that such expeditions were
divined about, suggests that hazards, or at least the possibility of failure,
were involved. It has been suggested that the forage gathered on these
expeditions was used to feed the royal herds which in turn provided vic-
tims for the royal sacrifices. 25 If the Gong were indeed officers associated
with the temple (as indicated by [12A] and [13]), it would not be unreason-
able for them to be involved in the gathering of fodder needed for continu-
ing the sacrificial offerings.
Several divinations presage the appearance of the officer known
throughout later Chinese history as the Si Kong, the minister tradi-
tionally associated with the direction of works in China for a period of
some three thousand years. 26 For we find divinations like
[17A] 己酉貞:王其令山司我舌
On jiyou (day 46) divined: "His Majesty will (qi) order Shan
to supervise Our Gong."27
[17B] 己酉貞: 山固王事
have issued orders and received them.28 Gong ([18]), however, was evi-
dently a figure of some importance at the Shang court. He is probably the
figure recorded as Zi Gong, "Prince Gong,"29 and the king, not sur-
prisingly, was concerned about his health. 30 As he had been in [18], Gong
was also a recipient of the king's orders on other occasions. 31 He made
offerings to various Powers. 32 He was involved with campaigns against
the Gongfang or another enemy.33 And like Shan, who was also to
supervise "Our Gong" ([17A]), Gong was expected to "strengthen His
Majesty's affairs."34 Once again one has the sense of a significant mem-
ber of the Shang court whose leadership of the Gong was only part of
his various obligations. It is thought that Zi Gong may have been one of
the numerous sons of Wu Ding; 35 if so, this would further strengthen the
link between the Gong-officers, whom he was to supervise, and the royal
family.
The importance of the Gong-officers in the Shang secular administra-
tion was reflected and reinforced by the existence of Gong-officers who
served in the spiritual administration of Di, the High God. These may
have been former Gong, now dead, who now served Di as they had once
served a Shang king. These Gong were referred to collectively as Di Gong
I, "Di's Gong." The inscriptions contain two references to them:

titles as living officers who served the Shang king below. "Di's Gong"
served in the same ghostly bureaucracy. Service to living kings, or dead,
was paramount.
Summary
To summarize the role of the living Gong-officers, we find them valued
by the king ([9]-[11]), who referred to them as "Our Gong" ([9], [17A]);
they played a role in religious ceremonies ([12AB], [13]); they sometimes
served on military expeditions ([14B], [15AB]), some of which may have
involved the collecting of fodder ([16C]), perhaps used to feed the herds
that supplied the sacrificial victims; and the king was concerned about
their casualties ([15AB]). Finally, we find that persons of importance, one
possibly a royal prince, were ordered to supervise the Gong ([17A], [18]).
The general impression is that the living Gong were valued royal servants,
not all of the highest rank, who performed a variety of functions related to
the religious and political needs of the Shang dynasts.
If we assume that the Gong were artisans or technicians of some sort, or
that they supervised such artisans or technicians, how do we explain this
wide range of non-technical employment and the absence of any specific
reference to their artisanal work in the records? The second question may
be answered first. To the best of my knowledge, few if any divinations
about the royal handicraft industries of Late Shang have been found;39
the absence of divinations that show the Gong at their assumed work,
36. Chen Mengjia 1956:362-64; S364.1-3,365.2-3; Allan 1981 and 1991 (see index); Keightley
1982:295, 300, 301; Henry 1987:24, n. 35; Sun Miao 1987:325-30; Y977.1-78.1, 980.1-81; Zheng
Huisheng 1998:184-208. Kaizuka's reading of Jimbun 2982.3 (= Heji 33309), 3060.1 (not repro-
duced in Heji), and 3067.1 (= Heji 11166) encouraged him to conclude that the Shang offered
sacrifices to a figure known as the Gong Ren E; a victim may also have been offered to the
Gong Yi EZ (Heji 22467) (Keightley 1970:22-23; see too 28-34). Kaizuka's readings, how-
ever, are uncertain; MZ, for example, reads as the name of the Pre-Dynastic Ancestor
Shi Ren (P5). I also find it unlikely that the Gong-officers would have been sufficiently
exalted for the Shang to have given them gan F-based temple names. I am not persuaded,
accordingly, that Kaizuka's interpretation was correct. Such divinations, moreover, suggest
that their subject matter was not frequent; the power of the Gong as manes was not thought
to be large.
37. Heji 14225, 14226, 35931.
38. Chen Mengjia 1956:572; Hu Houxuan 1959; cf. Creel 1970:37. Akatsuka 1977:499-506
introduces the nature and organization of Di's court.
39. Inscriptions [5] and [6] are rare exceptions.
3: The Artisan Corps
45
therefore, is at least consistent with the general silence of the bones about
work practice in general. It may be assumed that the complex techniques
involved in the process of bronze casting or chariot making, for example,
were not thought to interest the ancestors and other Powers who, accord-
ingly, were not liable, in the minds of the king and his diviners, to produce
the kinds of rebuffs and disasters that threatened the natural world, the
battlefield, or the rituals and health of the elites. The Shang king evidently
felt no need to divine about the routine activities of his artisans.40 The Duo
Gong might be subject to "disasters" and "calamitous fault" ([9]-[10]), but
we do not know the nature of their work that was open to such risks. This
explanation serves to emphasize a fact that must never be forgotten: the
oracle bones, as historical sources, are highly selective and incomplete.
To return to the first question, the Gong-officers' wide range of non-
technical employment corresponds to what we know about the unspe-
cialized nature of Shang officialdom in general; administrative jurisdic-
tions, in both Shang and Western Zhou, were flexible and broad. I would
suggest that the technical or administrative skills of the Gong-officers,
attached to the temple-palaces, earned them their title and provided their
routine, peacetime employment. When the king needed combatants or
foragers, then the Gong-officers, some of whom were located in the cult
center and thus readily available, provided one of the reservoirs of loyal
officers, experienced in the leadership and control of larger bodies of men,
upon which the king could call. The Gong, like many of those who labored
for the king-see zhong in ch. 4-represented leadership and manpower
that was trustworthy, for they had a vested interest in maintaining the
dynasty and their own privileged position within it. At a time when loyal
and trained manpower was at a premium, it is understandable that the
Gong-officers were called upon to serve in a variety of dynasty-related
activities.
It would be anachronistic, in any event, to question the absence of
sharply defined bureaucratic assignments and jurisdictions. The Gong
were generalists. There was no contradiction or confusion in Shang times
between the Gong's role as temple-officiants ([12A], [13]), as foragers
([16C]), or as soldiers ([14B], [15AB], and perhaps [9]-[11]). The Shang
state existed in large part to serve the needs of the royal cult and its sup-
porters, because the successful practice of the cult, it was believed, main-
tained the dynasty. The primary function of the Gong, like the function
of the Shang king himself or of his other officers, must be understood in
these comprehensive religious and dynastic terms. Temple service, bronze
40. This bias was to continue into imperial times. The mechanics of workshop production
are not frequently given for the early imperial age (Barbieri-Low 2007). As Falkenhausen
(2004) has noted of salt production in China, the Difangzhi records are interested
only in how the production of salt was administered, not in where or how it was produced.46
Working for His Majesty
3: The Artisan Corps
47
casting, chariot making, foraging, warfare-if these were among the tasks
that the Gong-officers supervised and practiced-should all be regarded
as part of a general continuum in which service to the king, working for
His Majesty, was not limited to one particular skill but was conceived in
terms of a total obligation to the spiritual Powers of the Shang universe
and to the political incarnation of those powers in the dynastic state.
The Dependent Laborers of Shang
The workforce of the Late Shang state was composed of officers-such
as the Gong in command-and of dependent laborers-by which I mean
laborers whose status was characterized by varying degrees of socio-
economic and ideological servitude that rendered them dependent,
again to varying degrees, on the wishes and good will of their superiors
(Keightley 1999b:285-86). The oracle-bone inscriptions' narrow vision
of Shang society provides an idealized, dynastic view of what would
undoubtedly have been a network of complex and by no means uniform
supraordinate-subordinate relations. Nevertheless, the diviners' view of
the matter, limited though it is, still permits us to speak of a society in
which the relationship between leaders and dependents-sanctified by
custom, legitimated by religious belief, and enforced, no doubt, by weap-
ons and punishments-permeated the organization of the Shang labor
force, a society in which the use of labor, one's own or that of others, like
the entire economic order, was embedded in the patrimonial social and
political order.
The Gong and the Duo Gong, "The Many Gong," presumably lay
toward the privileged end of this spectrum of servitude; so, I believe, did
the zhong (see ch. 4).
Studies on artisan identities... - including Hawai'i, Mesopotamia, and
among the Aztec, Maya and Inka-have suggested that artisans, who
often enjoy elite status, may be empowered by a variety of means to
determine or influence their own conditions of employment, compensa-
tion, and social standing.42
The line that separates dependent from forced labor is by no means sharp;
it is certainly not easy to determine at this distance in time.43
41. Inscriptions for the Duo Gong: [10], [12A*], and Heji 19434, 19435, and 32981.
42. Li Liu 2003:8, citing Costin 1998; 2001:282-84.
43. I had earlier (Keightley 1969:2) referred to the early Chinese conscript as a forced
laborer, defining forced labor as "work performed for another person (not related by kinship
ties) without control over the nature or extent of one's efforts" and citing penal servitude.
But this, I now suspect, is too simple a characterization of the Shang situation, in which vari-
ous kinds of working relations, embedded in various kinds of cultural assumptions, would
have existed.
Given the patrimonial nature of the Late Shang state, there is little
doubt that those who served near the king would have done so as a natu-
ral and virtually automatic expression of their dependent, quasi-familial
status; the king depended on them just as they depended on the king.
Conscripts at the lower levels of society, however, who served in the work
gangs and royal armies, may well have suffered from the stronger sense of
coercion and exploitation that may be said to characterize forced-or even
"servile"-labor. The term "corvée"-which is defined as "labor exacted
by a local authority for little or no pay or instead of taxes"44-might also
be used in parts of the Shang system, which was undoubtedly the ancestor
to the later, more systematic recruitment of corvée labor in imperial times.
The nature of our sources is such, however, that we cannot determine the
full character of whatever system of mobilization existed in Shang (see
chs. 7 and 10). The divinations-with each charge usually addressed to a
single, ad hoc event-would suggest that labor was mobilized irregularly,
at the whim of the ruler. But it must be borne in mind that the primary
function of the divinations was to ensure the approval of the Powers for
the particular mobilization or other event being contemplated. The regu-
lar recruitment of statute labor-which, for example, was performed in
theory for one month a year in the Qin-Han period 45-might well have
passed undivined, and would thus have been unrecorded in the inscrip-
tions. We can see the system of recruitment in action, but it is harder to
discern the customary expectations that supported it or the degree to
which they were regularly invoked.46
I choose the term "dependent labor" to indicate that the work we see
being done in the oracle-bone inscriptions was dependent in a variety of
senses, dependent upon both the wishes of the ruler and his elites and
dependent upon the wishes of his ancestors and other Powers. And in
both cases the good fortune of the workers-whether those at the lower
end of the social scale or the king himself-depended upon the good will
of their superiors or ancestors. To use the terms “forced” or “servile" labor
would, I believe, mischaracterize the situation. Coercion may indeed have
been involved, particularly at the lower social levels, but the Shang labor-
ers were also part of the Shang community; they would not generally have
been treated as prisoners or criminals but would have served as members
of a society in which such labor practices made cultural sense. 47 The oracle
bones contain no songs, but it is not out of the question that the zhong-

forced labor thus exacted."

music accompanied their labors.48

lot of persons, ren, were involved, particularly in the case of capture
and sacrifice (Y5.1-8.2): +XE, "they captured Qiang, 15 of them"
(Heji 26917); 用十人又五,“they used 15 men” (Heji 27023);俘人十又六人,

+, "there was indeed someone

(Heji 226); 三百羌,“300 Qiang”(Heji 294);以其五百六,“take forag-

graph for Qiang is not certain).
Clearly the Shang elites made many and various uses of the labors and
life of the ren, the Qiang, and others. Also they made use of a significant
amount of tribute, with cows, sheep, horses, pigs, and dogs brought in;
cowries and jades; and bones and shells to be divined.51 And they employed
the soldiers on the march, the servants who worked the royal palace and
temples, food rations for the dependent laborers who staffed the bronze
foundries, ceramic works, and so on. They employed numerous feasts and
banquets. And, above all, the sacrifices and mortuary rituals would have
been a major expense (Yang Shengnan 1992:88-94). Some of these will be
addressed below.
A Note on Population
What was the population of the entire Shang state? One estimate-based
on inscriptional, archaeological, and traditional evidence (frequently
problematic) has suggested a Shang population in the Xiaotun area,
by the reign of Di Yi (K28) and Di Xin (K29), of approximately 100,000.5

songs from the "Dan Ge," the "Catapult Song," from the Wu Yue chunqiu

Another estimate is for 25,000 households and 120,000 total population
(Yang Baocheng 2002:213-16). If, alternatively, one assumes that for every
conscript mobilized by the king, there was a household that included four
other people, then the numbers mobilized, most commonly 3,000, suggest
that a typical mobilization would have drawn upon a population base of
15,000. If one further assumes that the king mobilized some 10 percent
of the dynasty's available conscripts on such occasions, then a total core-
state population of 150,000 would not seem an unreasonable estimate.
"Thousands of tombs have been located in these several areas [around
Anyang], and bear witness to considerable growth of the center in the late
reigns" (Thorp 2006:129). A military levée of 3,000 or 5,000, for example,
would not be too onerous (see the beginning of ch. 9).
Shang state was some 780,000. Thorp (2006:129), by contrast, concludes "it remains impos-
sible to make plausible estimates of the population of the Anyang region during its prime."
For earlier estimates, see Keightley 1999b:277, n. 93. (The 1994 population of Anyang city, it
may be noted, was close to 4.8 million.)FOUR
The Zhong and the Ren
The oracle-bone inscriptions refer to the dependent laborers of Late Shang
as the zhong or ren (see Glossary for zhongren).' If treated as descrip-
tive, generic terms, they could be translated as "the many" and "the men."
But zhong, at least, served as a status term that designated a particular, and
not especially numerous, class of people in Late Shang society. And it is
not clear that zhong in the Shang yet had the sense of "many" (see Glossary
for zhong). I prefer, accordingly, to leave zhong untranslated. The zhong
in this view was not simply a social category but a Shang administrative
rubric, "the zhong." Raising the zhong (as in [68A], [70]-[72]) was compa-
rable, say, to raising "the Archers" (as in [73AB] and [74]).
The Shang word ren \, "man" or "person," was a generic term that
could be applied to a variety of statuses.2 The king might use ren of him-
self, as in the phrase yu yi ren A, "I the one man will have noth-
ing ominous" (Yingcang 1923);³ it might be linked to the zhong, as in the
term zhongren (see later); ren was used of men serving in the royal
armies,5 of enemy invaders, and of captives in battle. Ren was also a col-
lective term, as it was in later Chinese texts, used to refer to the people, or
at least to the ren, of a certain place. For all these reasons, I translate ren as

2001:438).

(Gassmann 2000:359). The term min was rarely, if ever, used in the oracle-bone inscrip-
tions (S101.3; Y207.2; Sōran, no. 0462).

8. See, e.g., [29AB], [118], and the discussion at ch. 4, p. 61 below. Nylan (2001:154-55) gives
the Zhou evolution of the terms for ren and min E.
4: The Zhong and the Ren

51
"man" or "men." Unlike the zhong, who represented a specific and finite
social and administrative group, the ren were less well defined administra-
tively. When the king referred to "raising ren," he was referring to raising
only some of the "men" available to him.
These distinctions are hardly watertight, partly because the divina-
tion inscriptions were primarily religious, not administrative, documents
(assuming one can make such distinctions in the Shang). The divination
charges certainly reflected administrative realities, but they were more
focused, where the Shang labor force was concerned, on the good fortune
of the general enterprise than on the niceties of status and command, even
if we assume that such niceties were observed in practice. When the king
divined about raising the zhong, we cannot tell whether he raised all of
them or just some of them (as would have been true when he raised the
ren); but he was nevertheless focusing on a well-defined group of depen-
dent laborers, not on a large, amorphous social class. And the matter is
further complicated by the existence of a third Shang term, zhongren,
which appears in a significant number of oracle-bone inscriptions involv-
ing labor use. I believe that it should be construed as the compound for
which zhong was the frequent abbreviation and that it may accordingly be
translated as "the men of the zhong,"10 that is, the men who constituted the
zhong:

should be treated as "the zhong and the ren." I have now changed my position for the reasons

Working for His Majesty
It is also possible that "the men of the zhong" meant "the men attached to
the zhong." The considerations given below lead me to reject this second
reading. In what follows, accordingly, I regard zhong as the diviner's or
engraver's abbreviation for zhongren.13
One can find cases in which zhong and zhongren appear in contexts that
suggest the functional identity of the two groups. The Shang general Qin
(see Glossary), for example, was sometimes instructed to "take" (i.e.,
lead) the zhong, sometimes to "take" the zhongren:

Such divinations about the loss of zhong and ren throw further light on
the status of the two groups. The Y editors have assembled some twenty-
two cases of the phrase sang zhong, "lose zhong" (Y520.1); they have
also assembled seven cases of the phrase sang ren, "lose men" (also
at Y520.1), always, unfortunately, on small fragments that provide no use-
ful context.15 I find only four cases of the phrase sang zhongren,
"lose zhongren."16 Loss of zhong (or zhongren) was divined more frequently

(by a factor of over three) than loss of ren, which suggests that the Late
Shang kings were more concerned about losing their zhong than they were
about losing their ren. This evidence not only indicates that the zhong
were of higher status than the ren, but it also suggests, once again, that
for the Shang diviners and administrators there was no sharp distinction
of meaning between zhong and zhongren, and that the term zhongren was
simply a fuller form of zhong.17
The Status of the Workers: Slave, Free, or Dependent
There has been considerable divergence of opinion about the status of the
zhong, some of it influenced by the general ideological climate in which
the authors wrote and much of it lacking convincing links between evi-
dence and conclusions. I would submit, as a preliminary caution, that the
relatively narrow information about the labor force that the divination
inscriptions provide is often unsuited to the broad, and frequently anach-
ronistic, political, and sociological judgments that some modern scholars
have been tempted to make.
Opinions about the status of the zhong generally fall into three major
camps: the zhong were slaves (particularly during the time of Chairman
Mao), free men, or somewhere in-between. I present the proponents of
each of these views next.18
Slaves. Chen Mengjia (1956:610), after comparing all the oracle-bone ref-
erences, concluded that the ren were free peasantry and that the zhong
(and zhongren) were slaves; he did not, unfortunately, define what
he meant by "free" or "slave."19 Guo Moruo pronounced Shang China a
"primitive-communal-clan society" (AAĦNAH), or its last
stages; in 1930 and 1937 found that the Shang represented the last stages
of clan society; concluded in 1945 that the Shang employed a large number
of slaves in agriculture; and in 1952, admitting his past mistakes, declared
merely a fuller way of referring to the zhong, who, unlike the ren, would have been counted
by hundreds ([81]) and not in their thousands.
17. Chen Mengjia (1956:610) concluded that, while the Shang king and the tribal statelets

the king. But this is uncertain. Qin leads the zhongren in [22] and Heji 31989, and also leads

were stationed on the borders more often, and that the king (as we have seen earlier) divined
about their loss more frequently. But he too concluded that the difference between the zhong
and zhongren was small.

4: The Zhong and the Ren
55
Dependents (neither fully slave nor free). Dong Zuobin (1948:11; 1951:390)
argued that the zhong were adult male lineage members; he denied that
they were slaves. Zhang Zhenglang (1973:119) saw them as peasant-war-
riors, who had their own families, and organized under the Yin #offi-
cers (who appear in [237A]) in groups of one hundred ([81]). Ping-ti Ho
(1975:308), relying on the "Pan Geng" section of the Shu, found that the
zhong were not slaves but were "the very foundation of the Shang state
and society." Lin Yun (1979:331) saw them as members of the patriarchal
lineage; in a similar vein, Kwang-chih Chang (1980:226–27)-quoting with
approval the conclusions of Zhang Zhenglang (1973)-thought the zhong-
ren were probably "the lowly members of each zu [], especially of each
lowly zu." Shima (1980:18-19) argued that, because they participated in
the jitian agricultural ritual, “the tilling of the sacred field" (discussed
in ch. 12), the zhong could not have been slaves (but on this see the Glos-
sary entry for ji). Vandermeersch (1980:182) suggested that the term
zhong referred to some type of "citizenship" reserved for the members of
the Shang ethnos who were more closely associated with the king than
the bulk of the Shang population. Zhang Yongshan (1982:261) concluded
that the zhong were agricultural producers, possessing unequal amounts
of private property, and serving as an important element of the state's mil-
itary power; they were in frequent communication with the slave lords,
their status was higher than that of slaves, and they had their own lin-
eage (zushi ) organization. 26 They were a major source of the military
power of the state and were never killed carelessly. Yang Baocheng and
Yang Xizhang (1983:32-33) argued: that the zhong were collective laborers,
of commoner status, organized as lineage members; that, serving in the
palace guard, they enjoyed a certain personal freedom and distinctive sta-
tus; and that most of the burials found in what they and others have called
the "commoner cemetery" at Yinxu West (see too p. 93 below) - with some
two thousand burials and with coffins as grave furnishings, no accompa-
niers-in-death, some with a dog, 27 a small number of pots, tools, weapons,
or ocean cowries as grave goods, and not many bronze ritual vessels 28 –
the word. Kaizuka and Itō (2000:190-93) see the existence of great family heads, commoners,
and the totally dependent class, including slaves and serfs.

were those of zhong and zhongren.29 Kolb speculates about the status of the

model that Wheatley (1971: 43, n. 163, 52-55, 59-61, 375, 376) had earlier
proposed; given the pressure of commands from superiors, Kolb (1991:48)
notes that they were not truly free.
My own review of the evidence leads me to conclude that the zhong
(and zhongren) are best understood as dependents located toward the base
of the royal, patrimonial administrative and social organization in the
Late Shang cult center.31 They may be regarded as "service dependents,"
measured in their hundreds, accorded a fair degree of social and political
status, and distinguished from the ren by their state of permanent attach-
ment and proximity to their patron, the king or another lord. 32 Although
we cannot be sure whether the zhong were actually involved in Late Shang
craft production, the elite status and privileged knowledge of craftsmen in
other cultures at a comparable stage of development make it plausible (see
the scholarship cited on p. 33 above). 33 Terms such as "freedom" and "slav-
ery" have little application to a society where customary obligations, hal-
lowed by tradition and religious belief, held sway. 34 As Finley has written:
Throughout most of human history, labor for others has been performed
in large part under conditions of dependence or bondage; that is to say,
the relation between the man who works and his master or employer
rested neither on ties of kinship nor on a voluntary, revocable contract
of employment but rather on birth into a class of dependents, on debt,

or on some other precondition which by custom and law automatically

57
57
And he quotes Lasker (1950:114) to good effect: "The concept of labor as a
salable commodity, apart from the person of the seller, is relatively recent
in the history of civilization."36
Terms like "slave" or "freeman" do not appear in the Shang records.
Attempts to assign slave status to particular groups recorded in the inscrip-
tions, or to read a particular oracle-bone graph as nu X, "slave," have
generally involved more theoretical assertion than palaeographic demon-
stration.37 Nor are there records, or even traditions, of any person being
bought or sold. 38 Where no legal concept of individual rights existed, it is
more useful to characterize Shang society in terms of degrees of depen-
dency and privilege rather than slavery and freedom.
In China from the Neolithic to the late Western Zhou and early East-
ern Zhou periods, there was no strict separation in status between
the lowest members of the society and the highest. All members were to
a certain extent unfree.39
The basic distinction was that between nobles-Wang E, Hou, Bo E,
Zi, Fu, and other officers-who had an ancestral temple, a surname
(?), and political power, and the commoners (who did not?). The com-
moners, at least those at the lower end of the spectrum of servitude, were
forced to labor under "slave-like" conditions which were undoubtedly
harsh, at least by our standards; they were people who endured the weight
35. Finley 1968:308; cf. Patterson 2001; 2001a. Barbieri-Low (2007:28) has cautioned that
"at different times, and under different historical circumstances, these variables"-commer-
cialization, craft production, market integration, etc.-"might be located at different posi-
tions on a scale"; he cites the work of Greene (1986) and Smith (2004).
36. The presence of a variety of dependency relations in Shang China further suggests
that slavery would not have played a significant role in that society. Consider the conclusion
of Finley (1968:308) that "slavery attained its greatest functional significance, and usually its
greatest numerical strength, in societies in which other, less total varieties of bondage had
either disappeared or had never existed." This would hardly describe the Shang context.

4: The Zhong and the Ren
of society without sharing its benefits. Nor was this system of dependent
labor "a dark gloominess hanging over the land," to cite an eighteenth-
century comment about slavery in the United States (Davis 1966:484). It
was the natural obligation of all. As Finley has written:
The pre-Greek world-the world of the Sumerians, Babylonians, Egyp-
tians, and Assyrians... -was, in very profound sense, a world without
free men, in the sense in which the West has come to understand that
concept. It was equally a world in which chattel slavery played no role of
any consequence.40
The world of Shang China should be added to the list.
The Differences Between the Zhong and the Ren
On the basis of the evidence presented in this book, Shang royal adminis-
tration, as viewed through its mobilization of the dynasty's labor resources,
would have been a complex, demanding, and religiously charged activ-
ity. The Late Shang kings, in making their decisions about the timing and
location of, say, their agricultural (ch. 12) or military (ch. 13) activities,
would have had to consider a variety of tactical and strategic factors that
played a role in both the realm of geopolitics and the realm of the ancestral
and other Powers.
Since the same officer, Ya Qin,41 might, on occasion, lead either the
zhong ([120]) or the ren ([242A]), and since both the zhong and the ren were
employed in agriculture, warfare, construction, and miscellaneous royal
service (Table 1, on p. 246), it makes sense in what follows to study the zhong
and the ren together. While the zhong (and zhongren) should be regarded
as dynastic laborers- that is, workers permanently attached by status and
custom to the ruler-I believe that the ren should be seen as members
of the general population, mobilized by the king and his officers for the
dynasty's work as the occasion demanded. Given the patrimonial nature
of the Late Shang state, there is little doubt that those who served near the
king would have done so as a natural and virtually automatic expression
of their dependent, quasi-familial status, as an expression of their loyalty;
the king depended on them just as they depended on the king. Conscripts
at the lower levels of society, however, who served in the labor gangs
and royal armies, may well have suffered from less immediate benefit, the
stronger sense of coercion and exploitation that may be said to character-
ize forced labor. And even the ren may have benefitted from the religious
activities of the ruler; they certainly benefitted from getting enough to eat.
40. Finley 1959:164; cf. Yates 2001:287.
41. See the Glossary entry for Ya
The exceptions presented below that challenge this proposed divi-
sion by status and function suggest once again that the Shang king drew
no hard-and-fast line between his zhong and ren, and that he-or his
diviners-did, on occasion, treat the ren like the zhong and vice versa. The
dependent laborers of Late Shang were involved in a continuum of servi-
tude, with, in ideal terms, the zhong, at one end of the spectrum, enjoying
more privileged status and being more fully identified by their permanent
attachment to the king, and with the ren, at the other end, being only inter-
mittently employed by the king or his officers, and, accordingly, being of
lower status. 42 In actual practice, however, there seem to have been some
cases in which the distinction between the zhong and ren was not, at least
in the divinations, clear-cut.
The zhong, as we have seen, by comparison with the ren, were more
closely and regularly associated with the king and were more highly val-
ued by him, as suggested by the phrase wang zhong E, "His Majesty's
zhong" ([85A-G]).43 Zhao Lin (1982:134-35), likening the wang zhong to
the wang zu E, "the royal lineage" (see the Glossary for zu), argues
that the zhong were organized by kin groups. Given the generally patri-
monial nature of Late Shang administration, this is probable. Although I
find no direct evidence of the zhong's internal organization, I am struck
by certain parallels with the wang zu E, who appear in twenty-plus
inscriptions (Y986.1-2). The meaning of many of these inscriptions is not
always clear, but it is clear that, like the zhong, the wang zu-the "royal
lineage" or "royal kindred"-could be sent on military activities, as in the
following.44

"If His Majesty calls upon the (royal) zhong and the Guards

45. Shen Jianhua 2003 deals with both inscriptions. A fragmentary version of a similar
inscription is recorded on Tunnan 880.
Zhao Lin (1982:134-35) believes that [29B] indicates that "the men of the
territory (?) of Lin and the men of Tuo" (my tentative translation) formed
part of the local zhong. Xiao Nan (1981a:129-30) believes that the zhong of
Bing ([83]) and the zhong of Qin ([234]) were members of the Bing and Qin
clans. This is possible. And there are, in any event, the wang zhong E,
"the royal zhong" ([85A-G]), directly attached to the king.46
The ren, by contrast, may have been identified in this way rather more
frequently (as in [64], [118]),47 and in these cases the ren may have been
regarded as locally based forces, not as forces directly subordinated to
the king. But still the king divined about them and gave the order. I am
struck by the relatively small number of these cases, by the fact that only
one of them ([118]) specifically appears to have involved warfare, and that
the significance of the others is not clear. 48 This suggests that the king's
authority to mobilize the ren of settlements that lay beyond his direct con-
trol, or to divine about such issues, may have been limited. It worked only
when the leader of the group gave his assent.
The power of the dynasty, in these and in other matters, was inti-
mately tied to the personal presence of the king and his immediate sup-
porters. The evidence about labor mobilization thus accords well with my
understanding of the Shang state as involving "a series of nodal points,"
with the king presiding over "a thin network of pathways and encamp-
ments . . . laid over a hinterland that rarely saw or felt the king's presence
and authority."
The king displayed his power by frequent travel, hunting, and inspecting
along the pathways of his realm, delegating little of his military power
and expecting no "legal" or "constitutional" military support in return.
State power traveled with the king and his court of diviners and retain-
ers, who prayed and sacrificed to legitimate the orders and expropria-
tions that he levied on the populations and groups that came into his
intermittent view.49
The oracle-bone inscriptions have many cases in which the zhong and
zhongren participated in campaigns (see Table 1.2-3 on p. 247). But the zhong,
in addition, participated in many other kinds of activities, just like the ren
46. Ishida (1964:58), in fact, had concluded that the word zhong was never qualified by a
tribal name.
47. Zhao Lin (1982:135) cites ten different combinations of place-name + ren.
48. There is no precise context, for example, for [64]: :, "Divined: '(We) call
out to raise (gong) (men) at Mei(?)""; or for Heji 9636: :, "Divined: 'It
should be (hui) the people of Ci whom (we) order to inspect at the southern border (?)"; and
for Heji 8720f: :, "Divined: '(We) raise the men of Que and cail upon them to
reside (zhai) (in) Que'" (see Que and other readings at Sōran, no. 0146).
49. Keightley 1983:551, 552; see too Lewis 1993:325; Keightley 1999b:272-73.62
62
Working for His Majesty
did, including hunts, opening up land, working the land, planting mil-
let, and strengthening the king's affairs (see Table 1 on p. 246). This shows,
according to one view, that the zhong participated in both warfare and
production. The zhong were not just professional soldiers. Their partici-
pation in warfare was limited, above all, by their engagment in agricul-
ture. I would propose that the zhong were a form of cadre. They were able
to fight, able to hunt, able to farm, able to energize the ren, the soldiers
and farmers whom they directed. And as we will see, the zhong were only
numbered in their hundreds (see p. 97 below).
Presumably, local systems of mobilization ensured that the ren were
conscripted in ways that shared the burden on a fairly equable basis, but
our sources provide no information on this point. It may be noted, how-
ever, that the Shang preferred to campaign in the winter months when
the peasantry's farming duties would have been lighter (Shaughnessy
1988a:52).
To reiterate, the zhong (and zhongren) should, I believe, be understood
as the service dependents involved in the royal, patrimonial organization
in the cult center-cum-capital.50 They were measured-like the Archers,
the Guards, and the Horse(-chariot officers)-in their hundreds, and,
composing the royal or official retinue, were distinguished from the ren
by their state of permanent attachment to the king. The ren, by contrast,
were "the men, the people," the population at large, who were recruited
as the occasion demanded, who were measured in their thousands, and
who generally resided beyond the cluster of the royal temple-palaces
(see too ch. 9). In terms of their military activities, one may think of the
zhong as a permanent standing force, the cadres of the army and the royal
labor gangs; the ren, by contrast, may be regarded as the militia, liable for
service as the king or their superiors needed them. The ren, as a militia,
may not have been constantly ready for action, but they were presumably
liable for service whenever the king found it expedient to mobilize them.51
50. Cf. Yang Xizhang 1991; Keightley 2000:56-58.
51. Lin Yun (1990:43) basically agrees: "Though the army system basically depended on
the levy or draft, at the same time, a professional, standing troop existed. In the wars with
enemy Fang states, the main force of the fighting army were the groups among the com-
moners, drafted by lineage."
FIVE
Punishments, Human Sacrifice,
and Accompanying-in-Death
Possible Punishments
The Late Shang inflicted a series of punishments and probably treated
prisoners of war badly.¹ Some of those were enemies of the Shang and
were only incidentally a part of Shang "slave economy" or "dependent
labor." The Shang certainly did not divine about punishments a great deal
and certainly did not inflict punishments on the zhong.
There was a particular punishment that involved, in the view of many
scholars, amputating one of the feet (yue).² This is possible.
[30]
[31]

One divination involved eighty men:
[32] 貞:刖僕八十人不蘊
Divined: "There will be an amputation (?) on the Pu, eighty
persons, but there will be no death (yun)."4 (I. Bin) (Heji 580f)
The graph certainly depicts a man with one leg shorter than the other, but
whether it may reveal an amputation or some other ritual or procedure is
not certain.5
The status of the victims-the Pu ("servant, follower, groom"), or kou
,"robber," or other modern forms-is not always certain either.6 Several
scholars, in the 1980s, regarded them as "a sort of slave" or "human sacri-
fices." At times eighty Pu (Heji 580) or five hundred Pu are mentioned (Heji
558-60, 562), and frequently as targets of yong H, "sacrifice" (see Glos-
sary). Shima regarded them not of slave status, but as subordinates of the
Duo She, "Many Archers." The term Duo Pu (or Duo Kou)
(Y762.3-63.1) appears at least seventeen times in the Period I inscriptions:

The Pu were soldiers (Heji 537-39), often attacking the Gongfang ([33AB]
and Heji 541, 542, 547, etc.).
We have divinations about the "amputation" of the Pu ([32]); death
may be involved ([31]) (cf. Keightley 1969:363-64). And we also have divi-
nation about the Duo Pu who were in the Shang army ([33B]). This has led
some scholars to devise a class of "condemed Pu," lacking a leg, but also

[34]
癸丑卜殼貞: 五百僕用.旬壬戌用僕百.三月
Making cracks on guichou (day 50), Que divined: "We use
(yong) five hundred Pu." (Postface:) In the next ten days,
on renxu (day 59), there was (you) use of one hundred Pu.
Third moon. (I. Bin) (Heji 559)
The numbers-five hundred and one hundred Pu-suggest again a dis-
ciplined group. There are another sixteen inscriptions (Y763.2) which
appear to refer to "manacling the Pu," zhi Pu but unfortunately the
fuller story is not known since the fragments only appear on bone pieces
or whole oracle bones that do not contain much other information.
If we assume that the Pu and that Duo Pu are linked, I find it hard to s
assume the Duo Pu were a regiment of amputees. It is conceivable that the
Pu were dismissed from military service, experienced amputation (either
pre- and post-service in the army), and were forced to work at more
domestic tasks. But the number of amputees was sufficiently large-as
many as eighty, one hundred, and five hundred Pu ([32], [34], and Heji
558, 560, 562 [Y763.1-2])-that the Shang were likely to have a slave revolt
on their hands. Also the charge, "but there will be no death," does not
strike me as the slave lord grieving for his slaves ([31], [32]). I propose that
the yue graph perhaps did not refer to amputation. Perhaps it referred to
wounds of the leg, or simply wounds in general. And the Duo Pu and Pu
were a branch of the Shang military, for which divinations about "death"
were not perhaps unusual. 12 They were not free men, but they were not
mainly, I suspect, amputees! And they appear only in Period I.
The Shang certainly had some slaves-mainly prisoners of war-but
were not a slave economy. They were mainly attended by dependent
laborers, who knew their place, socially and religiously, in the Shang cul-
tural system. The King was to be obeyed (see pp. 233 ff.). The basic distinc-
tion was between the nobles, who had ancestral temples and political
power, and the commoners, who did not.
9. Vandermeersch (1980:183-84) argues in part that the Duo Pu were members of the
Shang aristocracy and differs from the view of Guo Moruo (1957-63: vol. 14, p. 353) that they
were "condamnés-domestiques."
10. GSR, nos. 1185a-e; Schuessler 1987:762.
11. Sōran, nos. 1255-56; see, e.g., Takashima 1984:64; Kolb 1991:59.
12. Divinations about death (see yun [Glossary]; Y35.2-38.2), however, do not appear

dying: "X will perhaps (qi) die," from sickness, etc.66

Human Sacrifice and Accompanying-in-Death
The Shang evidence for the ritual taking of life on a considerable scale is
so well known that there is no need to treat it exhaustively here.²² One
scholar had argued that 13,052 humans were sacrificed in this way, with
another record of 1,145 instances of human sacrifices which does not
give the numbers of the victims. 23 The argument that the large numbers
of human victims associated with some of the larger royal tombs were
slaves, as opposed to ritual victims, is hard to accept.24 There is little evi-
dence that these victims were routinely employed in productive labor
prior to their execution. The human victims were, rather, one expression
of the continuing belief, both religious and political, that those related to
a lord by blood or service or captivity in life were expected to continue
Duo Ma Qiang was a telescoped form of Duo Ma and Duo Qiang, and Duo Ma Ya was a tele-
scoped form of Duo Ma and Duo Ya.

proper reading of the inscription, based on Zhang Zhenglang, should read: []
, "The king orders the Duo [Yin] to Qiang to open up fields." Qiang here is a place-name.

It may also be noted that no divinations ever record that the zhong were
offered in human sacrifice or transferred in tribute. 26 The human victims

were generally non-Shang captives taken in battle, as in the following
examples: [54CD], [232]-[233], [298].28
Human sacrifice was prevalent under Wu Ding; it declined under the
next kings, with Di Yi and Di Xin seeing the least. 29 The oracle-bone and
archaeological finds generally support one another. A number of schol-
ars have claimed Late Shang society was not yet able to absorb large
numbers of slave laborers.30 The number of human sacrifices must have
been due to the failure of the Shang economy to absorb a large number
of slaves. 31 On the basis of the 191 sacrificial pits containing at least 1,458
victims excavated at Wuguan cun North, it is noted that most of the
beheaded victims during the reign of Wu Ding were fifteen- to thirty-
five-year-old males or male teenagers close to adulthood. But that by the
late period, the human sacrifices in the area were mainly adult females,
youths, and children. 32 This suggests not only a change in the theology of
sacrifice but also a change in the source of the victims. Given the evident
reduction in Shang military superiority after the reign of Wu Ding,33 it is
possible that the last Shang kings had to obtain victims either as a form
