of tribute from the now conquered enemies who had previously been
the source of the male prisoners or from members of their own Shang
population.
Clearly, the Shang ancestors welcomed these offerings. One key point
to observe is the variety of statuses of the victims.34 Though it is easy to
distinguish the lowly sacrificial victim from the high-status dependent
accompanying a Shang king in death, it is by no means easy to assess the
precise sacrificial character of the victims, clustered in or around the great
Late Shang tombs, who fall within the middle ranges of status. Undoubt-
edly there was a gradation of roles.
One of the earliest instances of accompanying-in-death associated with
Shang cultural sites has been found in the Upper Erligang strata of such
locations as Baijia zhuang in Henan and Lijiazui in Hubei.
Distinctions of status and function were clearly present. All the victims
buried in larger tombs lacked coffins; some had been dismembered and
some were children. These were presumably prisoners or other inferiors
thought suitable for human sacrifice. Other victims, however, had been
buried with a variety of bronze implements such as knives, adzes, chis-
els, saws, and arrows. 35 Presumably, we see in such graves a conflation of
two traditions: of human sacrifice, on the one hand, and accompanying-
in-death, on the other. The burial of work tools and weapons strongly
suggests that services performed by some of the victims in this life were to
continue in the next. The presence of sets of ritual bronze vessels in these
tombs (Wenwu 1976:13) further indicates the way in which other aspects
of Middle Shang burial practice were being articulated and formalized.
The number of victims in these Middle Shang burials was relatively
small, generally one or two. A fairly large number have been found in
graves at Liulige (ca. 90 km to the south-southeast from Anyang):
M147 contained the skeleton of one victim in the earth fill of the tomb
chamber and six skulls in the undisturbed part of the burial chamber
itself; five skulls were found in the earth fill of M150, which contained
five flexed skeletons, at least three on the ledge, and one crammed into the
small waist pit, together with three pottery vessels; at least three dogs had
34. Yu Weichao (1981:50) reaches a similar conclusion.
35. Huang Zhanyue 1974:156; Yu Weichao 1981:50. At Lijiazui the bones of a dog or human
victims were found in the waist pit (yaokeng) of M2, together with a jade dagger-axe
blade broken into three pieces. Two followers-in-death, adult and child, were found in the
rammed earth between the inner and outer wall on the west side of the coffin chamber; the
adult had been provided with a bronze arrowhead, six bronze knives, and an adze, drills,
and saw at the head; a pot lay below the legs. Another victim lay at the north end of the
grave, between the coffin and coffin chamber; the body had apparently fallen off the lid of
the chamber (Wenwu 1976:12-14; Thorp 1980:52-53).72

Working for His Majesty
also been buried in the grave. 36 The varied placing of the human victims
and skulls in these Middle Shang burials reminds us of the analogous dis-
tinctions made in the Neolithic placing of the pig jaws or dogs.37
In one section of sacrifices at Yinxu:
The most famous concentration of sacrificial pits is that found in area B,
outside of building structure B7....
The B7 middle section pits were clearly part of a planned layout, centered
on human sacrifice. They were laid out in twelve long north-south rows
with a total number of 80 pits, most of which contained only human skel-
etons, numbering from 1 to 13 in each, most often 6-8, often red-colored,
divided into groups facing opposite directions (east or west). Almost all
had been beheaded before burial, sometimes with a cut through the man-
dibles, often with the skulls placed at the foot end of the bodies (as in
M161, M180 etc.). There were no animal offerings and rarely any grave
goods.38
The conclusion that, by Late Shang, prisoners were the primary sac-
rificial victims is supported not only by the oracle-bone inscriptions, but
by skeletal analysis. A sacrificial pit excavated at Dasikong cun in 1971,
for example, contained twenty-six decapitated bodies, some with their
hands behind their backs, and thirty-one skulls, the skulls tending to be
clustered, with seventeen of the skulls and bodies still linked (the result
of incomplete decapitation?). Analysis of some of the skulls revealed the
presence of several children, four to seven years old, and several adults,
about thirty years old; all were male. There were no grave goods or fur-
nishings (Kaogu 1978:71-72). A study of the teeth has also suggested signs
of malnutrition among the victims.39 And it has been proposed that the
victims from the sacrificial pits were more mixed racially than the accom-
paniers-in-death (xun ) found in the medium and small Shang tombs,
evidence it is thought for the non-Shang origins of the victims and the
Shang origins of the dependents.40 One should note, however, the con-

(YXFX:438-39; cf. 416; Yuan and Flad 2002).

40. Huang Zhanyue 1983:941, citing earlier studies when the slave society was preva-
lent; see K. C. Chang 1976b:55-56; Li Chi 1977:255-64. Pan Qifeng's osteological argument
(1989:297-303) argues "that the human bones from the medium and small burials of the Yin
dynasty ruins representing the Shang royal tribe show a combination of traits from East
Asia and North Asia. This is not the character of the inhabitants of the middle-lower reaches
5: Punishments, Human Sacrifice, and Accompanying-in-Death
73
73
clusions of Howells (1983:312) who, working with forty-five male skulls
taken from the sacrificial pits, wrote, "I can see no indication that the
people buried in the sacrificial pits were other than ordinary members of
the Shang Chinese population." Anthropologists are now in more agree-
ment. They studied eighty-four skulls excavated from 1950 to 1975 and
they concluded that the "free people" found in the medium/small burials
were "Mongolian, East Asian type." The racial type of the accompaniers-
in-death was the same as that of the grave lords. The age at death of skulls
shows that the age of the grave lords in the Middle/Small graves was gen-
erally higher than the age of the skulls in the sacrifice pits in the royal
burial sector. The accompaniers-in-death from large tombs were presum-
ably prisoners-of-war. The elites, servants, and slaves in other tombs were
mainly the close servitors of the grave lord.41
The number of human victims associated with the Late Shang royal
burials is awe-inspiring, as it undoubtedly was intended to be. 42 The
looted condition of the tombs renders an accurate count impossible, but it
may be estimated that a four-ramp tomb such as M1001 (probably that of
Wu Ding, but see Thorp 2006:137) might have claimed the lives of some
225-plus sacrificial victims and accompaniers-in-death: accompaniers-
in-death and human sacrifices, 23 whole corpses; headless corpses, 61;
skulls, 73 (in fact, there must have been more). They were buried vari-
ously on the grave floor, outside the outer coffin, on the second-level
ledge, in the ramp. In addition, on the eastern side of the large grave,
were a total of 68 accompaniers-in-deaths-a total of 225 individuals
(YXFX:449). The accompaniers-in-death were most numerous in the large
burials. The whole Xibeigang complex might then have claimed
the lives of over 5,000 victims, 43 either in the interments themselves or in
of the Yellow River basin, but instead might be related to the northern people" (Guo Da-
shun 1995a:179). See too Yang Xizhang 1989:311-14. This is resisted by Zhongguo sheyui
kexueyuan Lishi yanjiusuo and Zhongguo sheyui kexueyuan Kaogu yanjiusuo, eds., 1985
and Zhang Guoshuo 1997.
41. YXFX:450-51. The age at death varied, by one estimate, between 28.2 and 34.5 years
(Song Zhenhao 1994:116-18; cf. Guo Da-shun [1995a:169] on the early Bronze Age site of
Dadianzi : "The mean age of people who were buried here is about 20-30 years old,
with less then 10 percent estimated at 50-60."). From a study of Shangma statistics at
death-late Western Zhou to early Warring States-the average age was 39.09 years, but this
is possibly too high. "Indeed, such high life expectancies are difficult to imagine for any soci-
ety previous to the advent of modern medicine" (Falkenhausen 2001:97).

5: Punishments, Human Sacrifice, and Accompanying-in-Death

the subsequent sacrifices associated with the cemetery area. And many of

Moving from the bottom of the grave pit toward the top, the mortuary
victims in the royal burials may be classified as follows:
(1) Those buried, mainly with their bodies whole, in the waist pit (yao-
keng) or in the four corners of the burial chamber, furnished with a
bronze or jade dagger-axe; they presumably were to guard the deceased.
The presence of the jade blades, in particular, suggests their symbolic role.
Guards, furnished with more realistic weapons of bronze and stone, hel-
mets, and chariot ornaments, were also buried outside the main grave in
a series of independent pits. Independent pits were also provided for the
king's chariot horses and, sometimes, their attendants.45
(2) Those buried whole, with their own coffins, grave goods, and bronzes,
and even with their own accompaniers-in-death or an attendant dog. Gen-
erally placed on the ledge or on the roof of the coffin chamber, and show-
ing no evidence of binding or struggle, these presumably were high-status
relatives, close dependents, or personal attendants of the deceased. Their
gradations of status are revealed by the situation in M1, a non-royal burial
at Wuguan cun. Seventeen persons (probably men)-eight with wooden
coffins, five with bronze vessels and ornaments of jade, stone, and bone-
had been buried on the eastern ledge; twenty-four persons (probably
women)-six with wooden coffins and eight with grave goods-lay on
the western ledge. The male and female skeletons occupying the cen-
ter of each ledge were furnished with the largest coffins and the most
numerous grave goods; they appear to have been the leaders of the high-
status dependents buried with the grave lord. Quite possibly, dependents,
Huang went on to argue that the eight four-ramp tombs would have thus required taking
the lives of some 2,880 people. Even if we accept his earlier figures as base, such extrapo-
lation is only allowable if it can be shown that the quantity of victims did not vary as the
dynasty grew older. According to my rough calculations, the oracle-bone inscriptions about
human sacrifice indicate a marked decline in the number of human sacrifices divined, from
an average of 8.4 victims divined per year in the reign of Wu Ding (given only twenty years
of rule) to only 0.48 by the time of Di Yi and Di Xin. That the decline represented a change,
not simply in the divination record but in actual practice, is suggested by Huang's conclu-
sion (1983:942) that human sacrifices were rarer in the late-period large tombs. Cf. Tang
Jigen 1999a:180.
44. The number of Qiang-3, 9, 10, 15, 30, 100, 150, 300, etc.-served as the topic for ca. 566
inscriptions (Y48.1-55.1), mainly from Period I. See [35A]-[41B], [54C-E], [98]. It is clear from
the context that the records were of sacrificial nature. They are not prisoner or slave counts;
they were primarily victim counts.
45. E.g., Umehara 1964:105 (M175); Zheng Ruokui 1987:464 (M175, M7), 466-67; Yang
Xizhang and Liu Yiman 1998:49 (M40), 56 (M41); Yang Baocheng 2002:124 (M52); Thorp
2006:170 (M40).
relatives, and servants were all represented.46 Probably, some were also
accompaniers-in-death. In the tomb of Fu Hao:
she was accompanied by sixteen sacrificed humans (four on the top of
the chamber, two inside under the eastern wall niche, one inside the
western wall niche, one in a pit dug in the floor of the chamber, and the
remaining eight taken out of underground water with their original posi-
tions unknown but presumably within the chamber) and six sacrificed
dogs (one in the pit below bottom and the other on top of the cham-
ber). Among the sixteen humans, four were men, one of them young;
two were women, two were children; the sexes and ages of the rest were
unclear. At least one was killed; one other was cut in half at the waist.
(Kwang-chih Chang 1980:88)
Mortuary ritual involved the wealth of the community, in both grave goods
and personnel. It also was the engine that kept the organization running
at the highest levels of art and metallurgy. The needs of the dead master
were paramount. The power of protection of the lord must be thought to
continue beyond the grave. Accompanying-in-death was the ultimate ser-
vice, a logical extension of the servitude inferiors owed superiors.47
(3) The most numerous group consisted mainly of young males, in the
fifteen to thirty-five age range, and a few children, who were furnished
with neither coffins nor grave goods. Generally decapitated or dismem-
bered, they were frequently buried in the fill, in the ramps, or in rows of
sacrificial pits in the vicinity of the tomb, mainly with five-to-ten victims
to a pit. Their heads were frequently buried in the pit, their bodies in the
ramp; their hands appear to have been tied. These victims had presum-
ably been sacrificed in the fa (*, "dismember" beheading (see Glossary),
a ritual which the oracle-bone inscriptions reveal was applied sometimes
to prisoners of war. 48 In the case of M1001, they were apparently made
to kneel down in rows in the ramps, facing the grave pit, as the tomb
was being sealed. The executioner standing behind decapitated them in
sequence. They were then covered with earth and their skulls and corpses
were beaten into the ground as the fill was rammed hard-a powerful,
46. Guo Baojun 1951:13-15, 21, 23; Cheng Te-k'un 1960:73-74; YXFX: pl. 12; Keightley
1999:265, fig. 4.9. Fiskesjö (2001:75, n. 121) reports "a dichotomy not recorded from any other
tomb"; but the sexual differences are not frequently alluded to in the archaeological reports.
Von Dewall (1960:129-42) finds four kinds of burial represented: corpses with coffins and
bronze ritual items; corpses with coffins and bronze weapons; corpses without coffins but
with ornaments; and corpses without coffins. She suggests that they were representatives of
families allied or subinfeudated to the defunct.

symbolic expression of the dominance of the grave lord and his heirs.49
This is proof of low status, not necessarily of slavery.
The practice of accompanying-in-death was limited to graves of a cer-
tain status;50 the practice of human sacrifice in a mortuary context was still
more restricted. Only 18 of the 939 burials at Yinxu West contained vic-
tims, 38 bodies in all, who had accompanied a master in death.51
It is possible to estimate crudely the impact these ritual killings would
have had on Late Shang society. As of 1983, close to 2,000 sacrificial pits
had been found in the Xiaotun area, containing 2,327 human victims;
some 508 accompaniers-in-death have also been identified.52 If we accept
the estimate that approximately 10,000 human sacrifices were recorded
in the oracle-bone inscriptions, 53 and if we assume that the 2,327 victims
actually excavated may be subsumed within that 10,000 figure, then two
conclusions may be drawn. First, that sacrifice victims outnumbered
accompaniers-in-death by a ratio of about 20 to 1.54 The sacrificial tak-
ing of human life, presumably that of enemies and in a mortuary con-
text, was a routine matter; accompanying-in-death was not. Second, a
total of 10,000 victims distributed over the approximately century and
a half years of the Late Shang occupation of this site would represent a
sacrifice rate of 64.5 victims a year, or about 5 a month.55 If we assume,

Dingyun 1986.

about the same sacrificial event.

perhaps too conservatively, that only 20 percent of the victims have actu-
ally been excavated, 56 then the rate of sacrifice rises to about 25 human
beings a month. Not all these victims, of course, would have been sac-
rificed in mortuary contexts, but to the extent that most Shang sacrifices
were offered to the ancestors buried in the royal cemetery, the extended
impact of the combined mortuary and ancestral cult must have been quite
large (see too ch. 3, pp. 48-49). That impact would have varied with time.
During the reign of Wu Ding, accordingly, well over a hundred victims a
month might have been sacrificed. And the impact, of course, would have
been felt primarily by the enemies of Shang, defeated in battle, who sup-
plied the prisoner-victims.

The Verbs for "Mobilize"
The verb for mobilization was recorded in the bone inscriptions by two
graphs: (which I read as gong #[=gong #]), "to give, present, supply,
raise," as in [47] and [50], and (which I read as deng), "to make ascend,
raise up" (as in [49] and [51]), a word that, in the sense of submitting or rais-
ing up the records of mobilization, was still being associated with raising
men in the Zhouli (p. 4 above), both graphs being written with the two-hands
element included. I use the English word "raise" (for gong) and "raise up"
(for deng) to distinguish the two Shang terms, but the words were semanti-
cally and phonetically related (see Glossary entries for gong and deng), and
the Shang used them interchangeably in contexts of mobilization,' as in:

2. For why I supply the "should" in [49], see the gloss for *Heji 6173; similar logic explains
the "should" in [50]. The word order "verb + noun + numeral"-a pattern seen both with,
in [49], [51], [67], and numerous other cases (such as Heji 6167), and with, as in [47], [50],
[52AB], and Heji 6174, 6175, 6185, most of them divined by Que on scapulas-indicates
that both the noun and the numeral provided new information, so that, in this case, the focus
would have been on both the raising of conscripts and on their number (Takashima 1985).
The alternative order "verb + numeral + noun"-as in Heji 6639-43 and Yingcang 657 (all,
again, divined on scapulas and all, probably, divined by Que [the diviner's name is miss-
ing on Heji 6642])-suggests a focus on the number itself.

6: Labor Mobilization

Gongfang, (for We) will receive abundant assistance]."
(I. Bin) (Heji 6172)5
Que, the same diviner, used the two verbs for "raise"; and the calligrapher
was probably the same.
That references to enemy mobilizations appear rarely, if ever, in Shang
inscriptions is probably explained by the consideration that the enemy
forces, when they became a matter of Shang divinatory concern, had passed
the mobilization stage and were already in the field. That, moreover, no
charges after Period II ([71], [72]) refer to raising the zhong or ren of Shang-
despite the notable campaigns in which Di Yi (K28) and Di Xin (K29) partic-
ipated-may reflect either the routinization of Shang mobilizations (and
the development of a standing army?), the secularization of warfare, the
general shrinkage in the scope of the divinatory concerns of Period V (Di Yi
and Di Xin), or the accidents of retrieval. That, at that time, the Shang king
was still divining about the loss of zhong ([93A-C]) indicates, in any event,
that the zhong were still being employed in situations of danger.
The Shang was a powerful military power (see, e.g., [47]-[52]; ch. 13).
But such inscriptions also remind us that the mobilization of Shang labor,
like many other aspects of Shang administration, probably involved the
making of written records. The raising and consecrating of the written tab-
lets, the making of the primary divinatory records—which I have referred
to elsewhere as the "diviners' notebooks" (Keightley 2001)--and the incis-
ing of the oracle-bone inscriptions themselves, all point to a regime that
paid considerable attention to the making and veneration of written texts.
And the mobilizations themselves, involving the ren in their thousands,
would have likewise, in all probability, have involved the keeping of ros-
ters. The mobilization of Shang labor, in short, validated by the divinatory
guidance and approval of the Powers, and recorded on bamboo slips or
other perishable materials, and also recorded on divinatory scapulas and
plastrons, would have partly depended upon the training, recruitment,
and mobilization of a staff of scribes and engravers, whose contribution-
recording, validating, and preserving the choices that the king made-
was partly essential to the success of the Shang labor forces.³ (I will return
to this point in my conclusions.)

extensive than the oracle-bone and bronze inscriptions, see the discussion at Keightley 2001.

ing a lidless dou vessel (a bowl with attached stand)-was also used in
the inscriptions for offering to the Powers such grain-related gifts as mil-
let and fragrant ale. A number of modern scholars have read the graph as
an early form of deng, "to raise up, present."¹² The absence of the lid,
the placement of the hands lower down the vessel, and the different con-
texts in which the graphs were used seem to distinguish oracular from

I cite these deng cases, and, in which the verb was written with the
lidless vessel ([55]-[60]), for four reasons. First, because they demonstrate
a certain graphic and phonetic closeness between the verbs involved in
raising men ([49], [51]) and the verb involved in making sacrificial offer-
ings ([55]-[60]).
Second, and more importantly, because the key distinction seems to
have involved the nature of what was being raised or presented. When
grain-based offerings, like millet and ale, were involved, the graph used
to write the verb was the lidless deng, , "present," as in [58]-[59]." When
humans were involved-either as conscripts (as in [498] and [51]) 18 or as

the significance of this scheduling by gan day and temple name, see Keightley 2004:12-16.

Third, because graphs with or without the "spiritual" element (as
in [58] and [60]) were apparently interchangeable (as indicated at S585.3),
which suggests, once again, the degree to which the royal mobilizations
had their religious dimension.
And fourth, because the presence or absence of the lid does not seem
to have been critical, a "two-hands-and-a-vessel" graph could be used
in contexts of ritual offering-as in [54], where the vessel in the deng
graph was lidded, and in [55]-[58], where the deng was lidless-and,
also in contexts of mobilization-as in [49] and [51], where the vessel in
the deng graph was lidded. I doubt that any semantic distinction was
involved in these variations in graph form (see too ch. 13, n. 26 below),
but their use in contexts of sacrifice suggests the significantly religious
nature of the mobilizations themselves, between mobilizing the ren for
warfare and mobilizing the spirits for support (see too ch. 16). It should
be stressed, however, that this religious dimension was not explicit; men
were never "raised" to or for a particular ancestor, but the gong and deng
verbs presumably shared in religious and ritual power. 20 The conscripts
so mobilized would have been consecrated and blessed by the divina-
tory and ritual attention that the Shang devoted to raising and presenting
them.
The religious dimension of the Shang mobilizations is further under-
scored by the reference to another ritual (cheng ce or; see the
Glossary entry for cheng [ce]) which appears to have involved the ritual
raising and consecrating of tablets upon which military orders or prayers
had been recorded 21 (see too [78]):

It may be noted that [68A]-and probably the fragmentary Heji 23,
divined on the same day as and with a charge that appears to have been
identical to that of [68A], and also on a plastron-is the only inscription in
which the diviners associated the zhong, zhongren, or ren with "strength-
ening His Majesty's affairs ()" (Y256.2-58.1). I suspect that it was
primarily the various leaders and officers directing the work who were
thought to be involved in the king's affairs, not the zhong or ren who actu-
ally performed the tasks. 10 The king's affairs in [68A] would thus have
been conceived primarily in terms of the retainer's obligations to the king,
not in terms of the workers' obligations to the king.
In every case in which the zhong were "raised" the verb was gong (
rather than deng. If, as seems to have been the case, the diviners almost
never used the gong ¼ verb when they specified the numbers of persons
involved (see the Glossary entry for gong #), but used the deng verb
instead (as in [49], [51], and [223]; [50] represents the sole exception)," this
preference for gong when the zhong were being raised would provide fur-
ther grounds for thinking that it was not necessary for the king to specify
the number of the zhong, further grounds for concluding that they formed
an established cadre or ganbu + of royal followers, probably of limited
and known size.
That the zhong were seldom mobilized further suggests that, at least
under Wu Ding, their status was close to that of other administrative
units, such as the Duo Gong I ("Many Gong") (see [10], [12]) and the
Duo She ("Many Archers") (see [89], [109]) who were rarely "raised"
in this way. The few exceptions to this generalization follow the rule in
which the gong verb was almost never used when numbers were speci-
fied whereas the deng verb was used routinely when numbers were
involved. They include such cases as¹2

The distinction between the various royal retainers, officers, zhong, and
ren, accordingly, is once again not clear-cut. Wu Ding raised ren far more
frequently in the ninety-some cases referred to previously (see, e.g.,
[62AB]-[67])-than he did other groups, and he raised them in far greater
numbers (see too ch. 9). Raising ren, accordingly, would have been a sig-
nificant matter, which possibly strained the dynasty's resources in ways
that the mobilization of the small groups of officers did not, and which,
accordingly, demanded considerably more divinatory attention. But all
social groups and all statuses were subject to the king's call-in defense of
the realm, in defense of the harvests, in defense of the dynasty, in defense
of the ancestors.
[78]
[稱]冊ㄚ王臣|
"... [raise] the tablets (cheng ce) ... raise His Majesty's
Servitors...." (I. Bin) (*Heji 39830)
In all such cases, which use the deng verb, the king was "raising"
groups of men- the Archers, His Majesty's Servitors, the Many Quivers,
the Horse(-chariot officers)-who were presumably already in existence,
at least as named units with an organizational structure. The divinations
indicate that he was now mobilizing these cadres, and presumably sancti-
fying them for the task at hand. They represented the "regulars" to whom
larger units of men might then be attached. I believe that the zhong, as
dynastic workers, were likewise already available or "on call" to serve the
king at all times. In the case of [72], it is possible that the zhong were being

lay in the area occupied by the Chunqiu state of Zheng; and that, after extensive discussion
of the historical sources, the Shang region of Zheng was located in the area of modern Xin-
zheng (45 k south-southeast of Zhengzhou).

The Occupational Lineages
Very few divinations about Shang state manufacturing have been found.¹
The question of who the dynasty's laborers were, therefore, can be an-
swered only by a series of careful inferences drawn from the objects they
made, from archaeological finds like workshops and tools, and from our
understanding of Shang society in general. Guo Moruo, for instance, had
proposed that the bronzes of Late Shang and early Zhou had been made by
slave labor (see pp. 53-54 above). Another approach is based on the evidence
of the so-called "lineage names" incised on the oracle bones, bronzes, and
pottery of Shang. Some of these names suggest specific occupations, and
Satō Taketoshi (1962:14-20, 321-24), following the research of Ding Shan
and Shirakawa Shizuka, had proposed that skilled handicraft production
was in the hands of occupational lineages (shokugyō shizoku).
It should be stressed that no precision is implied by the use of the
word "lineages." In the discussion that follows I use it simply to refer
to a group of families bearing the same surname.2 That the divinations
refer to numerous such lineage groups and to various single zu lineages
(Y986.1-87.1), with many of the lineage names also appearing as insignia
at the end of Shang bronze inscriptions,³ indicates that the Shang king
1. Some of the discussion that follows is taken from Keightley 1969:29-39.
2. Kwang-chih Chang (1963:168; cf. 1968:243; 1977:284) had suggested that, in Shang times,
"the families of the Royal House, the nobility, and some of the craftsmen were of the extended
family type, probably patrilineal.... Beyond the family, unilinear lineages may have been
prevalent among the nobility and some of the craftsmen; there were possibly ramagelike kin
groups based on patrilineality and primogeniture." "In tracing the distribution of individ-
ual emblems in bronze inscriptions, one finds that it did not follow political borders. The ju
emblem, for example, occurs in bronzes known to have been unearthed in an area that
stretches from Qishan [] to Shandong and from Henan to Hunan" (Kwang-chih Chang
1980:253); see too Rao Zongyi 1985:302-03.
3. Hayashi (1968:34-43) identified 217 oracle-bone names-of Fang countries, Hou
and Bo É rulers, and places and persons-which appear as lineage insignia on Shang and
Western Zhou bronzes; the number has subsequently increased.
8: The Occupational Lineages
93
presided over a confederation of patrilineal descent groups. Composite
names, moreover, reveal the fission and union of lineages.4 The existence
of lineage groups may also be discerned in the cemeteries excavated in
the Xiaotun area. The graves in the Yinxu West cemetery, for example (see
too ch. 4, n. 27) may be separated into eight sectors, distinguished by both
their pottery assemblages and by the lineage insignia cast into the buried
ritual bronzes, showing that the lineage associations of the living were
preserved among the dead. According to Underhill and Fang (2004:134-
35), Xiao Nan (1981:269–70) has argued
that small graves in the large cemetery in the western district of Anyang
[Yinxu West] provide evidence to support the conclusion that there was
a high degree of occupational specialization. In more than one section,
some graves contain distinct sets of bronze, stone, and bone tools that
appear to represent specialized production. In Area Eight, 24 percent
of the small graves contain bronze knives and awls interpreted as tools
used to produce bone objects. The same tools were found in the remains
of a bone workshop nearby in the Beixin zhuang [] section of
Anyang.
<>屋存上加使之
The burial area of Dasikong cun shows similar divisions. 5 These zu pre-
sumably functioned as social and political entities whose members were
linked to the king by a differential hierarchy of kinship ties, benefits, privi-
leges, and obligations. Led by their lineage heads, they served the king in
warfare, in hunting, in agriculture, in other activities involving dependent
labor, and in the providing of tribute, and received, in return, his spiritual
and military assistance.6
With regard to the question of the services they may have performed,
there is no doubt that certain Shang lineage names can be related to occu-
pations. The name of Qin (=, "catch, capture"), for example," might
have belonged to a lineage which, at least in origin, might have been asso-
ciated with hunting (Satō 1962:15-26). Such linkages, however, are bound
to be speculative. The fact that a lineage took a dog and fish as its lineage
emblem on a bronze that it cast, for example, is no proof that it reared
4. Zhu Fenghan 1990a:94-104; Cao Dingyun 1995:48-49-
5. Kaogu xuebao 1979:113-17; Yang Xizhang 1983:931-32; Yang Hsi-chang 1986:56-57; Zhu
Fenghan 1990a:106-23; Zheng Ruokui 1995:97-92; Han Jianye 1997; Yang Shengnan 1999;
Yang Baocheng 2002:74-77; Thorp 2006:150-52. YXFX:447 gives at least ten burial sectors in
Yinxu West, with definite empty spaces separating the burial sectors. Yates (1997:84, fig. 5.7)
gives a zu-based map, based upon Zheng Ruokui 1995:86, fig. 2; see too Yung-ti Li 2003:
306, fig. 7.2.
6. The following discussion is largely based, with revisions, on Zhu Fenghan 1990a:35-90.
For further discussion of the Shang lineages, see Cao Dingyun 1995:44-54.
7. See [22]-[25], [71], [72].
[5:1]94
94
Working for His Majesty
dogs-as the Duo quan, "Many Dog [officers]" recorded in the bone
inscriptions (Li Xueqin 1958:6; Y606.2; JGWD:1097) are supposed to have
done-and caught fish. But even if the groups caught dogs and fish at
one time, there would be no certainty that such was still a central part of
their concerns.
We also lack any firm evidence that the various groups represented by
these names were organized as lineages. Whatever ties bound the royal
artisans together, they are not known. The Duo gong I, for instance
(who were presumably ancestral to the Bai gong EI, "Hundred Arti-
sans," of the Zhou), or the artisans ranked beneath them, may simply
have been corporate groups, partly hereditary in nature, but open also
to outside recruits. We have no evidence about their lineage organization
or putative lineage temples (cf. Barbieri-Low 2007:57). It may be useful,
in this connection, to consider the be of Yamato Japan-agricultural,
industrial, or service guilds of workers. 10 These be were held together more
by shared locale or occupation than by actual blood ties, yet, in the words
of John Whitney Hall (1966:34), "As a means of assuring cohesion, kinship
bonds were frequently assumed where none in fact existed." The fictive
kinship relationships within the be offer a possible model of what may
have existed in Shang China.
Seeking further support for his theory of occupational clans, Satō
(1962:14-15) turned to the Zuozhuan 左傳, Shiji 史記, Yi Zhou shu 逸周書,
and Shiben to draw up a table of twenty-seven or twenty-eight (one
is in doubt) Shang "lineage names," only a few of which seem to appear
in the oracle-bone or bronze inscriptions." That more than half of these are
also place-names does not invalidate the hypothesis, for place-name and
group name were frequently identical in Shang China (Zhang Bingquan
1967, esp. p. 689). Li Yanong, also turning to Zhou sources, believed that
eight of the thirteen clan names of Shang listed in the Zuozhuan might be
linked to occupations. The passage in Ding Gong TA4 (506 B.C.) which
he quotes does show that groups of conquered Yin (Shang) people, who
may have played a role in dependent labor, were thought to have been
organized as lineages at the start of the Western Zhou. The members of

(1) the Yu Fu Yi ding Z (Luo Zhenyu 1937:3.1.5); (2) the Shu si zi ding
(Guo
Moruo 1960:1-2; Barnard 1963:219; Akatsuka 1977:751-53; Bagley 1987:530). Guo believed
that the symbol represented the insigne of either the maker of the vessel or the writer of the
inscription.
9. See Keightley (1969:155-79) on the Gong and Bai gong of Western Zhou. For the Si Gong
I and his subordinates, see Zhang Yachu and Liu Yu 1986:22-24, 74-75; Itō 1987:248–58.
10. See Philippi (1969:468-69) for a succinct account of the be.
11. E.g., Xu, a surname; but other readings are also given (Sōran, no. 0184). The most
popular is tu, "road, way."
8: The Occupational Lineages
the Suo
95
95
("rope") lineage were rope workers, 12 of the Chang Shuo
("long ladle") and Wei Shuo ("bottom ladle" [?]) were makers of
wine vessels, of the Tao ("pottery") lineage were potters, of the Yi
("spread out") lineage were flag makers, of the Yi ("pot") lineage were
cauldron makers, of the Pan ("strap, belt") lineage were horse-tassel
makers, and of the Fan ("fences") lineage were makers of bamboo fenc-
es. 13 The hypothesis that the members of a lineage named Tao (pottery)
say, were potters, is attractive. But neither Satō nor Li Yanong provide
any evidence that the Tao lineage ever made pots. The only proof lies in
the fact that over half the lineage names listed in Ding Gong 4 can be
associated with manufactured products; this suggests more than a coinci-
dental link between lineage names and manufacturing occupations. And
at least four of these lineages-those who may have made wine vessels,
flags, cauldrons, and horse tassels-would have been making items for
the ruling elite.
It is not certain, of course, that the Zuozhuan may be trusted to give an
accurate account of events at the start of Zhou, but I find Satō's case plau-
sible, especially since the burial sectors were organized by lineages. In
lineage cemeteries with both elites and commoners, such as Yinxu West
and Dasikong cun, we find that each sector had about 100-plus to 300-plus
burials. Their bronze inscriptions show different clan insignia in the dif-
ferent sectors. The medium to small burials show similar clues, indicating
branch lineages and kinship status. In all the burial sectors the small buri-
als were in the great majority, the numerous commoners. The graveless
burials were all found in residence remains; they had no special cemetery,
no lineages. Oracle-bone inscriptions-and pre-Qin documents-show
the zu lineage or kindred may have been the basic unit of Shang soci-
ety, whether for production or assembling troops (YXFX:447-48).
The link between name and occupation is a logical one; in the West
too, as in Japan, names such as Baker, Carpenter, Draper, Fletcher, Mason,
Tinker, Weaver, and so on derived from actual occupations. I would sug-
gest, accordingly, that significant elements of Shang dynastic labor were
performed by corporations of skilled workers attached to the royal house;
it is probable that such corporations were organized on the basis of real
or assumed kinship ties, but firm evidence is lacking.14 Evidence from
12. The discovery of two early Western Zhou bronzes in southwest Shandong bearing the
name and symbol of the Suo, "rope," lineage (Guo Keyu et al. 1990:37-38; see too Underhill
and Fang 2004:134) provides possible support for Li Yanong's view.

Working for His Majesty
5,000. And the numbers may on occasion have reached as high as 8,000.⁹
And there was, as we shall see in [223], one reference to the 10,000 mem-
bers of the lü -regiment: . Indeed, the ren were virtually the only
object that the diviners counted by their thousands." And that close asso-
ciation between "men" and "thousand" was reinforced by the Shang prac-
tice of writing qian F, "1,000," with a single horizontal line through the
character for ren\\, “man.” Similarly, "2,000" was written with a double
line through the ren, "3,000" with a triple line, and so on. 12 The use of the
ren graph to write qian was no doubt partly motivated by phonetic consid-
erations (e.g., JGWD:218-19). The king's frequent divinations about rais-
ing men in their 3,000s testifies to Wu Ding's ability to stage such impres-
sive mobilizations on a fairly routine basis.
One should not, however, exaggerate the Shang's ability in this regard.
Dong Zuobin (1945:II:9:38a), for example, cited seven charges (including
[67]) that led him to the conclusion that Wu Ding raised a total of 23,000
men, for a campaign against the Gongfang, in a period of thirty-eight
days. But this conclusion may be questioned on at least three grounds.
First, none of the inscriptions he cited records a moon number, so it is not
certain that his reconstruction of the inscription sequence, based entirely
on the ganzhi day dates, is correct. Second, only four of the seven
inscriptions actually specify that the Gongfang were the enemy, so that
once again they may not all have been divined about the same campaign.
And third, even if the thirty-eight-day sequence is correct, and even if all
the charges were about the same campaign, it is possible that many of
the charges were duplicates in the sense that Wu Ding would have been
divining about the same 3,000 men with which he had started the divina-
tions, not about a fresh levy.

vention that gong was not used when the charge specified the number of men involved.

Accuracy of the Figures
13
99元
The royal workshops produced a variety of bronze armaments, includ-
ing dagger-axes, arrowheads, spearheads, helmets, shields, and chariot
fittings. Faced with reports of enemy invasions, the Shang king led his
forces in person, allied himself with other leaders, or dispatched his gen-
erals, men like Qin (Y1091.1-93.1; see Glossary), Qiao (Y667.2-69.2),
and Guo of Zhi (Zhi Guo Y911.2–16.1). And he mobilized his troops.
That the charges often link the mobilizations to particular campaigns sug-
gests the ad hoc nature of these levies and the need to have them validated
by divine sanction: [49] "(We) should raise up men, 3,000 (of them), and
call upon them to attack the Gongfang, (for We) will receive abundant
assistance"; [50] "In the present season, His Majesty should raise men,
5,000 (of them), and march to regulate the Tufang, (for He) will receive
abundant assistance." The king, according to the oracle-bone inscriptions,
only raised men by their thousands in Period I (or occasionally in Period
II; Y13.2-14.2).
The Shang usually raised their troops in numbers of 3,000 to 5,000, but
whether these figures are trustworthy merits consideration. 14 Later his-
torical texts record that when Wu Wang E of the Zhou attacked the
last Shang king in 1045 B.C., he had 300 chariots and 3,000 life-guards
(Shaughnessy 1988:229–31). These Zhou figures accord well with the
Shang mobilization figures of 3,000 men. Shaughnessy cites the large size
traditionally attributed to the Shang army in Zhou and Han texts, which,
according to the Shiji, was as large as 700,000 men, a figure that seems
impossibly large (see too p. 49).
One of the most striking features of the historical accounts of battle in
the late Warring States period-as opposed to the more theoretical pro-
posals in the treatises-is the remarkable size of the armies that the vari-
ous contending states put into the field. The texts indicate that, by contrast
with the armies of the Spring and Autumn period, which rarely exceeded
10,000 combatants, the armies of the Warring States period were appar-
ently far larger (Cho-yun Hsu 1965:67, 71). Many of the figures given for
the armies involved in the final campaigns that led to the unification of
China in 221 B.C. are, however, unbelievably large. We are told, for exam-
ple, that Qin cut off 240,000 heads after its forces defeated Han and
Wei in 293 B.C., and that Qin put more than 400,000 men to death after
it defeated Zhao at the battle of Changping in 260 B.C.15
13. See, e.g., Hayashi 1968; 1972 (the Yin sections); Wan Jiabao 1970; Chen Zhida 1989;
YXFX:266-67, 300-22; Shen Rong 1998.
14. See too Gibson (1938:15) for an early questioning of these figures in sacrifice victims.
15. Chavannes, tr., 1967:II:82, 91; Watson 1993:31. As Cho-yun Hsu notes, "The historical
records of Qin, the main source of information used by the Shiji for the Zhanguo period, may100
Working for His Majesty
Bodde challenges such figures in some detail. He notes that the force of
600,000, for example, which the Qin is said to have used in its campaign of
224-223 B.C. which led to the destruction of Chu,
seems incredibly high when compared with the figures, ranging from
130,000 to 300,000 recorded for troops and cavalry representing the entire
Han empire during the campaigns of 133-90 B.C., which took place under
Wu Di against the Xiongnu in Inner Asia.
And he notes, following the discussion of Michael Loewe (1974:92, 95-96),
that "even these Han figures, in fact, are very possibly inflated." If we
accept Sima Qian's figures,
the combined casualties thus allegedly inflicted by Qin on all its rivals
during the entire 130-year period [364-234 B.C.] amount to 1,489,000.
It should be noted that they represent only the losses inflicted by Qin
on other states; Qin's own losses are never recorded, though they too
must have been considerable.
It would not seem physically possible, for example, with the techni-
cal means at hand, to have massacred virtually completely an army of
400,000, even granted the known fact that this army had been weakened
by siege and hunger.... Nor does it seem possible that Qin's opponents,
or Qin itself for that matter, could have continued to raise huge armies
time after time in the face of such crushing losses. ... One partial answer
to the problem possibly lies in the word wan [] (myriad), a round num-
ber constantly appearing in military accounts (and in nonmilitary con-
texts too...), where perhaps it is only symbolic and is to be understood
as signifying no more than "large unit."16
A
Once again, therefore, the historicity of our sources is questionable
for Eastern Zhou, Qin, and Han. Either such figures testify to formidable
powers of labor recruitment or to considerable powers of exaggeration.
No independent confirmation of such figures has yet been discovered. Nor
have the logistical problems involved in supplying, say, contending forces
totaling some 800,000 men on a battlefield like that of Changping (if we
assume that the two armies were at least equal in size) been addressed."
have greatly exaggerated these figures, especially since they concern battles in which Qin
was the victor" (1965:67).
16. Bodde 1986:99-100; he cites Loewe 1974:92, 95-96; also see Lewis 1999:625-28. Further
reasons for questioning the accuracy of the early Chinese casualty figures are provided by
Contamine (1984:257), who concludes that such counts, as made or reported after medieval
battles in Europe, "cannot entirely be relied upon." His discussion of the size of armies in the
Classical world and early modern Europe (pp. 306-07) also suggests how truly extraordinary
the figures claimed for Warring States armies in China would have been.
17. Cf. the conclusion of Contamine (1984:307) that "the true problem for medieval pow-
ers (in any event after 1200) was not to assemble considerable armies but to maintain them in
the field for more than a few weeks."
9: Numbers
101
It is entirely possible that the Shang used a "thousand" (gian F) in the
same way that the Zhou appear to have used wan, as a unit that simply
meant "a large number." Nevertheless, the smaller Shang numbers seem
plausible, and they accord well with the numbers of laborers whose pres-
ence can be postulated from the scale of the tomb-building, wall-building,
and bronze-casting industries associated with Shang archaeological sites.18
So when the Shang used 1,000 or 3,000, etc., the figures are generally reli-
able. At the least the diviners thought them true enough; they did not lie
to the Powers.
Casualties among the Zhong
Since the zhong served in the royal armies, their risk and possible loss in
battle-quite apart from the number of men involved-was a significant
divinatory concern. Two related phrases were involved in the divinations
about casualties: sang zhong (or sang zhongren),19 and zhi zhong

There would appear to have been a strong ritual or incantatory quality
to this last set of divination charges, as the diviner invoked each of the
Guards' names in turn, divining the positive wish that they would not
lose His Majesty's zhong ([85A-E]), and then taking account of the less
desirable alternative, first for all five Guards ([85F]) collectively, and then,
presumably, for each one ([85G]).

Although the king frequently divined about mobilizing the ren for battle,³
he divined about losing them not at all. By contrast, although the king
rarely divined about mobilizing the zhong ([68A]-[70]), he divined about
losing them far more often ([82]-[83], [85A-G], [87]-[93A-C]).35 The Shang
diviners, furthermore, applied the verb for "loss," zhi, mainly to the
31. For a similar charge, see [85D].

[93C], and also after [93C] (above it on the scapula). See too [226A-C].

33 cases of sang zhong
(Y520.1, mainly in Period I) and 41 cases of zhi zhong
(Y666.1-67.1, mainly in the later periods), compared with only 7 cases of "raising"
zhong (gong zhong ); see p. 87, n. 6.
zhong and to "His Majesty's zhong" in contexts that were plainly military;36
in only three possible instances was zhi applied to the ren alone.37 In short,
the king rarely divined about mobilizing the zhong but frequently divined
about losing them; he frequently divined about mobilizing the ren but
rarely divined about losing them. This suggests once again that he val-
ued the zhong-members, and probably officers, of his permanent labor
force who qualified as his retainers-more than he valued the ren whom
he recruited as occasion required. Such evidence suggests that the zhong
were in a state of permanent readiness and did not need to be called up;
it suggests that, in consequence, they might have been more regimented
and disciplined than the ren (as implied by [239]-[241]); and it indicates,
once again, that the king was more concerned about their loss and val-
ued them more. The actual number of losses-among the zhong and the
ren-is not generally given in the divinatory inscriptions. The king might
"raise" 3,000 men. The king might fear their loss. But the casualty figures
are almost never given. 38

Work Schedule of the Diviners
The inscriptional information is too fragmentary for modern scholars to
reconstruct the working schedules of the zhong and ren. They appear to
have served at the pleasure of the king and his ancestors, but it is not pos-
sible to document the intensity of his demands. The divinations-with
each charge usually addressed to a single, ad hoc event-would sug-
gest that labor was mobilized irregularly, at the whim of the ruler. But it
must be borne in mind that the primary function of the divinations was to
ensure the approval of the Powers for the particular mobilization or other
action being contemplated. The regular recruitment of statute labor-
which, for example, was performed in theory for one month a year in the
Qin-Han period (Hulsewé 1984:196)-might well have passed undivined,
and would thus have been unrecorded in the inscriptions. We can see the
system of recruitment in action, but it is harder to discern the custom-
ary expectations that supported it or the degree to which it was regularly
invoked.
The activities of the diviners, however, do throw some light on actual
work schedules at the end of the second millennium B.C. The Li-group
diviners, for example, practiced their scapulimancy in the temples of rit-
ual units whose constituent ancestors had been given the temple name of
a particular gan stem-jia, yi, bing, ding, and so on. This is demonstrated
by the following sequence of postfaces (in italics) on a Li-group bone:
[94A] [甲寅貞: 其大禦王]自上甲用白家九[中示。几牛.]在大
甲宗卜
[On jiayin(?) (day 51?) divined: "(We) will (qi) perform a
Great Exorcism (yu) for His Majesty (to the ancestors)] from
Shang Jia (P1) (on down); will sacrifice-in-blood-covenant
(with the spirits) (meng) (?) white pigs (?), nine of them; [(to
the) Middle Ancestors (Xia Shi) will cut up (and offer the
blood of) (?) a cow."] Cracked in the temple of Da Jia (K3).

107
Related inscriptions on this and one other scapula (Heji 34047), probably
divined over a thirty-one day period (between days 24 [?] and 54), permit
1. Cf. Li Xueqin and Peng Yushang 1996:195, 205, 222.108
Working for His Majesty
a number of observations that bear on the working practices of these
Shang bureaucrats.
First, the temple in which the Li-group diviners conducted their pyro-
mantic rituals was strictly determined by the content of the divination
charge and by the day of divination. Thus, one charge [94A] was cracked
in the temple of Da Jia on a jia day, two charges [94BC] were cracked in
the temples of Zu Yi and Da Yi on an yi day, and three more were cracked
in the temple of Father Ding on a ding day [94D-F]. Other examples of
this linkage between day name and temple name can be found.² Whether
these moves were between temples or simply between "apartments" in
the same building is not always certain.³
Second, over the four-day period (inclusive) from jiayin (day 51) to
dingsi (day 54), the divining staff must have moved the Tunnan 2707 scap-
ula four times, from the temple of Da Jia (K3), to the temple of Zu Yi
(K12), to the temple of Da Yi (K1), to the temple of Father Ding (K21).
The scapula would have been moved to the temple of the appropriate
gan name as the week progressed from gan day to gan day and the divin-
ers accordingly addressed one gan-named ancestor after another. These
inscriptions bring us particularly close to the diviners' working habits
over three thousand years ago. The movement between temples, synchro-
nized to match the religious names of the days of the week, may even
remind us of the king's seasonal movements in the Mingtang "the
Bright Hall," as described in the "Yueling " sections of Lüshi chunqiu
(first twelve chapters), Liji (chapter 4), and Huainanzi Ħ
子 (chapter 5).6
2. E.g., the charges on Tunnan 3763 were cracked in a Ding temple on a ding day (day 34),
in the temple of Da Wu (K7) on a wu day (day 35), and in the temple of Da Geng (K5) on a
geng day (day 37); Tunnan 3764 was cracked in the temple of Zu Ding (K15) on a ding day.
The scapula fragment Tunnan 2742 (Period III) is also instructive. The charge involved cult to
Father Ji (= Zu Ji, the brother of Zu Keng [K22]), Zu Jia (K23), and Father Geng (= Zu Geng,
K22), but it was divined (on a ding) day and, evidently, in the Ding temple (Zhu Fenghan
1990:12). There was more than one system; perhaps the Ding temple was chosen to worship
Wu Ding, the grandfather.
3. "Modern archaeology offers modest insight into the actual altars and temples at Yinxu
where offerings were made"; details are provided in Thorp 2006:185. See too ch. 15, n. 23,
and p. 206.
4. I use the term "staff" advisedly because no Shang inscription that records divination
in a temple ever records the name of a diviner. The absence of the name was a characteristic
feature of the Li diviner group inscriptions in general (Lin Yun 1989:54).
5. The scapula Tunnan 3763 must have also undergone the same kind of movement—
between three different temples in a four-day period. Similarly the inscriptions on Heji 34047
(Period I-II, Li) indicate that the scapula would have been moved from the Great Temple to
the Small Temple.
6. See Wu Hung 1995:77-88; Lewis 2006:260–73.
10: Work Schedule of the Diviners
109
This theological detail indicates that the ritualists at the royal Shang
court had created a highly ordered situation in which sacrifices and
announcements to the ancestors were divined and performed according
to a strictly controlled schedule and in strictly controlled places. It also
confirms that divination, ritual, and sacrifice, the major modes of Shang
religious mediation, were, at least in certain cases, intimately related to
the royal ancestors, being performed in their temples and according to a
schedule determined by their names (Keightley 2004:20-26).
The diviners, in fact, routinely performed their duties on every day
of the week (see the preliminary evidence presented in Table 2, on page
248). The Shang regarded certain days as luckier than others, but whether
such hemerological bias affected the days on which divinations were per-
formed-rather than, as was certainly the case, the days for which particu-
lar actions were contemplated-needs to be tested for particular diviners,
particular topics, and particular periods. Some periodicities were certainly
involved. I provide eight examples:
exan
Keightley squeezed out some significant
details about work schedule of the diviners.

other day. This is partly because routine divinations of the form

(2) A count of the "receive harvest" inscriptions suggests that although
charge pairs like[132AB]-我受黍年/我弗其受黍年,“We will receive mil-
let harvest/We might not receive millet harvest"—were divined on every
gan day, there was a tendency for this particular ritual of pyromantic reas-
surance to be performed on the gui and jia days, that is, on the last and first
days of the Shang ten-day week.12
(3) The liao burnt offering, for example, was also divined most fre-
quently on gui (16.7%) and jia (14.5%) days; it was divined least on
wu (6.1%) days. You -cutting sacrifices were divined most frequently
on gui (22.2%) and jia (11.7%) days, with wu (3.5%) again being the least
used.13 The ritualists, therefore, seem to have preferred certain days, but
they did not favor them rigidly. And the figures at least permit the pre-
liminary inference that where divinations about rituals were concerned
there was heavier activity at the turn of the week, i.e., on gui and jia days.
(4) Since the sacrifices in the regular five-ritual sacrifice schedule were
offered to ancestors on the gan days of their temple names, 14 divinations
about the ancestral cult frequently took place on the name day itself:

甲戍卜賓貞:翌乙亥ㄓ于祖乙.用. 五月
Crack-making on jiawu (day 11), Bin divined: "On the next

The inscriptions concerning cult offered to Zu Yi (K12) in the reign of Wu
Ding may serve as an example. Seventy-seven involved divinations that
were made in advance of the actual day on which cult was offered. In only
twenty-three cases were the divination and the cult actually performed on
the same day (sample at Y1394.1-98.2). This suggests that approximately
70 percent of divinations about cult that specified the day of the cult were
made in advance. Furthermore, the majority of these preparatory divina-
tions were made one day in advance of the cult offering. 15 The evidence
is less plentiful for Wu Ding inscriptions about cult to Zu Xin (K13), but
similar conclusions can be obtained. Indeed, only one divination was per-
formed on the day of cult (Heji 959); forty-one cases were performed in
advance (sample at Y1406.1-08.2).
The diviners, and presumably the ritualists who accompanied them,
worked on a rigid schedule. And by the time of Di Yi and Di Xin, their
schedule was laid down in advance.

0
The divination was performed during the last day of the Shang week, on
the gui day, and the five rituals (only two of them listed here) and the
ancestral recipient were specified in each case. As the sacrificial schedule
became more regular, so did the divinations about it. And classification
was strictly observed with no error. The diviners had to know the family
tree and the ritual cycle. Clearly, the divinations had become rather rou-
tine, but they still had to be performed on schedule.
(5) Turning to enterprises that involved larger mobilizations of the labor
force, by the end of the dynasty divinations about the para-military royal
hunts, by which the king "showed the flag" to the peoples of his domain,
had become equally systematic. As Matsumaru Michio 1963 has shown,
the Shang kings and diviners of Period II started to reveal a preference
for divining about, and carrying out, these expeditions on yi Z, wu X,
and xin days; in Periods III and IV, they added ren E day divinations
and hunts to their normal divine-and-hunt schedule; in Period V, they
also included the ding T day. This means that, under the last two kings,
the pyromancers routinely divined the royal hunts on five out of every
ten days, on the second, fourth, fifth, eighth, and ninth days of the week.
As Table 2 (p. 248), column 3, reveals, the wu day was apparently the most
popular day for such divinations, followed by xin and ren. One may notice
that the hunt divinations evidently avoided the tenth and first days of the
Shang "weekend," the period when other types of ritual activity may have
peaked (see paragraphs 1-4 herein).
(6) A comparison of the columns in Table 2 does indeed suggest that a
day that was little used for divination about one topic might have been
more used for another. For example, divinations about divine "approval"
were commonly divined on jia and bing days, whereas divinations about
hunts were rarely performed on those two days. Put more generally, the
comprehensive figures given in column 5 indicate that, although the ding
(13 percent), wu (16 percent), and xin days (15 percent) saw slightly more
divinatory activity than the 10 percent that would have resulted from a
random distribution, the pyromantic "load" was, on the whole, distrib-
uted relatively equitably among all the days in the week. Nor is there
any evidence, on the basis of the figures in column 5, to conclude that114
Working for His Majesty
10: Work Schedule of the Diviners
115
the diviners preferred to divine on the first five days of the week rather
than the second; 50 percent of the divinations were performed on days jia
through wu, 50 percent were performed on days ji through gui. Nor did
the diviners show any preference for "even" over "odd" days; 361 divi-
nations were performed on days jia, bing, wu, geng, and ren; 361 were per-
formed on days yi, ding, ji, xin, and gui. It may be concluded, accordingly,
that the diviners, taken as a whole, maintained a fairly constant level of
activity. Every day was likely to involve a significant share of pyroman-
tic scorchings and crackings. The ritualists had ordered their activities so
that their work, and necessarily that of the diviners, was evenly shared.
There was no "weekend," at least in general terms, among the Shang
diviners.

was possibly no set time for the divination, but mainly a day or days in
advance:17

The Shang diviners must have frequently started work early; so must the
king (assuming the king was up). Such practice was maintained through
later dynasties. 20
(8) When we turn to the activity of particular diviners, preliminary
research has indicated no evident periodicity in their work schedules.21
There is no evidence, for example, that a diviner took certain days off in
any systematic way.22 And it is possible to show that at least some of the
diviners, and quite probably all of them, worked every day of the ten-day
week. This does not mean that they did not take days of rest; but it does
mean that, for these officers, no day was a regular day of rest. 23
It seems likely, in fact, that some diviners worked at their task without
break for a period of several months. 24 It is possible to conclude, for exam-
ple, that the Period II diviner Lü may have divined the good fortune of
the coming night, on a daily basis, for a period of at least forty-three con-
secutive days, as in:

frightfully early, about five or six o'clock in the morning" (Lien-sheng Yang 1961:51).
21. Durrant's early suggestion (1972) that divinations about charges containing zai +
(Y905.1-909.2), "to harm" militarily, tended to be divined in the last three days of the week,
i.e., on xin (14 days), ren (25 days), and gui (23 days), may also have merit; the yi (20 days)
may be added to the list. I base my count on Y905.1-11.1. The average count for the other six
days of the gan-named cycle is 7.

Working for His Majesty
Crack-making on bingshen (day 33), Lü divined: "This night
there will be nothing ominous (huo)." In the tenth moon.
[105B] 丁酉卜旅貞:今夕亡禍.在十月
Crack-making on dingyou (day 34), Lü divined: "This night
there will be nothing ominous." In the tenth moon. (II.
Chu) (Heji 26308)
Lü appears to have proposed this charge on a series of scapulas from bing-
shen (day 33) in the tenth moon to wuyin (day 15) in the twelfth moon.25
Since we do not have the charges for every day in this period, it is entirely
possible that Lü enjoyed some periods of repose; nevertheless, it is cer-
tain that he divined over at least eight consecutive days.26 Whether Lü also
engaged in other, non-divinatory activities, or presided over other divina-
tions about other topics, over this time period, cannot be determined. It
seems likely, however, that he was one of the diviners in charge of the apo-
tropaic night charges for some considerable period of time, and that this
would have been among his primary ritual responsibilities. But he certainly
had other responsibilities, involving divinations about rain, the day and
the night, the ten-day interval, travels, hunting, and above all sacrifices.27
Discussion
The increasing preference for even gan temple names (modern miaohao
E)-like yi, ding, and xin-shown in the rituals of the kings and queens
25. Dong Zuobin (1945:II:6:5b-8b) proposed the original calendrical reconstruction of
these divinations, based on Wenlu 42 (= [105AB]), 44 (not in Heji?), 50 (= Heji 26344), and
55 (= Heji 26293). I find his reconstruction of the missing scapulas and scapula fragments
plausible.
26. The charges on Heji 26308, represented in part by [105AB], run continuously from
bingshen (day 33) of the tenth moon to guimao (day 40) of the eleventh moon. We also have
Lü's identical charges for days 3, 4 (Heji 26286), 5, 6 (Heji 26289), and 7 (Heji 26293) of the
eleventh moon, and days 8, 10, 13, and 15 (Heji 26293) of the twelfth moon. The lack of any
"night" charges divined by Lü for day 41 to day 2 of the eleventh moon raises the possibil-
ity that he went off duty during that period; it is equally likely-if only some 4 to 14 percent
(later revised to 20 percent) of the inscriptions recorded have been preserved (for this crude
estimate, see Keightley 1978:165-69; 1990:40, 47, n. 46, 49)-that these putative inscriptions
were on scapula fragments now lost; we certainly have other Lü "night" charges for some of
those dates (see, e.g., Heji 26285 [day 2], 26323 and 26324 [day 43], 26325 [day 44], 26327 [day
45], and 26329 [day 57]), but without any record of the moon numbers; since the majority of
these (Heji 26285, 26323, 26325, and 26327) were divined on shell rather than bone, it is not
certain that they were divined at the same time as the scapulas of the [105AB] series. All the
charges are dated to Period II, Chu diviner group.
27. Rao Zongyi 1959:923-60; Y1497-98.
10: Work Schedule of the Diviners
117
of Shang, as well as the preference for even names in the corpus of bronze
inscriptions, 28 may be explained, in fact, in terms of "an even day for cul-
tic work, an odd day for rest" approach to ritual activity. I would suggest
that one reason the Shang preferred to leave intervals between cult days
was that the offering of cult itself required preparation and planning. This
may have been one reason why a large number of rituals to the ancestors
were divined about prior to the day on which the actual offering was to
be made (see p. 112).
It is true that there is little indication of a work schedule involving
alternating days of activity and rest in early historical texts. Han officials
apparently received one day off in every five as a day for resting and
washing the hair; in the Liang, and in the Tang through Yuan,
officials were given every tenth day off (Lien-sheng Yang 1961:19). One
day off in every two would seem an excessively liberal policy for the Late
Shang and generally at variance with the rather demanding work ethic
of the Zhou, and presumably Shang, elites. It is necessary, however, to
distinguish between work schedules and cult schedules. I am not propos-
ing that the Shang ritualists were off duty every other day. I am merely
proposing that they divined about offering cult every other day; they also
may have been performing other tasks.
I would note, furthermore, that the existence of the tradition of "hard"
and "soft" alternate days in late Zhou and Han does at least accord with
the willingness to place ancestral sacrifices on alternate days (see Table
3, p. 249). As the Liji, "Biao ji," says, "(In divining) about external
affairs they used the odd days; and for internal affairs, the even (: 4
). They did not go against the (intimations of the)
tortoise-shell and stalks."29 Such a tradition provides one more reason for
thinking that the "nei shi " (internal affairs) of Late Shang may have
consciously been structured in similar ways. That a concern with lucky
days still dominated the minds of the Qin bureaucracy is well revealed
by the importance of the rishu almanac texts that have recently been
excavated in significant number. 30 Presumably certain days continued to
be good for divination and offerings, other days did not.
All these pieces of Shang evidence, taken from the divinatory record,
indicate the degree to which the Shang ritualists worked within a highly
28. Keightley 1987; Ji Dewei 1989; Inoue 1990:257; Wen Shufa 2000:36.
29. Legge, tr., 1866:II:350; Couvreur, tr., 1899:II:511.
30. For example, Kalinowski 1986; Loewe 1994:214-35; Mu-chou Poo 1998:69-101; Li Ling
1999; 2000:325-26, 416-32. "Most of the 'chapters"" in the rishu "deal with daily life in a gen-
eral way by indicating the auspiciousness of certain days for various activities such as mar-
riage, building, farming, travelling, hunting, and even drinking and eating... one deals with
dreams..., and another with thieves...," and others "contain instructions for handling
various evil ghosts, spirits, and even gods" (Mu-chou Poo 1993:230–31).
IND118
Working for His Majesty
swill #3.5k 2
ordered and regularized schedule, performing their rituals in the appro-
priate temples, timing their activities to the appropriate days, always
keenly aware of the larger religious context in which they were perform-
ing. 31 Such work was not undertaken on a casual or ad hoc basis. To be
sure, we cannot tell how hard the diviners worked. It is possible, although
I think unlikely, that they spent most of their time swilling down ale, only
dragging themselves to the temple when it was "time to crack another
bone." Certainly, the engravers worked hard. I suspect rather, given their
responsibilities of communicating pyromantically with the Powers that
dominated the Shang world, that the diviners of Wu Ding would have
taken their duties with considerable seriousness. Later diviners may have
presented a more perfunctory record (see pp. xix-xxi).
To work as a Shang diviner was to work as part of the Shang sym-
bolic system and to draw strength, meaning, and discipline from it. To a
Shang ritualist, preparation had most likely to be made in advance. The
zhong and the ren may have been mobilized according to a predetermined
schedule where the royal hunts were concerned (see p. 113 above), but in
other areas such as warfare and agriculture, one suspects that they were
usually mobilized as the need arose. The ancestors, the king, the diviners,
the officers, and the zhong would presumably have shared many of the
same assumptions about the ways in which the world worked, the ways
in which the world should be put to work.
31. "The primary function or metafunction of liturgical performances is not to control
behavior directly, but rather to establish conventional understandings, rules and norms in
accordance with which everyday behaviour is supposed to proceed" (Rappaport 1999:123).
ELEVEN
Leadership
The king was the prime source of authority in leading the zhong and ren
(see Table 1 on p. 246ff.), at least in the oracle-bone inscriptions. Divina-
tions about performance of "His Majesty's affairs" (see the Glossary for
gu) were reserved for the royal retainers; the term was not usually
used to dignify the activities of the labor force itself." A study of the offi-
cers who were associated with the labor force shows they were frequently
led by a named retainer or ally of the king. They might also be associated
with some of the king's official retinue, like the "Many Horse(-chariot
officers)":
[106] 多馬☑令 眾☑

order... the zhong...." (I) (*Tunnan 4029)

(hu) to take (qu) (the zhong?); His Majesty will have no regrets."

"If (the Horse[-chariot officers]?) take the zhong, His Majesty
will have no regrets." (III. He) (Heji 26901)

An officer or ally of the Shang by the name of Bing was also at risk

A good number reveal that command over the zhong might be further
delegated and that such delegation was a fitting subject for divinatory
legitimation (see Table 1.2). The king could also specify individual lead-
ers for the ren (Table 1.3) so that once again the distinction between the
zhong and ren was not clear-cut. In all cases, I assume that the king was the
ultimate leader and that some administrative structure, which was not in
need of divination, already existed to organize and direct the labor force,
either zhong or ren, once they had been mobilized. The "Many Archers" of
[89] and [109], for instance, presumably formed part of the army's com-
mand structure into which the zhong or ren were temporarily incorpo-
rated. Indeed, one inscription indicates that these officers were capable of
raising the ren locally:
[109]
多射科人于皿
"... the Many Archers will raise (gong) men at Min." (I.
Bin) (Heji 5742)
The evidence presented in Table 1, in fact, again suggests that the zhong
were a more integral part of the Shang administration than the ren. Shang
divinations about officers and units leading the zhong (58 headings) or
zhongren (some 13 headings) were more numerous than those about their
leading the ren (16 headings). But at the same time there was an overlap
in the leadership; divinations show that the Duo She (Many Archers), the
Shu (Guards), the Ya Qin, and the king himself might lead both the zhong
(or zhongren) and the ren. And the question of who should lead the Shang
forces was a matter of considerable moment:

Such evidence again suggests that the zhong were more closely incorpo-
rated into the dynastic command structure; the ren-the men of Ge-were
more likely to be led by local leaders.
The king also linked his choice of a leader to the success of the royal
campaigns and to divine approval:
[119] 己卯貞: 令插以眾伐龍
On yimao (day 52) divined: "If (we) order Cha (cha; the
Penetrator?) to take the zhong and attack (fa) Long, he will
strike (bo) (successfully)." (I-II. Li) (Heji 31972)
On wuxu (day 35) divined: "If (we) order the zhong to cross
the river to the north of Long X, there will be no disasters."
(I-II. Li) (White 1654)
123
It is likely that, after the king had divined the choice of a leader for the
zhong in [120], he had continued, in [121], to be concerned with the tactical
details of their campaign.
In summary, the zhong and the ren were led by the king, or by officers
of the dynasty, either named or referred to by their titles. The zhong were
apparently closer to the king; the ren were more distant. There was evi-
dently a command struture, but the king selected his officers at need on
the basis of both administrative and personal qualities.

or close to the Late Shang center at Xiaotun. See Zhu Fenghan 1990a:94-104; Cao Dingyun
1995:48-49; Yan Zhibin 2002.

Heji 31996f.12: The Work: Agriculture

TWELVE
The Work: Agriculture
I turn now to a series of four chapters that describe the activities of the
zhong, the ren, and the officers who led them in the strategic areas of agri-
culture, warfare, the hunt, and construction.
Many inhabitants of Shang China would have had little notion
of the land that lay beyond their daily horizon. Travelling no fur-
ther than their local fields and woods, many peasants would have felt
themselves at the center of a small, familiar world that was intermit-
tently and unpredictably invaded by external forces-like the king on
hunt or campaign, marauding beasts, enemy raiders, voracious birds
and insects, and, above all, the onslaughts of wind, rain, drought, and
flood-that entered, often abruptly and unpredictably, from one hori-
zon, left their mark on a settlement, and then passed out of its ken.
The frequent peregrinations of the king and his entourage, by contrast,
combined with the reports, tribute payments, court visits, marriage alli-
ances, and so on made by his dependents, officers, and allies, indicate
that the Shang court's knowledge of a wider geography must have been
extensive. The well over five hundred place-names that appear in the
inscriptions (Song Zhenhao 1991:101) reveal Shang knowledge of a far-
flung series of settlements and their human and spiritual inhabitants. But
the basic peasant perception, that of the parochial inhabitant anxiously
scrutinizing the surrounding borders, attempting to understand and con-
trol the irruptions of benevolent or hostile Powers that lurked beyond,
may still be discerned in the diviner's cosmological conceptions. (Keight-
ley 2000:55)
The intention is not to provide a comprehensive account of these topics. I
am concerned, rather, to demonstrate the ways in which the Shang elites
mobilized and organized their main labor force in four important areas of
dynastic activity, the ways in which these activities were endowed with
their own ritual significance, and, finally, the ways in which the various
activities involved were inter-related. It is significant that most of the labor
force is stipulated for military purposes (Table 1). Presumably the peasant
was expected to work the land, under the direction of the leaders, both in
warfare and in peace-time pursuits.
The Agricultural Context
The Neolithic and Bronze Age inhabitants of the North China Plain prob-
ably worked harder, and were less handsomely rewarded, than their
hunter-gatherer forebears. Early agriculture is never as reliable as hunting
and gathering, it requires more work and more discipline, and it does not
provide a well-balanced diet. The negative impact of the so-called Neo-
lithic Revolution on the human condition is well demonstrated by the
skeletal evidence, which, with the advent of agriculture, reveals reduc-
tions in skeletal size and life expectancy.' Elvin (1993:13-14) notes that, in
China:
It also seems probable that from a medical point of view the agricultural
revolution was in some respects a disaster. Not only did it reduce the
variety of the human diet... but the denser populations that could now
be supported, and their sedentarization, made it possible for 'crowd dis-
eases' to flourish.²
According to Riley,
in societies where mortality rates are high, the threat posed by death to
the social system is great, a threat reflected both in ritualistic mourning
practices and in the social prescriptions activated when death occurs. By
contrast, in societies where the mortality rates are low and most indi-
viduals live out their full life course, the deaths of particular leaders or
particular family members are more predictable, hence less socially dev-
astating. (Riley 1983:211-12)
The death rate in the Neolithic was probably not known in the Shang, but
that made little difference. The Neolithic too had its own share of ances-
tor worship.³
Agriculture increases the people-carrying capacity of the land and
makes urban settlements possible. The larger populations also confer

Working for His Majesty
political power, because "a hundred malnourished farmers can still out-
fight one healthy hunter" (Diamond 1987:66). Agriculture's great cost is
that it then exposes the increased population to the dangers of famine.
This, in turn, requires the build-up of food reserves.5 And differential
access to those reserves plays a role in creating and maintaining social
stratification and high civilization.
Amano suggests that during the Yin Dynasty there were two kinds of
fields: the royal fields, cultivated by slaves under a centralized manage-
ment; and the clan fields, cultivated by the lower classes of the clan mem-
bers. This theory may have some truth in it, but no thorough general con-
clusion is yet in sight.6
Late Shang Agriculture
Late Shang Xiaotun lay on the western edge of the North China Plain
that with a rainfall somewhat more generous than now, and with its
flat, fertile loess soils-was well suited to be the agricultural, and thus the
political, heartland of the emerging state. The plains to the east and south
of Anyang would probably have received some 40 percent more rainfall
than the modern Anyang region itself.
