12: The Work: Agriculture
127
According to pollen analysis, the postglacial climatic optimum occurred
circa 5000-1000 BC in China when it was warmer and moister than the
present.... It is evident that the Longshan culture in northern Henan
witnessed a rapid population growth. Only about 62 Yangshao sites,
ranging from 15 to <1 ha, have been found here (Fig. 26)..., compared
to 201 Longshan sites (48-<1 ha) identified in the same area.
The rain in Anyang tends to fall in intense bursts. In summer, the clash
between warm monsoon air from the southeast-forced to rise violently
as it encounters, along the eastern edge of the Taihang mountains, the
cold air descending from the Shanxi plateau-leads to torrential, flood-
producing rains so that the average intensity of rainfall in July, in the
vicinity of the eastern slopes, is one of the highest in North China. 10 It
has been estimated that 30 to 50 percent of the annual rainfall in the area
may come in such heavy bursts." Anyang is not in the foothills to the
between 1951 and 1980, Anyang received an average rainfall of 618 mm; I base this figure
upon records that I consulted in the Anyang diqu qixiangju qixiangtai
(Observatory of the Anyang Region Meteorological Office) on 22 April 1981 (Keightley
2000:2, n. 3). Hu Huanyong (1935:85, 136, 155) gives rather different figures: on the basis of
statistics for 1920-26, 1932, and 1934, total yearly rainfall was 552.4 mm (vs. 618 mm from
my Meteorological Office figures). For the entire year, there were 33.5 days of rainfall (vs.
76 days from my Meteorological Office figures). (Wittfogel [1940:121] gives a rainfall total of
501.5 mm over 33.7 days; Chu Ping-hai [1967:360] gives a total of 527 mm). Whichever mod-
ern figures one accepts, the Shang weather was wetter (e.g., Chen Mengjia 1956:524; Li Chi
1977:194-99; Suetsugu 1991:189; Zhu Yanmin 2007a; cf. Shima 1958:513-14; 1966:6, 21-22).
Some oracle-bone inscriptions express the wish that it not rain (see, e.g., [123AB], [252],
and Heji 12324f); other inscriptions express the wish that it rain, even in "the first moon,"
with four separate occasions of rainfall for days 39, 41, 46, and 48 (Keightley 1999d:219-20
for Heji 12487); other inscriptions express the wish that it may rain or not rain with equal
force ([313AB]), in "the seventh moon." Local conditions probably played a role. Wei Jiyin
(2007:47-49) and Wang Xingguang (2008:60-61) see a period of lessening rainfall in the later
stages of the dynasty. But conceivably rainfall was divined less often (see the comments on
"diachronic analysis" in the "Note about the Sources"). And the frequency of the rainfall div-
inations may be liable to other interpretations; the increased frequency of rainfall may have
led to a decrease in divinations about the fall of rain.

Yu?; see Lewis 2006a:29). This is reflected in the pollen record starting 3000 B.C. The denser
distribution of Shang- and Zhou-period sites in the area from the Wei He north to the
foot of the Taihang X mountains may reflect the accelerated drying up of the lakes.

River
Taihang, lying as it does some 30-40 km to their east, but it is well situ-
ated to receive some of the heaviest rainfall in the area. According to
the staff of the Meteorological Office (see n. 8), rain was heavier between
Anyang and the mountains than at Anyang itself; the heaviest rain fell
at Lingqi on the upper reaches of the Qi River, ca. 55 km south-
west of Anyang, thus making the Qi liable to flooding. The Huan
was presumably equally susceptible to flood in Shang times; for evidence
that it did flood, see [207AB]. The rain, when it falls, tends to do so most
heavily in the afternoon, from about 2 to 7 P.M.12 This means that, in the
modern period, the heavy bursts could bring the farmer's daily work to a
stop (cf. Chu Ping-hai 1967:372). The Shang farmers would have suffered.
The Shang king, in his hunts and travels, and in performing his open-air
rituals, would have been similarly inconvenienced (see [335A] and Heji
27000,32327).
Hunting,13 fishing, 14 and herding were to provide valuable protein,
especially for the royal entourage, 15 who shared the ritual offerings of
cows and sheep with their ancestors, 16 but the economic foundation of the
dynasty was predominantly agricultural. This is indicated by the agrar-
ian economy of the Neolithic cultures from which the Shang developed,
by the divinations about the preparation of arable land and about the use
of royal labor gangs in agriculture, by the large numbers of stone harvest-
ing knives found through the area of Shang culture, and by the diviners'
concern with seasonable rains and harvest-prayer rituals. The oracle-bone
inscriptions show great concern about agriculture: prayers and sacrifices
for fruitful harvest to Di, Mountain and River Powers, and the ances-
tors (see, e.g., [122AB]-[124AB], [153AB]-[210]); rain-seeking divinations
occur; and divinations about the harvest are numerous (Y45.1-55.2). The
Shang king asked about issuing instructions to the zhong, wanting them to
plant millet, and also indicating the place of the planting; he also wanted

agricultural base.
The Powers and the Weather
The Shang diviners lived in a world in which the workings of Di, of the
Nature Powers-such as He (the River Power), Yang (the Mountain
Power), and the Fang (the Powers of the Regions)-of the High Powers
(such as Shang Jia ), and of the ancestors could all be manifested in
the wind, the rain, and the clouds.19
[122] 戊子卜貞:帝及四月雨
Making cracks on wuzi (day 25), Que divined: "Di (Di),
when it comes to the fourth moon, will order rain."
[122B] 貞:帝弗其及四月雨.王固曰:丁雨. 不由辛. 旬丁酉允雨

order rain." (Prognostication:) His Majesty, reading the
cracks, said: "On a ding day it will (hui) rain. If not, it will
be on a xin day." (Verification:) In the next ten days, on
dingyou (day 34), it did indeed rain. (I. Bin) (Heji 14138)
[123] 真:翌甲戌河其令[雨]

min 1998:57-73; Cohen 1999; Keightley 1999b:277-78; 2000:9-16; Meng Shikai 2007:167-78.
"Some millet, but not much, is grown in the Anyang area today; it is planted after the wheat
harvest in June and is harvested in October" (Yang Xizhang, personal observation, 20 April
1981).

[123B] 真: 翌 河不 令雨
Working for His Majesty
Divined: "On the next [jiaxu], He (the River Power) will
[not] order rain." (I. Bin) (Heji 14638f)
Apart from this single exception of [123AB] involving He, Di was the only
Power thought capable of issuing orders to other Powers or natural phe-
nomena. The theology of the diviners' negotiations with the Nature Pow-
ers, as it is documented in the divinatory record, was in fact relatively
straightforward. They expected the Powers to confer benefits in response
to the cult that the Shang ritualists offered, but they did not treat the
Nature Powers as systematically as they treated their own ancestors.
Just as Di's influence virtually disappeared from the oracle-bone record
after Period 1,20 the influence of the Nature Powers, in matters of rainfall
and harvest, also waned in the post-Wu Ding reigns. Later divinations
about their influence on rainfall and harvest were rare.
[124A] 甲午貞:今歲受
Crack-making on jiawu (day 31), divined: "In the present
season (sui) (we) will receive crops."
[124B] 丁酉貞:禾于盖燎五牢卯五牛
Crack-making on dingyou (day 34), divined: "In performing
a crop-seeking ritual (dao) to Yang (the Mountain Power)
(Yang), (we) offer in holocaust five penned animals (lao)
(and) split open (mao) five cows." (I-II. Li) (Tunnan 750)
The ancestors were rarely appealed to directly.21
The disappearance of the Nature Powers from the Period V divinatory
record provides a further indication of the way in which the kings had
reduced the scope of their divinatory activity by the end of the dynasty.
The religious beliefs that we can deduce from the divination inscriptions
of Wu Ding, which give us some sense of the active role of the Nature
Powers, presumably remained influential in later periods, even though
they were no longer explicitly divined in the oracle-bone inscriptions. As
with many of the topics divined under Wu Ding, one has the sense that
they represented the more primitive, "superstitious" beliefs of the middle
Bronze Age. In the later periods, they appear to have been secularized,
rationalized, humanized, rendered less potent, and thus less in need of
divinatory testing.
20. Keightley 1988:381; Y418.2-425.1.
21. Da Jia (K3) and Da Yi (K1) in [131AB]; Da Jia (K3), Zu Yi (K12), and Shang Jia (P1) in
[178AB]; Shang Jia (P1) in [192DE*]; Shi Ren (P5) in Heji 28271.
12: The Work: Agriculture
Agricultural Tools
lei
131
131
Archaeologically, the most common Shang production tools were wood
and stone. Tools for turning over and loosening the soil were wooden
ploughs, and stone, shell, and bone shovels (si ). The wooden lei
was a double-toothed, pronged tool. The oracle-bone form for ji ("cul-
tivate"; see Glossary) depicted a man using a lei, using his foot to turn the
earth. Earthen traces have been found in ash pits and the walls of graves.
Commonly seen harvest tools were clam-mussel sickles and stone sickles,
with the mussel sickles equipped with teeth. Recent articles have also spo-
ken of a small number of bronze agricultural tools-spades and picks and
pickaxes; these have been found in remains and tombs at Xiaotun. The
bronze picks were rather more numerous than the spades. 22 The whole
array of tools, from various sources and workshops, was a crucial supple-
ment to the labor of those who worked for His Majesty.
Although hunting and herding played an important economic role in
the life of the elite, affecting its diet, daily activities, and self-perceptions,
the Shang as a whole must be regarded as a cereal-based civilization. It is
generally agreed that the main cereal was millet.23 The only grain that was

jiong 1997:528-39; Lu Zhongfa 1999; Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan, eds., 1999:526-28. See
too Te-tzu Chang 1983; 1989; An Zhimin 1989; Fiskesjö 2001:154, n. 491; Kwang-chih Chang
2005:128-29. Wang Yongbo (2002) believes that the "spade-shape object" of jade associated
with the Shang ruler derived from the lei and si of Neolithic times.

collected at Taosi : "foxtail millet (Setaria italica), broomcorn millet (Panicum miliaceum)
and rice (Oryza sativa), accounting for about 75% of the total plant seeds unearthed, with the
foxtail millet grains forming an overwhelming majority" (Zhao Zhijun and He Nu 2006:77).

The population of the various communities within the Shang sphere of
influence-or, more precisely, within the sphere of Shang divinatory
concern-lived in a series of larger and smaller settlements known as yi

And the Shang cult center itself, probably the one at Xiaotun, was referred
to as zi yi, "this settlement" (Y116.2-17.2), as Da yi E, "the Great
Settlement" (Y118.2-19.1), and Da yi Shang, "the Great Settlement
Shang" (Y119.1).32 Other Shang residential units were known as Dan,
which appear in both the oracle-bone and bronze inscriptions of Late
Shang. They may also have been residential units involved in or devoted
to farming; some modern scholars have referred to them as "communes" Bejeg
(dan單).33
Since not all these settlements would have been walled-the site at
Xiaotun itself was not-it may be supposed that the square enclosure
(over a kneeling, sedentary figure) that formed part of the oracle-bone
graph for yiindicated that the inhabitants had demarcated their com-
munity, at least symbolically, thus separating it from the wilder landscape
that lay beyond. 34
These settlements were surrounded by cultivated fields (tian )
(Keightley 1999b:277-78). The king controlled a significant number of
fields, cleared and worked by royal labor gangs, and under the control
of various officers, like the Xiao Chen E, "Junior Servitor(s)" ([142]).
These fields were sufficiently fruitful and well organized to support the

have in134
Working for His Majesty
royal house, a privileged ruling elite, a series of labor-intensive craft
industries-impressive for their specialization, technical competence,
and sheer volume of production-and a considerable army. Numerous
charges demonstrate the king's pyromantic attention to rainfall, the open-
ing up of new fields, inspections, planting, harvest, and the agricultural
activities of the royal labor gangs. Many of these agricultural charges
involved the king's personal leadership, ritual presence, or administrative
decisions: [181] "His Majesty should not go to inspect the millet plant-
ing"; [192B] "If His Majesty orders the Duo Yin (officers) to open up fields
in the west, (we) will receive crops"; [192E] "(We) will pray for grain (to
the ancestors) from Shang Jia (on down)."35 The king was also concerned
about the agriculture of particular officers and locations, as in charges like
([165C]): "We will receive the harvest that Fu cultivates at Zi ." The
discovery, in the Xiaotun area, of large numbers of stone knives (see p. 13),
some still in the process of manufacture, is further evidence of labor orga-
nization that was devoted to bringing in the harvest.
There are virtually no oracle-bone records about the practice of local,
non-royal agriculture-farms worked by individual families, if such
existed-presumably embraced in the general blessings about receiving
harvest for which the king divined (as in [132A-C]). The divinations deal
only with the farming directed by the king and his officers.36
The importance of agriculture to the dynasty may be seen from the
many divinations that the king performed about rainfall and the year's
harvest. 37 Di, the High God, was probably a Power responsible for agricul-
tural fruitfulness, and the numerous prayers ending with "We (or some
other entity) will receive harvest" 38 may well have referred to receiving
harvest from him.39 The pre-eminent religious role played by the king
in matters of farming is further demonstrated by the fact that his divi-
nations about the harvest were not limited to enquiries about his own
crops associated with wang E, "His Majesty," 40 or with Zhong Shang
,"Central Shang," or with the Da yi E, "The Great Settlement

"42
135
(Shang)," or by wo, "Our" 43-but extended to other places within the
si tu, "the four lands," north, south, east, and west of the cult cen-
ter, that comprised the Shang domains (Y462.1). The area covered by the
king's harvest divinations was likely coterminous with the area of Shang
dominion. Such divinations were presumably one of the ways in which
the king demonstrated that dominion and rewarded his subordinates and
allies.
The Peasantry and the Population
inly.
I refer to the bulk of the population in Shang times, the ren, as "peasants,"
that is to say, as settled farmers bound by custom, political reality, and
economic necessity to the life-giving labor of agriculture, participating for
the most part only in local economic exchanges, and existing at or near
the bottom of a traditional social hierarchy. Their labor resources were
presumably organized around the household. They would have engaged
primarily in subsistence production with any surplus being extracted to
support the social strata above them, which included the kings, lords, and
diviners recorded in the oracle-bone inscriptions.45 We have little contem-
porary information about the ties that connected the peasant to the land
he farmed and the lord for whom he presumably farmed it, but it may be
assumed that arable land was developed and cultivated by the inhabitants
of the various local settlements that composed the Shang dynastic state
and that the ability to participate in or control any communal labor force
was more important to the elites than conceptions of land ownership (see
below). Freedom to leave the land would hardly have been an issue when
considerations of both survival and identity bound men and women to
42. On the significance and location of this site, which I believe was at Xiaotun, see Keight-
ley 2000:58-59. See Heji 32176, 33240, 33241, 33242.
43. E.g., [132AB], [153AB], [189C], Heji 902, 9731, 9732, 9929, 24436. A number of the wo
nian, "Our harvest," charges are fragmentary and it is possible that, in some of these
cases, wo was the name of a place, lineage, or person (Akatsuka 1977:234; Keightley 1978:167,
n. 14; Soran, no. 1526) rather than the adjective meaning "Our." In a number of cases, how-
ever, it seems clear that "Our harvest" rather than "Wo's harvest" was the object of concern,
as in [176A]:

the si tu, a term that rarely appears in the bone inscriptions, although the concept of "four
lands," named for the cardinal directions, certainly seems to have been present, see Keightley
2000:61-65; Aihe Wang 2000:23-63. See Sagart 1988 and 2004 for the etymological roots for
the four directions.
45. I take many of these criteria from the discussion at Gallant 1991:4. See too Kearney
2001; Myrdal 2001.136
Working for His Majesty
their traditional groups, cultures, and tutelary Powers. The wealth of the
Shang elite indicates that, as with most historical societies, the Shang peas-
antry did not consume all that they produced, but participated in an econ-
omy in which part of their labor and its products was appropriated by
local leaders, some of whom may have been representing the king. It is
significant that the ren (see Table 1.3) are seldom involved in agricultural
pursuits. Presumably, one issued orders to the officers or zhong who car-
ried them out at the local level. The peasants-as opposed to the soldiers,
or those who served as soldiers-did not emerge in the divinatory record.
There was much concern with the harvest, 47 much of it involved the king
himself, 48 but not generally for the peasants who carried it out.
Dynastic Agriculture
We have more information about the agriculture that was under the con-
trol of the king and his officers. Agriculture at the service of the dynasty
implies the existence of dynastic lands; a further word should be said
about elite conceptions of land ownership, and thus of the diviners' image
of the human landscape. The earliest forms of ownership were probably
associated with the group and based only upon customary occupation
and use 49-a situation, it may be remarked, that would have created and
reinforced the religious ties between a group, its leaders, and its lands.
Since disciplined, subservient labor was scarcer than land in the twelfth
century B.C., the customary ability to employ labor gangs on particular
lands for farming and hunting would probably have been a more impor-
tant consideration than conceptions of outright ownership.50 Concep-
tions of ownership, or proprietary interest, would have been associated
with usable land-fields where crops could be grown, and also, perhaps,
hunting areas where the Shang could "take to the field" (see the Glossary
for tian). Royal agriculture and royal hunts would both have required
the use of the dynasty's labor resources; possession would have meant
little without those resources. "Shang territory would have been partly
46. According to the criteria proposed by Thorner (1968:504, 507-08)-involving the pre-
dominance of agricultural production, a state of minimum size, at least 5 percent of the popula-
tion residing in towns, and family households as the most representative units of production-
the Shang state would qualify as a society "predominantly peasant in nature."
47. E.g., [16AB], [127AB]-[133AB], [144], [147AB], etc.
48. E.g., [134], [137], [139], [143].
49. E.g., Wu Ch'i-chang 1956:7; cf. Clawson 1968:552; Zhao Lin 1982:131. There is no evi-
dence to indicate that land-labor relations in Late Shang were conceived in terms of any for-
mal or legal definition such as rental or usufruct.
50. The situation may be likened to that in Yamato Japan where "the earliest evidence
relating to the uji [] shows that the great families were concerned not so much with land as
with manpower in the form of be []" (Hall 1966:40).
12: The Work: Agriculture
137
conceived in terms of areas where the king could hunt, partly in terms
of land that Shang laborers had rendered arable" (Keightley 1999b:280).
Shang labor also raised livestock, as witnessed by the frequent offerings to
the ancestors and other Powers of the cows and sheep.51
These activities on the land would have been assisted or hindered
by the Powers and tutelary spirits, but the activities proposed were ini-
tially the result of human planning and organization. Ownership implies
power-and power in Shang China was not primarily a matter of dispos-
ing of land but of disposing of labor resources. The relations between man
and land, therefore, would have been conceived in terms of its workabil-
ity, of the productive forces, both human and religious, that elites could
bring to bear on it. Disinterested appreciation of the land and the joys of
ownership are not, of course, likely to have been recorded in the inscrip-
tions. But it does seem likely that even the beauty of the landscape would
have been conceived primarily in pragmatic and religious terms. A good
landscape would have been, above all, an auspicious and a fruitful one. 52
By Late Shang, certain areas of arable land were regarded as special.
Terms like "Our fields" (wo tian, ) (as in [130] and [131B]), "the center
fields,"53 "the southern fields" ([129]),54 and "the northern fields" ([127AB];
see too n. 56) indicate, first of all, that arable land, like space itself, tended
to be conceived in cardinal points.55
The charges in which such terms appear further indicate that such
fields were under Shang management, as in

A "first moon" date on one plastron fragment, [134], suggests that the
king was making his agricultural plans for the zhong at the start of the
Shang calendar year. Similarly,
[142] 貞:車小臣令眾黍.一月

was also divined at the start of the year.61 Inscription [142] is of interest for
it suggests, once again, that the zhong were part of the royal administra-
tive hierarchy, being led, in this case, by one or more Junior Servitors, just
as they had been led by Cha, the Many Archers, the Five Warbands, the Ya
Qin, and others listed in Table 1.

Working for His Majesty
I would suppose that the "great order," divined in the eleventh moon,
was to ready the zhong for their agricultural labors at the start of the year

It appears that, on occasion, the zhong's role in agriculture involved the
danger of casualties:
[144]
卜貞: 眾作耤不喪
Making cracks on . . ., divined: "If the zhong engage in culti-
vation (ji), (we) will not lose [zhong?]...." (I. Bin) (Heji 8)
This was perhaps due to the risk of encountering enemy forces as the
Shang attempted to open up new lands (Akatsuka 1977:546). Such a con-
clusion is suggested by
[145A]癸已卜賓貞:令眾人入羗方愛田
Making cracks on guisi (day 30), Bin divined: "(We) should
order the zhongren to enter (the territory of) the Qiang-
fang to open up fields (ku tian)."
[145B] 貞:勿令眾人.六月
Divined: "(We) should not order the zhongren (to do this)."
Sixth moon.64 (I. Bin) (*Heji 6)
The quasi-military nature of some of the expeditions to open up new land
is suggested by the leadership of officers who, in other contexts, were
employed on military expeditions, by the use of the zhongren, "men
of the zhong," who also served as soldiers in war, and by the occasional
reference to expeditions in enemy territories (as in [145AB]). "Such royal
agriculture was part of a functionally diffuse system of dependent labor
in which both officers and conscripts might serve in warfare, hunting, con-
struction, or agriculture."
1165
Fruitful agriculture depended not only upon the cooperation of local
populations, but also upon the goodwill of the Powers, both Nature
([146] [148]), pre-Ancestral ([149]), and Ancestral ([150A-D]), to whom
the Shang king offered his harvest prayers:66

moon (Heji 9608) and in the ninth moon ([150A-D]); presumably they were not involved in
the start of the agricultural year.

(III-IV. Nameless-Diviner) (Heji 28216)
According to one estimate, the Shang kingdom's agricultural landscapes
were mainly distributed within the following provinces: Henan (46
names), Shanxi (20), Shandong (17), Shaanxi and Hebei (5 each), and 16
others which are hard to locate (Peng Bangjiong 1997:572-648).
The degree to which the Shang kings, particularly Wu Ding, con-
cerned themselves with agriculture and its rituals, performed either by
themselves or their subordinates, is impressive. The dao he, shou he
受禾 (Y523.1-26.1), dao nian 禱年, and shou nian 受年(Y530.1-35.1)-all
"receive harvest" inscriptions (see Glossary for dao, shou, and shou you
nian)-numbers about 700. Together with sacrifices it was one of the great
affairs of the dynasty. 85 One can see this in the royal concern about "We"
receiving harvests ([132AB], [154AB]),86 about the harvests of Shang ([157],
[158AC], [159]), and about, for example, the harvests of Zi, a major
farming region (see n. 82 above):
[165A] [丁][卜]賓貞:始受年
[Making cracks on ding]you (day 34), Bin divined: "Zi will
receive (shou) harvest."
[163]
貞:羊受年
Divined: "Yang will receive harvest." (III-IV. Nameless-
Diviner) (Heji 28215)
modern Yancheng, some 450 k south of Anyang. If so, it is of interest that Wu Ding was
divining about harvests at such a distance from his cult center.

(Heji 9572)
Given the frequent identity of the names of Shang persons and Shang
places, it is not always easy to tell if the king's concern was with the har-
vest produced by a certain place or with the harvest being produced for,
and under the leadership of, a retainer who was the lord of that place. In
some instances, the charge might be quite specific about who had been
doing the cultivating (as in [133B], [165CD]). In other cases, such as [160C],
"Fu will receive harvest," or [160E] and [165A], "Zi will receive harvest,"
it is not easy to determine if Fu or Zi was the name of the place or the
name of its lord.88 Since the Shang elites presumably conceived of most
places in terms of their social and political identities, one may assume
that such charges would have embraced both meanings. Shang was cer-
tainly a place-name, but it was also the name of a social and political
group. Charges about "Shang receiving harvest" ([157], [158A], [159A]),
accordingly, were not simply about the place but about the people whose
identity was linked to the place. Similarly, charges about "Zi receiving
87. For the Dan residential unit, see p. 133.

Wu Ding had performed the guan-overseeing ritual ([180A], [183AB]).
The difficulty of making such judgments is suggested, however, by one
further inscription, involving Fu Jing's agricultural role: [151] "If Fu Jing
calls out (hu), 'Plant millet (in) Qiu Shang,' (we) will receive [harvest]." Her
"calling out" presumably had a ritual element. It would seem that in this
case she was presiding over Shang dynastic planting, not over her own.
The Ritual Dimension
Such divinations, which appear to have involved the offering and approval
of acceptable prayers, were not the only expression of the king's agricul-
tural concerns. The king, his consorts, and his officers engaged in a series
of other ritual agricultural acts, some of which involved "tilling" (tian )

Working for His Majesty
It is hard to determine if the Shang kings regarded Di as their distant
ancestor. At least two considerations, based on the inscriptions, suggest
that Di's nature was non-ancestral or, at least, un-ancestral. The first rea-
son involves the lack of cult addressed to Di. If Di were an ancestor, at
least in the oracle-bone inscriptions, he was treated with uncharacteristic
parsimony. The living kings divined about Di not infrequently-mainly
for the harvest-but they offered him little or no sacrificial wealth. The
contrast with the generous way in which the kings treated their ancestors
is so notable that it implies a difference in kind.
The second reason is that the Shang diviners made little or no attempt
to influence Di's decisions, to "bargain" offerings with him, in the way that
the Shang kings bargained with their ancestors. The diviners, for example,
sought to determine if Di would order rain, wind, or thunder,93 as in,
[175] 辛未卜爭貞:生八月帝令多雨
Crack-making on xinwei (day 8), Zheng divined: "In the
waxing eighth moon Di will order (ling) much rain."94
[175B] 貞:生八月帝不其多雨
Divined: "In the waxing eighth moon Di (Di) may (qi) not
order much rain."
[175C] 丁酉雨至于甲寅旬又八日,九月
(Verification:) On dingyou (day 34) it rained, (continuing)
down to jiayin (day 51), for eighteen days. Ninth moon.95 (I.
Bin) (Heji 10976f)

Divined: "In performing a Di ritual to the (Power of the)
Western region, (which we) call Yi, (and to its) Wind
Power, (which we) call Wei (?), (we) seek harvest." (I. Bin)
(Heji 14295)
In this case, having divined about rain on day 48-and having presum-
ably (since it did rain four days later ([177A]) obtained an auspicious
response-the Shang diviner next sought to name the Wind Powers indi-
vidually and to associate them with their proper regional Powers, also
called by their names, to whom the Di rituals were individually addressed.
The purpose of the rituals, as [177C-E] reveal, was to seek harvest. That
these Region and Wind Power divinations were also evidently linked to
Di's control of the rain ([177AB]) is added reason for thinking that the Di
ritual was designed to confer Di-like capabilities. One has the sense in this
case that, having established the general proposition that Di would order
rain, the diviners then turned to the Winds and the Regions, Powers that
would be more directly responsible for the harvest, and to which, unlike
Di, they could offer cult. 100

-which combined appeals for rain or harvest with a sacrifice offer-
ing to the Power involved, were never addressed to Di. 101 Even the
basic requests for rain, for harvest, for progeny-that were frequently
addressed to the ancestors were more rarely made to Di ([175AB]). Di
was apparently not subject to human influence, or swayed by human
appetites, in the way that the ex-human ancestors were. He was never,
for example, offered a you -cutting ritual (see Glossary), one of the
most common ancestral offerings. Nor did the Shang offer human or ani-
mal sacrifices to Di. Perhaps he was not, or was no longer, sufficiently
anthropomorphized to be capable of enjoying such offerings. In any
event, these cultic distinctions set Di apart from the ancestral and Nature
Powers. 102

Shang agriculture, especially on the subject of divination, would cer-
tainly have had a strong ritual dimension, as represented in such contexts
by the guan "overseeing" ritual 105 and the ji "cultivation" ritual.106 And
105. E.g., [180A], [182]-[184], [188].
106. E.g., [165CD], [166C], [167], [188].160
Working for His Majesty
12: The Work: Agriculture
161
such ritual acts may have prepared the way for the jitian ritual of
Zhou in which the king opened the agricultural year by ploughing the
sacred field (but see too the Glossary for ji #).107 When the king himself
participated in the cultivation, as in [186]-[188], then we have an explicit
instance in which royal cultivation was associated with ritual, and it was
presumably from such a tradition that the Zhou term jitian came to obtain
its special meaning. That the one case in which the king "ji-cultivated"
and performed the guan-overseeing ritual ([188]) and for which the moon
number was recorded took place in the Shang twelfth moon, equivalent
to the Zhou first moon, further suggests a ritual link to the turn of the
year. 108 The shu millet planting took place in the thirteenth ([189]), first
([142], [154A], [155]), Heji 13 (quoted in n. 61 above), and second moons
([156]). There is good reason to suppose that the guan-overseeing and ji-
cultivation were rituals that were associated with the opening of the agri-
cultural year.
Some additional evidence for this may be found in the following
inscription whose translation, for [189A], is tentative:
[189A] 癸卯卜古貞:王于黍侯受黍年.十三月
Making cracks on guimao (day 40), Gu divined: "His Maj-
esty should go to the millet planting (?), (for if he does) the
Archer Lords (?) will receive millet harvest." Thirteenth moon.
[189B] 癸卯卜古貞:王勿于黍侯☑
Making cracks on guimao (day 40), Gu divined: "His Maj-
esty should not go to the millet planting (?), (for if he does)
the Archer Lord....
[189C] 王固曰: 吉.我受黍年.丁其雨吉.其他乙雨吉
His Majesty read the cracks and said: "Auspicious. We will
receive millet harvest. If on (qi) a ding day it rains, it will be
auspicious. If it be (wei) an yi day that it rains, it will (also)
be auspicious." (I. Bin) (*Heji 9934f)
Whether or not the Shang king was divining on behalf of his Archer Lords
is uncertain (see the Glossary for hou); indeed, the prognostication [189C]
"We will receive millet harvest" suggests that his main concern was with
his own harvests, although the "We" might have included his supporters
107. See, e.g., the "Yue ling " account in Knoblock and Riegel, trs., 2000:62.
108. A bronze inscription from the late Western Zhou, the Zai gui (?), dated to the
reign of King Xuan (Guo Moruo 1957:143b; JWGL 2:1298), indicates that the jitian
place in the Zhou first moon.
took
like the Archer Lords. The performance of this divinatory ritual-in the
thirteenth moon, the turn of the Shang year-suggests that [189A-C]
involved again an agricultural ritual in which the fall of rain was taken as
portentous.
I believe, in short, that the Shang king did engage in ritual cultivation,
as suggested by the guan, ji, and shu charges, 109 and that these
ceremonies were probably ancestral to the jitian ceremony of the Zhou.
Whatever the ritual significance of such episodes may have been, how-
ever, the shu "millet planting" was not simply a ritual act. The charge [182]
indicates that the millet planting and the guan-overseeing ritual might be
separable, at least when the king's dependents were involved. But there
would have been little if any separation between the king's ritual and eco-
nomic activity. And the planting of millet in various regions of the Shang
domains presumably ensured, by virtue of the king's charisma and his
divinatory invocation of the Powers, the success of the surrounding crops.
The bulk of the Shang dynastic agriculture that the king divined, and that
was conducted by the king himself, his officers, and the zhong-and also
by the ren ([136BD])?-was not merely ritual. It tested and demonstrated
the favor of Di-by producing fruitful harvests.110
Opening Up New Land
'to be out in the
field" sometime
There was evidently a close connection between hunting tian, "take
to the field," and agriculture (where tian had the sense of "to cultivate
fields"; see the Glossary entry), and it is likely that the Shang sometimes
considered hunting to be a preparation for farming as the hunters cleared Note
the land of wild animals. From Period III on, all the references to hunt- but here the word
#I simply meant
ing switch from the verb shou, "to hunt," to the verb tian, "to take to the
field, thus, to hunt"; and the inscriptions reveal that hunting areas, such
as Jing and Yu, were also often important farming areas. 112 And it is
possible that some hunting was undertaken with the opening up of the
huntings
land in mind, with the catch of game providing a supplementary benefit. Also d
Wild animals being numerous in antiquity, farming often suffered from ag
their depradations. Hunts, therefore, would also have served to protect
the crops.113

"(His Majesty gives the order that) at Long (we) will open

163
Whatever the sequence in which the charges [192A-G*] were originally
divined (see gloss), it is evident that the king was deciding the places at
which new fields were to be opened ([192ABCFG]), that he was deciding
whether or not the Duo Yin should be charged with the task ([192BC]), and
that, at the same time, he was offering prayers for good harvest to Shang
Jia (on down) ([192DE]). Such a series of divinations suggests the com-
plexity of the issues that the king faced, seeking to determine the favor of
his ancestors, the right location for the new fields to be opened, and the
officers to be charged with the task. No inscription mentioned the zhong
or even the ren. It is possible that the levies were strictly local, dependent
upon the local leadership.
Qiu Xigui (1989:23-25), basing himself primarily on what we know of
Zhou agriculture, proposes that the Shang kept some agricultural fields
in production for years at a stretch, but that, since their numbers were
extremely small, the clearing of land was important. He also suggests
that such land clearing may have involved subjecting the land to fire (see
below) and water, and that the vegetation that had been burnt and hacked
down would have served to fertilize the new fields. He cites a number of
inscriptions that, if we understand the verbs correctly, referred to the cut-
ting and clearing of vegetation, including the following. 120

role that Jing played in the political-religious landscape of the Shang.
119. Zheng Jiexiang (1994:266) locates Long about 20 k southwest of Jing, also in the
Henan.

only in three charges (Y504.1). These and other cases appear to confirm the strong interest
that the Shang kings took in farming, but it is not easy to specify exactly what activities
were involved.

Once again we see the Shang kings paid close attention to the hunt, to

The Use of Fire

and the inspecting of fields documented by [193A]-[201C] are also indi-
cated by the Shang practice of hunting by fire-burning the brush to drive

Irrigation
According to Karl A. Wittfogel, writing over forty years ago, "A realistic
interpretation of legends and protohistorical sources. points to the rise
of a hydraulic way of life long before the Shang dynasty."
129 He was "con-
vinced" that pre-Zhou and Zhou China constituted
not a hydraulically tainted feudal society, but a hydraulic society proper.
The climate and lay of the land made comprehensive hydraulic enter-
prises a basic prerequisite for permanent settlement and agricultural
prosperity in the cradle of Chinese civilization. (Wittfogel 1960:51, n. 4)
Wittfogel's declaration stemmed from his belief that the agro-bureaucratic
state, to him synonymous with hydraulic society and oriental despotism
(1963:8), had already started to form in the Shang. He argued that
agromanagerial despotism is essential to hydraulic society, and as far as
we know it is specific to it.... The corvée is not confined to hydraulic
societies; but... in agrohydraulic civilizations, different from feudalism,
corvée labor is imposed on the mass of the population by the state. (Witt-
fogel 1963:414, 415)
I do not accept his conclusions, at least for the Shang period and on
the basis of the oracle bones. 130 The Late Shang state, on the basis of the
129. Wittfogel 1963:33, n. b. The paragraphs that follow are based, in part, upon Keightley
1969:123-24. My wife and I had a pleasant lunch with Wittfogel in 1969, who had heard of
my doctoral thesis.
130. As Wertheim remarked (1968:424), "Wittfogel's picture of these ancient Asian
empires as strongly centralized units over which the prince exercised 'total power' would
appear to be far removed from historical reality." Needham's reaction was equally strong:
"a political tract which later generations will only be able to understand in the context of the
'cold war"" (quoted at Ulmen 1978:373-74). See too Chen Mengjia 1956:523-24; L. S. Yang
1964:2; Ping-ti Ho 1969:14; 1975:46; Wheatley 1971:22, 87 (nn. 62, 63), 289-98; Haas 1982:146-
48; K. C. Chang 1983:128; 2005:129; Bray 1984:161; Anderson 1988:22; Brook 1989:11, 13-14;
Kolb 1992:40-50; Lewis and Wigen 1997:94-100. Once, early in his career, Wittfogel explored
the oracle-bone inscriptions, but he did not pursue the issue: "The student of China's early
civilization can only raise the question" (1940:131). Wittfogel had a powerful theoretical
mind, but with insufficient data upon which to generalize. Yet Wittfogel still lives on today;
e.g., Jun Li 1996:viii, 1-48; Wang Wei 2004:74; Zhao Zhijun 2005:47, n. 23.
12: The Work: Agriculture
169
evidence presented earlier, might qualify as an "agromanagerial despo-
tism," for it surely levied some form of labor tax on the population. But
there is no evidence, either archaeological or inscriptional, that the Shang
relied upon large-scale irrigation agriculture, that their powers of labor
mobilization were stimulated by the need to construct large-scale irriga-
tion works. 131 Late Shang irrigation, at least as the oracle-bone inscriptions
tell us, required only leadership at the local level if that. The "realistic
interpretation of legends and protohistorical sources" refers to works of
Zhou or Later Han sources, not to the Shang inscriptions. "A rapid devel-
opment of irrigation techniques... was recorded only at the end of the
Three Dynasties period" (Kwang-chih Chang 2005:129).
Detailed archaeological studies have shown that complex irrigation sys-
tems did not accompany the rise of centralized bureaucracies but followed
after a considerable lag. That is, political centralization arose for some
other reason and then permitted construction of complex irrigation sys-
tems. (Diamond 1997:23)
This lack of evidence is supported by studies of the Late Shang climate
(see too pp. 126ff. above). 132

132. Palynological evidence suggests that a cooling period started about 3000 B.C. (reach-
ing to about 500 B.C.) (Cao Bingwu 1996), but the climate was wetter and warmer than the
present climate of Anyang (cf. Domros and Peng 1988:131; Zhisheng An, Stephen C. Porter,
John E. Kutzbach, et al. 2000:742). "The rainfall situation, another climatic factor, is still not
too clear, but there is evidence of changes when we get to the Longshan period. Most pre-
historic sites were located by rivers.... Among all the new developments in the Longshan
period, water wells were an important one. The discovery of water wells on the one hand
removes certain water limitations, but more importantly it may have been a response to a
water shortage" (Cao Bingwu 1996:42). Digging of water wells, however, does not lead auto-
matically to "agromanagerial despotism." The construction of cities, the practice of warfare,
the demands of agriculture and industry, etc., was a major factor (cf. Hsu Sheng-I and Wal-
Lok Siu 1992).170

typ may
Finally, and most importantly, we do have to deal with the phrase ku
tian, "to open up fields." If indeed the phase meant "to bank up" or
to develop the land by raising earth barriers between the fields (see Glos-
sary), we have a number of inscriptions such as [192FG], where His Maj-
esty was involved; [192BC], where the Duo Yin were involved [191BC];
and [145AB*], where the zhong ren were involved. And we have a tian,
"to cultivate the fields" (see Glossary), suggesting that the field my have
been cross-hatched with ditches. But this again seems a locally based work
of irrigation.
In short, although the Shang had the skills to construct complex irriga-
tion networks-as demonstrated by the system of drainage ditches they
built at Xiaotun 143-there is no archaeological evidence that they devoted
a significant share of workforce to such endeavors, there is no climatologi-
cal evidence that they needed to do so, and there is no mention of exten-
sive man-made irrigation in the oracle-bone inscriptions.144 Certainly,
there is now some evidence of local irrigation systems at various Neolithic
and Shang sites, but these would presumably have involved only local
labor inputs. 145 Settlements from the Neolithic and Shang periods were
also frequently surrounded by defensive moats, 146 but we are dealing with
labor mobilization in basically a non-hydraulic society, a wall-building
society, a field-building society. Probably, the walls or moats were defen-
sive in character, against the threat of enemy invasions or floods (Under-
hill 1989:229-31).
Much has been written on the effects on the development of ancient
society of the co-operative efforts required not only in the construction
of canals but also in their maintenance, but modern studies have shown
that relatively little manpower is required for either purpose, particularly
([145A]), and Yibian 1155 and 2044 (Heji 9472) as examples of quan; but others simply give it
as tian.

32; 1959:15-16, 202-10, 268-71, 272, table 110, and the flow map on p. 203; Loehr 1957:444,
447; Itō 1959:359-61, 363; Cheng Te-k'un 1960:48; Umehara 1964:16-17; Keightley 1969:134-
38; Kaogu 1975:29-30; Kwang-chih Chang 1980:76-78, 86; YXFX:77-78. For earlier drainage
ditches at Zhengzhou and Panlong cheng, see, e.g., Sato 1961:736, 739-40; Wheatley
1971:34-35; Hubei sheng bowuguan 2007:31. For the moat at Xiaotun, which had fallen into
disuse by Late Shang, see An Zhimin et al. 1961:66; Yang Xizhang 1989:307.
144. I use irrigation "in the sense of conducting water to otherwise dry fields which could
not have been used without it" (Coulborn 1959:108).
145. See, e.g., Murowchick's references (1997:30) to irrigation systems used in paddy-rice
production in Qujialing, Majiabang, and Daxi sites in central China.
146. For Neolithic defensive ditches and moats, see, e.g., Qian Yaopeng 1997; 1998; Under-

12: The Work: Agriculture
173
on the scale of an irrigation that would have been praticable among early
farming communities. 147 The means did exist in ancient China for levying,
on a centralized basis, a good number of worker-soldiers, particularly for
construction and warfare. But the organization existed prior to and largely
independent of hydraulic works.
Conclusions
The significant number of divination charges about hunting, land clear-
ing, and the burning of brush, a sample of which I have presented here,
suggests that when the Shang king and his retinue moved through his
domains, he would frequently have been combining sport, the replenish-
ment of his meat supplies, land clearing, and agricultural development. 148
His progress on such occasions must have required the investment of
labor as the royal gangs of beaters, burners, and land clearers, pursued,
captured, and slaughtered wildlife and, as they moved systematically
across the landscape, transformed it from wild brush to fields in the mak-
ing. Such hunting expeditions would have been impressive to witness,
with their ferocious yet potentially fruitful impact upon the land. Spe-
cial expeditions, involving royal officers and royal labor, were also dis-
patched specifically "to open up fields," 149 sometimes in enemy territory
(as in [145A]). The king and his officers were then involved in the adminis-
trative and ritual oversight of the fields, 150 and in requests for their protec-
tion and for their harvests, addressed to particular ancestors or other Pow-
ers. 151 In some cases the king divined about receiving harvests associated
with particular places or in general.152 And all of the agricultural activities
were conducted against the background of his general incantation, "We
will receive harvest." 153 Truly, for the Shang, agriculture was the base! But
it was not all!
147. Oates and Oates 1976:125, cited by Kolb 1992:50.
148. As Dong Zuobin (1962:412) indicates, the Shang ate two meals a day (Y81.2); he esti-
mated the time of da shi X, "the great meal," as ca. 9-10 A.M., and of xiao shi/, "the
small meal," as ca. 4-5 P.M. E.g., a verification: T, "On the ding day (day 34)
at dawn, it was overcast; at the time of the great meal, it cleared (Yincang 1101)." Cf. Cressey
1934:173. A low protein diet would result in less energy; meat eating would have been
important to status, giving energy and charisma to the elite.

charges at Y530.1-31.2 (see Glossary for dao).

153. See the ca. 264 shounian, "receive harvest," charges at Y531.2-35.1.THIRTEEN
The Work: Warfare
Leadership in Warfare
Military alliances were central to the operations and very existence of the
Late Shang state whose more distant zones were ruled by leaders identified
in the inscriptions as Hou ("Archer Lords" [?]) and Bo H ("Patrarch"
[?]; Boodberg 1979:217). The Hou were more likely than the Bo to appear
as allies of the Shang, sending in tribute and assisting the king's affairs;
they received in return military assistance from the king and benefited
from his divinations about their harvests (Keightley 1979-80:28). Numer-
ous divinations about whether the king should ally with various leaders
suggest the problematic and changeable nature of these alliances.¹
[211] 癸丑卜亘貞: 王佳望乘比伐下危
verb is here, Making cracks on guichou (day 50), Xuan divined: "It
not
(chock org.)
should be Wang Cheng (Wang Cheng) whom His Majesty
joins with to attack the Xiawei."2 (I. Bin) (Heji 811f)
With regard to ideology, the Shang dynasty was often cruel as it lorded
over the lesser tribes. For example, the taotie, the "animal mask," per-
haps linked to the ancestors, was chilling, at least to the modern viewer:
?
To some it is a monster-a fearsome image that would scare away evil
forces. Others imagine a dragon-an animal whose vast powers had
more positive associations. Some hypothesize that it reflects masks used
in rituals. Others that it carries over the face-like imagery on neolithic
jades from the Liangzhu [R] area. Still others see these images as
hardly more than designs. By tracing the evolution of the taotie over the
course of the Shang, it is possible to show how the vivid, highly animal-
like images of late Shang evolved from thin line and dot designs of early
Shang. Perhaps the taotie came simply to be associated with the Shang
1. Y61.1-64.1, 106.1-08.1, 911.2-14.1. See too n. 77 below.
2. Also see [303AB], divined on day 52.
13: The Work: Warfare
kings and the order they presided over, respected and admired because
it was an emblem of power and focus for identity more than for any asso-
ciation with real or imagined animals. (Ebrey 1996:36)³
175
The military and political domination of the Shang dynasty depended
upon the king's ability to mobilize his officers, his zhong and his ren,
for battle. As the "warfare" cases in Table 1 (p. 246) reveal, the zhong, the
zhongren, and ren were major combatants in the Shang armies. And they
were led by a variety of leaders from Table 1, mainly from Period I. About
twenty people led the zhong, five additional led the zhongren, and seven
additional people led the ren. Perhaps the zhong were generally accompa-
nied by the ren, but the divinations-apart from the "raising of the men"
(Y1.1-2.1)-focused on the zhong.
The raising of the zhong and ren were not generally associated with
their leaders. Of the 92 cases of "raising men," thirteen inscriptions were
presided over by His Majesty;5 one was presided over by Wo (Heji 795f);
and five were presided over by Fu Hao 6 only one was presided over
by the "Many Archers" ([109]). But men like Qin (Y1091.1-93.1)
do not "raise men"; they only "raise zhong" ([71], [72]) and preside over
the zhong and zhongren ([111]-[114]). Men like Qiao (Y667.2-69.2) and
Guo of Zhi (Zhi Guo Y911.2-16.2) did not "raise men." His Maj-
esty decided, after consulting with the oracle, to "raise men," and then the
administration, the regular army, took over. The Shang generals certainly
fought; but generally they did not "raise men." That was reserved for the
dynast who led the kingdom.
The battle against opposing forces was paramount, and the leaders
who would lead the zhong were highlighted. Political status, I suspect,
was often counted as much as professional specialization. The attempt has
been to divide the Shang bureaucrats into statesmen, military officers, and
historians, but that has been partly derived from Eastern Zhou history.

The Work: Warfare
177
The men who led the armies were not purely military officers; various offi-
cers served a variety of roles, both civilian and military.8
The oracle-bone corpus (Y1.1-2.1) contains, as we have seen, some
forty-five mobilization charges of the form gong ren and forty-six of
the form deng ren. The inscriptions explicitly refer to warfare in only
some twenty-five of these ninety-one cases, but some kind of military or
hostile action can be intuited in virtually every instance. This suggests
that Shang mobilizations were primarily employed for warfare; when the
ren were used in other activities, such as agriculture (see ch. 12 and Table
1), their mobilization was not generally divined; they evidently did not
need to be "raised" for the more customary and regular routines of daily
life.
Ancestor worship also encouraged a readiness to commit to the service
of ancestors and their living dependents. A large number of divination
inscriptions confirm this.' Additional examples not quoted earlier include
"abundant assistance" and "seizure" of the enemy: How the

The comparative rarity of such inscriptions, which were limited to Period I,
indicates that Wu Ding saw little purpose in divining about Di's sponsor-
ship of enemy incursions.14
What distinguished both Di and the king was that, at least in the world
of the divination inscriptions, Di focused his attention on no other living
individual and his activities. Welcome or unwelcome though this atten-
tion may have been, it cannot have failed to enhance the king's status in
the religious and political hierarchy.15

Working for His Majesty
The evidence from the oracle-bone inscriptions, although it can tell
us little about the actual conduct of battle,16 is significant for the light it
throws on the ability of the emerging state to organize its labor resources
for war in efficient and routinized ways, not only in the actual mobiliza-
tion and command of several-thousand-man armies (see ch. 9), but also
in the ability to supply them with chariots, horses, bronze weapons (see
below), and food.
Military activities, moreover, were closely related to other aspects of
the Shang polity." There was an intimate connection, for example, among
warfare, hunting (which was used for military training), and agricul-
tural development, with the hunters' burning of brush to drive the game
leading to the clearing and fertilization of new fields (see chs. 12 and
14). Warfare was practiced generally in the slack season, the winter and
early spring.18 but Shang military forces, however, were ready to march
at almost any time. 19 Hunting areas, in fact, were frequently major farm-
ing areas, with the hunts also serving to protect the crops from predators
(see p. 161). The quasi-military nature of some of the expeditions to open up
new land is suggested by the leadership of officers who, in other contexts,
were employed on military expeditions; by the use of the zhongren
laborers, who also served as soldiers in war (see Table 1); and by the occa-
sional reference to expeditions in enemy territory: [145AB*] "(We) should
order the zhongren... to enter (the territory of) the Qiangfang to open up
fields."20
The king, through his diviners, mobilized his men. A look at the "raise
men" (gong ren and deng ren \ [Y1.1-2.1]) divinations show the
diviner Que divined 34 charges, the diviner Bin, Wei #, and Zheng #
only 2 charges each.21 The "attack the Tufang" (fa tu[fang] {\±[F][Y460.2-

Similar charges indicate that Fu Hao's "going first to raise men at Pang"
involved the divinatory attention of at least two diviners over a period of
at least four days (inclusive count). 30 Inscriptions like these give the sense
28. The Guard Zhu already has appeared in [85D].
29. See the discussion at Keightley 1999:32-33. For the campaigns in which Fu Hao may
have been involved, see Fan Yuzhou 1991:214-16; Wang Yuxin 1991:149-52.
30. Inscriptions identical to [230], divined on the same day and couched in the negative
mode, all on scapulas, are found on Heji 7285, 7286 (both divined by Que ), and 7289
(divined by Zheng ); and just one day later, on bingxu (day 23), another identical charge

F, "(We) call upon Fu Hao to go first to raise men at Pang," was also divined, on scapulas,
of a relatively complex military administration in which a variety of alter-
natives were first subjected to ritual, divinatory, and, presumably, political
and military scrutiny before the king made his final, legitimating deci-
sions about personnel and strategy.31
Although the large-scale conscriptions provided the main fighting
power of the Shang army, there is little doubt that a core, standing army
existed, and there are intimations that, with titles like "Guard of the
Many Horse(-chariots)" (Duo Ma Wei ) and "Young Servitor of
Horse(-chariots)" (Ma Xiao Chen), the Shang army was organized
on command lines and in numerical units that may have benefited from, as
they may have stimulated, the genesis of Shang bureaucracy in general.3
The king's issuing orders to such groups as "The Many Horse(-chariots)"
(Duo Ma) (Y624.1-2)," "The Many Dog" officers (Duo Quan )
(Y606.2), "The Many Archers" (Duo She) (Y1010.2), and "The Many
Artisans" (Duo Gong I) (Y1118.1-2) suggests the beginnings of a more
impersonal form of administrative organization. The royal court was
also manned with numbers of Junior Servitors (Xiao Chen E), some of
whom, on the basis of titles like the "Junior Servitor for the Zhongren"
(Xiao Zhongren Chen E) or the "Junior Servitor for Cultivation (?)"
(Xiao Ji Chen/E), may have been charged with particular duties (see,
too, n. 50).33 The extent to which such titles involved specialized jurisdic-
tions and regulatory capacity rather than being ceremonial or customary
in nature is hard to specify. The administrative jurisdictions of Shang offi-
cialdom, however, appear to have been flexible and relatively unspecial-
ized. Military service was part of the general service owed to superiors.
The records of enemy slain and prisoners taken (ch. 5), and the numer-
ous and detailed head counts of animals captured in the hunt (ch. 14), fur-
ther demonstrate the interest of the Shang kings in recording manpower
and other types of figures. Words like dian, "tablets," which were
involved at certain points in the five-ritual sacrifice cycle (Y1136.1-2), and
ce (or) "wooden strips," which appear in charges about the offer-
ing of registers at times of military alliance and mobilization (Y1130.2-
31.1) and, as a verb, in charges about the offering of written promises to
the ancestors (Y1133.1-35.1), provide further evidence that written docu-
ments played a significant role in the operations of the dynasty. 34
on jiashen (day 21) (Heji 7283 = [229], divined by Que) and yiyou (day 22) (Heji 7288, divined

some occasions, they seem to have been designed to capture victims, as in:

2. There has been much speculation about his identity. Dong Zuobin (1965:91), for example,

in Shi Pan was an official title, hence "Pan of the Army (?)."
13: The Work: Warfare
187
Inscription Heji 26895 provides another case, from Period III-IV, in which
ren were involved in a penetration. 47 Generally speaking, however, such
military incursions were the province of the zhong, who presumably rep-
resented a permanent and more specialized striking force. And the occa-
sional overlapping function in these cases provides additional evidence
for the lack of any sharp distinction in the roles that the zhong and ren
could play. The king could evidently decide about sending the ren when
the zhong were not available or appropriate, or, more likely, when their
presence was already taken for granted and he needed to employ greater
numerical strength. The zhong were part of the royal infrastructure; the ren
were part of a larger force, called up as needed.
Horse-chariot Units and the Shang Army
That the Shang elites rode in chariots is confirmed by a number of char-
iot burials in the Anyang area. References to the use of chariots in war-
fare and in hunting, however, are few. 48 There is a record of what may be
regarded as China's "first traffic accident," in which Prince Yang (Zi Yang
7), riding in the same chariot as the king, fell out of the chariot ([3]).
Another inscription records the capture of prisoners and of two chariots,
taken from an enemy country (Heji 36481f).49 If, however, we follow the
argument of Wang Guimin (1983) and assume that the oracle-bone word
ma, literally "horse," referred to "horse chariots" (see the Glossary for
ma), then the inscriptional record becomes more informative and it is pos-
sible to reconstruct Late Shang army organization (ca. 1200 B.C.) in some
detail.
The "horse-chariot" units were sometimes referred to as the Duo Ma
, "The Many Horse(-chariots)," and the Duo Ma Wei, "the Guard
of the Many Horse(-chariots)" (cf. the Duo She Wei, "Guard of the
Many Archers," and Duo Quan Wei, "Guard of the Many Dog Offi-
cers"). Such units appear to have been commanded by second-ranking (?)
officers known as the Ma Ya E, "the Horse(-chariot) Ya," and Duo Ma
Ya E, "The Ya of the Many Horse(-chariots)" (see Glossary for Ya).
The Duo Ma Ya appear to have led the fighting chariots, but other officers
47. I suspect that the fragmentary Tunnan 776 (Y374.1) also divined the use of the ren in

were also involved, such as the Xiao Chen E, "Junior Servitors."50 These
groups were employed in both warfare and the hunt. We find such divi-
nations, involving the choice of personnel, as [117A] "It should be (the)
Ya (?) of the Horse(-chariot officers) who cross(es) the river (to catch) wild
buffalo," and [117B] "It should be the zhong who cross the river (to catch)
wild water-buffalo." A few oracle-bone inscriptions refer to boat travel.51
The archaeological evidence indicates that one chariot was provided
with a crew of three: the driver in the middle, with a spearman to one side
and the archer on the other. 52 Such a unit, which would have needed spe-
cial training, presumably served as the nucleus of the army. 53 There were
large shields for the charioteers (Kwang-chih Chang 1980:198); the status
of these chariot-riders was presumably middle petty elite and above. The
oracle-bone inscriptions, as we have seen, indicate that the Shang army's
chariot force involved 300 chariot-officers, with three men to a chariot,
and which were evidently divided into three units-right, left, and center
([222]). The inscriptions also refer to 300 archers ([8], [73AB]).54
Sometimes smaller units of chariots were also involved:

Shang Weapons
189
The royal house directed a significant proportion of the craft manufactur-
ing, some of it devoted to weapons production, in the Xiaotun core area.
The bronze casters of Xiaomin tun (a little over a kilometer to the
west of Xiaotun), for example, mainly produced ritual vessels, weapons,
and tools.57 Of the bronzes, 44.8% were ritual vessels, 28.6% were weap-
ons, and 8.8% were tools.58
Weapons were markers of social and political status and identity in Late
Shang society. 59 A number of inscriptions reveal that Wu Ding's consort,
Fu Hao (posthumously known as Mu Xin, "Mother Xin"), mobi-
lized troops for battle (as in [223]).60 Arrowheads were the most numerous
of all the finds at Xiaotun; more than 20,400 have been found. Most of
the arrowheads were found in the big tombs, e.g., 6,583 from Xibeigang
1001 (they were mainly incomplete, broken, and fragmentary).61 Else-
where, the three-man sets of charioteers were each equipped with a bow,
arrows, a dagger-axe, and a knife (Yang Shengnan 1982a:371). Barbieri-
Low (2000:35-36), 62 notes "the halberd weapons, ge X, of the Shang were
too short to have been used from a moving chariot and could only been
used for hand-to-hand combat."63
Shang petty elites were also provided with weapons in death. The weap-
ons found at Yinxu West constituted one-sixth of the grave goods found.
Dagger-axes, spears, and other weapons, generally made of bronze, were
found in 160 of the 939 burials, all male. Yang Baocheng (2002:181-92)
56. Bing was used as a classifier "butt, rump" (Takashima 1984-85:230-33; Sōran, no.
0674).

Chang 1980:87.
略

1997:119; Ma Chengyuan 2003:22-63; Guo Yanli 2006; Zhou Wei 2006:30-37.
60. The grave of Fu Hao, unrifled and excavated in 1976, was furnished with 468 bronzes
weighing over one-and-a-half metric tons, "the largest assemblage of jades ever unearthed"
(Bagley 1999:194), and over 6,880 cowrie shells (Kwang-chih Chang 1980:87-90; Zhongguo
shehui kexueyuan Kaogu yanjiusuo 1980:15, 220; YXFX:402; Bagley 1999:194-202).
61. Liang Siyong and Gao Quxun 1962:205; Hayashi 1972:349-61; YXFX:355, 389.

Working for His Majesty
describes the working of the yue dagger-axes and of the chan "shov-
els" (perhaps for digging graves?).64
The service and work one had performed in life was, as in the Neo-
lithic, frequently provided for in death. Fifty-six of the smaller graves at
Yinxu West were equipped with craft or agricultural tools such as adzes,
chisels, awls, stone axes, and spinning whorls.65 That some of these weap-
ons were minggi, replicas of bronze weapons that were made of lead,
suggests both the symbolic value of such grave goods and the economic
value of the bronze originals.66 That the weapons were buried with the
dead further suggests that, regardless of who had originally sponsored
their manufacture, they had come to symbolize the military and social role
of those who had borne them. Once again, one notes the "military" thrust
of much of this activity.
Shang Military History
The oracle-bone inscriptions under the reign of Wu Ding often express
the wish that His Majesty "will receive abundant assistance" (shou you
you X) or he "might not receive abundant assistance" (fu qi shou you
you
).67 There are about two hundred instances of the formula,
mainly from Period I; only seven cases from Period V (Y1215.2-18.1). One
may suppose that the wish that His Majesty "will receive abundant assis-
tance" was a general wish for "everything will be alright." As divination
evolved, the formula was simplified, part of the general simplification as
"the content and form of Shang divination changed considerably" (see
"Notes about the Sources").
The bone inscriptions, when read in the appropriate groupings, can
also reveal much about the progress of Late Shang military campaigns.68
It is generally concluded that, after a significant expansion of Shang cul-
ture during the Middle Shang or Zhengzhou period (ca. 1600-1500 B.C.),
an expansion that also stimulated the rise of a number of hostile bronze-
working countries on the Shang periphery, the Late Shang dynastic core,
64. A figure in Yang Baocheng (2002:186, fig. 68) shows the dagger-axes in action.
65. Kaogu xuebao 1979:49, 61, 80; Tang Yunming 1982; Yang Baocheng and Yang Xizhang
1983:30,31,50.
66. As Yang Xizhang and Yang Baocheng (1985:90) note, "the use of minggi... appeared
in the latter half of the second phase of the Yinxu period." "The gradual increase in the use
of minggi in burials coincides with a reduction in the food offerings, as the small mingqi could
not be used for containing food." As Tang suggests, "the distinction between life and death
was being reassessed, and that greater emphasis was being placed on the present life rather
than on the other world" (Tang Jigen 1999a:180; cf. Liu Yiman 1995:404-05).
67. E.g., [33AB], [47], [49], [50], [61], [132B], [166BDFH], [172B], [212AB], [224], [228], etc.
68. See, e.g., Průšek 1971:31-37, 53-64; Shaughnessy 1996a:62-69 (Xia Hanyi 2005:23-28).
One attempt to trace the military in Wu Ding's 29th year is Dong Zuobin 1945:II:9:4a-9a (see
also Tse-tung Chow 1968:174-75, with revisions); Liu Huan 2002a.
13: The Work: Warfare
191
despite some military successes under Wu Ding (died ca. 1189 B.C.), was
increasingly on the defensive, being forced back into its heartland in
northern and eastern Henan and western Shandong.69
The apparent absence of defensive walls at Xiaotun, however, together
with the fact that the moat, which ran to the west and south of the Xiaotun
settlement, joining the Huan River at both ends, appears to have become
choked by the end of the dynasty, suggests that the Late Shang core area
enjoyed considerable security against external invasion.70 No Period V
divinations, for example, record that enemy states were "going out" (chu
H) against the Shang (e.g., Y273.2-74.2, 1207.2-08.1); there are no Period V
divinations about the loss of zhong (Y520.1); there are no Period V charges
that hope for divine assistance in battle (Y1218.1). These silences may
reflect a change in the divination record rather than a change in reality.
No man was ever "raised" after Period I," but surely the Shang army still
mobilized its troops. The general situation toward the end of the dynasty,
as the kings chose to divine it, was of the Shang as the raided rather than
as raiders. And one plaintive note: about ten oracle-bone inscriptions, all
from Period V, end with the message:
"This evening the
army will experience nothing ominous and be peaceful" (Y761.2).
In the reign of Wu Ding, the Shang had engaged in a series of major
wars against the Tufang ± and Gongfang 3 and had obtained
a series of military victories in Shanxi, west of the Taihang X1 range,
often under the command of Qiao .74 Under the next king, Zu Geng
, the Shang armies, led by Qin, proved less successful in their battles
with the Shaofang (Y958.1-2) and Gongfang, and the Shang's west-
ern alliances began to crumble. They had been involved in wars against
the Qiangfang (Y47.1) in Period III-IV (Luo Kun 1991:416-17). And

Working for His Majesty
in the reigns that followed, the Zhou-an embattled Shang ally under Wu
Ding, that may originally have been located along the Fen River75-
together with other allies in western Shanxi, such as Gu (or), Yang,
Xuan, Yue, and Sui were lost to the diminishing Shang sphere
of influence as the power of the Gongfang grew in the northwest. Thus, we
find post-Wu Ding references to the Suifang, Yuefang, Xuan-
fang, and Zhoufang-with the suffix -fang indicating that they
had now become enemies or at least independent states (see Glossary for
fang). Other Shang supporters simply vanished from the record. In the
later periods, in fact, the kings do not appear to have relied upon mag-
nates analogous to Qin, Qiao, Cheng of Wang, and Guo of Zhi of Period
I who could lead their armies. The divination record, in which the king
had become virtually the sole diviner, represents him as the major mili-
tary leader, powerful but more isolated than his predecessors. The vir-
tual disappearance of many "personnel" divinations by the reigns of Di Yi
and Di Xin is striking."
The Shang were able to stabilize the situation closer to home, but the
size of the state, or the network in alliance with it, had grown consid-
erably smaller, at least from the inscriptions. It has even been proposed
that, by Period V, the kings' hunts ventured no further than 15 to 20 km
from his Xiaotun base, with only occasional expeditions to more distant
quarters.78 The most notable of these forays involved a lengthy campaign
against the Renfang to the southeast in Period V,79 which shows that
the last kings were still able to mount impressive expeditions when the
occasion warranted. By one estimate, the king spent up to 106 days on his
outbound march, 99 days on his return. 80 The scholarship on the dating

The Work: Warfare
193
and geography of the Renfang campaign is extensive.81 Evidently the king
did not need to be in a capital to function effectively.82 Indeed, throughout
the Late Shang period, it was presumably the king's presence, as chief
diviner and chief ritualist, that energized the Xiaotun core. And it was the
king's presence, on his travels, hunts, and campaigns, riding in his chariot
that glistened with bronze attachments and accompanied by his officers
and armed retainers, that intermittently brought the full authority of the
dynasty to the various settlements that served as the nodes of the early
state (cf. Keightley 1983:551-54). The last kings, however, acted within a
smaller compass, hemmed in by former dependencies and allies. And it
was to an army of one of these states, the Zhou, now turned enemy, that
the Shang succumbed in the middle of the eleventh century B.C.
A relatively minor lord who was observing the reception of a diplo-
matic mission in the year 578 B.C. is said to have remarked in a set speech:
“The great affairs of a state are sacrifice and war”(國之大事在祀與戎).83
There can be no doubt that warfare-and sacrifices-played a seminal role
in the historical development of Bronze Age Chinese culture.
Renfang campaigns were not to the east but to the valley of the Wei River

only about 9 k a day. If the Renfang lay about 475 k from Anyang-which lay only 53 days
on a direct march-there would have been plenty of time to do this.

The Work: Hunting
The manpower mobilized by the king could also be used in hunting, an
activity that was closely related both to military exercises-in which the
prey to be taken on the field was animal rather than human-to land clear-
ing, and to agriculture (see ch. 12).¹ Some 4,500 of the oracle-bone inscrip-
tions were devoted to the hunt, mainly in Periods III to V.² As to the
number of places listed in the oracle-bone inscriptions, they are over 150
listed by Chen Mengjia (1956:249-312) and 239 listed by Zhong Bosheng
(1972, appendix [cited by Fiskesjö 2001:133, n. 416]). The same groups of
conscripts or dependent laborers might have been involved in both war-
fare and hunting. The ability to command and deploy them effectively in
one activity presumably bore fruit in the other.³

Divined: "(We) call upon (hu) the zhongren to go out (to
hunt) deer (at) Ke (?)." (I. Bin) (*Heji 15)

zhu, "pursue," (Y328.1-30.2; Sōran, no. 1074) may also count as hunting terms. Fiskesjö
(2001:145-46) deals with hunt as military training. See too Chen Mengjia 1956:552–57; Cheng

As we have seen, the officer Guo, a subordinate of Qin, might be

[252]
今日王其田湄日不雨
197
and the charge of the Shang king and his archaic state in a dynamic pro-
cess of "domestication" that is central to state formation.
On this view, the royal Shang hunting expeditions embody the very
process of organizing and shaping the fledgling Bronze Age monarchical
state in relation to its surroundings.... [It] helped establish the king as
king. (Fiskesjö 2001:51)
One of the key conclusions that Fiskesjö reached is that the offerings
made to the ancestors were "almost exclusively... domestic animals,
as well as human beings (another 'domesticate')."5 The Shang ancestors
did not welcome meat from the hunt for sacrifices, or they did not divine
about it.6
The sacrificial offering made for the deceased included
set offerings of domesticate animals' limb bones (pig, cattle, and
sheep)....Dogs, alone or alternatively included alongside human hound
keepers.... Offerings of human guardians, slaves or other followers,
whose bodies are intact (thus presumably for a continued use in a func-
tion in the afterlife...).... Offerings of humans or parts of humans that
have been mutilated through decapitation, and thus not intended for use
in the afterlife." (Fiskesjö 2001:78)
The followers enabled the king to mobilize and train his dependents, to
show the flag as he toured and inspected his domains in some force, to
protect his crops against marauding beasts, and to stock the royal larder
with game (but not the royal sacrifices)."

And the fact that the diviners and other functionaries served to keep a
list of the game taken is one more example of having a record, a written
record, even when the hunt was far from home.12
The land was alive with wild game. The catch included the following
animals: elaphure (mi ): 209 in [257] and 700 in Tunnan 2626; a variety
of deer: 162 in Heji 10307, 451 in Heji 10344b, 159 Lu -deer (or closely
related deer) in Heji 10198f; foxes and wolves: 164 in Heji 10198; pheas-
ant (?): 27 in Heji 10197; wild buffalo (si ): 40 in Heji 37375; a variety of
other deer, hare or rabbit, pig or wild boar, tiger or large cat (hu ; see
Dang Ning 2005); and, rarely, elephants (xiang) or tapirs. 13 Keightley
(2000:107, n. 32) lists the birds: grebe, heron, kite, egret, etc.14
But the records of the hunt indicate that
the number of graphs for hunted wild animals (with the exception of
deer) is much smaller than those used for domestic animals, where there
is a great proliferation of forms, including in divinations specifying what
animals should be used in sacrifices to different ancestors. Because the
domestic animals were closer to home, detailed differentiation of color
patterns, etc., could be made at leisure.15
The hunt was not as large as a major military operation, but was still exten-
sive in its impact. It was a form of dynastic work in action, it allowed the
king to travel about his domain, and it spread the word of Shang domina-
tion and of the status of the hunters.

The Work: Construction
Wall Construction
Early evidence of communal labor mobilization can be discerned in the
earthen walls that surrounded Neolithic and early Bronze Age fortifi-
cations; more than fifty such settlements have now been found-such
as those at Haojiatai, Pingliangtai, and Wangchenggang
(all in Henan).¹ The enceintes were often surrounded by moats
(Xiaoneng Yang 2004a:117). One of the first known piled-earth walled
enceintes was in Li xian in Hunan; it formed an area 200 x 160 m,
with the base at the foot of a wall as 6 m wide. It has been dated to 5540-
5100 B.C.² "Building such ramparts and platforms required an immense
labor force and high-level management, which suggests that the operative
organization exceeded a clan or family unit."3

e.g., Cao Guicen 1987; Chen Shaodi 1987; Huber 1988; Ma Shizhi 1988; Underhill 1989:229-

wenwu yanjiusuo and Zhongguo lishi bowuyuan kaogubu 1992; Henan sheng wenwu yan-
jiusuo 1993; Li Feng 1994; Weisheu 1997:96-101; Li Liu 1996:254-77; Zhang Xuehai 1996;

3. Xiaoneng Yang 2000:174 (Yang Xiaoneng 2008:330); he cites Cao Bingwu 1996. See also
15: The Work: Construction
201
A major center has been unearthed at Yanshi in north-central
Henan.4
During Yanshi Phase I... a group of palatial architectural structures sur-
rounded by rammed-earth walls (4 ha) was constructed, and then a sec-
ond layer comprising a rammed-earth enclosure (80 ha) was added....
During Yanshi Phase II... the inhabitants of Yanshi enlarged their
small town into a big walled city (200 ha) with five gates.5
Evidently the palace foundations testify to the way that society was to
continue evolving into classes.6
It can be estimated that the massive Middle Shang rammed-earth
enceinte at Zhengzhou (ca. early fifteenth century B.C.), whose
walls-sections of which appear to have been built at different times-
were some 7 kilometers long, would have taken 10,000 laborers some 12.5
years to build. An Jinhuai assumes a workforce of 3,000 excavators, 1,000
using bronze, and 2,000 using stone tools. Thus they could dig up 700
cubic meters of loose soil per 10-hour day, 231,000 cubic meters of loose
soil per 330-day year (the other days being lost to bad weather). At the
same time, an additional force of 3,000 men would be required to trans-
port the earth from the dig to the top of the walls, and 4,000 more to
pound the loose soil into the frames. "The portion of the wall where the
[hangtu ; see below] courses run horizontally is about 10 m thick.
The overall thickness at the base is about 22 m, and the greatest surviving
height is 9 m" (Bagley 1999:166). These were dynastic works, for defense;
they were not "agromanagerial despotism" as motivated by water control
works.
Yun Kuen Lee (2004:178-79) on the "differential access to the ceremonies performed inside"
in the second millennium B.C. "Most likely, only privileged individuals were allowed to enter
into the courtyards."
