The discovery of part of what may have been a contemporary outer
rampart, also built in stages, and lying to the south of the inner city wall,
at a distance of 600-1,100+ m, and curving around to east and west, pro-
vides further evidence of the rulers' ability to engage in large-scale works
of this type. From the end of the third millennium B.C. on, if not earlier, the
ability of the local rulers to mobilize manpower on a large scale, for defen-
sive purpose, is evident.10
The Erligang remains at Panlong cheng in Hubei were also
walled." "A few sections survive to a height of 3 m and measure 26 m thick
at the base" (Bagley 1999:168). City walls in northern China were generally
built more meticulously than those in the south, i.e, wall layers were thin-
ner with more even gradations and were pounded considerably more
densely. (Xiaoneng Yang 2004:42)
The walls of Huanbei were discovered late in the 1990s. Just 2
km to the north of Yinxu, the "newly found Shang city measures about
470 hectares in its walled area, each of four rammed earth walls is about
2,150 meters long" (Jigen Tang et al. 2000:480). A Middle Shang city, it
might possibly be the capital of Pan Geng or before. 12 The building
of city walls, as at Yanshi, Zhengzhou, and Huanbei, like the casting of
ritual bronzes, symbolized the ruler's power and authority, his control
of impressive labor resources that not many could hope to match. The
links between labor power and lineage groups were probably increasing
(Zhang Zhongpei 2002:21-22). "It is clear that settlements with rammed-
earth walls from the Shang and Zhou periods are much larger than those
from the Longshan period."13
The walls of Xiaotun have not been discovered (see p. 191). Perhaps the
power of the ancestors was thought to hold enemies in check.14

Rammed-Earth Construction
Since rammed-earth construction was the building method par excellence of
ancient Chinese city walls and building foundations, and since an under-
standing of the process enables us to judge the type of labor and skills
involved, one must study the technique briefly. In Chinese the end result is
called hangtut, "stamped or rammed earth."15 In the West the process
is sometimes known by the French, pisé de terre.16 Rammed-earth construc-
tion was characteristic of the Chinese of the late Yangshao on." Before
the late Yangshao era, the dominant type of defense was ditches; thereafter,
ditches gradually evolved into fortified hangtu walls in Henan, Shandong,
Sichuan, and Hunan, and by groups of stone cities in Inner Mongolia.18
The process involves pounding earth into a wooden form, the form
being an oblong box without top or bottom, which is clamped over the
foundation or section of the finished walls. As the earth is pounded into
the box it assumes the shape of the box's interior (Merrill 1947:2, 145).
The form is removed, and the earth remains standing-as it had done in
the case of Zhengzhou, Huanbei, and Xiaotun (the temple-palace founda-
tions) for over three thousand years, some of it being "almost as hard as
concrete when excavated" (Creel 1938:178).
Some expertise is required. For example, the earth in the stockpile must
have the correct moisture content (Merrill 1947:166). And the forms-
made of wooden planks in the Zhou, and presumably also in the Shang-
must be strongly built and buttressed, "to stand up without bulging under
the tremendous pressure of the earth during ramming" (Merrill 1947:147).
The making of the planks, incidentally, would have been more work for
the state carpenters.
It is clear from the size and endurance of the Shang rammed-earth
construction that their techniques-in both wall building and house

rammer may appear on a Shang gu (Luo Zhenyu 1937, ch. 14, p. 21b).

At Xiaotun,
more than fifty large hangtu foundations were excavated... but it seems
likely that the earliest belongs to Wu Ding's reign and that the area was
occupied continuously to the end of the dynasty. The foundations vary
in depth according to the terrain and are sometimes as much as 3 m thick.
Many are 20 to 50 m long; the longest was a building that measured 14.5
by 85 m.22
Temples and Other Buildings

It seems possible that the zhong and other supporters were assembled
before the king to receive his orders in the courtyard (Du Jinpeng 2004:55),
but any firm evidence of this is lacking. It also seems likely that the cor-
vée or other labor was standard practice; it was not attended generally by
"misfortune" and other mishaps.
Drainage
Two pools have been excavated at Yanshi and Zhengzhou (Du Jinpeng
2006), where many ditches of different widths and depths have been exca-
vated, distributed among the pits and cellars; twenty-five post holes have
been found in one 5.5 m section of the ditch, twelve on one side, and thir-
teen on the other. The shape and dimensions of the Middle Shang drains
are similar to the Late Shang ones at Xiaotun, and it has been concluded
that their function was also one of drainage.3
30
The ditches found at Xiaotun have been well reported. A network of
thirty-one main and branch ditches has been discovered running under-
neath section B (and to some extent under section C) of the Shang site. These
subterranean ditches total 650.9 m in length, though the original system
must have stretched further. In cross-section they were trapezoid, with the
top ranging from 0.3 to 1.50 m in length, and their bottom widths ranging
from 0.15 to 0.8 m. 31 Some had hangtu at the bottom, and some of these had
their walls buttressed with posts. The hangtu trenches are better preserved
and may not have been in use as long as the more primitive channels.32 Five
hundred and fourteen stakes have been found along the walls on both side
of the twenty-four major long drains; the stakes were to prevent cave-ins.
There was one central reservoir (K1) near the highest part of the network,
and water gates existed to control the flow and depth of water in some
drains (K4, 11, 12, 17). The drains were not built at one time, and they were
built prior to the temple-palaces; they seem to have been most frequent
where the undergound storage and dwelling pits were most numerous. Shi
Zhangru had concluded that this system of water ditches was a drainage
network, designed to protect the pit-dweller in time of heavy rain.33
30. Satō 1961:736, 739-40; Kwang-chih Chang 1963:153; 1980:76-78.
31. Shi Zhangru 1947:69-71; 1959:202-72, table 10, pl. XII; Umehara 1964, pl. III; Cheng
Te-k'un 1960:48.
32. Kwang-chih Chang 1963:48; see too 1980:76-77.
33. Shi Zhangru 1959:15-16, 202-10, 268-71 (flow map on p. 203); YXFX:52. Another ditch,
Late Shang, was unearthed in 1973 (Kaogu 1975:29-30; YXFX:81); Yang Baocheng 2002:38-42.
15: The Work: Construction
209
Shang water control was not limited to such drainage networks. In
1958 and 1959, a large ditch was discovered 200 m west of the center of
Xiaotun village. This ditch was about 1,100 m long in its north-south axis,
650 m long in its east-west axis, and varied from 7 and 21 m in width, and
5 to 10 m in depth, winding around from southwest to northeast. No stake
remains have been found. It appears likely that the ditch originally con-
nected with the Huan River. The moat defended the settlement to its west
and south, the Huan River to its north and east. The section of ditch near
the Huan River had silted up by the closing years of the dynasty. This sug-
gested either that the rulers could no longer marshal the laborers to keep
the moat clear, or that they felt that a moat was no longer necessary. 34 The
moat was military in its function, not related to irrigation.
There was a drainage system of pottery pipes at both Neolithic and
Bronze age sites, e.g., Pingliangtai, Erlitou, and also at Xiaotun (Xiao-
neng Yang 2004a:112-13). At Xiaotun, "cylindrical pipes were used....
One end of the pipe was made thicker and had a larger mouth with a flat
edge, so that the end of another pipe with a smaller mouth could fit into
it" (Yu Weichao, ed., 1997:117). He Yanjie (2003) has studied the archae-
ology of waste disposal in the Shang cities, citing ditches and pits which
were filled with garbage. 35 Unfortunately I cannot tell if the oracle-bone
inscriptions about waste were human, or spiritual, or perhaps both. We
can take the word as yi, "epidemic, pestilence."

Wen Shaofeng and Yuan Tingdong (1983:25-25) see in the inscriptions that the Shang had
already mastered the skill of leveling the land with a water level (Li Yanong 1964:548-49
agrees). But Shi Zhangru (1959:269) rejected his initial thought that they were to provide
water levels for surveying purposes.

The presence of tooth decay and the casting of a spell does not sug-
gest very strongly the treatment of waste products. The people clearly
had drains but the construction of them was not a necessary subject for
divination.
Royal and Other Tombs
Tomb building was another major work of the Shang dynasty. 36 The fact
that large royal tombs have generally been found only in the Xiaotun
area further demonstrates the increased ability of the Late Shang rulers to
mobilize their labor forces.37 The royal tombs of this period clearly stand
as the last stage in the evolution of Shang burials, evolving both in size
and complexity (Cheng Te-k'un 1960:79). 38 At least two motivations were
involved: to honor and glorify the deceased, providing further strength
for the position of the living ruler; and to pacify the deceased, thereby
avoiding the possibility that the soul might become "angry" and cause
harm to the living.
The cemetery at Xibeigang, 2.5 k to the northwest of Xiaotun,
contains eight large, four-ramp tombs, probably those of the last Shang
rulers, Wu Ding it to Di Yi Z, with the ninth one, presumably that
of the last king, Di Xin #, remaining incomplete and empty. 39 It inter-

15: The Work: Construction
211
ests us both for its link to the rulers and for the systematic planning and
size of the undertaking. The burial areas for all of Xiaotun contain more
than 6,000 tombs, including four-ramp tombs, two-ramp tombs, pit buri-
als, and sacrificial burials.40
Guo Baojun (1951:59) estimates that one of the large graves, a two-
ramper, M1 of Wuguan cun, with a surface of approximately 340 m², and
a volume of approximately 1,615 m³, would have required a workforce
between 3,000 and 4,000 men for the entire project. Li Chi (1955:53, n. 13;
cf. Li Ji 1959:3) has estimated that each of the large graves would have
required about 7,000 man-work days for the earth moving alone. Unfor-
tunately, neither scholar gives the basis for his estimates, but the figures
point to the effective conscription of the labor force (see too Wheatley
1971:76-77). My own rough estimates indicate that such a grave would
have required, for digging alone, the labor of one hundred men for over
two hundred days; the four-ramp tombs would have made still greater
demands upon the population (cf. Thorp 2006:148-49). But interestingly,
the means for digging the tombs—the service of the zhongor ren, for insed
example-was not divined.

such valuable goods as bronzes, jades, chariots, hundreds of bronze

must have been, cramming, as it were, all of the economic activity of the
dynasty-from the use of dependent labor gangs, to the bronze making,
pot making, jade carving, chariot making, and weapon founding-into a
splendid monument for the dead.41 "The bronzes of Fu Hao," for example,

A major point
is that the tombs say less about the deceased occupants than about their
surviving heirs and about how the latter wished to assert their own posi-
tion in society. Some persons, of course, may have tried to affect the post-
mortem setting by preparing their tombs while still alive, or by leaving
explicit instructions. (Falkenhausen 2006:75)
The royal burials at Xibeigang must have been "royal" in every respect
and were probably started before the occupant had died.50 The king was
evidently intended for greatness while he was still alive.

18) and Lin Xiaoan (1990:47-48).

Some Elements of Ritual Concern
The Shang court was impregnated with ritual, the li of Shang.¹ For
Rappaport, a practitioner of ritual
is not merely transmitting messages encoded in the liturgy, he is also par-
ticipating in-becoming part of-the order to which his own body and
breath give life.²
As Bell observes,
norms and values, on the one hand, become saturated with emotion,
while the gross and basic emotions become ennobled through contact
with social values. The irksomeness of moral constraint is transformed
into the "love of virtue."3
Ritual, Lukes argues,
helps to define as authoritative certain ways of seeing society: it serves to
specify what in society is of special significance, it draws people's atten-
tion to certain forms of relationships and activity.4
1. The word li does not appear in the oracle-bone materials from Xiaotun or the Zhouyuan
. For the Zhouyuan materials, see e.g. Rawson 1980:91-92; Wang Yuxin 1984 (with index
of kaishu forms, pp. 349-72); YXFX:163-64. Li Xueqin 1992:133-35 concludes that the
date of the oracle bones started with Wen Wang XE and go down to Kang Wang and
Zhao Wang E. Wang Hui (1998) reviews three differing hypotheses; the oracle bones were
(1) made by Zhou hands, mainly of the time of Wen Wang, some from Cheng Wang E;
(2) not of Zhou date, but of the Shang royal house, divined by the Shang king in the Shang
temple; only a small number were Zhou in origin; (3) made by the Shang king who divined
these rituals, but that the recorders were Zhou people. Wang Hui (1998:20) concludes that
they were made by the Zhou. See too Liu Liang 1999; Qiu Xigui 2000:68-69; Xiaoneng Yang
2000:193 (Yang Xiaoneng 2008:366-67); Cao Wei 2002; Thorp 2006:228-30, 244-49. For another
example of the Zhouyuan bones, see Zhang Weilian and Duan Hongzhen 2008:60-61.
2. Rappaport 1979:192, cited by Mayfair Yang 1994:225.
3. Bell 1992:172, citing Turner 1967:30.
4. Bell 1992:175, citing Lukes 1975:301-02.
16: Some Elements of Ritual Concern
As Rawson (1999:22) has well remarked:
An essential contribution of ritual was that it enabled family structure
and the political order to be seen, and to be seen in a particular physical
shape that implied an ordering of society extending to persons not visible
at a particular moment.
And Underhill posits the following scenario:
The most significant factor driving changes in craft production during
the late Neolithic and early Bronze age, was, I propose, factional compe-
tition among descent groups for basic resources (good quality land, food
surpluses, labor). This competition spurred competitive feasting in both
residential and mortuary contexts, and production of greater varieties of
prestigious food vessels, including increased production of food vessels
exclusively for the dead.
... Changes in the nature of feasting can therefore be indicative of
increase in degree of social inequality.
The other significant pattern of behavior and belief regarding food
and food containers for China is negotiation of social relations with the
dead.... It was thought that prosperity of the living depended on the
satisfaction of the ancestors.5
215
Apart from the myths and legends associated with dynastic work,
which the oracle-bone inscriptions cannot tell us very much about, there
must have been an ideological component to these feasts, which the men
and spirits frequently shared. Some lineages might have been more suc-
cessful than others by claiming that it was their ancestors who had made
this good fortune possible. The guests had better acknowledge the power
of our forefathers, and also acknowledge the hosts power to mediate for
them. "The burial of lower levels of Shang society also seem to have fol-
lowed this syntax although in their case the vessels were pottery vessels"
(Haapanen 2005:86).
Rituals gave a structure to society, particularly in terms of its sched-
ule. Even under Wu Ding, the rituals were timed according to the ganzhi
days:6
5. Underhill 2002:18, 242, 243. Cf. Song Zhenhao 1999:51; Fung 2000; Shao Wangping
2000:206-11; Yun Kuen Lee and Naicheng Zhu 2002:719-20; Haapanen 2005:41. On the giver
and the gift and the impact of ritual in Shang religion, see Keightley 1978a:214-16; 2004:29-
30; Zheng Huisheng 1998:33-34. Julie Lee Wei (2005a:52) believes that the word Shang
"could earlier have meant 'gifts offered' rather than 'gift bestowed,' or it could have been in
the other direction, or was a bi-directional word that could be used in either direction." Also
see Zhu Yanmin 2007:263-309.
6. E.g., Keightley 1999b:260; 2000:32; 2004:24; Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan, eds.,
1999:608-25.216

16: Some Elements of Ritual Concern

The offering to Zu Yi was specified for the yimao day ([277]); the offering
to Zu Ding was specified for the dingwei day ([278]), etc. All this suggests a
considerable degree of abstraction and impersonalization; their cult role-
severe, dignified, formal-was more significant than their human one.
They had "bureaucratized" their hopes and fears.
No doubt, the rituals worked downward into the society (cf. Zhang
Deshui 2002). Since the Shang created every divination inscription to
implement and record their attempts to contact and influence the Powers
that dominated their universe, it is evident that the mobilization and use
of large numbers of soldiers and workers, as recorded in the oracle bones,
would also have been imbued with religious significance. Indeed, there
is some evidence that the king may have performed rituals on the zhong's
behalf. In the first place, the Shang king informed the ancestors of the
zhong's activities:

That such rituals were also performed for the zhong (as in [283]) suggests
the value that the king attached to them as lower-ranking retainers.13 No
divinations record that a protective exorcism was ever performed for the

[285] 貞:王勿往除眾人
Divined: "His Majesty should not go to purify (chu) the

0
Whatever the meaning of the term I have transcribed as chu (see Glos-
sary), it provides another instance of the king's concern, possibly ritual,
for the zhongren.
It may also be noted that, at least as we see them recorded in the oracle-
bone inscriptions, some work and service practices in Late Shang China
appear to have been highly ritualized, not in the sense that they were styl-
ized, but in the sense that they were undertaken in an ordered context of
religious approval and hemerological concern. Man acted, but he acted in
consultation with the rational, comprehensible Powers that dominated his
world and responded, if he were fortunate, to his ritual overtures. Anthro-
pologists have noted the way in which "repeated patterns of behavior
occurring in ritual contexts that seem to parallel everyday work activi-
ties... lend a power and intensity to the rituals that it is difficult for the
outsider to grasp."
//15
The work done for the Shang kings, when performed as a consequence
of divinatory and other rituals, was undoubtedly charged with such inten-
sity and significance, at least in the time of Wu Ding. Ritual, in the words

of the Zuozhuan almost a thousand years later, "governs States and clans,
gives settlement to the tutelary altars, secures the order of the people, and
provides for the good of the future heirs”(經國家,定社稷,序民人,利後嗣
#) (Legge, tr., 1872:33; Yin Gong 11). "The right to make sacrifices
to specified deities had been a hallmark of power and status in China at
least since the Shang dynasty" (Lewis 2006:228).
Warfare, to take one prime example of dynastic service, involved a
large series of formalized, religiously sanctioned procedures. There were,
first of all, the pre-campaign rituals involving the forming of the three
armies (san shi: [218], cf. [219], [220A]), the royal hunts-which over-
lapped with military training and in which the king's officers played a
role (see Table 1)-the divinations about ordering generals to raise troops,
about raising the tablets, about which general to appoint or to follow, and
about choosing the right time for the mobilization. During the course of
the campaign there were divinations about tactics, the weather, the tim-
ing, the nature of the enemy, the suitability of other factors, and, above all,
the prayers and sacrifices designed to ensure the assistance of the Pow-
ers. And after the fighting ended, there were more rituals, involving such
matters as the return of the ancestral tablets, the ritual reporting (gao )
to the royal ancestors, the presentation, disposition, and sacrifice of pris-
oners, the rewards given after battle, and so on. 16 Much Shang work, both
military and civilian, was undertaken under the eyes of the Powers in
this way—at least in the eyes of the elites who performed the divinations,
who led the troops, and who presided over the labors of others." That the
Shang also used another "two-hands-and-a-vessel" graph, (Y366.2), in
contexts of both ritual offering (as in [58] and [59]) and in contexts of mobi-
lization, (as in [49] and [51]), suggests the quasi-religious nature of the
mobilizations themselves, the absence of any sharp distinctions between
mobilizing the ren for warfare and mobilizing the spirits for support (see
deng in the Glossary). The ties of religion, linked to family, dynasty, and
the outside world, encouraged the Shang to fight.
16. K. C. Chang 1977a:190-91; Han Feng 1982:438-48; Itō 1990a:222-32; Zhong Bosheng
1991; 1992; 1993. See [279]-[281] and the inscriptions at Y247.1-49.2. "On 'warfare' as a
'prime mover"" in state formation, Fiskesjö (2001:164, n. 521) cites Carneiro's (1970) classic
paper and others. "The search for isolated 'prime movers' in the complex process of state

too ch. 17, n. 1.

strong military aspect." And as Xiaoneng Yang remarks (2000:175 [Yang Xiaoneng 2008:331]),
the "sacrifice, ritual, and warfare are the most significant attributes of early [Chinese] civili-
zation." See also Tang Jigen 1998:207; Wang Lin 2006.SEVENTEEN
The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
Ancestor Worship
What factors, then, in ancient China account for this routine and large-
scale employment of human labor by a central, proto-bureaucratic Shang
elite?¹ Certainly the Shang were well placed, in terms of agriculture and
resources, but Wittfogel's vision of an agromanagerial despotism moti-
vated by the need for large-scale water control works does not appear to
fit the Shang evidence very well. The Shang had the manpower resources
and technical skills to build fairly large-scale irrigation systems, but there
is no evidence that they did so or that they needed to do so, for the North
China Plain was, in terms of both temperature and rainfall, more hospi-
table to farming at the end of the second millennium B.C. than it is today.²
The Shang may have built city walls-at Erlitou, Zhengzhou, and Huanbei
(see, e.g., Dong Qi 2006:56-57)-but the walls were a one-time operation
and, once built, required only maintenance. The employment of labor was
far more extensive and in tune with the values of the society as whole.³
I would suggest that other factors influenced the development of Shang
labor mobilization even more significantly.
1. The paragraphs that follow are based, in part, upon Keightley 1969:346-55; see too Li
Chi 1977:247-54. Renfrew (1972:30, 37, 485) argues that we abandon the hope "for an indepen-
dent variable," urging that we concentrate upon "feedback loops" and "the multiplier effect."
Bradley (1998:66) notes that "this does not oblige us to think in terms of cause and effect, for
the relationship between these processes was surely a reciprocal one. What it does suggest
is that Neolithic ideologies and Neolithic economies were subtly intertwined and that both
emerged after a period of gradual change." Xiaoneng Yang (2000:189-93 [Yang Xiaoneng
2008:361-66]; 2004a:133-34) asks a series of good questions, with references. See too Haas
1982:209-17; Song Xinchao 1991:15-19, 93-97; Zhang Guoshuo 1995; Keightley 2004a.

may have built water control works locally (cf. Hommel 1937:49-54), but that was not a pri-
mary concern of the dynastic rulers.
3. Sivin (1972:113, 114) refers to "style defining choices... that reflect the continuous
influence of social values and priorities."
17: The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
221
As Underhill, addressing the general question of agricultural intensifi-
cation, has concluded:
Current data suggest that technological change was not a significant fac-
tor in the development of complex societies, since agricultural tools are
similar to those from the early Bronze Age, and extensive water con-
trol systems develop much later in time.... It is more likely that social
and religious factors motivated people to intensify agricultural pro-
duction and had a causal role in the development of complex societies.
(1997:149)
As K. C. Chang remarks:
There are two main ways to increase the forces of production which in
turn increase wealth: by swelling the numbers of the labor force, and by
improving the tools and technology of production. The archaeological
studies... show no signs of a major advance in tools or technology....
Agricultural tools were mainly of stone, wood, bone, or shell in the
shape of fork, hoes, shovels, and sickles, even after the advent of bronze.
(2005:128-29)
We must study the Shang cultural impact upon "the forces of produc-
tion," especially how the elites disciplined farmers, workers, and soldiers
in the sevice of the state.
Underhill writes: "A striking characteristic of the late prehistoric and
early historic periods in northern China is the predominance of labor-
intensive food vessels as prestige goods" (2002:1). Such a conclusion cer-
tainly accords with the Late Shang evidence. Many of the traits associated
with the Shang were evident in the Neolithic. By Late Shang these social
and religious trends had become greatly intensified as Neolithic and early
Bronze Age social distinctions grew sharper.5 Late Shang labor mobili-
zation-bronze casting, agriculture, the army, hunting, wall building-
required supervision. And the nature of the supervision was intimately
linked to ancestor worship.6
The royal tombs at Xibeigang having been repeatedly looted, we can
only guess at the opulence of their furnishings. Extrapolation from the
situation in M5, the tomb of Fu Hao, and other unlooted tombs of the
Late Shang suggests that the contents of the royal tombs would have been
truly remarkable in both quantity and quality. Veritable underground

See too Gao Guangren 2006; Miyamoto 2006.

sorts (Y1434.1-1447.2). See too Li Chi 1977:247-54.222

17: The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
223
storehouses of the finest products that Shang civilization could create,
they would have been magnificent subterranean monuments to the con-
trol that dead parents exercised over living descendants, to the intensity
with which the mortuary cult both exploited and stimulated the labors of
the community.
The continuing preservation of both status and kin, or kin-like, rela-
tions in a post-mortem existence was powerfully expressed by the sacri-
fice of family members, elites, dependents, or slaves to follow a grave lord
in death (see ch. 5). The practice of burying retainers and victims in elite
burials, frequently ranged in ancestral and social clusters, serves as one
of the distinguishing features of Late Shang culture. "In Taosi and Shan-
dong," in the Longshan period,
cults addressed to the ancestors were more pronounced than those
addressed to other deities. Ancestor venerations were directed to a
limited number of individuals, and were conducted by specific kin
groups. This individual-oriented ancestor worship became intertwined
with hierarchical social systems, with much energy expended in the
production of labor-intensive ritual-prestige items.... Ancestral ritual,
therefore, became a part of political institutions, and reinforced the strati-
fied, although still kinship-based, social systems.
With regard to politics, the form of a segmentary state works well. "The
spheres of ritual suzerainty and political sovereignty do not coincide. The
former extends widely towards a flexible, changing periphery. The lat-
ter is confined to the central, core domain."8 This suggests the degree to
which the intensification of the custom was associated with the dynastic
institution. It was the dynastic state, or its provincial emulators, that legiti-
mated itself—that was compelled to legitimate itself-in this way, by con-
verting the more fragile clay of kinship ties into the unyielding bronze of
dynastic demand.
In Weberian terms, then, we can refer to the hierarchical, contractual,
rational, routinized, mathematical, compartmentalized nature of Shang

remains from the Shang for the eastern coastal region, the Shang people--and also the Yi
people (?) (given the objections raised by Cohen 2001)-sacrificed humans and dogs and
practiced plastromancy and noble burial (K. C. Chang 1977a:192-93; Keightley 1987a:112-17;
1996a:28-29; Gao Guangren 2000:191-93; see too Yang Xizhang 1989:311-14; Wang Zhen-
zhong 1990; Song Xinchao 1991:75-80; Guo Da-shun 1995a:179; Sun Wei 1999:14; Li Xueqin

8. Southall 1988:52, cited at Mayfair Yang 1994:234. Song Xinchao (1991:201) gives a map
of China, showing the heartland, early and late influences, and Shang influences beyond the
borders.
ancestor worship as bureaucratic. But I am only led to use the anachro-
nistic term "bureaucratic" because of the later development of bureau-
cracy in Zhou and Han China.... Since Shang religion, as we see it in the
oracle inscriptions, was primarily a cult of the ancestors concerned with
the relationships between dead and living kin, it is plausible to assume
that the explanatory idioms we seek will involve such relationships. I
believe that the so-called bureaucratic logic of Shang religion can be dis-
cussed more accurately in terms of "generationalism," by which I mean
the willingness, and probably the necessity, to conceive of the world in
hierarchies of power based upon the relative age of generations.
Politics were dynastic and compliance relations were conceived pri-
marily in terms of dynastic advantage and the ancestral cult that legiti-
mated the dynasty.
In fact, death became a kind of promotion, since even a new ances-
tor was more powerful than the living king. Funerals and burials thus
assume signal importance. They commemorated the dead and car-
ried the spirit into a new realm and a new relationship with the living.
(Thorp 2006:184)
As Thomas (1999:129) remarks,
A number of authors (Huntingdon and Metcalf 1979:5; Bloch and Parry
1982:6) have made the point that mortuary practices do not merely reflect
the values of a community, but are instrumental in the creation and
reproduction of society.
I see little evidence that Shang China, on the basis of its human sacri-
fices, should be characterized as a slave society in economic or legal terms.
But one might wish to speak of an emotional or psychological slavery to
which all were in thrall. "Sacrifice" is perhaps anachronistic-though it
is generally used in the archaeological reports-for ethnographic anal-
ogy suggests that such "victims" went to their death, not willingly, but
because their worldview permitted no alternative choice. Just as status
and a meaningful existence in this life were determined by kin, marriage,
or other ties of affection, obligation, or servitude, so were they determined
in the next. This was as true for the dependent as it was for the grave
lord. The sacrifice of one upon the death of the other served to stamp such
9. Keightley 1978a:216-17; see too Fortes 1961:181-89; Schwartz 1985:33-39; Keightley
2004:26-29. Lewis (1997:73-74) objects, perhaps rightly, to the term "bureaucratic' mentality."
But "the Shang diviners did not regard themselves as proto-bureaucrats"; and the difference
in generations was palpable (Keightley 1978a:215-20; see, also, Keightley 2004:26-29; Wang
Haicheng 2007:217-18). As Lewis says (1997:96), "that gods were transformations of dead
men, was both an organizing rule of the archaic state and the ultimate origin of the 'bureau-
cratization' of the Chinese spirit world" (cf. Lewis 1999:16). I could not agree more.224
Working for His Majesty
inferior-superior relations of dependency and protection with the final
seal of social and religious approval.
By Late Shang, in fact, a major leap had occurred in mortuary practice.
Trends which had been evolving for hundred of years, if not millennia,
reached a final maturity. The royal burials at Xibeigang-in which a cross-
section of Shang society, from royal companions to war captives, accom-
panied the lord in death-can be regarded as dramatic incarnations of
the sociologist's "conical clan" or "pyramid of sacrifice" (Berger 1976), in
which those closest to the royal corpse had the greatest status, those most
removed or mutilated the least.
This taking of human life in mortuary contexts represented the ulti-
mate expression of service and obligation, the most radical expression of
the wish that status relations should continue after death. In death, gaz-
ing down at the dependents, male and female, and the lower classes and
captives, ranged on either side of the grave lord, would have given an
unforgettable vision of what filial service, dependency, obligation to a
lord involved. Such sacrifices indicate that similar demands for service
and obedience must have been part of the idealized, and in all likelihood
actual, political culture of the living. The practice of sacrifice was bound
to influence profoundly, if not terminate, the existence of survivors. And
it was bound to have given those who were attached to a superior a keen
self-interest in preserving his safety and his life. For by Late Shang they
would have known that a lord or a husband's life might be coterminous
with their own. Few aspects of early Chinese mortuary ritual expressed
the values of the culture more intensively or would have exerted a more
direct and powerful influence upon its development as a whole.
By Late Shang, the care of the royal and elite dead had become one of
the great Bronze Age industries. The constant draining of wealth-labor,
tools, weapons, bronzes, ceramics, jades, ornaments, food, drink, and ani-
mal and human victims-to provide for the dead, to say nothing of the
wealth and lives consumed in the subsequent ancestral sacrifices, was the
early Chinese equivalent of a planned obsolescence and conspicuous con-
sumption that cannot have failed to stimulate the productive powers of
craftsmen and laborers even as it denied them the material rewards of
their labors.10 The sustained consumption of wealth by the ancestors was
to bring blessings to the survivors and their dependents.
The construction of royal graves-symbolizing the power of those who
were ancestors and those who were about to be-was undoubtedly one of
the prime enterprises of the Late Shang state, one of the major goals for
10. For the economic stimulus arising from the regular burial of grave goods, see Renfrew
1972:377-78.
17: The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
225
which it mobilized its labor gangs with such effectiveness. The great care
accorded the dead from early Neolithic times onward had reached its logi-
cal conclusion." As status distinctions among the living developed, so did
those among the dead; and the highest status could only be symbolized
by providing them with the most perfect and most valued products of the
Bronze Age craftsmen. It is impossible to determine what percentage of
the society's wealth was actually buried in this way, but there can be no
doubt that it was significant. Bronzes and jades, alone, required extraordi-
nary productive effort.
Early Chinese politics and religion were inseparable. So were the living
and the dead. The dead relied upon the services of the living. The living
used the dead and the sacrifices which the dead demanded to enhance
their own status. If human sacrifice, in both mortuary and sacrificial con-
texts, was growing rarer by the closing reigns of the Shang dynasty (cf. ch.
13, n. 66), one must suppose that other forms of legitimation, indoctrina-
tion, and coercion, derived from the assumptions underlying such mortu-
ary sacrifices, were becoming available.
Rituals for the dead were a focus of central, strategic, and characterisic
activity in early China. 12 No matter how manipulative the elite burial prac-
tices, they could not have been effective legitimating devices had there
not been a profound belief in the power and prestige of the dead. 13 They
respected the ancestors. As has been argued by Bagley (1999:137-142), the
qualitative change in bronze production in terms of elite control occurred
in the Erlitou culture, when the small-scale metal production of tools,
mirrors, and ornaments of the previous Qijia culture was replaced
by a large-scale production of ritual bronzes at Erlitou.14 The changes in
production organization at Anyang bronze foundries may in fact be a
response to an increase in demand (Yung-ti Li 2003:300-301).
The economic, emotional, and political impact of such a belief in
the ancestors was remarkably deep and enduring, coloring all views of
11. Keightley 1998; 1999; 1999b:263-68; Bagley 1999:183-208. Luan Fengshi (2006) traces
the development of the double coffin in the Neolithic period. Zhao Huacheng (2006:41-44)
traces the path from collective cemetery to individual mausoleum. In particular, "farmers
have a stronger sense of genealogy than hunter gatherers do, and it also accounts for the
importance that they attach to ancestry" (Bradley 1998:51, citing the work Meillassoux 1972).
12. "The earliest formal burials in northern China date to late Pleistocene deposits at the
cave site of Zhoukoudian [] near Beijing," sometime between 18,000 and 10,000 B.C.
(see Underhill 1997:117). Peiligang (ca. 6,200-5,000 B.C.) "culture sites also have for-
mal cemeteries. Mourners prepared graves and interred pottery vessels, stone tools, and
other artifacts for the deceased to use in the afterlife" (Underhill and Habu 2006:126).
13. Matsumaru (1970:85) has even suggested that the true rulers of the dynasty, in the
mind of the royal court, were the former kings rather than the living incumbent.
14. For the Qijia culture, see Kwang-chih Chang 1986:280-85; Fitzgerald-Huber 1995; 2003.226
Working for His Majesty
17:
The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
227
authority. As Zi Bi of Zheng once remarked (dated to 543 B.C.):
"He has behaved properly to the dead; how much more will he do so to
the living!”(夫子禮於死者,生者乎).5 And one can see this genesis in
Erlitou:
Erlitou bronze vessels seem to have been distributed only at the Erlitou
site, while pottery ritual vessels of white kaolinitic ware have been found
from many major Erlitou sites across a broad region. These phenomena
suggest that the Erlitou rulers monopolized the casting of bronze ves-
sels, the production level was low, and the products were exclusively dis-
tributed among elite members with the highest social status. The white
ware vessels may have functioned as an alternative medium to create and
maintain a larger political-economic system.¹
Other Demands for Work
Geopolitics played a role. The alluvial plain of the Yellow River was cer-
tainly fruitful. But the relative openness of the plain, and its lack of prom-
inent topographical features east of the Taihang X1 mountain range,
meant that the developing Shang dynasty, confronted by numerous hos-
tile tribes, could not rely upon natural defenses but had to build and mobi-
lize its own. This situation had its origins in the Neolithic." As Li Liu has
noted, the archaeological evidence indicates that, in Shandong, the com-
peting Neolithic chiefdoms are "characterized by relatively stable inter-
polity relationships with little evidence of replacement among centers." In
Henan, by contrast, "some of the chiefdom systems are not environmen-
tally circumscribed, but are characterized by intense inter-group competi-
tion and frequent replacement of political centers."18
The walls of the Middle Shang site at Zhengzhou-and the wall at
Huanbei too-were presumably built to repel invaders. Wittfogel may
take some comfort in the thought that the newly discovered outer wall
at Zhengzhou might have served to repel Yellow River floods, a function
that the walls around earlier settlements might also have served.19 But that

the bureaucracy. Zi Gong (or Gong), perhaps one of the sons of Wu
Ding, was involved in divinations about sacrifices, fighting, strengthen-

229
She
("The Many Archers"), Ma ("Horse[-chariot officers]"), Shu
("The Guards"), and Wu Zu ("The Five Warbands") all served with
the army; so did individual leaders like Bing, Fu 33, Guo, Qin, Shi
Pan 師盤, Shou 受, Ya Qi亞气,Ya Qin 亞, and Yong 庸 (?).
On the basis of the evidence, I propose that the dependent laborers of
Shang were organized on a scale of increasing regimentation-with the
zhong, commanded and intensively organized by military officers, and the
ren, temporary conscripts rather than permanent state workers, less inten-
sively organized, but also led by military officers, or officers with military
experience. The use of the zhong and ren together, in warfare, hunt, state
agriculture, and presumably construction, indicate that the two groups of
laborers form a continuum of manpower resources, and were not sharply
distinguished in the ruler's mind or segregated in function.
There was, I believe, no sharp military-civilian distinction at this time,
no separation of wen X ("civilian") from wu it ("military"). The same
bronze foundries produced items for war and peace. The Gong served in
the temple and on the battlefield (see p. 44). The zhong and ren were the
king's farmers as well as his soldiers (Table 1). Presumably, the zhong
and ren also served in other tasks, but the divination inscriptions did not
record them. They were units for general service.
How effectively the king was able to control in actual fact is not clear.
We sometimes know that the Powers gave the auspicious replies, 25 but we
have virtually no record of the answer afforded by the real world of vas-
sals, officers, and conscripts. Nevertheless, the endurance of the dynasty,
its evident military strength, the luxury of its handicraft industries, build-
ing, and tombs, and the routine repetition of the divination charges for a
period of more than 150 years, all suggest that the autocratic ideal, partic-
ularly under Wu Ding, was no royal fantasy. Undoubtedly it was flawed
in execution. But it was frequently realized.
The previous chapters have demonstrated the impressive conscrip-
tion powers of the Shang state, and how, when large numbers of men
were raised, it was usually for war (Table 1). Many groups, officers or
conscripts, were required to serve as soldiers. The bronze, stone, bone,
and shell workshops of Shang all produced military items. The number
of bronze weapons greatly exceeded the ritual vessels, in quantity but not
in weight. In the 15 digs of the late 1920s and the 1930s, the archaeologists
excavated ca. 170-plus ritual vessels; the number of weapons, ca. 1,340,
greatly exceeded the ritual vessels. In the digs from 1950 to 1986, approxi-
per
charge
25. I made a brief attempt to figure out how many prognostications we have
in the Bin-group inscriptions. These figures, extremely crude though they are, suggest that
they recorded only 1 prognostication for every 83 divinations (Keightley 1996). The verifica-
tions are much less.
mately 650-plus ritual vessels were found; weapons were far more numer-
ous, about ca. 1,400-plus. In sum, they were the most numerous category
of bronzes, ca. 2,800, found by 1994. The evidence, including shields and
armor, all shows that the Shang army was well equipped.26 The number-
ing and scheduling of officers and men in neat groups of 100, 300, 1,000,
3,000, and so on, was primarily for military purposes.
The demands of warfare were bound to lead to an expansion and cen-
tralization of dynastic control in the civilian as well as the military areas
of life.27 The genesis of the Shang system of dependent labor was thus, I
suspect, stimulated to a significant degree by military concerns.
At the same time, Shang survival would have depended on get-
ting enough to eat. The frequent moves of the capital, together with the
demands of a relatively primitive agriculture (see ch. 12), would have
required the investment of much labor to clear new land. This could
not be undertaken on a large scale by individual farmers, and the divi-
nations show how this was a matter of royal concern, and how the land
was cleared by the same groups of conscripts, the zhong and the ren, who
manned the royal armies (see the discussion of [133AB]-[145AB]).
The demands of warfare and agriculture in this unfriendly political
environment-and I differ from Wittfogel in emphasizing the hostility of
the political environment, not the natural one-help to explain the need
for efficient labor control. The need was political and cultural, not pri-
marily economic. The need was met; the Shang flourished because they
adapted their social structure to the challenges posed by their political
environment (cf. Guo Ruiji 1999). But how were the Shang able to mobi-
lize their resources in this way? Part of the answer may lie in the qual-
ities of Shang religion, which emphasized the service of the ancestors
and their living representative on earth. There was a general tendency
to obey the superiors, both the ancestors and those who spoke for them.
The elite culture was not one of rejection, quarrelling with the gods and

the Shang in many customs.
The Role of Writing
Part of the answer to why ancient China evolved the way that it did, I
believe, may lie in the role that writing played in Late Shang culture. 30
Shang dependent labor could not have been mobilized so efficiently
without a system of record keeping to organize and direct it (Keightley
1989a:194-99). Writing was not limited to the oracle bones and it was not
limited to the king's use,31 but it is possible the writing system was lim-
ited in Shang China. There was no need for large-scale, plebeian trade in
basic commodities. What exchange relations there were involved "dynas-
tic" control, such as metal ores, jade, and turquoise. The writing system
that developed to keep track of these transactions was largely the pre-

serve of the elites. 32 And it was generally limited to the Xiaotun area and
to Henan.33 More writing may have been generally available on bamboo
strips and other perishable materials, 34 but the use of writing-to carve
the divination inscriptions which gave witness to the contact with the
Powers-gave peculiar force to Shang writing. 35
Furthermore, some of the dynasty's laborers, as represented by the
royal artisans at Xiaotun, would probably have been at least semi-literate
(see p. 25). The specialization that took place in their workshops must have
required some system of accounts. If the artisans could make inscriptions
on the objects they manufactured, they could presumably keep records of
materials used, time spent, cost, and so on. 36 And the spread of writing to
the Wei River valley gave added impetus to the next dynasty, the Western
Zhou.
In fact, the oracle bones alone show that the Shang diviners kept
numerical records of (1) military conscription, (2) casualties suffered by the
Qiang or other enemies, (3) animals offered in sacrifice, (4) animals caught
in the hunt, (5) tribute offerings of turtle shells and scapulas, (6) numbers
of days, (7) strings of cowries, (8) measures of wine, and (9) other miscel-
laneous counts.37
If we imagine the Erligang diviners having to deal with an increasingly
complicated round of sacrifices, one that required auspicious days to be
determined in advance for more and more ancestors and sacrifices, it is
easy to suppose that sooner or later they would want records of some
kind, a way to keep track of dates in particular.38

Working for His Majesty

The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
233
I am not among those who believe that the Shang developed their
writing system primarily for religious purposes, that they invented the
script so that they could write oracle-bone inscriptions. But I do believe
that a strong synergy existed between the way the Shang constructed
their ancestral cult and the way they constructed their conceptions of kin-
ship, their vision of the good society, their system of labor recruitment.39
Writing was clearly crucial to the charges engraved into the pyromantic
shell and bone; they completed the process of human-ancestral exchange.
The service of the ancestors and the service of the dynasty benefitted from
the same attention to jurisdictions, hierarchies, the issuing of orders, and
the expectations of obedience.
The Creation of the Ancestors
The Shang created their ancestors as impersonalized, hierarchical powers,
with ranked jurisdictions and functions (Keightley 1978a:214-20; 2004:11-
26). Such conceptions of the ultra-human personality-or, one might say,
ultra-human impersonality-were bound to reflect upon, as they reflected,
the ways in which the Shang organized their own social relations. The
Shang created the ancestors they needed; and the ancestors gave them
the authority and the skills and the administrative models to preserve the
dynasty and to wring blessings from the North China Plain. Shang labor
administration demonstrates the significant ritual concerns that accompa-
nied decisions about economic organization. And it also suggests the com-
plexity of Shang decision-making and administration (see later). Those
Shang dependents and laborers who worked for the dynasty did so within
a system of values that gave meaning to their service and that permitted
and encouraged subservience and obedience. "The answer," according to
Cheng Te-k'un (1960:197), "is to be found in the organization effected by
the Shang rulers."
But the answer was not limited to the upper levels of society. The
answer was also current in the scribes and diviners who were promoting
their bu -cracking and writing. They were the ones who also benefitted,
and who claimed the benefits for the other elites, of being able to forecast,
record, schedule, etc.
Authority... "lies in the capacity to produce consequential speech" (cit-
ing Lincoln 1994:4).... What is central to authority is the effect produced
by speech. Authoritative speech reassures the doubtful, wins over the
ambivalent, and defuses the opposition. (Smith 2003:106)
39. See, in particular, Keightley 1978a; 2004. Weld (1997:159), writing of the covenants
from the Eastern Zhou, finds they "do not put human conduct under the supervision and
control of the natural world, but instead extend the human jurisdiction of the ruling lineage
across the barrier between the worlds of the dead and the living."
Perhaps these divinations were in part "make work" by the diviners, to
justify their employment as officials or bureaucrats of the Shang court
(Keightley 2006:194-95); indeed, multiple motivations may have been
involved. Shang writing and ancestor worship were, in any event, an
important part of the "feedback loop."
The Shang, moreover, created ancestors who needed bronze-bronze
for the prestigious and gleaming ritual vessels in which their descendants
could make the offerings appropriate to the status of all concerned.40 The
need for lead and copper ores certainly helps to explain the expansion of
the Erligang polity into south China.41 The needs of the Late Shang bronze
founders would have required a continuing supply of ores and fuel. 42 And
the Late Shang bronze-casting industry required, once again, the mobili-
zation of a significant labor force.
The bronzes were also to confer prestige. The larger the amount of
bronze buried with the corpse, the higher the status in the ancestral cult.
These also applied to the military in terms of weapons:
There are 12 combinations of bronze weapons in Yin tombs at Anyang,
with ge []dagger-axes as the most frequently seen key objects. Among
the tombs with bronze weapons, as the tomb-owners varied in status
before their death, their funeral weapons bear distinct differences in type,
number, and quality. High-ranking officers possessed yue [] battle-
axes, ge, spears and arrowheads, with swords added in some cases....
Middle- and low-ranking officers had some dozen weapons in the ge-
spear-arrowhead or ge-spear set.... Ordinary soldiers were armed with
one of the ge. (Liu Yiman 2002:63; abstract)
The more bronze weapons, the higher the rank of the deceased.
And finally the king commanded (ling ) the various supporters of
the dynasty. Di commanded the weather, for example (Y127.2), 43 or enemy
attacks ([217B]). The king commanded his officers. There are only about 60
wang ling inscriptions, most from Period I (Y127.2-28.1). Mainly, the

Working for His Majesty
king commanded the various officers, like the Duo Qiang *, the Junior
Servitors (Xiao Chen E), Qin, and the Three Warbands (San Zu
E). There was certainly negotiation, and even perhaps resistance on occa-
sion, as the Duo Yin 多尹, Duo Jun多君, Duo Zi 多子, Duo Zi zu 多子族,45
and others-members of the royal household and others-considered the
royal orders. But virtually everything that His Majesty caused to write in
the oracle-bone inscriptions was in fact an order, direct or indirect, to his
human subordinates. The orders were mainly carried out. They were for
control of a ruling elite. And frequently, the commands were for warfare.
There are no simple or single explanations for why Shang socio-
economic structures took the forms that they did, but the evidence does
show that religious responses played a significant role in their validation
and intensification, that lineage and other structures were being reflected,
validated, and energized in the political and social arrangements recorded
in the inscriptions. Shang dependent labor was "ancestral" in more ways
than one. "The three most powerful insulators against the enviroment are:
ceremonial centers (against the unknown), writing (against time), the city
(against the outside)" (Renfrew 1972:13). The Shang had all three. And
writing was particularly significant, as it contributed to the development
of ancestor worship, bureaucracy, and a whole system of dependent labor
(see n. 33 herein).
A final comment. As I reviewed the inscriptions that I chose to include
in the body of this book, I was encouraged to reflect on the complexity of
the evidence, the complexity of the social and political arrangements that
are recorded in the divinations. One cannot argue for the existence of a
fully functioning Shang bureaucracy, such as one finds in the Zhouli or
in imperial times. Shang administration was probably too irregular, too
liable to the role as a "social relationship" (guanxi),46 too liable to the
whim of both man and the Powers, for that model to yet apply-although
the Powers certainly appear to have played by certain rules. 47 But at that
same time, we are clearly in the presence of a dynastic elite that paid con-
siderable attention to matters of personnel selection, the issuing of orders,
the choice of strategies, the validation of choices made, and the recording
of many of these complex concerns, all within the structure of a system of
religious belief that set great store on good order, on a generational hier-
archy, on contractual sacrifices, and on proper observance of jurisdictions.
obey him (Y128.2-30.2). There were only a few oracle-bone inscriptions, involving the graph
ling, that were non-royal; e.g., Keightley 1999c:185; Smith 2008.
45. Y986.1-87.1. See further Akatsuka 1977:808; Lin Yun 1979:323-24; Chao Lin 1982:4-10.
46. Mayfair Mei-hui Yang 1994 offers a good introduction to the topic; see, in particular,
"Ritual Power and the Power of the Law" (pp. 222-32).
47. Keightley 1978a; 1998:793-814; 1999b:251-68; 2000:97-103; 2004. For the Western Zhou

17: The Role of Geopolitics and Culture
235
Shang "bureaucrats"-like the Duo Gong I, Duo Chen, Duo Ma
馬, Duo Shi 多豕, Duo Fu 多父,Duo Ya多亞, etc. (at least 26 groups have
the prefix duo, "many"; Y1270.2-71.1)-clustered around the royal
personage. Some may have referred to spiritual beings than actual liv-
ing beings, but Shang administration was, once again, "ancestral" to what
was to follow (Keightley 1978a). As Mark Lewis has so eloquently written:
Even in the developed bureaucracy the palpable religious origins of
many practices, the overlap of the forms of cult and administration, and
the incorporation of new state practices into the imagined realm of the
dead blurred any separation between politics and religion. Instead the
world of the spirits became the first imaginary double of the emerging
state, a doubling marked by their shared use of written forms. (1999:13)
And the construction of the Shang state may have owed as much to the
way that the Shang constructed their ancestors as the ancestors owed to
the construction of the state.
Their claim to cultural "fatherhood," to an authority that could not easily
be escaped, is well justified. The Shang did indeed make their ancestors
and they had made them useful. The living had put the dead to work.
And the dead had responded in kind. (Keightley 2004:45)
As I wrote, the Shang "made their ancestors" (2004:3).18: The Legacies
237
EIGHTEEN
The Legacies
Shang traditions were still vital a millennium after the fall of the dynasty.
The sixty days still followed the ganzhi system (Chen Mengjia
1956:236) and, perhaps, even the yearly calendar.¹ The chariot was prized
in both the Shang and Zhou.² Shang rituals and burial practices were fol-
lowed in the Zhou and beyond.³ The Shang enemies continue to appear
as "names of the ruling class" of Zhou (Průšek 1971:57, n. 18). The states
of Lu and Song #were regarded as repositories of Shang culture. The
Zhouli records the use of the zhong in the army, the hunt, and in
corvée.5 Myths and legends about the Shang were preserved, some in the
successor state of Song.6 If the particulars flourished in this way, so one
may suppose did the ethos and worldview. Every idea, every pattern of
thought, has its genealogy, and some of the mental habits central to Zhou,
Qin, and Han culture can be traced back, as I have attempted to show, to
the ideas and thought patterns of the Shang. "From a wealth and variety
of literary evidence it is now recognised that divination and the consul-

2. Creel 1970:282; Barbieri-Low 2000:57-68.
3. E.g., Waterbury 1942:7-24; Karlgren 1950:55; 1950a:186; Dong Zuobin 1951:397; Itō 1956a:
423; Kaogu xuebao 1978:319; Kwang-chih Chang 1976b:59; 1986:361-67; Maspero 1978:106;
Kung-chuan Hsiao 1979:96, n. 40; Ray Huang 1981:117-21; Li Zizhi and Shang Zhiru 1986:10-
14; Kaogu 1987:19-20; Wenwu 1988:39-48; 1989:60, fig. 2; 82, fig. 39; 83-84, figs. 40-44; Falken-

Brooks and Brooks 1998:114, 304; Rawson 1999:31-32; Zhang Weilian 2008.

tation of oracles played a far greater part in Qin and Han life than has
always been appreciated." There were evolutions and refinements, but
many characteristic forms of Chinese thinking had been established a mil-
lennium or so before the golden age of philosophy in the Eastern Zhou,
Qin, and Han.
The oracular impulse lies deep in Chinese culture. One of the marks of the
Confucian junzi [], the political-philosophical paragon from whom
even a ruler could learn, was his ability to foresee and prognosticate.8
And the Yijing says: "The superior man takes thought of misfortune
and arms himself against it in advance" (#)(Wilhelm
1969:245).
The Duke of Zhou said: "We cannot fail to mirror ourselves in
the Xia; also we cannot fail to mirror ourselves in the Yin" (E
于有夏, 我不可不監于有殷). One of the most impressive features of the
organization of labor in early China, especially when we consider that the
Zhou displaced the Shang by conquest, is the continuity of administration
and culture in the work activities of the first two historical dynasties.10
Ancestor worship was important. Before the time of Mu Wang E
(956-18 B.C.), seventeen of the twenty most common rituals recorded
in the bronze inscriptions of the Western Zhou were the same as those
recorded in the oracle bones of Shang." The military was important. Over
fifty Western Zhou officials
are referred to as shi [] in the [Western Zhou] bronze inscriptions....
We must consider the possibility that officials with the title shi probably
had all served in military functions at some point in their life. (Li Feng
2004:21,23).
The continuity is revealed by the persistence of Shang manufacturing
and rammed-earth construction techniques after the conquest (Keightley

walls of Han, they were built by the hosts of Yan ()... in those
(regions) he made walls, he made moats, he made acres of fields, he made

Wu 吳,
before the crops had ripened, raised an army (J), and launched
a military expedition northwards. He excavated a deep channel which

We also have an account of the Da Sima
of Chu, who, in 548 B.C.,
On jiawu,... set about describing the [different] lands; measuring the
forests; defining the meres; marking out the higher lands and the downs;
distinguishing the poor and salt tracts; enumerating the boundaries of
flooded districts; raising small banks on the plains between dykes; assign-
ing the wet low grounds for pasturage; dividing the wide rich plains into
jings [#]...; determining the levies according to the incomes of each;
assigning the [contribution of] carriages and of horses; and of footmen;
with the number of buff-coats and shields. (Legge, tr., 1872:517; Xiang
Gong 襄公 25)
Public work in ancient China was often military, civilian, or both.13
It is also revealed by the proto-bureaucratic way in which both dynas-
ties managed the workers; in certain cases the Zhou even adopted Shang
titles such as Duo Gong I (which presumably became Bai Gong I,
"The Hundred [= Many] Artisans") and Si Gong I ("Master of the
Artisans"). The roots of that title presumably derived from such Shang
usage as the si wo Gong I of [17A].
In general, the Zhou appear to have enlarged and diffused the ele-
ments that had formed the public work system of Shang. It is not easy,
however, to discern the degree to which the Zhou system of public work,
as it was practiced in the WeiRiver valley prior to the conquest, may
have been similar to that of Shang. The character Gong I appears in only
one of the Zhouyuan oracle-bone inscriptions (H11:102), and it may have

roy Zuo Zongtang [] during the 1870's."

served in that instance as a name. 15 The word zhong does not appear in
the Zhouyuan bones, and the zhong, significantly, do not play a large role
in the bronze inscriptions of the Western Zhou.16 The Shi does contain one
reference to the zhongren:
/, "Order all our men / To be
provided with their spades and hoes."17 This is similar to [121], [145AB*],
and the eight other inscriptions at Y69.2-70.1 which contain the phrase
ling zhong .18 This suggests that the Shang institution of retainers who
formed a standing labor and fighting force for the king may not have been
maintained in the Zhou documents we now have or, at least, not under
the old rubrics. Indeed, in the Shang, the word zhong appears rarely in
Period IV and never in Period V.19 The same conclusion applies to the term
gong renor deng ren (Y1.1-2.1-with the exception of [286*]); all
were Period I. Perhaps more efficient administration was taking hold, and
the luck of the ancestors was no longer so deeply involved. Dependent
labor, nevertheless, was employed by both dynasties, Shang and Western
Zhou, because it was the most culturally effective way to perform the tasks
of the state. Given the absence of free labor or a monetized economy, such
labor was, of necessity, fundamental to the agriculture, warfare, manufac-
turing, and construction of the emerging Chinese state. 20
Its endurance is partly explained by the natural inertia of precedent.
But the quality of Shang public work, and the richness of its products,
must have also made the system attractive to the successor dynasty. It
would be tempting to write of the "efficiency" of Shang public work, but
Moses Finley's cautions based upon the early Greek evidence are worth
bearing in mind:
The very few Ancient writers who mention division of labor at all do
so in a context and from a point of view which are essentially different
from Adam Smith's. They were interested in the quality of manufac-
ture, not in quantity or efficiency. Indeed, the very notion of "efficiency"
is one of the best examples of a modern concept which, though taken
as self-evident, turns out to be missing (in such contexts) throughout
Antiquity.21

Working for His Majesty
Such considerations suggest that the Shang elites would have been more
interested in results than in their cost, more interested in the product, in
the work product-whether harvest, victory in battle, rituals, the casting
of impressive bronzes, game caught, settlements built, fair weather, good
fortune, etc.—than in efficiency and human cost. 22
There was no phrase in ancient Chinese to express the idea of depen-
dent labor or public work; the unifying concept was the responsibility,
shared generally by the king's subjects and diffused downward through
the patrimonial and patriarchal political and social ranks, to serve the king
and the lineage heads. Though there was certainly a fundamental social
cleavage between those who benefited from the work and those who
labored to provide it, there was also an acknowledged continuum of obli-
gation that required all, high and low alike, to serve their superiors. Some
tacit social contract-though not, in a stratified society, a contract that was
always mutually beneficial-must have existed between the Shang king
and his zhong and ren, between a Shang lord and his peasants. Those at the
lower end of the political and social scale provided the labor, those at the
upper end provided-or claimed to provide—the blessings of the ances-
tors, of Di, and of other Powers.
The concept of servitude, in fact, seems to have been generally accepted
by people at all positions on the social spectrum. The Shang king worked
for and sought the approval of his ancestors, working hard at his divi-
natory tasks at least as recorded.23 The king rose early in the morning
(see p. 115). He was no freer of dependent obligations than the subordinates
over whom he ruled, although he was, of course, far more handsomely
rewarded for his services. And this general, pervasive expectation of social
and political dependence, hallowed by the practices and assumptions of a
pervasive ancestor worship, affected early Chinese attitudes toward work
in general. The Duke of Zhou (Zhou Gong A), in offering his own life
in place of that of the young king Cheng, explained, according to the
"Jin teng "chapter of the Shu, that the young king was not as capable
as he was at "serving the ghosts and spirits" (E).24 This Western
Zhou legend, probably composed during the Eastern Zhou, would have
likely had its roots in Shang or Western Zhou cultural practices. Just as the
Shang diviners worked every day of the week, their rulers did the same.25
And as is well known, "the notions 'service' and 'work' are in Chinese
22. Rare texts reveal some concern with efficiency in the Eastern Zhou: Legge, tr., 1872:310
(Xuan Gong 11), 741 (Zhao Gong 32); see Oshima 1959:63-64.

regularly expressed by one and the same word," that is, shi, "serve …….;
service, office, occupation...; functionary."26 The oracle-bone wish that
various dependents would gu wang shi, "strengthen His Majesty's
affairs" (as in [17A], [27], [68A]), 27 testifies to one early manifestation of
this concern. Even today,
The Chinese make three general assumptions about ancestor worship.
First, all living persons owe their fortunes or misfortunes to their ances-
tors. Second, the departed ancestors have needs similar to those of the
living. Lastly, the departed ancestors continue to help their relative[s?]
in this world, just as their living descendants do to reciprocate. (Mak
2001:35)
There was no glorification of leisure in the thought of early China; even
in the Eastern Zhou period, few elites were free from the demands of state
or office. In the texts that have been transmitted to us, the Odes were gen-
erally chanted not for pleasure but for statecraft. It was bad if a ruler loafed
and enjoyed himself, 28 bad if he exploited the labors of others only for his
own pleasure.29 The Shu and Shi propaganda against the last Shang ruler
included the stereotypical charges that he had put pleasure before work
and had been slothful (cf. Fiskesjö 2001:137). A king was expected to work
like everybody else. The Shu, "Duo shit," records how, in the opinion
of the Zhou conquerors, "the earlier (Shang) kings toiled for their house"
(E).30 Accordingly, again, to the Shu, "Wu yi," "from morning to
midday, and from midday to sundown," Wen Wang XE "did not allow
himself time to eat (自朝至于日中昃,不遑暇食) (Legge, tr., 1865:469),
so hard was he working for the people. As the Duke of Zhou (speaking
for the young king Cheng) told Feng #f, his younger brother in the Shu,
“Kang gao 康誥”
恫乃身,敬哉...往盡乃心,無康好逸豫,乃其又民...惠不惠,懋
不懋
"...(pain your body =) exert yourself intensely and be careful.... Go
and (exhaust your heart =) do your very best. Do not repose and love

18: The Legacies
243
idleness and you will (well) govern the people.... Make compliant those
who are not compliant, make energetic those who are not energetic."31
The Shi, "Zheng Min," repeats the same theme:
夙夜匪解
以事一人
Never idle, day nor night
In the service of the one man.
(Legge, tr., 1871:543)
Shang and Western Zhou bronze inscriptions include variations of the
standard phrase yong zuo F, "used to make (for an ancestor)," and
yong shi, "use (these honors, gifts, etc., in my) service" or "(in my)
affairs," or "to serve (me)."32 This generally austere and pragmatic view
of work was further developed by the Confucianists, Mohists, and Legal-
ists of Eastern Zhou, whose teachings continued to stress obedience to the
state. As Zi Xia is reported to have said in the Lunyu 19.10,
君子信而後勞其民,未信則以為厲己也
The noble man, having obtained their confidence, can then impose labors
on his people. If he have not gained their confidence, they will think that
he is oppressing them.33
Persuading the people to work for the ruler was one of the primary
goals of most Eastern Zhou political theorists. The king's-service ethic of
Shang helps to explain the strength of the state-service ethic of later times,
and it was not challenged till the rise of Taoism, a philosophy whose
appeal derived in part from the pervasive ethic of service and accomplish-
ment that it was challenging. The work ethic of the Shang had finally pro-
duced its philosophical reaction. 34
The Shang ruled the North China Plain for better than 150 years, per-
haps as long as 580 years. 35 They gave us a series of benefits-the religious,
philosophical, and economic ordering of the society. They gave the Shang
dynasty its power. They worked hard for a living. They endured. And I

when the king returned from marching to regulate the Renfang," that was attached to the
standard gui-day incantation, "In the (next) ten days, there will be nothing ominous."

Inscription Glosses
Inscriptions discussed in this section are marked with an asterisk in the
main text. They are listed alphabetically and numerically. Each inscription
discussed in this section can be found in the main text by consulting the
"Key to the Inscriptions Translated" (p. 483).
Heji 1. Chen Mengjia (1956:606) and S25.2 both supply the wang E at the
start of [143]; MZ and Heshi do not. I believe, however, that the bottom
right corner of the wang graph can be seen in the rubbing. Furthermore,
it may be safely supplied on the basis of Heji 5, 貞:王大令眾人曰
"... Que divined: 'If His Majesty will greatly order the zhongren say-
ing. engraved in a large calligraphic style and on a large scapula
fragment similar to that of Heji 1.
Li Xueqin (1985:101), building on the arguments of Zhang Zheng-
lang 1983, has proposed that the xie (see Glossary) in this inscrip-
tion referred to the name of a wind, so that the charge meant [143]: "His
Majesty's great order to the zhongren says: 'It is the season of the Xie
wind, the fields will receive a good harvest."" (On Xi as the name of
the wind of the East Fang, see Heji 14295; e.g., Allan 1991:79-83; Keightley
2000:70-71; Aihe Wang 2000:35; Smith 2008:366-73.) Li notes that there are
also charges of the form ""; this, he believes, indicates that the
Shang recognized that when the Xie wind came at a certain season there
would be a good crop. Li Xueqin (1985:99-101; 1989:104-110) and Aihe
Wang (2000:29, 35-36, 50), among others, discuss the Four Winds and the
Four Seasons. Such an interpretation cannot be excluded. Li Xueqin and
Zhang Zhenglang (1983:1) punctuate by putting a comma before the tian
of [143], thus 王大爷眾人曰:吾,田其受年, but I know of no comparable
case in which a charge ended with , "the fields will receive har-
vest." Nor am I comfortable that “His Majesty's great order" to the zhon-
gren would, in this view, turn out not to be an order at all but a comment
on the weather. I would also note that, according to the records for 1951-
Appendix 1: Inscription Glosses
251
80 that I consulted in the Anyang diqu qixiangju qixiangtai
** (Meteorological Observatory of the Anyang Region Meteo-
rological Office) on 22 April 1981, there are now no months in the year
when the prevailing wind at Anyang is from the east. The modern situa-
tion, of course, does not preclude the possibility that Shang wind patterns
were different; for the situation in Shang times, see, e.g., Wen Shaofeng
and Yuan Tingdong 1983:155-57: the wind direction was mainly from the
north, one instance from the west, and there were no eastern or south-
ern winds. That [143] was divined in the Shang eleventh moon-roughly
equivalent, in my view, to modern October (Keightley 2000:43-44)-does
not strongly support the view that the ritual significance of the charge
would have been linked to the start of the agricultural season. In October,
in the period from 1951 to 1980, only 4 percent of the winds blew from the
northeast, 2 percent from the east-northeast, 1 percent from the east, 1 per-
cent from the east-southeast, and 4 percent from the southeast.

obtain the graph for from Jiakao 3510.4); or for the character that fol-
lows Qiangfang. Also see JGXS:2.

Heji 18. Inscription [86] was inscribed on the right side of a plastron; a
solitary gong graph remaining on the broken left side suggests that the
matching negative charge of the charge pair would have been some version
of: CE, "This season, the zhong might not have success (gong)."
(See the Glossary entry for gong I, .) Major (2002:463-64) speaks about
the question of why the Shang "did not explicitly name the four seasons"
(Keightley 2000:71; see too Yao Xiaosui and Xiao Ding 1985:143); this is
one instance of the evident appearance of chun (Y514.2-15.2; Sōran, no.
0053; Zhu Qixiang 1999a:4-5). At least six instances each, of chun # (Sōran,
nos. 0053, 0781, 3190, 4045, 8121) and qiu (Soran, nos. 0040, 1203, 1570,
4232, 8126), have been identified; some may be correct (see, for example,
Cui Hengsheng 1998:4; JGXS:5). Also see zhe in the Glossary. Dong
Zuobin gives the "eleventh moon" for the date of the divination (Waibian,
no. 452); MZ and Zhu Qixiang (1999a:4) give the "tenth moon"; the rub-
bing is not able to resolve the issue.

Heji 24. The translation of [69] is problematic. Provisionally, I treat as a

Heji 27. The presence of wang E, "His Majesty," as the subject of [113],
a virtually identical charge, supports the insertion of the implicit royal
"We" at the head of [112] and numerous similar cases.

I read, with no great confidence, as mian ([212AB]), see, e.g., JGWD:

Heji 39. I follow Chen Mengjia (1956:606) and Ikeda (1964:116; commen-

Heji 43. I am uncertain how to translate the inscription, particularly the
qi (see the Glossary entry) in [236]. I have tentatively translated it as
"seek." It is possible, however, that it might have served as a person's
name in this charge, which would thus start: "As to the zhongren that, on
the next xin[wei] ..., the Ya (officer) Qi will take to penetrate Dinglu...."
But I know of no clear instance in which qi served as a name in the oracle-
bone inscriptions. Alternatively, the qi might have meant "to stop, finish":
"As to the zhongren that, on the next xin[wei] ..., the Ya (officer) will finish
penetrating to Dinglu...."

MZ and Heshi. (I have not found another rubbing; the bone is no. 2338 in
the Peking University Library collection.)

Heji 903f. On the basis of his analysis of you, Kwok-ching Chow
(1982:341-46; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:292-94) would presumably trans-
late [335AB] rather differently, "On the coming yihai, we should perform a
you-offering to Xia Yi; (and then) we should offer fifteen dismembered vic-
tims, etc.," that is, he would treat the charge as a compound sentence that
indicated a sequential plan of action, rather than, as I do, as a comprehen-
