The presence of the si, "four," at the end of [335B], is puzzling.
Takashima (1973:391) and Serruys (1981:351), who offer translations of the
inscription (as Bingbian 197.4), omit the character, but it is present on the
plastron (Y37.1; JGXS:148). Takashima (2010:I:394) translates it "four..."
Zhang Zhenglang (1983a:20), transcribing this inscription as Zhuihe
173,
took the si as the first character of a full verification that he supplied as
[I], "After forty[-one days, on yihai (we) did not make
a you-offering (because) it rained]." This is plausible, but it means that
[335B] would have been divined on yiwei (day 32), for which there is no
independent confirmation on this or any other oracle bone. If Zhang is
right, then [335B] would be evidence of a ritual divined forty-one days in
advance.
Heji 1402f. The grammar of [319A-K], with particular attention to the
negative bu , the passive voice, and the particle yu F, is discussed by
Takashima 1973:84-87 as Bingbian 39 and 338; see further in Takashima
2010:II:197-82.
Many commentators have read oracle-bone as xian rather than
cheng (Y52.1; JGXS:224, etc.). Some have taken Xian to be an alternate
name for Tang (or ) (e.g., Zhang Bingquan's commentary to Bingbian
39, p. 69). Akatsuka (1977:321, 323, 555; cf. Rao Zongyi 1959:148), however,
has argued that the "Xian" of these inscriptions was an abbreviated refer-
ence to Xian Wu X, whom he would equate with the traditional figure
Wu Xian, "Shaman Xian." I believe, however, that the oracle-bone
form was either an engraver's error or, more probably, an alternate
form for X, Cheng. My arguments may be summarized as follows:
(1) As many scholars have noted, the kou ☐ element (written U) in
was easily confusable with the ding T element (written) in (e.g.,
Chen Mengjia 1956:411; Shima 1958:82-83; Zhang Bingquan, commentary
to Bingbian 39, p. 69).
(2) It would be anomalous to find Xian, a non-royal ancestor, included,
in [319], in what is otherwise a series of ancestors who were all on the
Shang main line of descent; if Xian is read as Cheng (= Da Yi, the dynasty
founder), the anomaly disappears.
(3) At least seven other inscriptions or inscription sets group Cheng
(or Da Yi or Tang), Da Jia, and Xia Yi, the ancestors recorded on [319]:
Heji 231, 300, 1369, 1403, 6947f, 14867, and, most importantly, Heji 248f.
This last pair of charges is crucial because it shows that cult could indeed
start with Cheng and end with Xia Yi, as it does in [319] if "Xian" is read
as equivalent to Cheng. Other inscriptions group Cheng and Da Jia (Heji
1244), Cheng and Xia Yi (Heji 1371), Tang (= Cheng), Da Jia, and Xia Yi
(Heji 300 [all from Period I]), Da Yi (= Cheng) and Xia Yi (Heji 27080 [Period
III-IV]; Heji 27147 [III]), and Da Yi (= Cheng) and Da Jia (Heji 27137 [III-
IV] and 27149 [III]). No other inscriptions group Xian Wu (see the
inscriptions at Y938.2) with these ancestors. It is true that a small number
of inscriptions do group "Xian" alone with Da Jia and Xia Yi (Heji 1403),
with Da Jia (Heji 1397), and with Xia Yi (Heji 1399). That the Cheng cases
greatly outnumber the Xian cases in such ritual contexts suggests that
Cheng (= Da Yi) was the intended or equivalent reading in all cases.
(4) The "hosting" rituals divined on Heji 1402f were to take place on an
Zyi day, a day that the Shang ritualists primarily reserved for the cult to
Yi-named ancestors like Cheng (= Da Yi), Xia Yi, and Father Yi (on temple
names, see, e.g., Ji Dewei 1989; Keightley 2004:12-16). There is no record
that Xian Wu (see the inscriptions at Y938.2) received cult on an yi day.
In short, whether one reads the graph in question as Cheng or Xian it is
likely that the referent was Da Yi (cf. Liu Huan 2005a:4-5).
Heji 5158B. For the possibility that wang (E in [187] had a ritual sense, see
the entry for wang E, "king," in the Glossary.
Heji 5603. It is uncertain whether Xiao Ji Chenin [185B] should
be taken as a title, "Junior Servitor for Cultivation," but this is the way
that many scholars have construed it. See, e.g., Hu Houxuan, "Preface"
to Xucun, 4-5; Chou Hung-hsiang 1968:269-70; Qiu Xigui 1989:35; Zhu
Qixiang 1999a:41-42. The charge [142], "It should be the Junior Servitor(s)
who order(s) the zhong to plant millet," supports the view that chen E,
"servitors" (see Glossary), were involved in royal agriculture. Shima
Kunio (1958:492), however, took the xiao /to be a verb, hence "Should
Wu (?) cry out, 'Reduce (the number of) Servitors for Cultivation."" Since
I know of no other divinations in which xiao was used verbally in the
administrative sense of "reduce," I find it reasonable to take Xiao Ji Chen,
like the Xiao chen/of [142], as a title.
Heji 5628. My reading of [18] follows that offered at S418.1 (for Xucun 1.70)
and MZ. The original rubbing, Xucun 1.70, is clearer than the Heji ver-
sion. Xiao Nan (1981:266; for Xucun 1.70) and the Y editors transcribe the
inscription as follows: E: "Making cracks on renchen
(day 29), divined: 'It should be (hui) Gong (a person) whom (We) order
to have charge of the Gong (the Artisans)."" It is possible, however, that
the charge could also be reconstructed as []I, *"It should be
Gong whom (We) order to have charge of [Our] Gong." Compare, as in
[17A],
], "His Majesty will order Shan to supervise Our
Gong."256

Heji 5760. I do not know how to translate the end of [74], which is frag-
mentary. The oracle-bone word (Y104.1) I have rendered as jian has also
been treated as jin, a comprehensive term for disaster (Chen Mengjia
et al.); as jian, "difficulty" (Luo Zhenyu et al.); as jin , "dearth" (Guo
Moruo et al); as a disaster word related to han, "dry, parched," and thus
to han, "to burn, roast." See the discussion at Shima 1958:192 and the
entries at Sōran, no. 1599. It is not usually taken as the name of a person.
It is possible, accordingly, that [74] had the sense of "(We) raise Archers,
100 of them, and order them to perform a burning ritual....(?)" I know
of no other case, however, in which 100 Archers or any other group of 100
officers were to perform a ritual and I cannot, unfortunately, make out the
two broken characters that follow the jian.
Heji 5769. The reading of [8AB] is clearer in the original rubbing (Yibian
7661) than it is in the Heji version.
Heji 6173. The characters in square brackets in [47] may be supplied from
the parallel inscriptions, Heji 6172 and 6174 (Y361.2), divined by Que on
the same day. I supply the injunctive "should" at the start of the charge
on the basis of a number of fragmentary wu gong, "(We) should not
raise...," charges, most notably: "(We) should not raise
ren to attack the Tu[fang]..." (Heji 6414);, "At the pres-
ent season His Majesty should not raise ren to march to regulate (zheng)
(some enemy)..." (Heji 7280); and, "We should raise
ren/We should not raise ren" (Heji 9811). I assume that such negative
charges would have originally been part of the postive-negative charge
pairs now represented only by their positive charge (as in the case of [47],
[49], [50], etc.).
1) the men raised in the North Gong

Heji 7294f. It is not entirely certain whether the gong of [63] should
be translated, as I have taken it, as a place-name or as the designation of
a group, the "Gong-officers." If one adopts the second reading, then the
charge would read: "Order that the Gong, (stationed in) the north, raise
(gong) men." This would provide a further instance in which Wu Ding
would have delegated the power to raise troops to his subordinates. Guo
Moruo, however, in his commentary to the inscription, when published as
Cuibian 1217, took the Gong to be a place-name (see too Chao Lin 1972:73)
I also incline to that view because of three other charges, such as [66],
which link the raising of men to an attack on the Gongfang (see too
Heji 6174 and 6178). It is plausible, accordingly, that Wu Ding's diviners
would have proposed: "Order that at North Gong (one) raise men."
Heji 7337. Inscription [48AB] has been published only as a photograph;
I follow the transcription offered by MZ, Heshi, and JGXS:908. Given the
fragmentary nature of the charge, I am uncertain about the meaning of
the final jian (xian?) in [48B]. In addition to "observe" it might also
have meant "to have audience"; thus, "Raising (deng) 1,000 (ren), (We) call
upon them to have audience with the king." See the range of definitions
for jian offered at Sōran, no. 1072; HYDZ:193; Zhang Guiguang 1999; Smith
2008:67.
Heji 7350f. There is no agreement as to what the modern equivalent of
the graph that I have transcribed as in [77]. Among the equivalences
and meanings proposed for this context are: fa 1, "attack," rao "to
give trouble," mie (=mie ), "to destroy," and wu it, "martial" (see
the entries at Sōran, no. 3965; Cao Dingyun 2007). The graph, a drawing
of a figure holding an implement or weapon behind its back, led Tsung-
tung Chang (1970:16, n. 1; 98, n. 1) to propose a meaning of xi, "make a
surprise attack"; this was quite properly challenged by Serruys (1974:19,
90-91), who discusses some of the alternatives.
The inscriptions on this scapula provide additional evidence for the
interchangeability of the mobilization verbs, deng and gong M. An
inscription on the right side of Heji 7350f reads: "Mak-
ing cracks on guisi (day 30), Que divined: '(We) raise (deng)....""; and on
the left side, reads: "Making cracks on jiawu (day
31), Xuan divined: '(We) raise (gong) the Horse (-chariot officers) and call
out, "Attack (?)...." Unfortunately, both inscriptions are incomplete so
it is impossible to tell whether the contexts were the same, impossible to
tell, in particular, if the subject of Que's divination was mobilization at all.
But at least this is a case in which, on one bone, Que was using deng and
Xuan, a day later, was using gong .
Heji 8659. The fragmentary and, perhaps, abbreviated nature of [80] makes
its meaning hard to determine with assurance. I supply the zhong in
parentheses on the basis of the thirty-three sang zhong, "lose zhong,"
charges recorded at Y520.1.
Heji 9535. Zheng Jiexiang (1994:26–27), citing this inscription among others,
proposes that the bei in [168] was not a direction, as I have tentatively
rendered it, but a place-name, Beit, which he locates 15k southeast of the
present Tangyin xian in Henan, ca. 23k south of Anyang. The charge
would thus read, "If (We) call out, 'Plant millet at Bei,' (We) will receive
harvest."258

Heji 9547. The graph which Yang Shengnan (1992:82) read as yi XII, "mows"
([137]), is not in S, Y, JGWD, or Sōran.
Heji 9552. For the place-names in [156] that I have tentatively read as Long
, You, and Xiang, see Sōran, nos. 1378, 0782, and 3193, respectively.
Zheng Jiexiang (1994:266) places Long to the northeast of Zhengzhou;
similarly (p. 260), he tentatively locates Xiang to the northwest of modern
Yanjin
xian in Henan, which is itself 70 km northeast of Zhengzhou.
Heji 9556. MZ 9556 give the "first moon"; Heshi gives the "eleventh moon."
Heji 9560. For the periodization of [284], see Qiu Xigui 1981:314. The offer-
ing of an exorcism ritual to the sun is unusual-such rituals were normally
offered to ancestors-but Qu Wanli (in his commentary to Jiabian 2121.8),
S407.1, and Y1094.2 all read the last graph as ri H, as do I; the rubbing is
clear. Qiu Xigui (1981:314), by contrast offers grounds for reading oracle-
bone as (= T), so that the exorcism ritual on Qin's behalf would
have been offered to a Ding ancestor, possibly Wu Ding. I do believe that
the Shang engravers on occasion wrote ☐ to record the word ri H, "day"
(Keightley 1997:517-24); it is possible that such graphic flexibility also
worked in the other direction, as Qiu is assuming. It is evident, in any
case, from an earlier divination on the plastron, divined on jiazi (day 1),
that Qin had been ill; the exorcism ritual-whomever it was addressed
to-would have been designed to assist his cure (see Qu Wanli's commen-
tary to Jiabian 2121.1 and 2121.8).
Heji 9607f. Qu Wanli, in his commentary to Jiakao 3001, understood the
charge of [183A],, to mean "Fu Jing will hunt at Huan." He did
not, however, know that the other half of the charge pair, [183B]
, had been inscribed on the same plastron (as rejoined at Heji 9607f);
the presence of the removes the possibility that the could have
been a place-name. The in these cases has to be verbal; cf. [180AB],
[182], and [184].
Heji 9610. I follow Kaizuka, in his commentary to Jimbun 277, in supply-
ing the missing bu in [180A]; also see JGXS:1164. The charge [183B]
, also divined on a plastron, provides some support for doing
so. It is possible that tian in these inscriptions-[180A], [183AB], [184]-
meant "to take to the field" and thus "to hunt."
Heji 9668f. Zhang Bingquan, in his transcription of Bingbian 169.5-6,
renders the opening phrase of the two charges as [*]() for [153A]
; also see
and (*) for [153B]; Heshi 9668f gives [] and
JGXS:1171. Both jin sui (e.g., [157], Heji 9646-50) and jinlai sui (e.g., Heji 9653-
55) appear in the inscriptions, with the first occuring far more commonly.
Neither S357.4 (for Yibian 7811) nor MZ supply the lai in [153AB]; I do not
see any certain trace of it in the rubbing. Its presence, in any event, does
not greatly affect the meaning of the time phrase. The lai that preceded
ganzhi dates indicated that the day date in question lay beyond the current
ten-day week (Handel 2004; see too Chang Yuzhi 1998:247-55; Keightley
2000:42, nn. 16, 17); whether it served a similar function-indicating, when
present, a "season" that lay further in the future-has not yet been tested.
It should also be noted that [153AB] is unusual in that the positive,
wished-for charge, [153A], which was presumably divined first, was
placed on the left side of the plastron. This was an exception to normal
practice, which placed the desired charge on the right (Keightley 1978:51,
n. 124). The Heshi editors, in fact, place the undesired [153B] ahead of the
desired [153A]; the MZ editors adopt the order I have followed here.
The reading of the place-name in the postface of [153B] as Duan is
tentative; see Sōran, no. 5010.
Heji 9741f. In [160] I follow Zhang Bingquan's commentary to Bingbian
332 and Heshi 9741f, in presenting the sequential order of charges selected
at [160A-H]; JGXS:1179-80 and Takashima (2010:1:545-46) both concur.
The MZ transcription, by contrast, transcribes the charges in the order
[GHCDEFAB]. The absence of any prefatory ganzhi dates makes it hard to
resolve the issue.
Heji 9934f. My translation of [189A] assumes that the hou was a chiefly
title, here used as generic term: "The Archer Lords will receive harvest."
The character that I read as hou, however, which is usually written with
the arrow pointing upward, is here inverted, with the arrow pointing
down. Shima (S367.1; 1958:433, 502) and Rao Zongyi (1959:515) read the
character as hou . Kaizuka, however (commentary to Jimbun 0792, n. 1),
suggested that the variant graph form represented an agricultural ritual,
linked to millet, that involved an arrow stuck into the ground to drive
away the evil spirits of the earth. Thus: "If His Majesty goes to the mil-
let planting and performs the hou ritual, (He) will receive millet harvest."
Characters, however, were commonly inverted in this way. Heji 401 and
Tunnan 2293 also have the hou inverted, but I cannot see any agricultural
ritual involved: see too Keightley 2006:189. The possibility exists, how-
ever, that hou, when used with no place-name prefix (see the Glossary
entry for hou), was the name of a particular leader. This is the way, for
example, in which Akatsuka (1977:589) appeared to take it for Yibian 4055.260
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 1: Inscription Glosses
261
Even if the charge is understood in this way, it would still provide an
instance of the Shang king divining about the harvest of one of his allies or
dependents. Another alternative would be to take [189AB] as Shu Hou
1, "the Lord of Shu"; this was the possibility raised in conversation with
Takashima, spring 2000, who took the yu Fas elliptic, with the wang E
omitted. That shu would have been used as both name and verb ("plant
millet") in the same charge, however, strikes me as unlikely; nor do the
inscriptions contain any cases of the putative non-elliptic form **.
Heji 10084. For the hai in the xinhai date of [147B], I follow S194.3 for Zhuihe
340, Qu Wanli's transcription of Jiakao 1691.4, Yan Yiping's drawing at
Zhuixin 12, and Heshi 10084.
Heji 10111. I am not certain of the sequence in which [150A-D] would
have been divined. My transcription follows the ordering given by Qu
Wanli in his commentary to Jiakao 2905 (= Heshi 10111) and JGXS:1223; this
indicates that the diviner Gu moved from left to right across the scapula,
progressing toward the right edge of the bone. This means, however, that
"the ninth moon" recorded in these charges would have covered at least
thirty-three days (inclusive count), from the dingyou (day 34) of [150A] to
the jisi (day 6) of [150D], possible evidence that the Shang had introduced
a second "ninth moon" as an in-year intercalary moon (e.g., Yang Sheng-
nan 1986:14-16; Chang Yuzhi 1998:307-18). Akatsuka (1977:548-49), by
contrast, lists the inscriptions starting with the divination on guihai (day
60); this reading would give us "ninth moon" dates covering thirty-five
days, i.e., from guihai to dingyou. MZ starts the series with the divination
on jiazi (day 1), which would give us "ninth moon" dates covering fifty-
four days, i.e., from jiazi to dingyou. It may also be noted that one of the
other charges on this scapula, divined by the same diviner, Gu, contained
the phrase Z, "On the coming yihai (day 12)," and was also dated
to the "ninth moon." The prefatory date of this divination has been lost,
but since lai was usually used to refer to dates beyond the current ten-day
week (Handel 2004:67-72; Keightley 2000:42, n. 17; see too Chang Yuzhi
1998:247-55), it is likely that it would have been divined on guiyou (day
10). If so, this would extend the "ninth moon," in the scenarios proposed
by Qu Wanli and Heshi, by at least another four days, i.e., from dingyou
to guiyou. Such a reading, however, would upset the smooth progression
from left to right posited above as the diviner, having reached the right
edge of the bone with his jisi divination, then jumped all the way back to
the left with the putative guiyou divination.
I should also point out, however, that the divination date for [150A],
which every other scholar (including Shima at S150.1) has read as dingyou
(day 34), ought possibly to be read as jiyou (day 46); the rubbing at least
permits that possibility. And if the date were jiyou then all the "ninth
moon" inscriptions would fit into a twenty-five-day period, from jiyou
(day 46) (not dingyou) to the putative guiyou (day 10), thus removing any
need for an in-year intercalary moon. We would still, of course, be faced
with the awkward fact that the diviner had jumped back to the left to
divine that final guiyou charge.
On whether the Shang graph for this Pre-Dynastic Ancestor should
be read Naoor Kui, see Y577.2-79.2; Sōran, nos. 0702, 0703; see also
Childs-Johnson 1998:55-56; Liu Yuanlin 1993; Keightley 2000:14, n. 18; Liu
Huan 2002:287–303.
Heji 10197. For a relatively recent study of [256], see Hu Houxian 1987.
Heji 10124b. What the last graph is in [176C] is problematic (Y262.2-63.2).
It may have been a region; it have been a sense of "harm" (Sōran, no. 3197;
see, in particular, JGWD:107-08).
Heji 10408f. Zhang Bingquan, in his transcription of Bingbian 102.1 ([202A])
reads the oracle-bone graph (Sōran, no. 4587) as ni, "fawn," rather than
tu, "rabbit"; Takashima (2010:I:493, II:435-36) concurs. I tentatively fol-
low Yao Xiaosui (1981:50) and MZ in taking the word in [202A] to be tu,
primarily because of the long ears depicted in the graph and the lack of a
neck. Heshi 10408f offers no modern transcription of the word. The rub-
bing for [202CD] is not clear; I generally follow the rubbing and transcrip-
tion provided for Bingbian 102.3-4 and the Heshi transcription. Cf. Zhu
Qixiang 1999a:143.
Heji 11484f. I am uncertain whether the prognostication, in
inscription [12A], should be translated as I have done in my text, "There
will be calamities, (but) (We) should not expect rain" (so that the calami-
ties the diviner feared might have included rain falling) or, "There will
be calamities; (one of which would be that) (We) not expect rain" (this
is the way Takashima 1973:161 takes it; cf. Takashima 2010:I:188; Xue-
shun Liu 2005:227). I prefer my translation for two reasons. First, I regard
this inscription as a validating display inscription (see Keightley 1975:13;
1978:46, n. 90; 1988:372-73; 1999:221, n. 25). That the Shang, as the verifica-
tion records, were able to perform their ritual on yiwei suggests that it did
not, in fact, rain, thus confirming the meaning I assign the prognostica-
tion. I see no reason why the king, divining about a ritual to be offered on
the yi day, would have accounted "no rain" a calamity; on the contrary,
the Shang preferred sunny weather for their rituals, many of which took262
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 1: Inscription Glosses
263
place outdoors (see, e.g., the verification for [335A] and Heji 21021; Keight-
ley 2000:25-30, 41). Second, I believe that the verification "in the night
of jiawu, the moon was eclipsed" records the lunar eclipse of 4 Novem-
ber 1198 (Keightley 1978:174, n. 19; 1982a:550-52; 1997b:1-4; Fan Yuzhou
1986:324, 333; Feng Shi 1990:24, 26; Zhang Peiyu 1999; 1999a:61; 2002:353).
Xueshun Liu (2005:212-13), by contrast, refuses to date it. Calamitous rain
would not have been likely at that time of year. I believe, accordingly,
that the king was merely excluding one type of calamity that might have
interfered with the rituals planned for Zu Yi ("but it will not rain"); he
was able to do so with some confidence because he was fairly certain that
rain would not fall at that point in the year. (Suetsugu [1991:243] gives
only three cases of rainfall in the eleventh moon in the oracle-bone inscrip-
tions.) Other datings for this eclipse have, of course, been offered; see, e.g.,
Dong Zuobin 1945:II:3:24b-26b; Dubs 1951:324, 331-32; 1953:104; Zhang
Bingquan, commentary to Bingbian 57, pp. 91-93; Keightley 1970:52-53
(gloss to Bingbian 57); Yan Yiping 1981.
With regard to the main ritual activity proposed in the charge, **,
Chow Kwok-ching (1982:246; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:269-70), discuss-
ing the inscription as Bingbian 57.1, notes that "when the type B verb
we can justifiably interpret it as
is placed in front of type A verb...,
'to you-sacrifice (in order to) perform a present-ritual."" If, however, Taka-
shima is correct in interpreting the you-ritual (see Glossary) as a "cutting"
ritual, its link to the presentation of millet is not clear. I do not, accord-
ingly, link the two rituals as Chow proposes.
The meaning of the last three characters in the verification of [12A] is
uncertain and probably unknowable. I follow Zhang Bingquan (transcrip-
tion of Bingbian 57.1) in reading them as shuai tiao qian, the Duo Gong
"all raised up the offering" (or, perhaps, "were all dispatched in an orderly
manner"), presumably to perform some ritual. Rao Zongyi (1959:246; cit-
ing a previous and unpublished [?] work), however, reads the last two
characters as chai qian, interpreting chai (on the basis of Jiyun)
as qi and qian as dian, hence, the Duo Gong "all raised up the offer-
ings (?)." That the shuai should be read as a short form of lü, "flesh
used in sacrifice," is supported by the existence of the phrase shuai mu
(Heji 32385) and shuai liao (Heji 34185), which appears in the bone
inscriptions, and may have had the sense of "offer in sacrifice the blood
and fat of a cow" (Yu Xingwu 1941:23b-24a; 1996:3181-84; Li Xiaoding
agrees [JGWZ:3907]). The possibility that the shuai was a loan for shuai
E, "to lead," should also not be excluded (Yu Xingwu:1941:24a). Some
scholars, to complicate matters further, have read the qianas qian,
"fault, error, blame" (e.g., Ikeda 1964, commentary to Houbian 2.3.10, citing
Wen Yiduo and Guo Moruo); Rao Zongyi (1959:246) links it to the gian
found in Zhouli (ZLZY 51:8b; Biot, tr., 1851:II:51), the ritual "sending off" of
a royal corpse. For my further inconclusive ruminations on the problems
presented by this inscription, see Keightley 1970:53-56. There is, in short,
no agreement on the meaning of these last three characters. Takashima
(1973:161) gave the "many gong-officers accordingly performed a gian sac-
rifice." Serruys (1986:209) gave "the many artisans following (or: being
led) will be... dispatched"; Keightley (1991:263) offered "the Duo Gong all
raised up the offerings." Takashima (2010:I:188 for Bingbian 57.1) is more
agnostic, offering only: "The Many Construction-workers all (or: lead-
ingly)?." As he notes (2010:II:216, n. 2, comment to Bingbian 57), "Any trans-
lation of the unusual phrase . . . cannot escape from being speculative."
Heji 11546 = [95A-C]. The crack numbers on this plastron are not always
easy to read. Qu Wanli (commentary to Jiakao 2106+2122) gives a crack
number of "2" for [95B]; Heshi, by contrast, gives a crack number of "3."
For [95C], Qu Wanli, Heshi, and JGXS:1385 all give a "2." I cannot see the
number on the rubbing; if it is "2," I would suspect, since every other crack
on the plastron was probably numbered "3," that "2" was an engraver's
error.
Heji 12883. The two characters for wo #, "us," in [311AB] are visible in the
rubbing of Yibian 4742 and are reproduced at S303.3 and at Heshi 12883;
they are unclear in the Heji rubbing and are omitted at Y829.1 and Zhu
Qixiang 1999a:70. The same pattern appears in [313AB].
Heji 13505. My transcription of [166A-H] varies in minor detail from that
offered by Y and JGXS:1599. I follow the original rubbing, Yibian 3212,
and the drawing provided by Zhou Hongxiang 1969:165. See too Zhong
Bosheng (1991a), who cautiously relates the ritual to the founding of a
settlement.
Heji 14407. My reading of inscription [210] is tentative. I follow Jin Zutong's
commentary to Yizhu 835 and Shima (1958:492; S180.1) in seeing the shui
k in the charge. (Heshi 14407 offers a more "exploded" transcription:
[N]; cf. JGXS:1714.) For another instance of the
phrase yu shuik, see Heji 10152, also divined on xinyou, the same day
as [210], and also on a plastron fragment; it is this inscription that encour-
ages me, as it evidently encouraged Jin Zutong, to link the yu and the
shui 7k in [210], even though there might have been space for a missing

present: k, "offer an exorcism about the great floods."264

Heji 14912 = [27]. I find the rubbing difficult to decipher; the original rub-
bing, Qianbian 7.38.2, is no clearer. I follow the readings provided by Ye
Yusen, MZ, and Y. The Heshi and JGXS:1776 transcription adds one or two

Heji 14915. My translation of the last character in [26], which I have tran-
scribed as ge, "dagger-axe," is uncertain (cf. JGXS:1776). I follow Ikeda
(1964: commentary to Houbian 1.22.1 and 2.8.9) in reading it as meaning
fa, "to attack." Lu Shixian (1969:1) read it as ge, "to cut, hack." Most
scholars, however, have not taken the word in that way (see Sōran no.
1503). A further problem is caused by the graph itself, whose bottom half
is missing. It is possible, accordingly, that the original Shang graph might
have been wu it, which one scholar, at least, Chen Weizhan (1987:61), has
taken to mean "attack" (Sōran no. 1510).
Heji 19441; JGXS:2245. Qu Wanli (Jiakao 1161), Chou Hung-hsiang (1968:
326) and Mickel (1976:93) supply the wo #, "Our," in [9]. Certainly a
graph seems to be missing before the gong I, but there are no certain
graphic clues as to what it might have been.
Heji 26888. Takashima (1988:663) offers a quite different translation of
[28AB] (as Yicun 5): "In attacking Wei (and) Pi, (we) shall deploy the royal
masses," etc. I respectively disagree with his reading of xu as fa (, bu
Tas pi E, and bo 4 as zai *. Cf. JGXS:3043.
Heji 26898. I supply the hu in inscription [29A] on the basis of the par-
allel charge [29B]. For other interpretations of the oracle-bone graph that
I have taken to be a variant of cha fi, "penetrate," see Sōran, no. 0892;
most of the meanings offered involve the sense of "attack." For what I now
regard as my previous mistranslation of this inscription (cited as Yesan
43.6+46.7), see Keightley 1969:78-79. My present translation is tentative. It
is conceivable that the initial clause in the charge, 王其呼眾戍插受人....
could be understood as "If His Majesty calls upon the zhong to guard Cha
and confers men..." (as proposed by Zhao Lin 1982:135), or "If His Maj-
esty calls upon the zhong, the Guards and the Penetrators (cha) to receive
men...," or even, "If His Majesty calls upon the Many (zhong) Guards to
penetrate the men of Shou...." But I know of no parallel usage to justify
taking shou ren as "confer or receive men"; nor are there any grounds
for thinking that the Guards might have been qualified by the adjective,
zhong, "many"; there are no references, for example, to the *Duo Shu
and *Zhong Shu X, "The Many Guards." A fragmentary version of
a similar inscription is recorded on another scapula, Tunnan 880 (Y71.1),
the commentary to which offers some additional speculations about its
meaning.
Heji 27826b. I am uncertain about the precise nature of the grain being
offered in inscription [59] (cf. JGXS:3127); the graph drawn by the engraver
is not easy to relate to other graphs. MZ and Heshi read it, as I have done,
as shu, "millet." For the possibilities involved, see the entries at Sōran,
nos. 0854, 0889, and the parallel entries at S198.2-4, Y366.2-67.1. I take the
final yiyou Zas part of the charge, rather than as an abbreviated veri-
fication ("On yiyou [we] presented the millet"); by analogy with [57], the
yiyou of [59] is best taken as an abbreviation of *.
Heji 28274 [327B]. MZ 628.2 gives Da Jia as the recipient for the har-
vest prayer. Heshi and JGXS:3166, by contrast, give Da Yi Z. The rub-
bing of the original, Jingjin 3895, is not clear.
Heji 28520 [252]. Serruys (1969, 1974) and Takashima (1973) have argued
that the word bu negated intransitive, passive verbs and actions not
under Shang control; Takashima (1988:127) has further argued that bu
appears in non-modal, stative/eventive contexts. The discussion suggests
that the phrase bu yu might most accurately be translated as "(We)
will not be being berained" or "(We) will not be being rained on." I avoid
the awkwardness of such constructions in my translation-and follow
Serruys (1974:28), who simply translates bu yu as "it will not rain" -but
their import should be kept in mind. See too Takashima 2010:I:46-50.
Heji 28904. I do not know what the graph is in [201C] (Y226.1; Sōran, no.
0439). Used as a verb, perhaps it meant "to clear the land"; it consisted of
a deer's head, a couple of sprouts, and "to beat" (cf. Jiakao 56; JGWZ:2043;
JGWD:340-41; Qiu Xigui 1988:126; 2000:190).
Heji 29687. See the gloss for Yingcang 2567.
Heji 30439. I have been unable to locate all of [117AB] on the plastron. I
rely upon the transcriptions offered by Qu Wanli in his commentary to
Jiakao 3916, MZ, Heshi, and by JGXS:3390. Shima, curiously, does not seem
to record the inscriptions in his concordance. I also rely upon Qu Wanli in
supplying the sense of "catch" between the she and the sigraphs. It
is possible that the she should be read causatively: "It should be the Horse-
(chariot) officers (and?) Ya who make the wild buffalo cross the river"; "It
should be the zhong who make the wild buffalo cross the river."266

Heji 30977. There is uncertainty about how the last part of inscription [58]
should be read and construed. Here I follow the reading of S408.1, Heshi,
and JGXS:3445. Y1096.1 errs in inserting a hui in the charge; that char-
acter, I believe, belongs to the fragmentary inscription further down the
bone. Guo Moruo in his commentary on the original rubbing, Cuibian
910, also added a mao (= liu; see Glossary under mao) after the yong
, which is certainly allowable; Zhang Bingquan (1968:212) also reads it
in this way. If one follows their transcription the translation would end:
"... two cattle should be sacrificed and split open." For a reading that
places the mao at the start of the second charge, see Takashima 1984:38.
Heji 31970. Takashima argues (1988:664, 676-78) that the "X Verb/
Verb" pattern is emphatic, so that a phrase like X 4 in inscription [246]
meant not just "have occasion to strike" but "certainly (or in fact) strike."
Heji 31973. I follow the transcription offered by Jimbun 2525, S503.1, and
MZ in reading the prefatory date of inscription [114] as dingchou T (day
14). The ding, however, is largely illegible, and Heshi 31973 and JGXS:3547
offers, instead, a date of jichou (day 26). A number of identical or
similar charges, all, like Heji 31973, recorded on scapulas, were divined
on dinghai (day 24; [234], Heji 31975), gengyin (day 27; Heji 31979, 31980),
and xinmao (day 28; Heji 31977); this would make a date of jichou (day 26)
plausible for Heji 31973. Similar charges, on scapulas, were also divined
on jiachen (day 41; Heji 31976) and, possibly related, yihai (day 12; [237B]),
so the case for the jichou dating is by no means certain.
Heji 31974. I supply the yi, "take," in [234] on the basis of the parallel
with [114], [235], and Heji 31973, 31975, and 31977. It would appear that
the engraver neglected to include the graph. (Keightley [2006:189] lists a
few other errors.) If the yi is not understood to be present, then the text
would mean, "If His Majesty orders Qin's zhong to penetrate and attack
the Shaofang, he will receive assistance" (JGXS:3547). Since there is no
other reference to Qin having his own zhong, this reading seems less likely.
Heji 31978. The commentary to Tunnan 38-identical to [213] and also
recorded on a large scapula fragment-takes the last word, zhi #, "man-
acle," as a noun, thus "protect against the prisoners (the manacled ones)
of the Shaofang." I prefer the translation that I have given: "they will seize
(zhi) them."
Heji 31984. The presence of a fragmentary charge, TT, "...to
the (Yellow) River, to the Yang (Mountain Power)... sacrifice," on the
same bone fragment as [87] (cited as Shiduo 1.410), led Akatsuka (1977:50)
to argue that these inscriptions show us the zhong (or zhongren) participat-
ing in a royal ritual, but this is uncertain. I follow Akatsuka and the MZ
editors in seeing the graphs ru shan in the charge; other scholars,
however, have read it as one character, perhaps a ritual; see Sōran, no.
5440, Heshi 31984, and the commentary to Tunnan 806, a similar charge.
Heji 31993 (= Tunnan Fu 3) = [283]. The editors of the Tunnan shiwen,
Heshi, and JGXS:3549 inserted a niu 4, "cow," after the Zu Ding T
in [283], which would support the view that two rituals were involved.
(By contrast, the MZ editors do not see the character in the rubbing.) An
inscription on Heji 32729 (= Tunnan Fu 5), excavated at the same time
as Heji 31993, reads: ZZ, “(We) perform an exor-
cism ritual to Father Yi (by using) a sheep, an exorcism ritual to Mother
Ren (by using) five piglets, (and to) Brother Yi (by using) a dog." The fact
that three offerings to three separate ancestors were combined in one
charge encourages me to take [283] in the same way. For another possible
translation of [283], see the Glossary entry for lu, "burn" (?).
For an introduction to the non-royal features of [283] and the inscrip-
tions on this and twenty other scapulas like it excavated ca. 160 m west of
the village of Xiaotun in December 1971, see Guo Moruo 1972.
The inscriptions on this scapula are unusual in several respects: the
engravers have not recorded the day-date of the divinations or the name
of the diviner; nor have they numbered the cracks; the ancestors them-
selves do not appear to be the usual kings and consorts who regularly
received ancestral sacrifices. These features suggest that the divinations
were performed by diviners other than those who normally divined the
king's affairs. The archaeological context and the affinities with other
diviner groups of inscription style and content suggest that these diviners
were probably active during the reign of Wu Ding (d. c. 1189 B.C.E.) or
slightly later (Keightley 1999c:185).¹
It is unlikely, for example, that the Zu Ding HT, to whom the exorcism
ritual of [283] was directed, was the fifteenth Shang king; I reach this con-
clusion in part because the Ancestress Gui (Bi Gui), who was associ-
ated with Zu Ding as a recipient of the exorcism ritual, was not one of Zu
Ding (K15)'s consorts (who were Ancestress Ji [Bi Ji ] and Ancestress
Geng [Bi Geng]; see Keightley 1999:59) who received cult in the rit-
ual cycle (see fig. 1). For further commentary, see Qiu Xigui 1972:44, who
notes that other inscriptions on the same scapula refer to exorcisms for the
mu (shepherds?) and chen (retainers), as well as for two individuals.
1. Yang Yuyan 2005 dates them to Period III.268

Heji 31996f.貞:(再)眾北至于東,“(We) [raise (cheng)] the zhong from (bi)
(?) the North and go to the East....”;其他西(?)再眾,“We will from (?)
the West (?) raise the zhong." My translations are speculative in each case.
The reading of xi, “west” is uncertain; cf. JGXS:3550. Xu Jinxiong, in his
Heshi to Menzies 2674, transcribes the graph as xi; the presence of "north"
and "east" in the other inscription on the scapula fragment lends cred-
ibility to this view. MZ and Heshi 31996f, however, merely transcribe the
Shang graph form, offering no modern equivalent (entries at Y393.2), and
presumably treat it as a name. Scholars offer a number of readings for the
graph at Sōran, no. 4879, none of which seem to suit the present context.
For the reading of the " in this inscription as cong M (the way in which
MZ and Heshi 31996f both take it), see the Glossary entry for bi L.
A third charge at the top of this scapula fragment indicates that the
Archer Penetrators (see the Glossary entry for cha ) may have been
involved in this campaign, but I cannot translate it with certainty.
Heji 32572f. The ancestral title Yi Zu Zin inscription [56] was presum-
ably a scribal transposition of Zu Yi, Ancestor Yi (K12); this is the way Guo
Moruo took it in his commentary to the original rubbing, Cuibian 908; Jin
Xiangheng 1965:2a agrees.
Heji 32829. No satisfactory modern graph has been proposed as the mod-
ern equivalent of oracle-bone ✓ (in [275] and [276A]). Hu Houxuan
(1944a:7b) treated it as si E. For the view that the word meant "bury" or
"encoffin," see Akatsuka 1977:257 and the commentary to Tunnan 2273. A
fragmentary but similar inscription appears on Jimbun 2437 (= Heji 32831).
See too zang in the Glossary.
Heji 32967. Xiao Nan (1981:266) transcribes inscription [16A] (as Shiduo
1.431) as : EI (the Heshi editors and JGXS:3666 agree);
S173.4 gives the charge as EI]; the MZ editors give it as E
I. I find the rubbing unclear at the critical point, but believe that
some version of the following translation is justified: "On jiyou (day 46)
divined: 'His Majesty will order Shan to supervise [Our] Gong."" It should
also be noted that modern scholars do not always find it easy to distin-
guish the oracle-bone graphs for shan I and huo X; see, e.g., JGWZ:2913,
3140-41; Sōran, no. 1197.
Heji 33209. I am uncertain about the sequence of the [192A-G] divina-
tions. My transcription follows the charges as they move up the scapula
fragment. This sequence, however, requires a fifty-six-day interval (inclu-
sive count) between the wuchen (day 5) divination of [192D] and the guihai
(day 60) divination of [192E]. It is possible, however, that the placement
of the inscriptions on this bone was irregular and that the diviners first
addressed the topic of opening up fields (in [192A]), addressed the role of
the Duo Yin (in [192BC]), and the suitability of a generic prayer for grain
to Shang Jia (in [192E])-all on day 60; on yichou (day 2) they returned
to the question of opening up fields; and on wuchen (day 5), they further
specified that the prayer for grain would involve a burnt offering. Serruys
(1974:38-40), who followed the order given at [192A-F], also considered
the divinatory scenario involved, but did not address the time gap that
was implied. Cf. JGXS:3698.
Kaizuka, in commenting on this bone as Jimbun 2363, had proposed
an exploded rejoining that linked it to inscriptions on three other scapula
fragments, Cuibian 1221, 1223, and 1544, that deal with similar topics (see
Jimbun: Hombun hen, 578, fig. 92). Serruys (1974:40), however, found this
"hardly convincing," noting dissimilarities in the graph forms used; Yan
Yiping (Zhuixin, "Ding'e T," 2) likewise rejected the rejoining, conclud-
ing that the fragments come from different bones.
Heji 33347. There is disagreement about the reading of [209]. The MZ tran-
scription does not include the yi, "one," before the niu ; and it does
not include the wang shou you in the charge. Guo Moruo's drawing
of the inscription (as Cuibian 148) includes the yi, as does the transcription
offered by Tsung-tung Chang 1970:123, Heshi, and JGXS:3716. Shima's
transcription (1958:492) of Cuibian 148 reads the yi as er, "two" (as does
Itō 1961:269); Shima (S123.2) also includes the wang shou you (as do Tsung-
tung Chang and Heshi, but not Itō). I am tempted by the er, because that
would indicate that one cow each had been offered to Shang Jia and to Da
Yi; the rubbing, however, inclines me to an yi.
Heji 34058. The reading of the last graph in [316A] is uncertain. S270.4 (Jia-
bian 717) and MZ read it as shi +. Heshi 34058, however, reads it as yu F.
And Qu Wanli (commentary to Jiabian 717) ended the charge: ... T
. I agree with the scholars who see the gao (?) as part of a third charge
on the scapula fragment (e.g., the MZ and Heshi editors), not part of [316A].
Since I see no horizontal stroke in the last graph, I am tempted by the read-
ing of shi+. This would be supported by an inscription like [317] in which
the ji zong phrase was followed by a numeral and a type of victim. At the
same time, however, the frequency of the pattern "a Power" -seen
in [315A], [318C], and [334AB] (and in Heji 34062)—persuades me that the
reading may be equally probable.

Heji 34482 [19]. I follow Shima (S417.4), Akatsuka (1977:500), Heshi, and
JGXS:3849 in reading the title in Xucun 1.1831 (= [19]) as Di Gong . The
MZ editors, however, read it as Di Xiao Gong E, "Di's Lesser Gong."
It is true that a row of four small dots does appear between the and the
. But xiao in the bone inscriptions was written with three dots, not
four, and the dots used to write xiao were never all in a line (see JGWB
no. 0061; Sōran, nos. 0061-62). A similar problem appears in the case of
[20], presumably divined on the same occasion. Here too the line of four
small dots appears above the 4, suggesting that they were not adventi-
tious. But it is significant that the character for xiao / also appears in the
same charge (in the phrase xiao lao), and that there the xiao is written
with three small dots in a row, not four. That no reference to Xiao Gong
appears elsewhere in the inscriptions also weakens the likelihood that this
should be the reading here. I am at a loss as to how the four small dots,
which seem to form part of the gong = graph, should be taken. Presum-
ably they served to embellish the graph, but for what purpose is not clear.
(In another inscription in Heji 34157, Zhu Qixiang [1999a:127] reads the
graph as+as equivalent to Di . Y772.1 and Heshi, however, read it
Xiao Di 小帝.)
Heji 35344. Unfortunately, both the transcription and translation of [220A]
are problematic, and the reference to the "Men of the Right," may be illu-
sory. The rubbing [220A] was originally published as Jiabian 2562; my
transcription follows, in its structure if not in its details, that provided
by Qu Wanli in his commentary and by Heshi. A number of other schol-
ars have followed Qu's version; see, e.g., Lefeuvre (1976-78:51) and Yan
Yiping (1978:736), who places the inscription within the context of the
Period V Renfang campaigns and who argues that Zhonglu was the name
of the Pyromancer (buren ^) rather than the diviner; see too Zhang
Bingquan (commentary to Bingbian 381, p. 446); Kolb (1991:72); Yang
Yuyan 2005:466; JGXS, no. 35344. I cannot, however, find the charge as Qu
Wanli (or Heshi) transcribed it in either the Jiakao or Heji rubbings of this
scapula fragment. Shima did not record this version of the charge in his
concordance (e.g., S99.2-3); at S492.2 he provided only: T
以口. MZ gives丁酉在兮貞:其以 but also transcribes ☑亥卜在☑日口,
which appears nowhere in Qu Wanli's transcription. Given these uncer-
tainties, and until confirmed by independent examination of the plastron,
the reading offered at [220A] is problematic.
Heji 35406. I take the characters for inscription [306] from Chang Yuzhi
1987:50, from Heji, and from my own reading of the Cuibian 1113 rub-
bing; cf. JGXS:3959. This reading differs at several points from that offered
by MZ (which labels the text a “practice inscription" [xi ke]). Chang
Yuzhi claims that Dong Zuobin, Chen Mengjia, and Shima agree with her
reconstruction.
Heji 36518. My translation of this charge is speculative:
[286]
乙巳王貞: 啟呼祝日盂方[人] 其出伐,師高其令東會[
于] 高弗悔不詳(?) 4.王即曰:[]
On yisi (day 42) His Majesty divined: "Qi (?) called upon
the Invocator (zhu) (?), saying: 'The Yufang² have raised
(gong) [men]...; they will go out to attack (fa) Tun Shi Gao
(Gao of the Tun army?); he will order an eastern meeting
[at]... Gao, there will be no regrets, (the Yufang?) will not
specifically (xiang) strike (us?)." His Majesty read the cracks
and said: "[Auspicious]." (V. Huang) (Heji 36518)
Unfortunately, this unusually long charge is fragmentary and its meaning
is not certain, but it would suggest that the Shang of Period V understood
that other Bronze Age statelets, like the Yufang, were also capable of mus-
tering their human resources as the Shang did. This is reasonable, given
the ability of many of these groups to organize their own regional bronze
industries.³
Another possible translation, however, equally speculative, would be:
"(We) start to call out the prayer, saying: 'As to the [men] (we have) raised
against the Yufang..., they will go out to attack Tun Shi Gao; he will order
an eastern meeting [at] ... Gao, there will be no regrets, (the Yufang?) will
not specifically strike (us?)." The critical question is: does the phrase
MA] refer to the Yufang raising men (as in my translation of [286]), or
does it refer to the men raised against the Yufang. My justification for the
second reading would be the divinatory formulas, shou X fang you X
X, which do not mean "receive the assistance of the X-fang," but "receive
assistance (against) the X-fang," as in [287A]:
2. Zheng Jiexiang (1994:218-19) follows Guo Moruo (commentary to Tongzuan 581 and
582) in situating the Yufang to the south of the Yellow River, northwest of Suixian
in Henan, some 200 k south-southeast of modern Anyang. Hayashi Taisuke ***, by
contrast, had placed it in central Shanxi (see his map, reproduced at Matsumaru 1963:110,
fig. 15); similarly, Shima (1958:374, 382, 423) located it some 400 k to the north of Anyang.
Shaughnessy (1989:9-13) locates Yufang to the west of Anyang. Zhong Bosheng (1989:78-82)
reviews the evidence, and places Yu in Hebei, southeast of Puyang, but places the
Yufang in south Shanxi (1989:182–87).
3. Bagley 1999:136-39, 208-231. As he concludes (p. 229), it is now possible to glimpse
"complex societies scattered over a large part of north and central China," all belonging "to
the latter part of the second millennium." See too Keightley 2000:122-23.272

.."(If) His Majesty attack the Tufang, (he) will receive
abundant assistance (shou you you)." (I. Bin) (Heji 6431)
These and similar charges (e.g., Heji 8478, 8479, 8481, 8501-05, 8511-13,
20613, etc.) were not about receiving assistance from the Fang, which were
normally hostile to the Shang; they were about receiving divine assistance
from the Powers in their campaigns against the Fang. But I cannot be cer-
tain which of my conclusions is correct.
Heji 39483. I am uncertain how to translate the conclusion of the charge of
[160], ID, which I have tentatively rendered as "Take the Gong
to cut fodder and take (foragers?)." It is also possible that the chuwas
a noun (see Glossary), which would yield another tentative translation:
"Take the Gong and the Foragers (chu) and take (cutters?)." Or even, "Take
the Foragers (chu) of Gong and take (them)." For a charge that would sup-
port this reading, see Heji 98: ., "Hou will take the forag-
ers of Gu.' (Prognostication:) He really did take (them)." In many of these
proposals, the lack of any object after the final yi in [16C] is puzzling.
Other charges, such as [134] E, "His Majesty will go, taking
the zhong, to plant millet in Jiong," further indicates that the yi was used in
contexts of agricultural labor.
Heji 39830 [78]. My transcription follows that offered at S95.1 (for Ninghu
3.71) and Heshi, rather than that offered by MZ, which ignores the broken
cheng稱.
Tunnan 580. My translation of inscription [281] is tentative; for other inter-
pretations, see the Tunnan commentary. A related charge on the same scap-
ula,, may be translated: "If Ya Qin continues (to go?) he will
not arrive on (?) the geng (day)." The precise thrust of these divinations is
hard to grasp, but evidently the geng-day timing may be important.
Tunnan 722. For scholarship on the place-name in inscription [203B], see
Sōran, no. 0648. For the reading of Yuan for the place-name in [203AC],
which is not adopted by the Tunnan commentary, see Sōran, nos. 1283,
4762, and the MZ transcription.
Tunnan 2350. I find the transcription of inscription [239], taken from MZ,
preferable to that offered by the Tunnan heshi, which differs by reading the
end of the inscription differently; cf. JGXS:6255. The rubbing is not easy
to decipher, but the zai jiu E, I believe, belongs to a separate inscrip-
tion unit. The differences in transcription, however, do not affect the point
at issue, namely that the zhong in this case were to act in concert with the
right and left lü-the left lü would probably have been recorded on the
missing fragment-a point that the Tunnan heshi explicitly comments on
(as does Xiao Nan 1981a:126; cf. Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan, eds.,
1999:480).
Tunnan 2707. In inscriptions [94A-C], I follow the detailed argument of
Chao Fulin (1989:161) in reading the oracle-bone graph = (which some
scholars have read as qi Z; see the Glossary), as a form of zhong,
used in contexts where it stood in contrast to the other temporal qualifiers,
shang and xia F. Y397.1 and MZ read it as Xia Shi (see Glossary).
I supply the day dates in the prefaces of the inscriptions on this scapula
on the following basis:
(1) [94A]: since [94C] was divined on bingchen (day 53), and since [94A]
concerned cult from Shang Jia onward and was divined in the temple of
Da Jia, I look for the jia day prior to bingchen, which is jiayin (day 51).
Such a dating is supported by Heji 32329f (Y149.1), another Period I-II (Li)
charge that, like [94A], refers to a Great Exorcism to the ancestors from
Shang Jia onward to be performed on jiayin. I would also note that Heji
34013 (I, Shi-Li), a Great Exorcism charge nearly identical to that of [94A],
was recorded on jiachen (day 41) (cf. Heji 32330 [I-II, Li]; both these Period
I-II inscriptions are recorded at Y149.1); this lends credence to the view
that such charges were divined on jia days. The jiayin date also receives
support from my dating of [94B].
(2) [94B]: Since a mao is visible as part of the date, and since [94C]
was divined on bingchen (day 53), I assume that [94B] was divined on
yimao (day 52).
(3) [94E]: Disagreeing with the Tunnan commentary, I follow the origi-
nal report (Kaogu 1975:42) on H103:18+20 in placing the broken stem graph
and the prefatory zhen with [94E] rather than [94D]. I believe this is sup-
ported by both the handwriting of the inscription and the day date I posit
for it. I believe that the broken stem can be read as mao; if so the day date
was probably dingmao (day 4), appropriate for a divination performed in
the temple of Father Ding.
(4) [94F]: I assume that this was divined on the same day as [94E]. That
Heji 32330, whose content was similar, was divined on dingwei (day 44),
offers grounds for thinking that such charges were cracked on a ding day.274
Working for His Majesty
Tunnan 3038. My transcription of inscription [91] depends on those offered
by MZ, Tunnan shiwen, and JGXS:6332. I find much of the rubbing to be
illegible.
Tunnan 4029. My version of inscription [106] follows the transcription
offered by the Tunnan shiwen, Y624.2, JGXS:6422. That reading, however,
is far from certain. Given the fragmentary nature of this inscription, it is
possible that the "Many Horse(-chariot officers)" were to order the zhong-
ren, not just the zhong. Furthermore, the presence of a partial graph for
what was probably A (wang E), "king," to the right of the inscription,
and quite possibly part of the same charge, further complicates the mat-
ter. The meaning of the inscription is problematic; but it does associate the
zhong and the officers. The charges [107AB] may provide further evidence
of such a connection.
Yingcang 150=[223]. The introductory matter to Yingcang ("Qianyan,"
p. 5; "Introduction," pp. 9-10) corrects a mistake in previous transcrip-
tions (as per Dong Zuobin 1945:II.9:39a; Chen Mengjia 1956:276; Luo Kun
1991:412). A Qiang * is not included as the object of Fu Hao's attack. Prob-
ably [223] and [228] are related: both divined on xinsi, both the diviner
Zheng presiding, and both related to Fu Hao's raising men.
Yingcang 2567. The two inscriptions apparently about bronze casting, [5]
and [6], are frequently treated together. See, in particular, Yan Yun 1973;
Wang Yuxin 1981:156-57; Qiu Xigui 1986:203, who remarks on the similar-
ity of the Shang and Western Zhou bronze form for tong, "bronze" (JWGL
14, no. 1744); Lu Liancheng 1996:142-43; Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan,
eds., 1999:570. Sōran would support the meaning given: nos. 0552, 0579,
0645,0843,1586, 1615, 1718.
APPENDIX TWO
Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
For many years, the standard (although increasingly dated) source for
identifying Shang graphs had been Li Xiaoding's JGWZ (introduced at
Keightley 1977; 1978:60). Its reproduction of numerous scholarly discus-
sions remains invaluable, and in this Glossary I generally avoid repeating
what has already been discussed there. A more recent survey of scholarly
opinion, which provides citations to a more extensive range of opinions,
is Matsumaru and Takashima's Sōran (reviewed at Keightley 1997:513-
17), "interpreted by 471 scholars around the world in their publications
from 1904 through 1988 (including a few that appeared in 1989)." I gener-
ally give only the main reference; subsidiary references can be consulted
in the index: Sōran: 648–68, 686–718. JGWD, JGWJ, Ma Rusen 1993, and
Yu Xingwu 1996 may also be consulted as a guide to many of the issues
involved.
The entries herein address the usage of the words as they appear in
certain inscriptions translated in this book; the entries are not intended to
serve as a comprehensive account of all Shang uses of a word. The English
translations offered are sometimes "functional," in the sense that they are
based on a contextual understanding of the words in question rather than
precise philological analysis. The resulting imprecision, as evident, for
example, in the "disaster" words-hai (= hai ), huo, jiu, you £,
zai and 1-obscures our precise understanding of Shang theology
(but see the Glossary entries).
The entries are arranged alphabetically (by modern pronunciation)
and, when romanizations are identical, by the order of the traditional rad-
icals. The first modern character in each entry generally represents the
modern word(s), that in my view, best represents, graphically and seman-
tically, the Shang word.
1. As Mickel (1976:63) noted, "very few persons have attempted to discern differences
among the disaster graphs, and circular definitions . . . have been the rule."276
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
277
*
*
*
*
bi (Y59.1; Soran, nos. 1024-26, 1028). For why I now read oracle-bone >>
as bi L, "ally with, join with," rather than cong M (= 1, "follow" [Keight-
ley 1978:77-78]), and for how these two graphs may be distinguished in
the inscriptions, see Lin Yun 1981:69-74; see too Gao Ming 1980:11, 28. For
a contrary view, see Takashima 1990:57, 82, n. 37. The graphs were close
in meaning. I would also note that, in some cases, such as *Heji 31996f,
it does make better sense to read the form as cong rather than bi. It is
not certain, accordingly, that the Shang engravers observed the distinction
between the two words consistently.
Bi: "Ancestress" (Y1434.1-48.2; Sōran, nos. 1024, 1028; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 0002). More precisely, the temple name Bi Gan F meant "(deceased)
'Mother' Gan," with "Mother" referring to the female ascendants of
the mother's or more distant generations. According to Chen Mengjia
(1956:379), the term referred to consorts two generations or more removed.
Thus a Period V reference to tz, "Da Yi's consort, Mother Bing"
(see shi in Glossary) referred to a "Mother" sixteen generations distant.
On the use of the term Bi, and the role of the royal consorts, both alive and
dead, see Keightley 1982c:17-19; 1999:30–46.
bin (; on the graphic distinction, see bin yu): "to host" (Y766.2-70.1,
771.1; Sōran, nos. 0795, 0917, 0937, 0994, 1438. See too Yu Xingwu 1996,
nos. 2065, 2066; Lefeuvre 1997:347-51; 2001). That the bin recorded in the
oracle bones was a ritual in which the Shang king hosted his deceased
ancestors is indicated by the etymology of the word itself, by Shang usage
(useful discussion at Kwok-ching Chow 1982:355-58; cf. Zhou Guozheng
1983:298-99; Takashima 2002:116), and by Zhou evidence. HYDZ:524, 525,
546, etc., give "present himself" at a sacrifice. As a minister of Song
reported to have said in 641 B.C. (Zuozhuan, Xi 19, para. 4),
(2) The bin graph used in the bin yu formula was in every case writ-
ten with a "roof" and an inverted zhi IE, "to stop, to rest in" (= 1) (e.g.,
JGWD:703):
[288A] 父乙賓于祖乙
"Father Yi will be hosted by Zu Yi."
[288B] 父乙不賓于祖乙
"Father Yi will not be hosted by Zu Yi." (I. Bin) (Heji 1657)
When the king was the subject of bin, however, and the bin was not fol-
lowed by yu, the graph was almost invariably written with the additional
foot element, 2 as in:
[289A] 乙亥卜行貞:王室小乙脅亡尤. 在十一月
Crack-making on yihai (day 12), Xing divined: "His Majesty
hosts (bin) Xiao Yi (K21) and performs the xie (xie) ritual; he
will have no fault (you)." In the eleventh moon.³
[289B] 己卯卜行貞:王室兄己脅[亡尤]
Crack-making on jimao (day 16), Xing divined: "His Majesty
hosts Brother Ji and performs the xie ritual; [he will have no
fault].4
[289C] [庚辰]卜行[貞:王]室兄庚[吾]亡尤
is
.
[290]
, "Sacrifices and prayers are carried out for the sake of human
beings. The people act as hosts to the gods" (Watson, tr., 1989:208). How
this worked in the Shang case is partly explained in the following entry.
bin yu F. I translate the formula "A bin yu B" (AFB) in the passive
voice, "A is hosted by B," for the following reasons (cf. Takashima 2002a):
(1) The preposition yu only appears after bin when A is the king
or another living officiant. Conversely, if A and B are both ancestral spirits
then the bin never appears without the yu. The rigor of this post-bin dis-
tinction, between contexts without yu, in which the living host the dead,
and contexts with yu, in which one Power hosts another, suggests that bin
and bin yu should be translated differently.
Crack-making [on gengchen (day 17),] Xing [divined: "His
Majesty] hosts Brother Geng [and performs the xie ritual;]
he will have no fault."5 (II. Chu) (Heji 23120)

Crack-making on dingsi (day 54), divined: "His Majesty will
host Fourth Ancestor Ding (K15) and perform the xie-day
ritual; he will have no fault." (V. Huang) (Heji 35713)

3. See also Zhu Qixiang 1999a:92-93.
4. Brother Ji was a reference to another son of Wu Ding, who did not come to the throne,
but who appears in the ritual cycle and Shiji as Ancestor Ji; see Keightley 1978:206 and n. ae.
5. Brother Geng was Ancestor Geng (K22), the deceased elder brother of Ancestor Jia; see
Keightley 1978:206, n. ag.

By Period V such matching had become entirely routine:
[291] 戊辰卜貞:王室大祭亡尤
Crack-making on wuchen (day 5), divined: "His Majesty
hosts (bin) Da Wu (K7) and performs the ji ritual; he will

The It foot element presumably had semantic significance; it may be that,
following Guo Moruo's argument (in his commentary to Tongzuan 39),
the forms should be read in the sense of bin, which Shuowen defines
as "to lead, to guide" (bin dao ye ); in these cases, the Shang king
was "hosting" the spirits by guiding them with, leading them to, his offer-
ings. The foot-less forms in the bin yu formula should be read as bin
,"guest," and when used as a verb, "to be guested," and thus, "to be
hosted." I indicate this distinction in the following and elsewhere in this
book by using (to indicate the Shang graph without the IE), "to be
hosted [by]," and (to indicate the Shang graph with the IE), "to host."
(3) As Takashima (1973:84) has pointed out, the bin yu cases in the
divination charges are negated only with bu ; to Takashima and also
Serruys (1969; 1981:341-42), the bu is an indicator of intransitive/passive
verbs (references at Keightley 1978:70, n. 63). Takashima accordingly
translates Bingbian 39.4-5 (= 319DE),大甲賓于咸/大甲不賓于咸, as “Da
Jia will be treated as guest (in rank) with Xian/Da Jia will not be treated as
guest (in rank) with Xian"; Serruys (1981:335) gives, "Da Jia will be made
guest to Xian (or: Cheng)." Tsung-tung Chang 1970:227, n. 2, and Akatsuka
1977:316 also take bin yu as passive. I would translate this charge as, "Da
Jia will be hosted by Cheng." These cases may be contrasted with those
in which the fuor wu negatives were employed: /, "Yue
will host (some spirit)"/ "(Yue) may not be able to host (some spirit)" (I.
Bin; Yingcang 1152); and FZZ, "To Father Yi (His Maj-
esty) should serve as host"/ "To Father Yi (His Majesty) should not serve
as host" (I. Bin; Heji 2273f).
bo (?) (oracle-bone 4): "strike, seize" (Y905.1-11.1). I follow Zhang
Zhenglang (1981) in distinguishing oracle-bone 4 (Y905.1) from oracle-
bone (see the Glossary entry for "zai "). The two words were con-
flated in the 1934 edition of Jiaguwen bian but were separated in the 1965
was regularized by the Chudiviner group; for an introduction to the scholarship on the
five rituals, see Keightley 1978:115, n. 102; 2004:20-26; Chang Yuzhi 1987; Itō and Takashima
1996:92-104. For the meaning of "day ritual," as in xie ri, "the xie-day ritual," see the
Glossary entry for ri.
7. The ji
ritual was another one of the five major rituals.
edition. JGWZ:3777 also conflates them, as does Sōran, no. 1508. The
graphic distinction between the two words, however, is clear. Zhang
Zhenglang (1981:136, 139-40) suggests the reading of bo for 4 with con-
siderable uncertainty. Analysis of oracle-bone usage (the word appears
no less than a hundred times), together with related graph forms found in
bronze inscriptions (three cases), permits Zhang to conclude that I was
a transitive verb (as in [28B], [29AB]) and that, in the divination charges
about warfare that contained more than one verb, it followed and did not
precede other verbs of attack, such as fa (q.v.). This suggests, therefore,
that concerned the results, rather than the initial steps, in a campaign.
Takashima (1973:110-11; 1988:664) gives "harm," "strike," and a "dam-
aging blow," and Serruys (1974:25-26) gives "destroy" for 4, but their
transcriptions do not distinguish 4 from 4. HYDZ:310, 311, 322 give
"smite."
bu "to make (pyromantic) cracks; a crack" (Y1319.2-23.2; Sōran, no.
0454). As Shuowen defined the word,, "bu means to burn
and cut the turtle shell." Takashima's "discovery" (quoted at Keightley
1978:21, n. 93) that a scapula cracked over a charcoal fire cracks with the
sound of "pak" (Schuessler 1987:47: "puk") led him to note that the Shang
word, reconstructed as *puk, was onomatopoetic. The "pukking" of the
shell or bone was, I believe, precisely "what His Majesty heard" (Keightley
1991:106-14; 2008) as he assessed the pyromantic responses of the divinee,
the Power or ancestral spirit so addressed. For other scholarship on bu,
see, e.g., Dong Zuobin 1929:107-09 (reprint, 1967:1:57-59); Qu Runmin
1983:164, 169; Zhang Bingquan, commentary to Bingbian 381, pp. 446-47;
Lefeuvre in Faguo:294-95. Takashima (1988-89:14) takes bu as intransitive.
In some later texts, bu also had the sense of "predict." We find two
examples of this usage in Shi: "The (dead) lords say:
'We predict (bu) for you a myriad years of life, without limit""; and
, "the spirits enjoy the wine and food; they predict (bu) for you
a hundred blessings." This further supports the view that many Shang
charges served as wishes and prayers.
ce: "pledge" (in sacrificial contexts) (Tsung-tung Chang 1970:70; Y1132.2-
36.1; Sōran, no. 0585; HYDZ:63 gives "a ce-sacrifice of recording"; HYDZ:
104 give a "record on bamboo strips"; see too cheng [ce] and Smith 2008:
8. Mao no. 166; Karlgren, tr., 1950a:110, para. 4. Legge, tr., 1871:257 gives, "We give to thee/
Myriads of years." Italics added in both text and footnote.
9. Mao no. 209; Karlgren, tr., 1950a:163, para. 4. Legge, tr., 1871:371, gives, "They confer
upon you a hundred blessings." Italics added in both text and footnote.280
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
