281
155-67, 386, 387). The Shang graph may have depicted wooden writing
slips over a mouth or (with Shirakawa 1955:14; JGWZ:1606) placed in a
box; but see Harper 1985:472-73; Venture 2002a:53, n. 107. On the basis of
Shang usage I regard ce, when it took a sacrificial victim as its object, as a
verb of cult, recording a ritual in which large or important sacrifices were
made to the Powers "on account," with the actual offering either (i) to be
made after the ritualist's prayer had been answered, or (ii) consisting only
of a "paper" pledge. It was a spiritual cost accounting; it was recorded
but not necessarily paid unless the results were satisfactory. (It was used
less frequently as a noun; see Liu Xinfang 1990:41). Thus, in the following
typical case:
[292]
貞:禦于父乙斲三牛世三十伐三十宰
Divined: "In making exorcism to Father Yi (K20), (we) will
cleave (zhuo) three cows and pledge (ce) thirty dismem-
bered victims (fa) and thirty penned sheep (lao)." (I. Bin)
(Heji 886)
The (ce)-"on account" model is used for niu +, yang #, or lao #, or more
rarely in human sacrifice: e.g., [292], Heji 1027, 6664f, 32183, and Yingcang
1977 (Y1133.1-35.1). The model assumes that if the exorcism worked (i.e.,
if, for example, the living human who was being "defended" in this way
recovered), the king would actually offer the large numbers of humans
and animals he had pledged; in the mean time, he offered only three cows
([292]). As Shirakawa (1955:8-9) originally noted, the discrepancy between
the small number of victims offered in other ways and the large number
offered in the ce ritual is striking. Heji 1027f wants to pledge 1,000 cows
and a 1,000 ren and:
[293] 卜爭貞:百羊百牛百豕縠五十
Crack-making on..., Zheng divined: "In making a burnt
offering, (we) pledge one hundred sheep, one hundred
cows, one hundred pigs, and piglets (hu), fifty." (I. Bin)
(Yingcang 1256)
A particularly revealing case is:
[294A] 其四十牢又羌
"(We) will pledge ten penned cows (lao) and a Qiang."
[294B] 二十牢又羌
"[We will pledge] twenty penned cows and a Qiang."
[294C] 三十牢又羌
"[We will pledge] thirty penned cows and a Qiang." (III-IV.
Nameless-Diviner) (Heji 26936)
乙
My interpretation follows from evidence such as this and the argu-
ments and translations of Shirakawa 1955:6-18; 1971a:19a; Zhang Bing-
quan 1968:224-25; Tsung-tung Chang 1970:70 ("offer a vow"); Taiping
Chang 1981:368-76 (the "Record-Presentation Ceremony"); Serruys 1986:
221 ("not necessarily actual offerings, but rather promises"); Yan Yiping
1980a:14; Schuessler 1987:55 ("pledge in writing to the spirits"); Lewis
1999:17 and n. 10; HYDZ:104, 154, 155 ("record on bamboo strips").
Noting the Shuowen definition,, “ce is to report," many scholars
(see Sōran, no. 0585) have argued that the ce ritual involved a report to
the spirits, a meaning that is compatible with the idea of "pledge" pro-
posed earlier, e.g., Shima 1958:388; JGWZ:1606; and Akatsuka's sugges-
tion (1977:242) that the ce was a text that accompanied, as it recorded, the
offering of large numbers of sacrificial victims (cf. Serruys 1974:102, n.
24: "make announcements by written document"; Liu Xinfang 1990:40:
"make a ritual report on strips").
Yu Xingwu (1979:172-74; see too 1996:2964-69), by contrast, argued
that the Shang graph was close in sound and meaning to shan and kan
FJ, "cut" (a conclusion also accepted by JGWD:499), and he concluded
that the Shang word indicated the way in which the ritualist dismembered
the sacrificial victims. This argument is resisted by Liu Xinfang (1990:40),
but Takashima (1984-85:252, n. 21) has proposed, in similar vein, that the
Shang graph was a loan for ci, "stab, pierce" (also Campbell 2004:24, n.
16). I would agree, in any event, that the ce ritual was closely related to the
zhuo "cleaving" ritual (see zhuo entry below), as in [292].
These interpretations are not necessarily exclusive. Even if the word
meant to cut or pierce, it may have been a ritual activity reserved for
victims that had been, as inscriptions such as [292]-[294A-C] suggest,
pledged in advance. Shirakawa's telling observation (1955:14) that in no
case was ce ever used for a single animal victim suggests that the verb
had some special function, as does Li Xiaoding's observation (JGWZ:1606)
that the recipient of the ce ritual was always a particular ancestral spirit,
so that whatever "report" was being made was directed to a particular
Power (and thus, in my view, likely to involve some sort of contract).
Kwok-ching Chow (1982:210; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:210) regards ce as
a type B verb (defined under dao on p. 286 of the Glossary; also see p. 298),
representing a specific method for sacrificing victims.
My preference for "pledge" is also based on later historical and ethno-
graphic analogy. In Shu, "Jin teng "(para. 8) for example, the Duke of
Zhou made precisely such a conditional pledge to his ancestors:282
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
283
Now I will issue the charge to the Great Turtle. If you grant me my wish
(that the king may recover), I will take the bi []-disk and gui [E]-scepter,
return, and await your order (to have my life taken in substitute, as I had
offered). If you do not grant me my wish, I will shut up the bi and the gui
(which will thus not be offered to you)." (Karlgren, tr., 1950:35, adj.)
The Duke of Zhou then placed the tablets (ce "tablets") recording the
divination in the coffer. Shirakawa (1955:10) cites Zhouli, "Xiao Zong Bo
," etc., for further evidence of "reserving" sacrificial victims for later
offering. For a similar type of "pledge," made by Bo Juyi to the
rain god in the Tang dynasty, see Waley 1949:148; see too the entry for gao
below. Middleton (1960:35-36; also p. 87) reports that the Lugbara of
East Africa consecrated and dedicated the sacrificial animal to their ances-
tors, "with a promise to sacrifice if the patient should recover from his
sickness. If he does not recover, then the oracle was mistaken and there
was no point in making the sacrifice." The Shang charges were designed
to influence the future; I find it plausible that, where sacrifice was con-
cerned, they might well have adopted a bargaining, wait-and-see posture
viz-à-viz the divinee.
cha (=): "thrust, penetrate, invade" (Y371.2-76.1; Sōran, no. 3099). See
Guo Moruo and Li Xiaoding at JGWZ:2410, 2414-15. The verb was used
either separately (as in [231] and [242A]) or in the combination chafa
,"to penetrate and attack" (as in [114] and [235]), in divinations about
Shang military expeditions. Cha was also the name of a place (e.g., Li Yan
1970:313; Keightley 1983:535; Zheng Jiexiang [1994:297-98] locates it in
Shanxi, Yuci xian, just southeast of Taiyuan). It lent its name to an
important officer of Wu Ding, Cha Zhi Hua EL (sometimes Cha Hua
Zhi as in Heji 6648), thus "Zhi Hua of Cha" (Y371.2-72.2), or, possibly,
"the Penetrator, Hua of Zhi." For an account of the numerous inscriptions
about his exploits, see Li Xueqin and Peng Yushang 1996:387-96.
The appellation She Cha, I believe, referred to a title, the "Archer
Penetrator," or to the elite expeditionary force that he led, the "Archer
Penetrators" (cf. Zhu Qixiang 1999a:6). In a charge like [233], for example,
She Cha has to be construed as two nouns and not as two verbs ("to shoot
and to penetrate"). (But Zhu Qixiang [1999a:6] sees She Cha as an official
name plus a personal name.) The appearance of what seems to be an offi-
cial title, the Duo She Cha, "The Many Archer Penetrators," would
seem to support the view that the She Cha could be many in number.
The meaning of the two inscriptions in which the putative title appears,
however, is uncertain. Thus, Tunnan 7,
could mean
either "... order the Many Archer Penetrators (and Many) Horse (chariot-
officers) (ma)... (to go) to X," or "... order the Many Archers to penetrate
the Ma(fang)... at X" (this being the interpretation offered by the Tunnan
shiwen). Similarly, Tunnan 503, "," could mean either "Order the
Many Archer Penetrators” or “Order the Many Archers to penetrate.” In
view of this uncertainty, I translate She Cha as the "Archer Penetrator," but
with the understanding that the title could naturally have embraced both
him and the other "Penetrators" who composed his military unit.
The Archer Penetrator of Period I apparently led Shang military expe-
ditions (as in [233] and Heji 5755, 6771, 32023, and the inscriptions at
Y373.1-2 and 373.2--74.1). Heji 163 (Y373.2), which is fragmentary, seems
to end with a verification in which the Archer Penetrator(s) caught ten
(plus?) Qiang (+). On these matters, see, e.g., Guo Moruo (com-
mentary to Cuibian 81) and Hu Houxuan (1944a:4b-5a), citing the She Ren
,"Archers," of the Zhouli; Shirakawa (1959), who took She Cha to refer
to the Archers in the Cha clan; Hayashi (1968:86), who attempts to show
how one branch of the Cha clan had been enfoeffed in another place. The
most extensive study of the place and the persons is Zhang Bingquan
1958; see too 1967:701-02.
chen: "servitor(s)" (Y226.2-29.1; Sōran, no. 0394; Zhong Bosheng 1995;
2009; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0651; Gao Ming 2001:94-103). Guo Moruo's the-
ory that the chen were slaves has no validity; it was based upon a subjec-
tive reading of the graph form itself (challenged on philological grounds
by Dong Zuobin 1948:11; cf. Chou Hung-hsiang 1970-71:359, n. 2; Qiu
Xigui 2000:166)- which he thought depicted the eye turned in a vertical
position, thus representing the bowed head of a submitted prisoner (Guo
Moruo 1931:65-66; cf. GSR, no. 377). The Shuowen definition, XL. ●
***RE, "chen means qian (pull, drag, lead); serving the ruler is
shown through a posture of submission" (cf. Serruys 1984:675), encour-
ages us to characterize the status of chen in terms of dependency, but, in
the absence of other firm evidence, there is no need to assign the status
of "slave" (see, e.g., Keightley 1969:357-79; 1999b:283-84, 285-96; Zhao
Lin 1982:137; see also p. 53). The inscriptions, in fact, indicate that the chen
were royal officers, many of whose individual names were recorded, who
served in the royal court, received the king's orders to launch military
expeditions and other tasks (as in [142], [185B], and Heji 641), and received
royal gifts (Chen Mengjia 1956:505-07; Keightley 1969:190-91, 367-69; Qiu
Xigui 1980:156-57; Yang Shengnan 1983). Chou Hung-hsiang lists a vari-
ety of titles from the oracle-bone inscriptions: Xiao Chen E, Wang Chen
王臣, Xiao Wang Chen 小王臣, Yuan Chen 元臣, Di Chen 帝臣,etc. “To those
familiar with traditional literature, the character 'statesmen,' 'subject,'
symbolizes concepts of officialdom, majesty, respect, as well as obedience,
loyalty and modesty" (1970-71:359, n. 52). Serruys (1974:31) gave a "small
servant" for Xiao Chen/E.284
Working for His Majesty
Guo Moruo's view influenced a large number of his contemporaries
and their successors (see, e.g., Hou Wailu 1955:64; Li Yanong 1964:65-
66; Lin Yun 1979:325; Sun Miao 1987:498-502; Xiao Liangqiong 1991:358,
374-75). Recent interpretations have, however, been more nuanced, ready
to evaluate the dependent and diverse statuses of the chen in terms of a
society that was itself evolving (e.g., Han Feng 1983:55; Zhang Yongshan
1982:224; 1983:77-78). Maspero (1978:389, n. 9) treats the chen in the light
of the bondsmen, the leudes, of Frankish chieftains. Han Feng (1983) reads
chen as subjects of various statuses. Zhang Yongshan (1983:78) read the
status of Xiao Chen/of various social levels. Kolb (1991:48-50) trans-
lates chen as diener, "servant, attendant, etc." Zhou Yan (2000) and Wang
Ningsheng (2002) posit that the Xiao Chen referred to "officials who origi-
nated from non-Shang lineages, or were promoted from lower statuses
but served the Shang court" (Li Liu and Xingcan Chen 2003:49). Also see
Zhu Qixiang 1999a:10-11 (front). Pankenier (1995:171, with references)
finds it likely that "Di's 'Five Minister Regulators' (Wu Chen Zheng
TE [Heji 30391]) refers specifically to the five planets whose behavior and
function qualified them for high rank in the supernatural hierarchy."
cheng or cheng ce (Y1195.1-2; Sōran, nos. 0528, 0259; cf. Xiaoneng
Yang 2000:153-54 [Yang Xiaoneng 2008:287-88]): "raise (the tablets), call
out (the orders)." HYDZ:285-86 gives "upholds the proclamation" for
[61] and "announce (His Majesty's) edict" for Heji 6468 (HYDZ:298). In
Period I, cheng, "to raise, set forth, display" (Schuessler 1987:70; cf. Qiu
Xigui 2000:337), was used in contexts of mobilization, as in [61], in the
formula cheng ce. It is thought that the ce in such cases referred to written
slips on which the orders, plans, or names were recorded (see the Glos-
sary entry for gong dian)--the muster roll or roster, perhaps - and that the
cheng referred to their proclamation, perhaps in a ritual in which the slips,
bearing the instructions, were offered to the Powers for approval and
consecration. See Dong Zuobin 1945:II:2:2a (and II:9:39a on the related
phrase cheng ce; cf. Britton 1940:19-20); JGWZ:1405-08; Chang Tsung-
tung 1970:217, nn. 2, 4; Liu Zhao 1989:102; Zhong Bosheng 1991:107-16.
Shirakawa (1971a:18b-19a; 1972:165), noting that warfare also involved a
battle of spiritual Powers on either side, proposed that the cheng ce rit-
ual was designed to drive away, to exorcise, the harm represented by
the enemy groups with whom the Shang were about to engage. (For a
possible Eastern Zhou legacy, see Lewis 1993:336, n. 15.) Xu Jinxiong, in
his commentary to Menzies 2674 (= Heji 31996), assigns the meaning of
"mobilize, call up, raise," to cheng by itself. Yu Xingwu (1941:13-14 [cited
at JGWZ:1406-07]) noted a number of Eastern Zhou cases in which cheng
and related words had the sense of "relate, speak."
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
285
Takashima (1984:32-33), following Guo Moruo (1933:158a), takes the
oracle-bone phrase ruo cheng in Heji 10406 as "just as (His Majesty)
has prognosticated," taking cheng ("lift," which was cognate with
[GSR, no. 894d]) in the sense of "evaluate." This provides additional
grounds for thinking that may have involved some pronouncement or
proclamation.
chu: "cut fodder, foragers" (Y348.1-50.1; Sōran, no. 0048; Yang Sheng-
nan 1992:84; JGWD:55-56; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0914). The word chu was
used as a verb in some contexts (e.g., [16C] (?) and Heji 96), but it could also
be used as a noun, "forager," as in the charge to [2AB] and the prognos-
tication to Heji 94f: 旬又二日乙卯允來自光以羌五十,“After twelve
days, on yimao (day 52), there was indeed someone bringing Qiang forag-
ers, numbering fifty, from Guang." See too Heji 22043.
chu : "clear out, (hence) purify" (as in [285]). Oracle-bone (Y333.1) is
frequently transcribed as tu,"road." Many scholars, under the influence
of Marxist theories of slave society, still follow Yu Xingwu (JGWZ:556-57)
and take the word in the sense of tu, "to butcher" (see the entries at
Sōran, no. 0184); the two charges-Heji 67 and 68-about tu zhongren
(Y333.1-2) are thus understood as references to the Shang custom of
butchering their slaves (e.g., JGWZ:557; Wang Guimin 1980:67; Sun Miao
1987:525). Since it is evident that the Shang valued their zhong (q.v.) as
permanent retainers attached to the royal house, and since the verb is
also applied to Shang elites (also valued, one presumes, by the king), I fol-
low Rao Zongyi (1959:177) and Kaizuka (commentary to Jimbun 1073, n.
1) in believing that the word referred to a ritual that could "remove evil"
and tentatively read it as chu. See too the discussion at Zhang Yongshan
1982:259-60. Qiu Xigui (1983a:18) also rejects the reading of tu, proposing
instead that the word had the general sense of "welcome" or "meet." If so,
one can still imagine that the "welcoming" or "meeting" could have had
a ritual dimension.
cong M. Consult the bitt entry.
dao "pray for" (Sōran, no. 1259). In contexts of prayer and requisition,
oracle-bone form * (frequently transcribed, as at Y564.2, as hu or hui *)
has usually been read as qiu *, "pray for, entreat, request," even though
the graphic filiation is unclear; oracle-bone (Y572.2) has also, on occa-
sion, been read in the same way. See, e.g., Hu Houxuan 1944c:17a; Ikeda
1964 (commentary to Houbian 1.1); JGWZ:2736, 3238; Tsung-tung Chang
1970:94-107; Liu Huan 2002:402; HYDZ:605, 641, only give "conduct the286
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
287
hu -sacrifice." Qiu Xigui (1986) had suggested that depicted a cen-
tipede and should be regarded as an early form of qiu ("many legged
insect") or, in the sense of qiu *, "seek" - and would thus have
been a loan. Ji Xiaojun (1991), however, relying upon contextual and
graphic analysis in bone, bronze, and later texts, has now demonstrated
that oracle-bone * (and presumably, in the right contexts, *) should
be read as dao, "pray for," which is adopted here; also see Takashima
2002:102, 111, 125 n. 10. The Shuowen definition that Ji cites (1991:40), **
, "dao means to report matters (to the spirits) and seek bless-
ings," probably reflects Shang usage.
Kwok-ching Chow (1982:191-218; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:232-50)
classifies dao as a type A verb, that is, a verb of major, comprehensive
ritual-sacrificial activity (like gao, "make a ritual announcement"; yu
御 ,"exorcize") that usually involved more specific type B verbs (such as
ce, "pledge"; liao, "offer a burnt offering" ["holocaust"; Takashima
1973:251]); you, "you-cutting sacrifice"; you, "offer"; zhuo, "cleave")
in its execution.
Da Shi : "The Greater Ancestors" (Y398.2-99.2; see Shi herein).
See Kaizuka (commentary to Jimbun 2366, p. 580) and Zhang Bingquan
(commentary to Bingbian 395, pp. 460-62). As Chao Fulin (1989:158–59)
and Zhu Fenghan (1989:40, 42, 45; 1990:5-6) demonstrate, the term was a
collective title, not for all the ancestors who composed the direct line of
descent (i.e., the da zong of later usage), as has commonly been sup-
posed (e.g., Yang Shengnan 1985:4), but for the six direct-line ancestors,
starting with Shang Jia, the first pre-dynastic ancestor (see too the entry
for Xia Shang), and followed by the first five dynastic kings whose tem-
ple names were prefixed with Da X, "Great": Da Yi (K1), Da Ding (K2),
Da Jia (K3), Da Geng (K5), and Da Wu (K7). Chao Fulin notes (1989:161)
that the Da Shi was limited to these six ancestors and that they received
more cult than other ancestors. Chao Fulin (1989:162–63) further argues
that terms like da and xiao had a temporal sense and that the Shang
ritualists divided their ancestors into three groupings: the senior, referred
to as "Da Shi" (or "Shang Shi E," "Yuan Shi"); the middle group,
referred to as "Zhong Shi "; and the junior, referred to as "Xiao Shi
" (or "Xia Shi F"). Zhu Fenghan (1989:41-45) agrees with many
of these equivalencies and, following the original suggestion of Chen
Mengjia (1956:414), notes that the two temple names given to K12-i.e.,
Zu Yi Z and Xia Yi FZ-may have been related to the distinction
between the "Shang Shi," "Higher Ancestors," and the "Xia Shi," "Lower
Ancestors." See Liu Xueshun (2003) and Keightley (2004:16-20) on ances-
tral clustering in general.
deng : "raise, raise up" (Y365.1-66.2; Sōran, nos. 0612, 0663; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 0858; Wang Rencong 2000; see too gong #). Oracle-bone (in,
e.g., [49], [66], [67], [136], [223]) was written with two hands about a dou
serving stand (or gui tureen) equipped with a lid. The interchangeabil-
ity of the two words deng and gong is revealed in [47], [49]-[52], [66], and
[67] (see too S583.3). Inscription [77] may also suggest their interchange-
ability, but the charge containing the unfortunately, is incomplete.
See GSR, no. 883bc; Shima 1958:310; JGWZ:1763-65 (see, in particular, the
view of Tang Lan, cited at JGWZ:1763); Luo Kun 1991:413; JGWJ:318-19;
Yu Xingwu 1996:853-55; Qiu Xigui 2000:11, 75. Oracular has also been
understood as an early form of yong, meaning "cooked food" in Zhou
texts, but in the contexts of mobilization it is also thought to have had
the sense of "present" (Tang Lan, loc. cit.). Yang Shuda (cited JGWZ:1764)
and Jin Xiangheng 1965:3b-who cites tempting parallels in classical
texts-argued that was deng, read as zheng 1, "levy"; but on this
see JGWZ:465-69, 1673-77; Li Yan 1970a:282; Serruys 1974:113, n. 59; Lin
Zutai 1978:48-50. For other discussions on the use of this verb in contexts
of labor conscription, see, e.g., Dong Zuobin 1945:II:9:39a; Chen Mengjia
1956:606; Shima 1958:387-88, 473; commentary to Jimbun 671; JGWZ:779-
82; Akatsuka 1977:605-06, n. 22; Liu Zhao 1989:94-95. I adopt the reading
of deng as a convenience. Various modern, "artificial" graphs, such as
and, have also been proposed for.
It would be tempting to argue that the two hands were "raising" the
vessel in the graph, but in most cases the engravers placed them on
either side of the lid, and not below the vessel itself (see, e.g., the selec-
tion of graphs at JGWB 5:17a-b, no. 663). That the graph with the hands
placed lower down, on either side of the vessel stand, is found in the
Li 2 inscriptions (Li Xueqin and Peng Yushang 1996:205, 222, tables 24,
25), but was placed higher on the vessel (now lidless) in the Li-Name-
less Transitional inscriptions (271, table 31), suggests that the distinction
may have been epigraphic rather than semantic. Jin Xiangheng (1965:4b),
however, had argued that the placement of the hands in this and related
graphs had semantic significance: the hands were placed low in the case
of offerings of millet and fragrant ale (as in [56]), and high in the case of
labor mobilizations (as in [49], [51], [66], and [67]). As Jin was forced to
note, however, Kufang 310 (presented as [223]), one of the most famous
of all mobilization charges, used the deng twice, the first time with the
hands high, the second with the hands low. The editors of Yingcang
("Introduction," 9-10), in fact, provide two different transcriptions in this
case, and, but I doubt that any semantic distinction was involved;
other scholars have not distinguished the graphs in their modern tran-
scriptions (see, e.g., Dong Zuobin 1945:II:9:39a; Wang Yuxin, Zhang, and
Yang 1977:2; Y185.2). That in [57] (Period III) the two upraised hands288
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
289
were placed below the stem and base of the dou, whereas in [58] (Period
III-IV) they were placed higher up, below the bowl-with both charges
involving the offering of aromatic ale (but see you "cutting sacrifice"
in Glossary entry)-provides further grounds for wondering whether the
placement of the hands conveyed the precise semantic significance that
Jin proposed.
Di
(Y418.2-25.1; Sōran, nos. 0006, 2371; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 1132; see
also Shima 1958:198-212; Tsung-tung Chang 1970:211-42; Shi Shaohua et
al. 1993; Qi Wenxin and Wang Guimin 1998:137-47; Wang Hui 2000:18-
35). In Western languages, the Di (or Shang Di ) of the Shang docu-
ments has been translated as "The One on High above All," "The Supreme
God," "high god" or "Lord," "Supreme Being," and "The God."10 He pre-
sided, albeit remotely, over a group of High Powers, who stood at the
head of the ancestral hierarchy and whose own status might at varying
times be vaguely, or in some cases explicitly, ancestral. There was, at least
in the inscriptions, no extensive cult addressed directly to Di. If Di were
an ancestor, he was treated with uncharacteristic parsimony. The living
kings divined about Di infrequently and they offered him little or no sac-
rificial wealth. The contrast with the generous way in which the kings
treated their ancestors is so notable that it implies a difference in kind."
That the Zhou were able to coopt the role of Shang Di also indicates that
the Shang was not related by blood to Di.
Even the basic requests-for rain, for harvest, for assistance in bat-
tle-that were frequently addressed to the ancestors were made to Di far
less often.¹² Di was apparently not subject to human influence, or swayed
by human appetites, in the way that the ex-human ancestors were. He
was never, for example, offered a you -"cutting sacrifice" (see Glossary
entry), one of the most common ancestral offerings. Nor did the Shang
offer human or animal sacrifices to Di. Perhaps he was not, or was no lon-
ger, sufficiently anthropomorphized to be capable of enjoying such offer-
ings. In any event, these cultic distinctions set Di apart from the ancestral
and Nature Powers.13
10. Menzies 1936:2; Tung Tso-pin 1948:127; Dubs 1958:220; K. C. Chang 1976c:190.
Pankenier (2004:236) argues that "by bringing together a variety of evidence from archaeol-
ogy, the paleographic record, and the history of astronomy, it is possible to piece together
a coherent picture that identifies the Celestial Thearch or Lord-on-High with the potency of
the pivot of the heavens, at least by the founding of dynastic Shang in the early Bronze Age,
and perhaps before."
11. See, e.g., [20], [36], [37], [39], [41AB], [148], [178AB].
12. See, e.g., [122AB], [165EF], [175AB], [176A-C], [214]-[216], [302AB], Heji 34150, Ying-
cang 1133f and 1139.
13. Chen Mengjia (1956:580), Tōdō (1969:471), Tsung-tung Chang (1970:239, 260), etc.,
reached similar conclusions.
That Di was conceived as being "above" is indicated by his ability to
"send down" disasters and approval on men below.14 It was the High God
who confers approval and assistance, 15 hosts the senior ancestors, orders
disasters, rain, thunder, and wind, 16 and gives assistance in battle.17
I provisionally accept Eno's suggestion that Di may also refer to a dead
king:
The absence of sacrifices to Di, inconsistencies in the apparent referential
range of the term, di, indications that the term could be used as a plural
noun, and the term's use as an honorific all combine to suggest that what
di denoted was not a single supreme diety.18
And evidently the Period V inscriptions do refer to Wen Wu Ding XT
(the 27th Shang king) as Wen Wu Di X.⁹ Hu Houxuan (1944g:10b-
11a) and Qiu Xigui (1983a:1-4) had earlier made the same point: Fu Ding
Twas called Di Ding T, Fu Jia was called Di Jia 20 But the
point is not salient. Di primarily referred to the High God.
The male dominance of Shang society, especially notable in the realm
of ancestor worship, together with the use of Di in the temple names of
certain dead kings, but never in the names of dead queens, virtually pre-
cludes the possibility that an "executive" Di would have been female.
The original meaning of the word di remains to bother us. Scholars have
attempted to see the graph as depicting a celestial, vegetative, anthropo-
morphic, or ritual object, or have attempted to provide a primarily philo-
logical explanation.21 Guo Moruo had called attention to the Babylonian
link in 1945, pointing out that, in addition to the graphic similarity, the
Babylonian and Chinese words shared the same initial and the same
usage, which applied the word to both divine and temporal rulers.²² As he
also pointed out, however, the cuneiform was originally a picture of a star,
not the sun (Guo Moruo 1963:12).

this point. The title of Shang Di E, "the Upper Di" or "the Di on High," also implies
that Di resided above; it rarely occurs in the oracle-bone inscriptions, however (Heji 10166
and 24979, and perhaps 30388), and with not much accompanying text (Shima 1958:197 and
JGWZ:1 cite the same three cases).

Working for His Majesty
Duo Yin : see Chen Mengjia 1956:517-18; Shima Kunio 1958:469;
Y353.2-54.2; JGWD:286-87; Zhong Bosheng 1999. See [192BC], [237A], and
[271]. Perhaps "The Many Commanders" (?).
Duo Yu: "Many Progenitors." Many scholars read this oracle-bone
title (see the inscriptions at Y185.2-86.2; Sōran, no. 1698) as Duo Hou
, "The Many (later Ancestors =) Descendants"; see, e.g., Qiu Xigui
1985:31 (cf. 2000:190); Chao Fulin 1989:159; JGWD:1581-82; Yan Yiping
1989:1:37. Chen Mengjia (1956:468) suggested that Duo Hou referred to
both the direct-line and collateral kings from Shang Jia on. I agree with
Zhu Fenghan (1989:42-43), however, that this collective term must have
referred to the kings of the direct line who came after the Da Shi . I
believe, however, that the second word should be read as yu (see yu in
the Glossary); I note that Qiu Xigui (1990:14) uses this transcription. The
title referred to the "Many Progenitors," that is, to those main-line kings
who had produced sons who became kings in turn. I suspect that if the
ancestors listed in Heji 32384 (tr. at Keightley 2004:17) stopped with Zu
Yi (K12 = Xia Yi)-—since the scapula is fragmentary, we cannot be sure
of this-the collective title of Duo Yu was another way of referring to the
ancestors known as the Yuan Shi T, "Primary Ancestors" (see Yuan Shi
in Glossary entry, where it is shown how Xia Yi was the last of the Yuan
Shi). I would thus argue that the ritual unit, "from Shang
Jia to the Many Progenitors" (see the inscriptions at Y1361.2-62.1), was
equivalent to the ritual unit Z, "from Shang Jia to Xia Yi."
Ershi Shi (Y401.2): "The Twenty Ancestors" (see Shi). Cao Ding-
yun (1990) argues that the full phrase,
(from which the zi
Écould be omitted), "(the) Twenty Ancestors from Shang Jia (on down),"
was a collective term for all the ancestors, pre-dynastic and dynastic,
main-line and collateral, from Shang Jia (P1) down to Zu Yi (K12). (The
phrase appears in Heji 34120-22 [see too Y1354.2], all datable to Shi-Li
Transitional [Li Xueqin and Peng Yushang 1996:79].) This is plausible; see
too Yang Shengnan (1985:6), who argues that Ershi Shi referred to ances-
tors both main and collateral (at p. 14 he discusses the kings on the Shiji
king list who were not recorded in the oracle-bone inscriptions). See too
the entry for Yuan Shi
It must be noted, however, that Cao Dingyun (who does not address
this issue in the scholarship I have read) can only obtain the full count of
"twenty" by including Nan Ren and Qiang Ding T, kings who
appear in Sima Qian's "Yin Benji
" but whose presence in the orac-
ular record is uncertain (see kings K4A and 4B at Keightley 1978:204, 207,
table 15, nn. f, g; Nan Ren may appear on the fragmentary Heji 24977).
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
291
Y1474 provides a convenient comparison of the oracle-bone and Shiji
genealogies; the Y editors did not include either king in their oracle-bone
king list. If Cao is correct, it would seem that Sima Qian had access to a
tradition of royal descent that the Shang Period V ritualists were no longer
celebrating in their cult schedule.
I would suggest that the designation "Twenty Ancestors" was created
precisely to squeeze all the eligible ancestors into a limited amount of rit-
ual space. As Chang Yuzhi (1987:137) has shown, there would have been
no room to fit Zhong Ren + (= Nan Ren), for example, into the regu-
lar schedule. Cao's method of counting, incidentally, appears to resolve
the difficulty raised at Shaughnessy 1982-83:9, n. 4, about the number
of generations separating Wu Ding from Shang Jia-twenty rather than
seventeen.
fa : "attack, dismember" (Y887.2-97.2 et al.; Sōran, no. 1007; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 2410). Fa in military contexts meant "attack"; see the Glossary
entry for zheng IE, "march to regulate." In ritual contexts, fa has often
been thought to indicate the killing (and, on the basis of the graph form, a
dagger-axe laid across the neck, a "beheading") of human victims and, by
extension, the killing of animals in sacrifice (e.g., Zhang Bingquan, com-
mentary to Bingbian 413, p. 487; JGWJ:239, 314; Taiping Chang 1981:350-
56; Qiu Xigui 1981:44-45; 2000:190). Thus, Serruys (1981:337) offers "to
sacrifice by axe blow' (or 'to sacrifice with axe dance')." (For possible links
between fa and music, see, e.g., Luo Zhenyu [cited at JGWZ:2657-58]; Zhu
Fangpu 1935:31a [1972:294]). If we discount the shape of the graph, I find
no evident reason in the oracle-bone or classical texts to take fa in the sense
of "behead"-although certain passages would permit such a translation;
HYDZ:620 gives "decapitari." On the basis of both oracle-bone and Zhou
usage, I suspect that, as a ritual verb, fa meant "to cut up, to cut off" (a
sense that was presumably involved in the contexts of "attack") and that,
as a ritual noun, it referred to those victims, either human or animal, that
had been cut up. Qiu Xigui (2000:190, 294) gives to "chop." I find plau-
sible, therefore, Takashima's argument (1984-85:292; cf. his translation
at 1990:23), both philological and contextual, that fa in such contexts
meant "dismember" or "split, cut up" (Takashima 2010:II:39). He relies
heavily upon one inscription (as Bingbian 153.10), which, following his
arguments, I would translate as follows:
[295] 王[其伐]若乙丑允伐右暨左卯隹七牛
"[If (qi)] His Majesty offers [the dismembering sacrifice
(fa)], there will be approval." (Verification:) On yichou (day
2), he really did offer a dismembering sacrifice; what was292

Finally, if read correctly, the charge and notation on Heji 32202 (II. Shi-
Li)−十伐. 卯二牢.茲用,“(We) offer ten dismembered victims (fa). We
split open (mao) two penned cows (lao).' This was used" - once again sug-
gests that fa cult could involve animals as well as humans. My reading
of Heji 32202 follows that of the MZ editors and Heshi. Huang Tianshu
1988:268 believes that the notation, |, represented additional cult,
added to the original offering of ten decapitated human victims.
Such evidence also indicates that although the fa, mao, you,
and zhuo proceedings and offerings (see the Glossary entries) involved
some form of cutting up victims, they were distinct, either in time (see
the suggestion of Kwok-ching Chow, quoted in the entry for you, that
the you offering was preparatory to other sacrifices) or in procedure (as in
[292], [295], [299], [300], and [335AB]).
fang: "border, country" (Y1203.2; Sōran, no. 1057; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
3119; also see Keightley 2000:68-72). Y1203.2-04.1 lists 40 different fang;
S458.2 lists 42. The explanation of the graph, as Karlgren notes (GSR, no.
740a-f) is uncertain, but the Shuowen etymology of fang-
, "boats side by side; it depicts two boats with their prows com-
bined and abbreviated"-suggests "going beside," "side" as a primary
translation, with an extension to "square, quarter, region" (i.e., something
that has sides or that is beside the center). For political units, the trans-
lation of "country," from Latin contra, "against, opposite," seems suit-
able (I am grateful to William Boltz for the last suggestion, reprinted from
Keightley 2000:66, n. 25). The Bafang of [301AB] would thus have been the
people of the Ba country, but with the sense that they were on the side or
edge of the Shang. Their name, accordingly, probably had the sense of Ba
border people. In a group name, however, I leave fang untranslated, thus:
Bafang, Guifang, Tufang, etc. As Yates (1997:80) notes:
There is scant evidence that the fang were states... ; most of them could
just as well have been hunting-gathering tribes with territories whose
resources they exploited but which lacked fixed borders; while some of
these fang may indeed have possessed urban centers or citadels protected
by stamped-earth walls, the Shang do not specify in their inscriptions the
level of cultural sophistication of their opponents.
As an area prefixed by a direction, I translate fang, with no great assur-
ance, as region, not quarter. Thus, beifang = Northern Region (as in Heji
14295). Fang certainly had the sense of quarter in Western Zhou texts, and
the
presence of the si fang, "the four fang," in the oracle-bone inscriptions294
Working for His Majesty
(Y1204.2-05.1) encourages the sense of "four quarters" even for Shang,
though the original meaning may have been closer to "four sides," "four
border areas." Allan, in fact, has concluded that "whereas tu [±, q.v.]
were real lands to the north, south, east and west of the Shang..., the
fang [] were primarily important as spiritual entities" (1991:84). That the
same graph, and presumably the same word, was evidently used to refer
both to cosmological conceptions and real political entities occasionally
makes it difficult to translate certain references to fang with certainty.
Fu: "Lady" (?). The reading of oracle-bone (Y1149.2; Sōran; nos. 0981,
1417; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2983) and the status of the Fu have both been a
subject of considerable debate. Chou Hung-hsiang (1970-71:380-88) lists
eighty-eight Fu recorded in the inscriptions and notes twenty-one cases in
which the name of a Fu was the same as that of a place or other leader.
It was partly the great number of these women, in fact, that led Hayashi
(1968:50-51) to propose that they must have been the wives of the Duo Zi
7, the "Many Zi (sons?)," of the royal clan; he cites the Erya definition
in support:, "the wife of a son (zi) is a Fu." Kwang-chih Chang
(1980:177) has suggested that Fu Hao might have been a generic title
for at least three of Wu Ding's consorts. It is not even agreed that the Fu were
necessarily female. Matsumaru (1970:77-79) has attempted to place the Fu
and other Shang titles in a network of social and political relationships
with other clans and statelets, arguing that the combination Fu Mou, *,
did not designate a woman (or women) who came to marry into Shang, but
her tribe (or tribal leader) of origin (e.g., Matsumaru 1970:79). Takashima
(1984-85:255-56, n. 23) summarizes some of the issues and, responding to
Matsumaru's suggestions, raises the possibility that may have been "a
title or occupation of some sort used for both male and female." (I note,
however, that Takashima's claim that "the graph itself is never written
complete with the 'woman' radical" is contradicted by at least one case,
Heji 14025 [= Buci 723, cited by JGWJ:54]: "... Lady will give
birth.... it will be good." JGWB, no. 470 cites two more cases: Jingjin 2027
[= Heji 18060 (Y1153.1)] and, more problematical, Yibian 8713 [= Heji 22249];
and also Heji 22250-51 (Y176.2-77.1)]. That the Fu could give birth-see
[102AB] and the numerous inscriptions at Y783.1-85.2-provides strong
grounds for treating the Fu, at least in these contexts, as women. On the
grammar of these child-bearing charges, see Takashima 1988:125-26.
That the status of the Fu was politically high is suggested by various
charges of the form "make the son of Fu X a Childe (Zi, )" (cited by Yang
Shengnan 1982:117; e.g., Heji 21065 [Y202.1]). Li Xueqin (1958:70) has pro-
posed that the royal women had the rank of Xiao Chen E, "Junior Servi-
tor" (based on charges about a Xiao Chen giving birth, a recent example of
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
295
which is Tunnan Fu22:, "The Junior Servitor will give birth
and it will be good"). Yang Shengnan (1985:13) has even proposed that
the status of particular Fu as legal wives (rather than the status of their
sons) determined the royal succession. Zhang Zhenglang (1983b:540;
Chang Cheng-lang 1986:112) argues that not all Fuat the Shang court
were royal consorts; many, he believes, were, by analogy with the shifu
of later texts, "female officials, living in the palace, attending to the
sacrifices, to the entertainment of guests, and to mourning rites"; Rao
Zongyi (1985:305-07) endorses and develops this view. A count based on
the evidence presented in Chou Hung-hsiang (1970-71) indicates that the
names of at least twenty-nine Fu were recorded in the marginal notations
of Wu Ding, either as sending in the bones or preparing them. See too
Y1149.2-51.1.
I suspect that the Fu were not all of equal status. It is clear, for example,
from the numerous divinations about her well-being and childbearing
and about her military and political activities, from divinations about her
activities after death, and from the splendor of her burial, that Fu Hao's
status was exalted. (For her activities, see, e.g., Chou Hung-hsiang 1970-
71; Yan Yiping 1981a.) That-unlike other Fu (Y403.2-06.1)-Fu Hao
was never recorded as "ritually preparing" oracle bones may be further
grounds for regarding her status as exceptionally high. In the case of Fu
Xi, by contrast, Zhang Zhenglang's characterization may be appro-
priate. I suspect, in fact, that Fu as "Lady" was a generic term that may
have been widely applied both to exogamous Shang consorts and to royal
daughters who had married out to other clans. This is also the opinion
of Zhao Cheng (1983), who found that the Fu were relatives of the king
(sisters, daughters, or nieces) before marriage who were subsequently
married to the king, to his son or nephew, or to someone else.
fufu (?) (Y995.2-96.1; for other possible readings see the entries
at Sōran, no. 0460). When used as a name, the oracle-bone graph has fre-
quently been transcribed as fu ("a quiver"), as in [160C], "Fu
will receive harvest"; this is how Zhang Bingquan transcribes the graph
in his transcription of this charge as Bingbian 332.3; Heshi and Takashima
(2010:1:545) agree. I also take Duofu as the title of a group of mil-
itary officers, the "Many Quivers" (as in [76]). Schuessler (1987:173)
gives a quiver; JGWD:486-87 gives a quiver, a method of sacrifice, and a
name. When the word is used as a verb, I tentatively follow Yu Xingwu
(1941:19-20 [reproduced at JGWZ:1125-26]; 1996, no. 2561, esp. p. 2558),
who believes it to be a method of sacrifice, "to split, divide." Kwok-ching
Chow (1982:220) offers "split-sacrifice."296
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
297
(as in
"negate
fu ruo, bu ruo . It is hard to determine the grammatical and
semantic difference between fu ruo (as in [173B]) and bu ruo
[187]). Schuessler's proposal that, in Zhou Chinese- and
objective, inadvertent situations or events which are, from the speaker's
viewpoint, either beyond the control of the agent, or affect the speaker
with inevitability," and that and "are volitional, they negate situa-
tions or actions over which the agent is thought to have some control, i.e.,
things the agent 'cannot' do or 'does not want to' do" (1987:48)—provides
for a distinction of emphasis that I seek to follow in my translations. I also
attempt to apply the thinking of Takashima (1988a:115-17, 127; 1990:79)
who argues that both and are "*p-type negatives" that negate verbs
of "uncontrollability" (i.e., involving actions that the living Shang could
not control); that bu is a stative/eventive negative, while fu is a non-
stative/non-eventive one. (See the Glossary entry for "shou.") In this
view, bu refers back to the state or condition of the agent (i.e., a Power)
whose presence is accordingly implied, as in my translation of [187],
"His Majesty's going to perform the guan-overseeing ritual for He (the
River Power) (which the Shang cannot control,) is not (bu) approved"; in
Schuessler's terms, this would have been an objective negative. Takashima
(1990:63) has also suggested that the *p-type negatives were non-
modal.
The distinction between the functions of fu and bu may be demon-
strated by contrasting similar formulas. The first used bu:
[301A]貞: 王馘比伐方帝授我又
Divined: "It should be (hui) Zhi Guo whom His Majesty
joins (bi) to attack the Bafang, (for if he does) Di (Di) will
confer assistance on us."
[301B] 王勿佳沚馘比伐巴方帝不我其[授又]
"It should not be (wei) Zhi Guo whom His Majesty joins to
attack the Bafang (for if he does) Di might (qi) not [confer
assistance] on us." (I. Bin) (Heji 6473f)
The second used fu:
[302A] 丙辰卜爭貞:馘王比帝若授我又
Crack-making on bingchen (day 53), Zheng divined: "Zhi
Guo has started off (qi) (on campaign); His Majesty should
ally with him (bi) (for if he does), Di (Di) will approve and
confer assistance on us."
[302B] 貞: 沚馘啟王勿比帝弗若不我其授又
Divined: "Zhi Guo has started off (on campaign); His
Majesty should not ally with him (for if he does), Di will
not want to approve (fu ruo) and may not confer assistance
on us." (I. Bin) (Heji 7440f)
I take the predicate of [302B] to mean that Di "may not (be in a volitional
state to) confer assistance on us."
I would also suggest that the diviners used fu when the reference was
to some further action on the part of the Shang; fu + verb, often implied a
verb, that was governed by the negated verb; thus, [173B], "His Majesty
should not go to inspect the millet planting and offer cult, (for if he does)
(some Power, which the Shang cannot control) will not want to approve
(fu ruo [i.e., fu + verb₁] his going to inspect [verb₂]);
[303] 乙卯卜殼真:王比望乘伐下危受ㄓ又
Crack-making on yimao (day 52), Que divined: "His Majesty
should ally with Wang Cheng (or a 'scout, patrol') (Wang
Cheng) to attack the Xiawei, (for if he does, some Power)
will confer abundant assistance (shou you you)."
[303B] 乙卯卜貞:王勿比望乘伐下危弗其受又
Crack-making on yimao, Que divined: "His Majesty should
not ally with Wang Cheng to attack the Xiawei, (for if he
does, some Power) cannot (fu) expect (qi) to confer assis-
tance." (I. Bin) (Heji 32)
Such usage might help explain fu's eventual role as a fusion of bu and
zhi (a remnant of verb₁?) in Zhou texts (Pulleyblank 1995:104-06).
For further explorations of the bu/fu distinction, see the Glossary entry
for shou 受.
gao: "Make (or offer) a ritual report (or announcement)" (Y247.1-55.1;
Soran, no. 0085; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0720; Li Xueqin 2001:68; Yinxu 121.
See too Harper 1985:472-73; Venture 2002a:53, n. 107). Tsung-tung Chang
(1970:108-26), Taiping Chang (1981:265-67), and Zhang Yujin (2005) pro-
vide a sample of usage; Serruys (1974:77-78) provides a brief discussion
of the word's etymology. HYDZ:215, 657, give "make the prayer ritual"
and "make a prayer." As Takashima (1988:681; cf. 2002:109–11) has noted,
this ritual usually communicated news of the king's illness or other mis-
fortunes (like enemy invasions or troop movements: [279]-[282]). Or the
charge frequently specified the ancestral recipient of the "report," as in
[209], [280], and:298
Working for His Majesty
[304]
貞: 告古方于上甲
Divined: "(We) make ritual announcement (gao) about the
Gongfang to Shang Jia (P1)." (I. Bin) (Yingcang 546f)
0
Matters of such import were apparently not under the purview of any
ancestor less senior than Zu Yi (K12). 24 The ritual was frequently accom-
panied by a sacrificial victim ([280], [282]), the medium through which
the announcement was conveyed; and the agent who did the announcing,
when specified, was the king. Well over 200 charges about the gao "ritual
reports" are listed at Y247.1-55.1.
That these ritual announcements served as prayers is suggested not
only by oracle-bone usage but also by numerous passages in Zhou (and
later) texts like Shu, "Jin teng"; see, e.g., Venture 2002a:53-54. Such a pro-
cedure was also observed in the Eastern Zhou. In the Zuozhuan, "On set-
ting out on any expedition, the duke announced [gao] the movement in
the ancestral temple. On his return, they drank in celebration of that in the
temple; and when he put down the cup, he had the transaction entered
in the tablets; this was the rule”(凡公 告于宗廟.反行飲至舍爵策勳
,) (Legge, tr., 1872:40; Huan Gong 2.) In Hou Han shu there is
and Shan sacrifices of A.D.
a record of how an account of the Feng
56 was engraved on jade tablets, placed in a golden casket, and deposited
in the temple of the dynasty's founder (Bielenstein 1979:179). The Chi-
nese custom of informing the ancestors about important family events has
been practiced down to modern times (Thompson 1969:51, 57, 74; Ahern
1973:91-219.)
The importance that the Shang and later dynasties attached to the gao
ritual raises the possibility that the Shang divination charges-which,
although frequently prospective when first pronounced, in the sense that
they concerned cult that was about to be offered, were also, when incised,
records of cult that had been proposed and found auspicious-functioned
in part as "reports" to the ancestors, to keep them informed of what their
descendants were doing (or were pledging to do; see the entry for ce ###).
Kwok-ching Chow (1982:191-218; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:232-50)
classifies gao as a type A verb, that is, a verb of major, comprehensive ritual-
sacrificial activity that usually involved more specific type B verbs (such
as ce "pledge"; liao, “offer a burnt offering”; you , "you-cutting sac-
rifice"; you, "offer"; zhuo, "cleave") in its execution.
24. Y273.1-2 lists twenty-one charges involving ritual announcements about the Gong-
fang in which we can identify the Power who received the announcement (and who was,
presumably, the divinee). Shang Jia (P1) was the "reportee" in six cases, Bao Yi (P2), Shi Ren
(P5), and Da Ding (K2) in one case each, Tang (K1) in four cases, Da Jia (K3) in three cases,
Zu Yi (K12) in two cases. Other Powers were also so addressed: the River Power in one case
and Huang Yin in two.

gong : "raise" (Y1.1-2, 361.2-63.2; Sōran, no. 0288; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 1022; Wang Rencong 2000; see too deng in Glossary). Oracle-bone
(seen, e.g., in [47], [50], and [62AB]-[64])-frequently transcribed as
4X (Soran, no. 0288)-was a picture of two hands raised; it may be taken
as the original graph for gong #, "present with both hands, offer, fur-
nish" and, probably, for gong (#, "present, supply, offer in worship," and
,"lift with both hands." See Karlgren 1923:158; GSR, no. 1182a-c, e-f;
JGWZ:469, 781; SBSG 3:85; Gao Ming 1980:318; Lin Zutai 1978:48-50; Kolb
1991:38; Zhu Qixiang 1999:43. Lin Zutai (1978:49) followed Yang Shuda
(1954:24b-25a) in taking the meaning of the word to be zheng 1, "to levy."
Serruys (1986:223, no. 10) gave "provide men" as a translation for gong ren
(which he transcribed as 4X^) in Bingbian 299.1 (= [62]); Takashima
(2010.I:188) gives "the mobilized men" (cf. Takashima 2010:II:185-86).
On the interchangeability of the two verbs and 4, see the Glos-
sary entry for deng. For additional scholarship on this and related points,
see, e.g., Dong Zuobin 1945:II:9:39a; JGWZ:779-82; Rao Zongyi 1959:606;
Shima 1958:473; the commentary to Jimbun 671.
It may also be noted that, so far as I can determine, in contexts of labor
mobilization, the Shang almost never used gong when they specified
the numbers of men involved; the only exception I have found is [50]. This
suggests that deng may have been the more tactical, the more admin-
istrative of the two verbs, while gong may have been more strategic
and, possibly, more ritual. With the one exception of [223] in which both
the deng forms, and, appeared in the same charge, the verb for Fu
Hao's "mobilizations" was always gong and not deng; see, e.g., [229],
[230]; Yingcang 151; and Heji 7285-91, 7293. (The diviners were both Que
and Zheng.) This may be taken as an indication that Fu Hao's role
in military matters may have been more significantly ritual-"raising" the
troops by blessing them-than tactical.
Gong /I: some kind of worker (?) (Shirakawa 1969-74:5:928-31; Y1118.1–
19.2; Söran, nos. 0120, 0580; Yu Xingwu 1996, nos. 0738, 2095; Schuessler
2006:255; and Glossary entry for "Gong I, IX, "). Most oracle-bone
scholars read oracle-bone and I, in the appropriate contexts, as gong I
(see Gao Ming 1980:2; JGWD:493-94). For Duo Gong (12A]), Serruys
(1986:209, no. 44) gives "the many artisans"; Takashima (2010 I:188 for
Bingbian 57.1) gives "The Many Construction-workers."
Xiao Nan (1981:269) lists a series of five Western Zhou texts in bronze
that record the Duo Gong, from Cheng Wang E to Li Wang E. In
Eastern Zhou texts, the word is translated as "workman," "mechanics,"
or "artisan."25 The traditional etymology, which goes back to at least the
25. Legge, tr., 1872:32, 466, 769; 1893:297; Brooks and Brooks 1998:137.300
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
301
Han, states that the pictograph I derives from a drawing of a carpen-
ter's square (GSR, no. 1172). The Shuowen jiezi (A.D. 121) says that I
飾也,象人有規榘,與巫同意,“Gong is to embellish with skill; it depicts
a man with a (combination) compass-carpenter's square. It has the same
meaning as wu (shaman, medium)" (SBSG 5:928; cf. Serruys 1984:688). Xu
Shen regarded the guiju as one implement, for three entries later the
Shuowen says: E, E, "the carpenter's square is a compass-carpenter's
square" (SBSG 5:932). The Zhoubi suanjing
is a mathematical
treatise whose text was stabilized by about the first century B.C. (Cullen
1996:xi), but may contain pre-Confucian materials. Needham (1965:613)
explains: O, AŒNAĦ, "One rotates the carpenter's square to
make a circle, one combines (two) carpenter's squares to make a square"
(Zhoubi suanjing 1:17b). In other words, the ju-square, laid on its side and
rotated, acted as a gui-compass. Cullen (1996:182), however, offers a trans-
lation that is quite different: "A square may be trimmed to make a circle,
or a circle may be cut down to make a square."
Xu Shen's claim that gong had the meaning of wu N is puzzling. Duan
Yucai believes that Xu meant only that the graphic forms of gong
and wu were similar; but see Serruys (1984:688) on the connection with
wu. In Shang times, however, the two graph forms were quite dissimilar:
(I) and (=). (It is true, however, that in the Late Shang the wu
Powers was associated with the quadrate formed by the four cardinal
directions [Keightley 2000:72-74].)
Relying on the idea that gong did depict a carpenter's square, Need-
ham concluded that "in Chinese culture, primarily eotechnic as it was,
engineering work par excellence was wood-work" (Needham 1965:9).
Akatsuka (1977:500) speculated that the graph depicted the shape of a
chisel and that it had the original meaning of "work, worker." An alterna-
tive view-that the bronze form for gong depicted an axe-would also
associate gong, as an implement, with carpentry; this is the theory of Wu
Qichang (cited at JGWZ:1591), but it must be noted that he was
referring to the Western Zhou bronze form of the graph, which is some
centuries later than the forms we are considering. I also suspect that
the Western Zhou bronze form is not a drawing of an object, but simply
an ornamental form of I, manifesting the calligraphic tendency found on
Zhou bronzes for a weighted, curved base to be added to certain charac-
ters, such as wang E, you, shan, and zai E (JGWB, nos. 0034, 0523,
1236,1704).
Rao Zongyi (1958:3; 1959:24-25) has argued that oracle-bone
should
be read not as gong I but as shi, "ancestral tablet," and hence as zong,
"temple." For a detailed rejection of this view, see Keightley 1970:28-34.
Xu Shen did note that the guwen form of the graph was gong plus shan
("feather"); according to Duan Yucai, the ½ indicated that the gong was
good at embellishing (cf. Serruys 1984:688, 716). Both scholars apparently
regarded I as the basic element of the graph, depicting the carpenter's
square. A study of the oracle bones, however, indicates that the modern
form, I, only became prevalent toward the end of the Shang dynasty;
prior to that the character was usually written .26 Thus would seem
that the earliest form of the graph, , may not depict a carpenter's square
but something that may provisionally be described as a box, weight, or
blade on the end of a rod or handle. A number of scholars have, accord-
ingly, taken it as a depiction of an earth rammer on the end of a handle.
Stamped-earth construction was widespread during the Shang. We know
from archaeological finds that
the Shang hang tu [+] builder used two implements for the pounding.
One, a pestle, slightly tapering at the end, left small concave impressions
on the surface of the stamped earth.... The other had a broader flat base
and left large impressions on the surface of the pounded soil. (Cheng Te-
k'un 1960:49)
But archaeological finds, at Zhengzhou, lead also to the conclusion "that
the principal tools used in hang tu construction were perhaps tied-up bun-
dles of wooden sticks" (An Chin-huai 1986:26). Nor is there any evidence
that persons bearing the title of gong in Shang and Zhou were primarily
associated with earth construction. It is certainly possible to find Eastern
Zhou texts in which the Si kong, the "Minister of Works," was asso-
ciated with the building of rammed-earth structures and walls, and the
leveling of roads (see, e.g., the passages cited at Zhang Yachu and Liu Yu
1986:23).
Other commentators, starting perhaps from the thought that Eastern
Zhou artisans sometimes worked in jade, and taking a passage in the
Huainanzi ("Daoying xun ") as a clue-which seems to
show that gong was used as a unit for three pieces of jade2-have linked
the original meaning of the graph to that semi-precious stone. Thus, Sun
Haibo argued that gong depicted the linking together of jades (yu lian E
); therefore, a man who worked with jade was called a gong-artisan
(JGWZ:1591). But the oracle-bone form for yu E (Y1261.2, 1263.1; Sōran,
nos. 0034, 0800) was unrelated to the form in question.
Li Xiaoding, working primarily with post-Shang texts, argued that Xu
Shen saw a carpenter's square depicted in the character because the square
was a tool, and the meaning was thus extended to mean "work," "accom-
plishment,” “skill," and "ability" (JGWZ:1593). There was undoubtedly a
tool-work-accomplishment nexus to the word in Zhou and Han, and it is
certainly possible that such a nexus also existed in Shang.

Tōdō Akiyasu (1969:302-06) sought to show that gong I, gong IX, and
gong, were all part of a word-family whose basic meaning is a "hole,
hollow, to pierce, penentrate, bore into." He believed that gong I orig-
inally depicted a vertical stick used for excavating, and that it was the
original character for kang #I, "to carry on the shoulder by putting a stick
through the baggage." But the original shape of the graph gives no sup-
port to his "stick penetration" theory, though the "box," if it be a box,
would support the idea of "hollow."

Gong I,, . There is less agreement about the meaning of gong
in the phrases Nㄓ召, N其ㄓㄛ, N其舌, or N亡其舌 (Y1118.2-19.1; Sōran,
no. 0580). Zhao Lin (1982:133) takes the you gong 4 of [86] as mean-
ing zuo gong I, "do work," but some scholars, following Ye Yusen,
have read oracle-bone in military contexts as gong IX, "attack"; see the
entries for Ye Yusen (Bingbian, p. 93) and Xiao Nan at Sōran, no.
0580; Takashima 1994:19; 2010:I:215. Kolb (1991:35), basing himself on Yu
Xingwu 1979:72-73 (see too Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2905, esp. pp. 2912-13),
reads the [86] phrase as "the zhong will (provide tribute
=) be ready." I find it more likely that the oracle-bone word, in such con-
texts, should be understood as gong J, "success, achievement" (Keightley
1969:18, 25, n. 6; Takashima 1973:407-08, n. 18; Schuessler 2006:255), as
in [305EF] and Heji 4246 and 4247: : “Divined: '(Our) army
might not have success."" Those who do not follow me in this reading may
still read the word as gong X, in the sense of a "successful attack."
Although I have been unable to document the use of gong I for gong
I in Western Zhou bronze inscriptions (JWGL:0591), the use of gong I
for gong does occur (see Wu Qichang as quoted at JGWZ:1591-92) and
the "merit" or "achievement" referred to on the bronzes was sometimes
that of a victorious attack (e.g., Guo Moruo 1957:10b, 20b, 146a). That
might have the sense of "successful attack" is further supported by the
consideration that Eastern Zhou bronze inscriptions do write the title of
Si gong, "Minister of Works," as Si gong IX (Zhang Yachu and Liu
Yu 1986:22). The Shang use of gong in this "attack/success" sense was,
I believe, common in oracle-bone Period I; see, e.g., [305EF]. Schuessler
(1987:196-97), in fact, links all these various meanings under one major
gong I, "work, merit, artisan," entry: (a) I, "Work, task"; (b) "(Work:)
achievement, merits"; (c) I, "Artisan"; (e) IX, "To work, apply oneself";
(f), (To work on the enemy:) "To attack." He does not, however, include
the sense of, "to present"; see the next entry.
Gong dian I: I provisionally read the phrase (Y1118.2, 1136.1-2-written
in Period II, Iin Period V) as, "present the tablets," "raised
up the tablets," as in [331]. I assume that gong I and gong were written
with the identical graph but were semantically distinct (cf. Boltz 1994:60:
"often called rebus writing"). The "presenting of the tablets" was a ritual304
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
305
that the Shang performed in the ten-day week prior to the start of each of
the five rituals of the ritual cycle (e.g., Dong Zuobin 1945:II:2:2a-b; Chang
Yuzhi 1987:140; Lian Shaoming 1997a:65-66). Yu Xingwu (1941:10b-12b;
1996:2917) (who read the phrase as gong ce I) provided a detailed
exposition of why oracle-bone I stands, in this context, for ; his conclu-
sions have been accepted by Li Xiaoding (JGWZ:1593) and other scholars
recorded at Sōran, no. 0580; see too Chang Yuzhi 1987:140. I have not
found Yu Xingwu's arguments as strong as I would have wished, how-
ever (Keightley 1970:35-41). Much of his argument appeals to passages
in Eastern Zhou texts-Shangshu, Zhouli, Zuozhuan, and Guoyu-to show
that ce "tablets" with prayers written on them were offered to the Pow-
ers. Unfortunately, in not one of the passages he cites was gong the verb
or ce the noun that was used. Both Shima (S422.4, 425.1, 415.2) and Yao
Xiaosui (Y1130.2, 1136.1), moreover, provide separate entries for the two
oracle-bone words ce and dian . And by Zhou times, at least, the two
words appear to have been distinct; according to the Shu, "Duo shi
士,” King Cheng had told the defeated Shang: 惟爾知惟殷先人有冊有典
, "You know that the earlier men of Yin had documents [ce] and
records [dian] of how Yin superseded the mandate of Xia" (Karlgren, tr.,
1950:56; see the discussion at Creel 1970:124-25). Nor do their archaic pro-
nunciations appear to have been similar.
Nevertheless, it is possible that the Shang had used the two words ce
and dian interchangeably. Li Xiaoding (JGWZ:1583-84), supporting Yu
Xingwu's interpretation, had argued that, although the graphs differed,
the two words had the same meaning and were thus interchangeable.
Shima (S584.1) cited four pairs of inscriptions that indicate the Shang
did, on occasion, substitute one graph for another. And the editors of the
Sōran, in fact, combine the two graphs in one entry (no. 0259). It is pos-
sible, accordingly, that the gong dian of the oracle-bones may indeed
be taken as equivalent to the putative gong ce of later texts with the
meaning of "present or raise up the tablets." (See too the Glossary entry
for "cheng (ce).") It is on such a supposition that I based my tentative trans-
lation of "present the tablets." Unfortunately, the dian graph appears only
in this compound, gong dian, so the possibility of establishing the meaning
of the compound with certainty is not large.
As to what may have been written on the tablets, Dong Zuobin
(1945:II:2:2b) rejects Yu Xingwu's argument that the dian documents were
prayer reports. He suggests, instead, that they listed the titles of the Shang
ancestors and ancestresses, serving as the "ritual tablets" (si dian E) for
the five rituals, in sequence, of the cycle and their days of performance,
thus providing a record akin to that of a genealogy. He cites two examples
of what these records might have resembled: Cuibian 113 and 114 = Heji
35406 (= [306]) and 39455; these do indeed appear to be non-divinatory
records that linked particular ancestors to their particular ganzhi days, as
in:

gu : "strengthen" (Y256.2-59.2). There is little agreement about the mod-

thought to have the sense of "assist" or "participate in" or "accord with";
Zhao Lin (1982:133) takes the verb to mean zuo hao F, "to do well." A
number of scholars have provided a modern transcription of the word
as gu, and I find attractive, therefore, Nivison's explanation (1978:3, 5)
of the word as gu, to "support, reinforce, strengthen" as it was used
in the phrase (Y256.4-58.1), "strengthen His Majesty's affairs" (as
in [17B], [27], [68B]) or , "strengthen Our affairs" (Y258.1). HYDZ
offers "(be able to) manage the king's affairs" (p. 93) or to "render service
to His Majesty" (p. 320).

2651-52): a vessel filled with snakes(?). See [272C]. See too Harper 1998:98.
guan (huan): "the guan-overseeing ritual" (Y657.1-59.1). As a survey
of the entries at Sōran, nos. 0506 and 0507, indicates, the meaning of the
oracle-bone graphs-which I transcribe as (normal reading zhui or huan;
on these two words see Qiu Xigui 2000:312]) and (Yu Xingwu 1996, no.

Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
307
to the nature of the ritual involved. (Peng Bangjiong [1997:431] takes the
shu huan in the sense "of gathering in the crops.")
There are good grounds for seeing the two oracle-bone graphs as inter-
changeable (Shima 1958:327; Bingbian:176-77; S586.2; Lin Zutai 1978:35;
JGWD:408-09; Chen Xi and Lu Yang 2002:27), but there is no certainty
about the identity of the word being recorded. Shirakawa (1956:834), for
example, left it simply as guan ("a heron, small cup") without assign-
ing any other meaning. Qu Wanli at times read it as guan, "to inspect,
observe, oversee," at times as a place-name (as it undoubtedly was), and
on occasion was unable to assign any meaning. See his commentary to
Jiakao 3420, where he cites other scholars who have treated and as
the same word; see too Jiabian 1850, 3001; Li Xiaoding (JGWZ:1299) gener-
ally agrees. Serruys (1974:56) gives "watches the ploughing" for guan ji
(for [188]). Kaizuka's commentary to Jimbun 0344, n. 2, noted the ten-
dency for the simpler form of the graph, E, i.e., without the two "mouth"
elements, to be used in agricultural contexts, and for the fuller form,, to
be used, as above, in ritual contexts. Ikeda (1964: commentary to Houbian
2.28.16) concluded that had the sense of "gather in the harvest" (Chen
Mengjia [1956:535] had reached a similar opinion) while was a ritual.
And Dong Zuobin (1965:100) observed that the form appeared in ear-
lier inscriptions, the form in later ones; I have, in fact, found no form
in Period I and no form in Period V. As Takashima indicates that guan
may be "to irrigate," but "this meaning is not generally accepted by
investigating specialists" (2010:II:489; also see II:705). The "ritual" mean-
ing is more appropriate than the "irrigate" meaning.
In the appropriate contexts, I had followed the interpretation of Shima
(1958:327)-see too Akatsuka 1977:712; Lin Zutai 1978:34-35; Shima 1980:
16-17; Lefeuvre 1997:125, 140; Takashima 2002:120-21-that the word was
guan (= guan), "to offer libation" (Keightley 1969:116-19). But that
reading depended to a significant extent upon the supposed existence of
the compound jiu guan, “an ale libation," in a number of charges,
such as: Jiabian 1850 (= Heji 27115): HAZ[L]LE, *"If (we) offer the
guan-libation to Da Yi (K1) with a libation [of ale], His Majesty will have
regrets"; Cuibian 452 (= Heji 30828): Z±(?)\,*"(When) on yiyou
(day 22) (we) offer an ale libation, (we) will meet with assistance" (see too
the examples at S232.4).29 Since I now read the oracle-bone graph as
you, a "cutting sacrifice" (see the Glossary entry for you), rather than
as "jiu, ale," these inscriptions, in my view, no longer support the link
between the guan ritual and liquid libation. I would now, accordingly,
29. Guo Moruo, in his transcription of Cuibian 452, read the gou as shou; the charac-
ter is hard to make out. Here I follow the tentative gou reading of S232.3 and MZ, but note

translate the two previous inscriptions merely as "If (we) offer the guan-
libation to Da Yi (K1) with a you-cutting sacrifice and a guan-overseeing
ritual..." and "(When) on yiyou (we) perform the you-cutting sacrifice

I see no conclusive evidence in the bone inscriptions to determine
whether the and forms were a way of writing guan, "oversee." The
association of [180B], which deals with "inspecting" (sheng; cf. Fiskesjö
2001:107-08), and with [180A], which deals with huan, does encourage

associates guan with religious oversight. The second, more significant,
is from "Chen gong EI" (Mao no. 276):

Bright is Shangdi;
(we) request his providing a prosperous harvest.30
Ordering our zhongren,
"Prepare your spades and hoes,"
we (Extensively =) everywhere oversee the sickles mowing.
The harvest prayer and the order to the zhongren allow the possibility that
the lord's guan "overseeing" was also part of the religious ritual involved
in Zhou agriculture and thus, possibly, in Shang.
Yan Yiping (1980:165-69) also argued for the reading of guan, "over-

309
Dong had noted that the gong dian ritual usually took place on the jia day
([331]); that it had taken place in this instance on the yi day was irregular.
Yan cited the Gu Liang zhuan 穀梁傳, Yin5:常視日視,非常日觀,“Regu-
lar observing is called shi, irregular is called guan" (cf. Malmqvist 1971:79
[changing all romanizations to pinyin]: "The term shi ['to regard'] is used
of regular events; the term guan ['to look at'] is used of irregular events").
It was the "irregularity" in [307] that had led the Shang to employ the guan
ritual in this case. I am not, on the basis of this single instance, prepared
to believe that the Shang only employed the guan when the situation was
irregular. But inscription [307] provides the only instance of a qi guan E
in a postface and the only postface instance of the qi preceding any ritual
verb in which the day was irregular. All the other cases in which qi was
present involved, entirely regularly, a jia day (e.g., [331] and Heji 35399,
35756, 35397, 35891, 37867, 41840).
While, like Yan Yiping, I have opted for the reading of guan, "over-
see," my choice is provisional. And the ritual, whatever it involved, was
associated with a number of activities. It was (as), for example, offered
to He, the Yellow River Power (Heji 5158AB, 5159); and it was performed
together with the ji "tilling" ritual (see Glossary), as in [180A], [183AB],
[188], and Heji 9501, 28200.
hai : "harm" (Y683.1-86.2 [abbreviated]). Qiu Xigui (1983b:218),
following the analysis of Zhang Zhenglang (1979), rejects Luo Zhenyu's
interpretation of oracle-bone (Y683.1) as tuo (which the Shuowen
equates with chong, and which Luo and others had equated with she
E, "snake"); see this and other opinions at Sōran, no. 1576. Takashima
(2010:II:150-55) discusses a variety of "disaster" words. The charge XZ
Serruys translates as "Father Yi is pressing (or: impeding) the king";
Takashima translates "Fu Yi will cause an affliction to His Majesty." HYDZ:
105, 402, translates it as #4: "mean that there is an (ancestral) mishap"
and "under a curse"; and E as "not cause a mishap on His Majesty."
On the basis of the graph forms found in Qin and Han strips found
at Shuihudi and Mawangdui, Qiu (1983b:219-22) demon-
strates that oracle-bone was an early form of hai; it depicted a foot
being bitten by a serpent, and had the sense of "harm," being used as both
a verb and a noun. (Cf. Mickel [1976:110-25], who concludes that the word
generally referred to disaster, in contexts of illness and sacrifice, that was
inflicted by ancestors or other Powers.) The harm might be manifested in a
variety of contexts, e.g., Di might harm the harvest (as in Heji 10124fb), the
River Power might harm the king (as in Heji 776f), the Mountain Power
might harm the rain (as in Tunnan 644, 2438, and Yingcang 2444 at Y467.2);
toothache or other sickness might be one manifestation of such harm,
caused by ancestors (Heji 822f and 13646f), and there was the frequent
concern that rituals not be afflicted with harm (see, e.g., the charges about
cult at Y685.2-86.1 that end with the incantation, wang hai, "there will
be no harm"). The inscriptions occur mainly in Period I.
Hai does occur rarely in the inscriptions with a living person as its sub-
ject, as in:
【308] 癸卯卜賓貞:苗圃呼令沚害羗方. 七月
Making cracks on guimao (day 40), Bin divined: "It should
be (hui) Pu whom we call upon to order Zhi (Guo) to harm
(hai) the Qiangfang." In the seventh moon. (I. Bin) (Heji 6623)
Such occurrences remind us that the harm that Zhi was expected to inflict
on the Qiangfang had its spiritual dimension. In almost every other case
in which the formula was "N hai wo" (N), however, the subject N was
always a Power, either ancestral or natural, like the He or Fu Yi (Heji
2251: 父乙害牛, “Fu Yi (K20) is harming the cows”).
He J: "River, The River Power" (Chang Tsung-tung 1970:167-83; Y488.1-
96.1; Sōran, no. 1266; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 1328; Zeng Fan 2009). In reli-
gious contexts, He in the Shang inscriptions referred to the Yellow River
and its Power. Zhang Bingquan (commentary to Bingbian 124, pp. 184-86),
e.g., finds that the word had four uses in the inscriptions: as the name of
a Shang ancestor, place, river, or diviner. The word he only became a gen-
eralized term for any river in the post-Han period, when Huang He
was first used to refer to the Yellow River in particular (Akatsuka 1956; Qu
Wanli 1959; 1960; Li Xingjian 1987; Rao Zongyi 1998; Luo Kun 2000). That
the geographical distribution of the word he, "river," was associated with
the languages of north, rather than central or south China (Hashimoto
1983:24), suggests that the River Power was both indigenous to the Cen-
tral Plains and ancient.
he: "grain, crops" (Sōran, no. 871; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 1482). The Li-
and He-group diviners frequently used this word in place of the nian,
"harvest," favored by other diviner groups. See Qiu Xigui (1989:12-13), Ji
Xiaojun (1991:35), Peng Bangjiong (1997:352-53), Boltz (1999:121), and the
inscriptions at Y523.1-26.2.
hou : "Archer Lord" (Y982.1-86.1; Sōran, no. 0687; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 2558)-the translation for this chiefly title proposed by Boodberg
(1979:217). Schuessler (1987:43-44) prefers to leave such titles untrans-
lated. In the divination charges, the appearance of the Hou title, prefixed
by a place-name, implies that this Lord and his lands at that place were
affiliated in some way with the Late Shang state. Chou Hung-hsiang310
Working for His Majesty
(1968:169-97) identified forty-four Archer Lords. They served as military
allies, sent in reports, and brought in captives; the king traveled to, and
ordered agricultural work in, their lands; he divined about their harvests
and their health; they participated in Shang rituals. And the place-names
associated with their titles are, in at least seven cases, the names of Shang
diviners, suggesting that some diviners were supplied from the same
regions.
hu : "call upon, call out" (Y1314.2-19.1; Sōran, no. 0599; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 3345). Usage in both the Shang oracle-bone and Western Zhou bronze
inscriptions indicates that this verb was applied to the king's pronounce-
ments as they were issued and recorded at court; see, e.g., JWGL:2991-
93. Generally, the object of the hu was a royal officer or group of royal
officers (as in [29AB], [45], [77], [107A], [133B], [166A], [197], [225], [236],
[237A]), or even a consort ([228]-[230]). But in some Shang cases the king
called upon groups of his supporters, such as the zhongren (as in [21AB],
[68A], [245], and Heji 40, 39477, 39478), the zhong ([29AB]), the Xiao zhon-
gren ([79]), or the ren (as in [47], [48B], [49], [51], [66], [67], [223]). In both
kinds of situations, I assume that the recipients were involved in some
formal, ritual, and administrative relationship with the king, and that the
use of the verb hu lent special weight to the royal command. And in some
cases it seems that it was the actual act of calling out for which approval
was being sought (as in [151], [167], [168]).
hu: "piglet" (?). Oracle-bone nan was used to write the word "south"
(Sōran, no. 0776), but in contexts of cult (Y1108.2-09.2) the word evidently
referred to an animal victim. Here I tentatively follow the argument of
Guo Moruo (commentary to Cuibian 1268; see too Shirakawa 1954:98-99)
that the graph should be read as hu, which was phonetic in and which
was in turn phonetic in hu . According to Shuowen,, "hu is a
young suckling pig." Li Xiaoding, following Tang Lan, comes to a simi-
lar conclusion, although opting for a different modern word (JGWZ:2535).
See too JGWD:685; Zhang Bingquan 1988:138; Yu Xingwu 1996:2859-72.
Takashima (2010:II:798 and HYDZ:104) gives "piglets."
hui (in Period II and after; see, e.g., Ikeda 1951:4-7; Keightley
1978:217, n. 12): "It should be" (Y1138.1-40.2; Sōran, no. 0459). Hui was
a modal, non-explanatory copula that was used in contrast to, and was
quite distinct from, the non-modal copula wei (see Glossary entry). (It
is regrettable that many scholars have treated the two words as virtual
equivalents; the Y editors, for example, misleadingly transcribe both hui
and wei as wei ; this obscures their distinct grammatical roles.)

The most comprehensive study is Takashima 1990 (see, in particular, his
conclusions on pp. 63-66, and his discussion of the paleography at pp.
67-70), which argues that hui was both extrovertive ("having an element
of the speaker's active and outward-affecting will" [p. 63]) and control-
lable. Hui, in addition to its modal function, served to mark sentence focus
"by means of the cleft construction, NOUN, PREDICATE'; 'it is NOUN
who(m we should) PREDICATE(s)" (Herforth, communication of 9 Octo-
ber 1990); thus, (Heji 23390), N, "It should be the first crack that we
use," and [301AB]://T." /should/should not/
be Zhi Guo whom His Majesty joins to attack the Bafang...." This second
case also demonstrates how, in charge pairs, hui in the positive charge ("it
should be") was consistently negated by bu wei TE("it should not be") in
the negative charge. For studies of the two words and how hui functioned,
see Takashima 1973:246-53, 399, n. 1; 1990; Serruys 1974:24-25, 74, n. 64.
=
huo : "(bad omens →) something ominous" (Y827.2-33.2; Sōran, no.
0457). See Mickel 1976:67-94; cf. Keightley 1978:69-70; Lefeuvre 1997:332;
Yu Xingwu 1996:2158-72; Zhu Qixiang 1999:43-46; Qiu Xigui 2000:245;
Liu Huan 2002:312–38; HYDZ:79, etc., give "misfortune." Mickel surveys
the scholarship and oracle-bone usage; he concludes that the word was a
non-specific indication of impending disasters. "It was used in the oracle
inscriptions to represent the concept of any kind of disaster which had
not yet happened or not yet been indicated by the divination cracks." He
notes that "when actual disasters are discussed, it is always in terms of
other disaster graphs, not " (Mickel 1976:94). Zhang Bingquan (Bingbian
304, p. 365) suggests that anybody about whom the king divined, X wang
huo (XL), must have been close to the king's favor.
The oracle-bone graph (Y827.2) is a drawing of a pyromantic bu
crack in a scapula. The various oracle-bone forms for are usually glossed
as huo, "calamity" (e.g., GSR, no. 18f; JGWZ:1497, 1499; Schuessler 1987:
260-61). Tōdō (1969:628) gives *huar for . But it appears to have been
virtually interchangeable with you (see Glossary entry): e.g., S579.1;
Shima 1958:194-95; Qu Wanli's commentary to Jiakao 12, 17; Wu Qichang
1971:42-44; see too Karlgren 1964, no. 1204. The huo word has usu-
ally been understood as "disaster" or "calamity." Takashima, for example,
gives "baneful influences," "trouble," "inauspicious (or an inauspicious
sign)," and "misfortune" (1973:27; 1980a:82, 87; 2010:I:356-57: Bingbian
165.16-17, etc.); see too Takashima 1979-80:49-50, where he suggests that
"a vague 'disaster' value commonly attributed to huo means specifically
the harm caused by spiritual forces." Serruys (1986:223, 224, no. 25), for
Bingbian 201.10-11 (= Heji 11018f), gives "disaster."
I adopt the reading huo, partly on the basis of the graphic inter-
changeability, which suggests phonetic similarity, between huo X and312

of you and thus served as a loan for you . He compares, for example

[309] 戊寅卜旅貞:王賓大戊亡禍
[310]
Making cracks on wuyin (day 15), Lü divined: "His Majesty

戊午卜旅貞:王賓大戊亡尤
