Once again, I would agree that the huo and the you shared a sim-
ilar semantic space- and perhaps a similar phonological space (e.g.,
Schuessler 1987:260, 766)-so similar, in fact, that engravers, recording
similar charges that may have been divined only twenty-one days apart
(inclusive count), might have felt at liberty to replace one with the other.
But the Shang used both words, huo and you, in such large numbers of
cases that I am reluctant to believe that huo and you should be read
as the same word.
The charges in which huo appears refer to a wide variety of situa-
tions: "In the next ten days there will be nothing ominous" (e.g., [95A-
C] and others at Y444.2-46.1); "This evening there will be nothing omi-
nous" (e.g., Heji 293 and others at Y437.2-38.2); "His Majesty's dream is/is
not ominous" (e.g., Heji 272f); "This settlement might have (some Power)
sending down something ominous" (Heji 7852), and so on, but give little
sense of the specific nature of the dangers or misfortunes involved.
Huo was the most general of the large vocabulary of disaster terms
available to the diviner (Mickel 1976 provides an intensive study of eigh-
teen disaster terms). Appearing in a good number of the charges trans-
lated in this book, it could be used of any misfortune that might occur or
be forecast in the future. See, e.g.,
[311A] 甲申卜爭真:茲雨佳我禍
Crack-making on jiashen (day 21), Zheng divined: "This
rain is (wei) ominous (huo) for us."

This sense of danger extended from Period I to V. Every gui day, the last
day of the ten-day week, it was divined, standing as a guarantee for the
ten-day week ahead.
The way in which Wu Ding's diviners used huo in balanced charge
pairs suggests to me that the main reference of this word was symbolic
rather than material; see [311AB] and Heji 6033, etc. That is to say, if huo
had referred to material disasters, one would have expected the diviner
of [311AB], for example, to weaken the possibility of an ominous rain by
inserting a qi in the undesirable charge, [311A]: *"This rain might (qi)
not be ominous for us." But in fact we have [311AB] and:
[313A】 辛酉貞卜:乙丑其雨不佳我禍
Crack-making on xinyou (day 58), Que divined: "On yichou
(day 2) if (qi) it rains, it will not be (wei) ominous (huo) for us."
[313B] 貞: 乙丑其雨佳我禍
Divined: "On yichou if it rains, it will be ominous for us."
(I. Bin) (Heji 6943)
In cases like this, the diviners did not attempt to make one charge stron-
ger than the other (see p. 329). The diviners were interested in discovering314
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
315
the symbolic value of huo, what it meant, rather than in preventing it from
happening.
The divinations about bad dreams ended with "there might be some-
thing ominous" versus "there will be nothing ominous" (versus
)(sample at MZ 396.1; cf. Song Zhenhao 2006). And other ominous
occurences ended "there will be something ominous" and "there will be
nothing ominous" (and T). Partly for that reason and partly
on graphic grounds, I extend Takashima's conclusions to suggest that huo
referred in origin to bad omens revealed by the pyromantic cracks and, by
extension, to ominous happenings in general. The rarely recorded authors
of huo were Di (as in Yingcang 1133f [not in Y and MZ]) and Huan, the local
River Power (as in [207AB]), and such authors were named only when the
"something ominous," like an enemy attack or a flood, had already mate-
rialized or was about to materialize. I believe that material disasters, by
contrast, were represented by the word jiu, "calamities" (q.v.); huo, evi-
dently, could produce or be incarnated in jiu. This is demonstrated by the
numerous Period I cases in which an apotropaic charge ending with A,
"there will be nothing ominous," was followed by a prognostication that
started, "There will be calamities" ([322AB], etc.).
to have meant, "there
I thus understand the common incantation
will be no omens (in the cracks)" and thus, by extension, "there will be
nothing ominous (in the world, both symbolic and material, at large)."
The impact of many of the divinations, accordingly, was not to discover if
something was in itself "a disaster," but whether, as in the case of dreams,
it had ominous, hence disastrous, implications:
[314A] 寅卜設貞:王夢兄丁佳禍
Crack-making on... yin, Que divined: "That His Majesty
dreamed of Brother Ding is (wei) ominous (huo).32"
[314B] 貞:王夢兄丁不佳禍
Divined: "That His Majesty dreamed of Brother Ding is not
ominous." (I. Bin) (HJ 892f)
It may also be noted that huo, "bad omens," and hai , "harm" (see
Glossary entry) were on occasion used in virtually identical contexts. Two
scapula fragments, Heji 23113 and 23114 (both II. Chu), for example, were
divined on the same day, jiaxu (day 11), and started with the identical
charge, ZFZ, "On the next day, yihai (day 12), (we) will per-
form the rong ritual to Xiao Yi (K12)." The first charge, however, ended
32. Brother Ding was probably an older brother of Wu Ding who had pre-deceased him
(cf. Zhang Bingquan, commentary to Bingbian 86, p. 118).
with the incantation and postface, L., "There will be no harm
(hai). In the first moon." The second ended with the variant incantation
and postface, L. EEA, "There will be nothing ominous (huo).' In the
regulating zheng moon." This suggests that huo (bad omens caused in the
bones or elsewhere by the divinee or other Powers) and hai (harm caused
by ancestors and other Powers) were close in meaning.
ji: "sick, be sick" (Y1177.2-84.2; Sōran, nos. 0964-65; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 3067). See too Qiu Xigui (1988:123; 2000:186) for chuang, "to lie sick,"
but as Karlgren notes (GSR, no. 1260b), there are no supporting text exam-
ples for the definition that appears in Shuowen. Ding Shan, Qu Wanli, Yang
Shuda, and others all read the word as ji (JGWZ:2518-22; Yan Yiping
1951:1:6a-10b; JGWD:837-39). The charge, prognostication, and verifica-
tion of [272A-C] suggest that ji could refer to an acute or fatal sickness, a
reading supported by the early meaning of "urgent" that was also present
in the word (e.g., Schuessler 1987:269). In the Shiming one notes:
EZ, "a sickness that arises in a hurry" (ZWDZ:9604: no. 22629, mean-
ing 2), as well as the fact that ji in early texts could also mean "violent"
(Shijing) or "hurried" (Lunyu; GSR, no. 494a–c). Cf. Harper (1999:874–83;
2002) on the Eastern Zhou to Han texts. Even in Taiwan, an anthropologist
"encountered cases in which ancestors were said to have brought about
serious debility or even death" (Ahern 1973:201; cf. DuBois 2004:67).
ji /: "to cut up (and offer the blood [of a sacrificial victim])" (Y1276.2).
The reading and meaning of oracle-bone Л has puzzled scholars; see, e.g.,
Sōran, nos. 4855, 5518. As Ikeda (1964: commentary to Houbian 1.25.9 and
2.39.9) observed, the meaning is uncertain, and we can only conclude with
certainty that it was a verb of sacrifice; Qu Wanli (commentary to Jiakao
690.1) concurred. Yu Xingwu (1979:22-25; see too 1996:3341-45) inter-
preted the graph as blood above a bowl (thus a sacrifice in which one
offered the blood of animal or human victims); also Lu Shixian
(cited by Ikeda) read it as ji. The graph form of ji is given by the Y edi-
tors. Shuowen defines ji as, "a sacrifice in which one
smears the blood of a cut-up victim." I note that Jiyun and a subcom-
mentary to Zhouli read ji as interchangeable with the ji, Z, "to cut up"
(ZWDZ:1654: no. 1928; cf. GSR, no. 517p), that appeared in the Shuowen
definition. The existence of the phrase -L, "cut up a ewe," in Shan-
hai jing, "Zhongshan jing " (ZWDZ:1647: no. 1902.1), moreover,
accords with the "will cut up (and offer the blood of) (?) a cow"-
found in [94A-C], where the ji sacrifice seems to have been linked to the
meng sacrifice (see Glossary entry), which may also have involved
the offering of blood. For these reasons, I tentatively adopt the meaning
given at the start of this entry.314

the symbolic value of huo, what it meant, rather than in preventing it from
happening.
The divinations about bad dreams ended with "there might be some-
thing ominous" versus "there will be nothing ominous" (versus
) (sample at MZ 396.1; cf. Song Zhenhao 2006). And other ominous
occurences ended "there will be something ominous" and "there will be
nothing ominous" (and ). Partly for that reason and partly
on graphic grounds, I extend Takashima's conclusions to suggest that huo
referred in origin to bad omens revealed by the pyromantic cracks and, by
extension, to ominous happenings in general. The rarely recorded authors
of huo were Di (as in Yingcang 1133f [not in Y and MZ]) and Huan, the local
River Power (as in [207AB]), and such authors were named only when the
"something ominous," like an enemy attack or a flood, had already mate-
rialized or was about to materialize. I believe that material disasters, by
contrast, were represented by the word jiu, "calamities" (q.v.); huo, evi-
dently, could produce or be incarnated in jiu. This is demonstrated by the
numerous Period I cases in which an apotropaic charge ending with A,
"there will be nothing ominous," was followed by a prognostication that
started, "There will be calamities" ([322AB], etc.).
to have meant, "there
I thus understand the common incantation
will be no omens (in the cracks)" and thus, by extension, "there will be
nothing ominous (in the world, both symbolic and material, at large)."
The impact of many of the divinations, accordingly, was not to discover if
something was in itself "a disaster," but whether, as in the case of dreams,
it had ominous, hence disastrous, implications:
[314A] 寅卜設貞:王夢兄丁佳禍

dreamed of Brother Ding is (wei) ominous (huo). 32"

It may also be noted that huo, "bad omens," and hai, "harm" (see
Glossary entry) were on occasion used in virtually identical contexts. Two
scapula fragments, Heji 23113 and 23114 (both II. Chu), for example, were
divined on the same day, jiaxu (day 11), and started with the identical
charge, ZFZ, "On the next day, yihai (day 12), (we) will per-
form the rong ritual to Xiao Yi (K12)." The first charge, however, ended
32. Brother Ding was probably an older brother of Wu Ding who had pre-deceased him
(cf. Zhang Bingquan, commentary to Bingbian 86, p. 118).

with the incantation and postface, LA, "There will be no harm
(hai). In the first moon." The second ended with the variant incantation
and postface,. EEA, "There will be nothing ominous (huo).' In the
regulating zheng moon." This suggests that huo (bad omens caused in the
bones or elsewhere by the divinee or other Powers) and hai (harm caused
by ancestors and other Powers) were close in meaning.
ji: "sick, be sick” (Y1177.2–84.2; Sōran, nos. 0964–65; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 3067). See too Qiu Xigui (1988:123; 2000:186) for chuang, "to lie sick,"
but as Karlgren notes (GSR, no. 1260b), there are no supporting text exam-
ples for the definition that appears in Shuowen. Ding Shan, Qu Wanli, Yang
Shuda, and others all read the word as ji (JGWZ:2518-22; Yan Yiping
1951:1:6a-10b; JGWD:837-39). The charge, prognostication, and verifica-
tion of [272A-C] suggest that ji could refer to an acute or fatal sickness, a
reading supported by the early meaning of "urgent" that was also present
in the word (e.g., Schuessler 1987:269). In the Shiming one notes:
EZ, "a sickness that arises in a hurry" (ZWDZ:9604: no. 22629, mean-
ing 2), as well as the fact that ji in early texts could also mean "violent"
(Shijing) or "hurried" (Lunyu; GSR, no. 494a–c). Cf. Harper (1999:874-83;
2002) on the Eastern Zhou to Han texts. Even in Taiwan, an anthropologist
"encountered cases in which ancestors were said to have brought about
serious debility or even death" (Ahern 1973:201; cf. DuBois 2004:67).
ji /:"to cut up (and offer the blood [of a sacrificial victim])" (Y1276.2).
The reading and meaning of oracle-bone has puzzled scholars; see, e.g.,
Sōran, nos. 4855, 5518. As Ikeda (1964: commentary to Houbian 1.25.9 and
2.39.9) observed, the meaning is uncertain, and we can only conclude with
certainty that it was a verb of sacrifice; Qu Wanli (commentary to Jiakao
690.1) concurred. Yu Xingwu (1979:22-25; see too 1996:3341-45) inter-
preted the graph as blood above a bowl (thus a sacrifice in which one
offered the blood of animal or human victims); also Lu Shixian
(cited by Ikeda) read it as ji. The graph form of ji is given by the Y edi-
tors. Shuowen defines ji as, "a sacrifice in which one
smears the blood of a cut-up victim." I note that Jiyun and a subcom-
mentary to Zhouli read ji as interchangeable with the ji, Z, "to cut up"
(ZWDZ:1654: no. 1928; cf. GSR, no. 517p), that appeared in the Shuowen
definition. The existence of the phrase -, "cut up a ewe," in Shan-
hai jing, "Zhongshan jing " (ZWDZ:1647: no. 1902.1), moreover,
accords with the Л-"will cut up (and offer the blood of) (?) a cow"-
found in [94A-C], where the ji sacrifice seems to have been linked to the
meng sacrifice (see Glossary entry), which may also have involved
the offering of blood. For these reasons, I tentatively adopt the meaning
given at the start of this entry.316
Working for His Majesty
ji: "till, cultivate." The oracle-bone graph (S27.1; Y74.1) has generally
been interpreted as ji (Sōran, no. 0571; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0093; Qiu
Xigui 2000:222). It is thought to be a picture of a man holding the long
handle of a two-pronged, wooden digging stick or spade, while tread-
ing down on it with his foot. See, e.g., Chen Mengjia 1956:533, 540; Shira-
kawa 1956:139:887; Shima 1958:342; 1980:17; Yu Xingwu 1964:153; Qiu
Xigui 1989b:19; 2000:222; Peng Bangjiong 1997:364-71. In later texts the
word was also written or , all three forms-in certain contexts, and
specifically in the context of the jitian ritual, "the tilling of the sacred
field" -being interchangeable (GSR, no. 798i, a', b'; Ōshima 1960; Zhu
Qifeng, ed., 1965:1:603).
Rao Zongyi and Shima have argued, in fact, that oracle-bone ji referred
not simply to ordinary cultivation but to the specific, ritual tilling of the
sacred field by the king. Rao's argument (1959:708) depended solely on
identifying oracle-bone for "till" (in Heji 21595) as yi 1, "exert, do service,
labor," and then proposing, on the basis of passages in the Mengzi and the
Guangya, that the phrase in that inscription referred to the zhu
Elabor tax by which the Shang rulers borrowed the people's strength to
help cultivate the gong tian. I find this speculative. Shima's argument
(1958:327, 340-42 [also 1980:18-18]), followed, e.g., by Akatsuka 1977:588),
depended upon reading oracle-bone guan (or #) as guan; he then
identified two agricultural rituals recorded in the oracle-bone inscriptions.
One was the shu guan, a ceremony in which millet was planted and
an ale libation offered to the Powers; the second was the tian guan, a
ceremony in which the field was cultivated and an ale libation offered. Shu
did not simply mean "millet, plant millet"--but in harvest divinations
such as :, "Divined: 'If (we) call out "Plant millet," (we) will
receive harvest' (Heji 9540)-shu referred specifically to a ritual planting,
conducted by the king or his ministers, leading the zhong to till the jitian
sacred field (a classic account of which is given by Guoyu, "Zhouyu
shang "[Wei Zhao, ed., 1929:6b-8b; Hart 1973:275-78]). Since I
now find the reading of uncertain (see the Glossary entry for guan),
I am not certain that we can directly associate the Shang king's activities
with the jitian of later times. The fact that other officers, besides the king,
could engage in the ji cultivation, as in [165CD] and Heji 13505, indicates
that such cultivation was not the exclusive prerogative of the king, nor
was it limited to a specific location. Much cultivation was undoubtedly
imbued with religious significance, and its commencement was probably
accompanied by ritual acts (see too pp. 136ff.).
ji /(?): "raise, assemble" (Y666.1; Sōran, no. 0529). The various theo-
ries are summarized by Ikeda (1964, commentary to Houbian 2.38.9; Zhang
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
317
Yongshan 1982:214). I previously translated the word as "issue formal
orders (?)" (Keightley 1969:85). The oracle-bone graph is composed with
one or two short-tailed birds (##) perched on a stand (which some schol-
ars have transcribed as ran #). It is generally agreed that the word had the
meaning of ji, "assemble, gather together," but a modern graph or its
equivalent has not been identified. It should be noted that another Shang
graph, composed with a short-tailed bird over a mu *, has been taken
as the Shang graph for ji (Sōran, no. 0520). I use "ji (?)" to indicate that
the graph, which appears in my transcription of [280], indicates only the
word's semantic range, not its modern graph form.
ji zong : "to approach (and to make an offering) in the temple." My
understanding of this phrase is tentative. Ji was used as the name of a
diviner (as in Heji 24179), but when used as a verb it is generally thought
to have had the sense of "approach" (in a ritual context); see the entries
at Sōran, no. 0656; cf. Zhu Fenghan 1990:6, who understands the ji in the
sense of jiu xiang, "to approach with offerings." That conclusion
is based partly on the graph, which is taken to be a picture of a person
approaching food (Luo Zhenyu, cited at JGWZ:1749-50. See too Akatsuka
1977:44-45). I find the term ambiguous in some cases, but my tentative
conclusion is that, in the oracle-bone phrase ji zong E, it was the Shang
ritualists, not the Powers, who were the subject of ji, and that the phrase
did not mean that a Power was to approach the temple (as most scholars
have taken the phrase to mean) but that the Shang were to approach, and
thus make an offering in the temple, to a Power.
I base this conclusion on the following evidence:

The presence of the prohibitive, controllable negative wu in [315B]
and [316B] indicates that the ji zong activity involved the action of
the ritualists, not of the Powers. And the presence of the san niu
"three cows," in [317] (and also, probably, the shi +, “ten,” in [316]),
as the object of ji zong, again indicates that the ji zong activity involved
a Shang choice of the number of victims to be offered. This view
would seem to be supported by the charges on the following scapula
fragment:
[318A] 五牢于盖
"(Offer) five penned cows to Yue (the Mountain Power) (Yue)."
[318B] 九牢于[河]

[318C] 辛未貞:車上甲即宗于河

He." (I-II. Li) (Tunnan 2272)
The charges seem to be concerned with the sacrifices addressed to Yue,
He, and Shang Jia, the last to take place in the temple dedicated to He
(cf. Tunnan 1116). But I cannot exclude a different translation of [318C]:
*"It should be Shang Jia who approaches the temple at the He." In this
view, the ji zong term would have involved an attempt to determine
which Powers were descending to which temple, so that, presumably,
they could be suitably addressed. The use of the copula hui, however,
in this charge, does not, in my view, support such an interpretation. The
"chain of communication” charges in [319A-K], which sought to deter-
mine which ancestor was being hosted by which ancestor, did not employ
such a formula.

jiu: "calamities" (Y572.2-77.1; Sōran, nos. 0411, 0715, etc.; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 0838). Karlgren gives "fault, blame, inauspicious, calamity, wick-
edness" (GSR 1068a). I follow the analysis of Qiu Xigui (1986:204) in read-
ing oracle-bone * (Y572.2) and (Y326.1), in the appropriate contexts,
as jiu, "calamities" or "cause calamities." Other readings, in particu-
lar sui ("harm"; e.g., JGWZ:2997-3001), sha ("pernicious, impre-
cate" [Takashima 1988a:115]), and sui ("evil forces, noxious influences"
[Takashima 1990:21]) have been proposed. Takashima also offers "to lay
a curse, to hex, to give pernicious influences, to be hostile" (2010:II:152).
HYDZ:238 offers "hex." See too Kunst (1985:163-66), who discusses
jiu, the most common "disaster word" in Yijing, and its possible Shang
antecedents.
Precisely how jiu differed in meaning from huo (q.v.) is not clear; the
two words do, on occasion, appear in the same charge, as in:

Consider too the charge of Heji 34708, X, "There will be calamities
but they will not be ominous (?)." Mickel (1976:79-94, 100-110) discusses
the previous scholarship and oracular environments in which "calami-
ties" and "bad omens" might occur. He concludes that the Shang used
jiu to refer to particular disasters whose existence had been revealed
by divination; that jiu was always used of disasters that befell the Shang
and never of difficulties that the Shang imposed on other peoples; that
jiu could be caused by ancestral or other Powers, and that those ances-
tors (with the irregular addition of Huang Yin ) were restricted to
those on the main line of descent. Since such Powers were frequently the
authors of jiu, I translate 4, literally, "there will be calamities" or as
"(some Power is bringing) calamities (on us)." That jiu, "calamities," and
hai,"harm" (see Glossary entry), were related yet distinct is revealed322
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
323
by the prognostication on Yibian 6948 (S308.4) (= Heji 2960f), L
"There will be no harm (but) there will be calamities"; this suggests that
"harm" (hai) may have been more feared than "calamities" (jiu). This may
have been because jiu was merely a sign of general, even accidental,
misfortune, whereas hai was a sign of a focused and intended harm,
generated by some Power, frequently ancestral.
Why the diviners used two different graphs, and *, to write jiu
needs more study.
ku tian: "to open up fields" (Jimbun 281, n. 3; Y465.1-2; Sōran, nos.
1591-93; Kolb 1992:117-20: "make arable"; HYDZ:639, 641: "to reclaim
land"). See [1], [145A], [190]-[192ABFG]. The Shang employed at least
three related graphs to record the opening up of fields for agriculture:
,(S173.2), and (S502.4); see JGWZ:4001-08; Yu Xingwu 1972;
1996:1192-201; Kwang-chih Chang 1980:223; Zhang Yongshan 1982:202-
03, 204-05; Wen Shaofeng and Yuan Tingdong 1983:196-98; Qiu Xigui
1989:30-31; YXFX:178; Kolb 1992:117-20; Liu Huan 2002:120-23. I tran-
scribe them all as ku .
All depict hands heaping up earth. Guo Moruo, Dong Zuobin, Shima
Kunio, and Rao Zongyi are among those who agree that it is equivalent
to the word ku, which in the small seal style of the Qin and Han was
written with one hand (the right) over the earth element; it does in fact
appear in precisely this form in a Shang inscription (Jiabian 377 = Heji
34239). According to the Shuowen, "Between the Ru and Ying (rivers), the
application of labor to the soil is called ku ()." The
meaning of the word was not limited to cultivation, but included, as Dong
Zuobin noted (1945:II:4:6a), the idea of ploughing, planting, and opening
up new soil. See too Rao Zongyi 1959:I:257; Shima 1958:494; Shirakawa
1969-74:13:138-39. Qiu Xigui (1989:32) also provides a valuable discus-
sion, finding it proper to interpret the word as yong, "to bank up" (see
next paragaph). The theory of Ding Shan (1956:38) and Chen Mengjia
(1956:538) that it should be read as fen, "to fertilize," is not, in my view,
convincing. See too the discussion at Jimbun 2363, n. 2.
The first pictograph, ", is simply an alternate of the second (Rao
Zongyi 1958:1:257). The meaning of the third,, however, is not certain.
Dong Zuobin (1945:II:4:6a) took it as an elaborate form of the first two, with
the central yong element depicting an agricultural implement. Qu Wanli
(commentary to Jiakao 3510.4) believed it meant "to open up new fields."
Shima (1958:494-95) defined the meaning more precisely. He started with
the three elements of hands, earth, and yong in the modern character yong
, defined by the Shuowen as "wall" () (Shirakawa 1969-74:13:136).
These elements appear related to the character in question, which always
precedes the word tian, "field," in the Shang inscriptions. Shima believed
accordingly-arguing strictly from the picture of this last graph (two earth
elements, one above the other, with two hands to the side)-that the graph
meant to build boundaries by piling up earth, and hence that meant
to develop the land by raising earth barriers between the fields (cf. Qiu
Xigui [1989:32], who reaches a similar conclusion). I am inclined to accept
Shima's hypothesis because the building of boundaries between the fields
was a prominent feature of ancient Chinese farming (see, e.g., Shi, "Mian
"[Mao no. 237]; "Gong Liu "[Mao no. 250]; "Songgao " [Mao
no. 259]; "Han yi " [Mao no. 261]; Keightley 1969:310-12), and because
the character yong appears to have had precisely this sense in two of
these "Daya" odes (Mao nos. 259, 261). But until we can find evidence from
the inscriptions themselves that the verb had this special meaning, I think
it safer to limit myself to Dong Zuobin's more comprehensive meaning of
"to work the soil," with the understanding that the exploitation of land
and the raising of barriers may be included in its meaning.
I do not believe that these three verbs simply meant "to cultivate,"
because all three are invariably used as the verb immediately preceding
the object tian. Since tian was always the direct object of the verb, I
believe that it implied doing something to the fields, such as making them,
clearing them, making boundaries for them, as well as farming them-"the
application of labor to the soil" as the Shuowen said. There is no English
word that combines the ideas of clearing and demarcating the land and
cultivating it, of making the fields usable, as well as using them. I shall use
the clumsy phrase, "to open up fields." Zhang Zhenglang (1973:93-103),
who proposed that the oracle-bone characters in question were equiva-
lent to the pou found in the Shijing (where it has the meaning "collect,
assemble, be together" [Schuessler 1987:464]), also agreed that the phrase
pou tian referred to land reclamation. (See too the discussion at Van-
dermeersch 1980:223; Kolb 1991:31.)
Akatsuka (1977:209-10) cited a series of inscriptions, proposing that
the ku tian was a ritual related to harvest prayers, and that the Shang king
picked communities (Akatsuka referred to them as states), such as the Jing
and Long of [192FG], to represent the states of the Four Regions (si
fang on this term, see Keightley 2000:68-72; Chang Yuzhi 2006). While
I am ready to believe that the opening up of fields had its ritual dimension,
I believe that it involved substantial agricultural labor; and I suspect, pend-
ing further demonstration, that the king selected the places for a variety of
economic and political reasons rather than a purely symbolic one.
lao,:"penned cow, penned sheep" (Y581.1-82.2, 590.1-96.2, 598.2-
602.2; Sōran, no. 0082; Yu Xingwu 1996, nos. 1548, 1564; Fiskesjö 2001:70,324

78, n. 134; Yuan and Flad 2005:253-54).36 HYDZ:648, 664, etc. give for
"specially reared bovines"; HYDZ:29, 220, etc., give for "specially reared
ovines." The oracle-bone graph depicts a cow or sheep penned under a
roof; cf. the Shuowen definition, ‚¯‡Ø±, “lao is a pen for enclos-
ing and rearing cows and horses," thus suggesting an animal especially
reared for sacrifice. Hayashi (1968:40, fig. 17.34) gives two bronze clan-
name inscriptions where the animal is surrounded by the pen. See too
Chen Mengjia 1956:556; Zhang Bingquan 1968:214-15; Serruys 1974:87-88;
Yao Xiaosui 1984:32-35; Liu Yuan 1999:91-92; cf. Fiskesjö 2001:143.
Scholars, however, disagree about the precise meaning of oracle-bone
lao. There are basically three major positions. What we may call the tra-
ditional view is represented by those who argue that Shang usage resem-
bled the assumed usage of the Zhou or Han ritual texts, as explicated, for
example, in Zheng Xuan's comment that **, "a cow, sheep,
and pig together form a lao" (ZLZY, j. 6:7a). Thus, e.g., Hu Houxuan (1939)
argued that the Shang lao consisted of a paired bull and cow; more recently,
Yan Yiping (1970; 1982) concluded that a lao consisted of one cow and one
sheep, that a da lao referred to one cow, one sheep, and one pig together.
Zhang Zhenglang (1983a:19) suggests in passing that a lao probably con-
sisted of two (penned) sheep. Such views have been challenged on archae-
ological grounds by Huang Zhanyue (1983:942) who notes that no trace
of a cow, sheep, and pig as a unit has ever been excavated, that remains of
sheep and pigs were not frequent, and that the most common sacrificial
victim (after dogs) was the legs of one cow or one sheep.
element
A second position has suggested that the niu + or yang
used to write or had no semantic significance and that the two
graphs were interchangeable. Thus Qiu Xigui (1978:168-69; cf. 1983-
85:302; 2000:207) had argued that had, in origin, been a way of writing
, "a penned calf," although he admits that it may have subsequently
developed a special meaning. That, in these few cases at least, the engrav-
ers appear to have used a graph containing the sheep element to record
a cow victim, lends support to the view that the and elements in
lao had no firm semantic reference (see too Li Xueqin 1991:166, 175-76).
A similar situation appears to have obtained in the case of the cult verb
(Y586.1), which appears to have had the sense of "(ritually) drown (a
victim)." As Kwok-ching Chow (1982:347, n. 17; see too Zhou Guozheng
1983:295, n. 15) has noted, although the graph was written with a 4 ele-
ment it could take, as in Qianbian 1.32.5 (= Heji 14559), ¥, "penned sheep"
as its object-victim. Chow concludes that the verb could mean both "to
sink a cow" as well as, simply, "to sink."
36. For my use of "cow" I follow definition 3 in The American Heritage Dictionary (1992): "a
domesticated bovine of either sex or any age."
The third position, argued by Yao Xiaosui (1984:30, 32), concludes that
referred to cows and to sheep (cf. Yu Xingwu 1996:1517–22). Indeed,
I would suggest on the basis of the statistics he has compiled that xiao lao
, "(young penned cows =) penned calves" (13 cases) probably grew
into da lao, "(large penned cows =) penned cows" (7 cases), just as /\
,"(young penned sheep =) penned lambs" (14 cases) grew into X,
"(large penned sheep =) penned sheep" (5 cases). I believe that the graphs
did have a different meaning (see too Liu Yuan 1999), partly because, as
Yang Shengnan (1985:4, 7) has noted of Shang ancestral cult, offerings of
cows were more highly valued than those of sheep; it is plausible, accord-
ingly, that the animal depicted under the roof in the two lao characters
would, in many cases (cf. Chow's conclusions about in the previous
paragraph), have had significance. I maintain that distinction accordingly
in my transcriptions and translations, "cows" and "sheep.”
lin : "granary" (Y747.1-2; Sōran, no. 0695). It is not certain (in [140]) the
graph in question should be read lin, as Chen Mengjia (1956:536) takes it.
It might also be interpreted as bi, "border region," and even as a place-
name. See Ye Yusen 1933: juan 5, pp. 8b-9a; JGWZ:1877–78; JGWD:613-14;
Yang Shengnan 1992:82-83; Yu Xingwu 1996, nos. 2016-17; Guo Xiaowu
2001:93-94.
lu: "burn" (?) (Y821.1-2; Sōran, nos. 5146-48; Yu Xingwu 1996, nos. 1698,
2208, 2217). I tentatively follow Guo Moruo (1972:4) and others in taking
the word as an early form of lu, "brazier, stove," a reference to the way
the victim was offered. JGWD:534 agrees that the oracle-bone graph was
an early form of lu, also "brazier, stove" (but he then proposes, with
no evidence, that the word meant "cut up" when used in sacrifice). If lu
served as a place-name rather than a verb of sacrifice, then [283] would
refer to the offering of "a pig from Lu." See also Yu Xingwu 1996:2129-39.
ma: "horse," "Horse(-chariot officer[s])" (Chao Lin 1982:83; Y624.1-27.1;
Sōran, no. 1152 [JGWD:1067]; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 1630). Since the Shang
did not ride much on horseback, if at all (Yang Shengnan 1982a:379, 381;
Shaughnessy 1988:233-34; Zhong Bosheng 1992:64-66, 141-46; Mair 2003;
Drews 2004), and since they attached much symbolic value to their horse-
drawn chariots, I find attractive the argument of Wang Guimin (1983) that
main contexts of hunt and war referred not just to horses, but to horse-
chariots, so that the Duo Ma (Y624.1-2) would have been the "Many
Horse(-chariot officers)" ([77], [106], [107A], [117A], [222], [243AB], [244],
[332B]). There would have been little point in sending "Horse (officers)"
into battle if they had not been equipped with the weapons with which to326
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
327
fight (also see Han Feng 1982:426). "This book will argue that the riding of
horses in battle" - as opposed to horse-chariots-"did not become histori-
cally significant until the eight century BC" (Drews 2004:1; also see Průšek
1971:88-95). And, in particular, metal stirrups are crucial; they have only
been found "from the fourth century... in northern China, and from
China the innovation worked its way westward, although not very rap-
idly" (Drews 2004:167, n. 101; cf. Boodberg 1979:112-13; Goodrich 1984;
Dien 1986; Qi Dongfang 1993; Yates 2002:57-66). "The very different situ-
ation in the Far East may have been due to the fact that the ridden horse
(as opposed to the chariot horse) began to be used in China only very late"
(Littauer 1981:104). Men in armor would probably have been too heavy to
ride astride (Epstein 1971:100-101; Engels 1978:128-29; Smith 2009:128).
Some support for reading ma in this larger, horse-chariot sense can be
found in the sections of the Zhouli (ZLZY, j. 54-57) that deals with the
position of the Da Sima, "Grand Supervisor of the Horse." (Hucker
[1985:471] gives a literal translation: "great manager of mounts," and thus
the "Minister of War" in Zhou times.) In discussing his functions and his
subordinates, the Zhouli mentions chariots at least fourteen times, horses
only five times: e.g., "He ranges the (warriors) of the
chariots and the soldiers on foot, following the order of battles" (ZLZY, j.
56:5b; Biot, tr. 1851:II:176). It is evident that the Zhouli references to chari-
ots assume the presence of horses to pull them. I assume, similarly, that
references to Horse officers in the Shang inscriptions assumed the pres-
ence of the chariots that they pulled.
It should be noted, however, that Kolb (1991:57-58), endorsed by
Shaughnessy (1996:162, n. 5), rejects Wang Guimin's argument (and also Yu
Xingwu 1955), noting that in the Western Zhou "the chariot remained...
primarily an implement of status" (Von Dewall 1964; Creel 1970:153-54).
The numbers of chariots bear on this. In [222] we find: "(We) will deploy
the Horse(-chariot officers)-the left, right, and centre-300 (of them)."
If we count three men to a chariot, that will figure as 100 chariots, which
is not unreasonable. (We have also a summary of Shi Zhangru 1952:453,
447-79 at Chao Lin 1982:83.) We also have reference to 50 chariots (Heji
11459) and 30 chariots ([243AB], [244]); see Takashima 1984-85:231-33;
2010:II:393. It is evident that the chariot had started as "an implement of
status" even in the Shang but it was also a fighting vehicle.
mao (Y1335.1-39.2). HYDZ:190 to "split open." For the argument, origi-
nating with Wang Guowei (Lin Zutai 1978:57), that mao should be read
as liu, with the meaning of "split open, divide in two," see Takashima
1979-80:52; 1984:36, n. 11, 65-69; 1984-85:293; cf. Kwok-ching Chow (1982:
205, 206, 219, and passim), who offers "dismember-sacrifice." It is generally
accepted that the verb involved sacrificial killing (Sōran, no. 1702). Kwok-
ching Chow (1982:219-21; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:250-52) argues that
mao was a type B verb (defined on pp. 286 and 298 of the Glossary). For a state-
ment of an alternate view, which takes liu, following the Erya definition, to
mean chen, "display," see Lian Shaoming 1989:130–31.
meng: "sacrifice-in-blood covenant." I am not certain of the reading
or meaning of oracle-bone ✓ (and related forms; Y1022.2), which appears
in [94A-C]. I follow JGWZ:2273, Y1023.1, and Sōran, no. 0843 in reading
as meng; as JGWD:749 proposes, it appears to have been a method of
sacrifice. See too Yu Xingwu 1996:2636-38. Meng in Eastern Zhou texts
has frequently been glossed as ming, in the sense of "announce matters
(a covenant) to the spirits" (ZWDZ:9849: no. 23553, meaning 1). Such a
ritual was thought to involve smearing the lips with blood. Another inter-
pretation, in fact, reads oracle-bone ✗) as xue III, "the offering of blood in
a bowl" (e.g., Qu Wanli's commentary to Jiakao 221; Tsung-tung Chang
1970:89, n. 3; Kaogu 1975:42; Yao Xiaosui and Xiao Ding 1985:317, Tunnan
2707; Lian Shaoming 1989a:49-50, who argues for the existence of three
different oracle-bone graphs, equivalent to meng,"covenant," min IIII,
"bowl," and xue III, "blood," that were used interchangeably; see too ji
in the Glossary). My translation, "sacrifice-in-blood covenant," clumsily
combines both interpretations.
mi*: a variety of "millet" (Y697.1; Sōran, no. 0890; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
1888), offered by the Li-group diviners (Qiu Xigui 1985:15).
ni : "grain, plant grain (?)" (Y529.2; Sōran, no. 4231; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
1500). Although most scholars agree that the oracle-bone graph should be
read as ni, "rice, to plant rice," its Shang meaning, since it appears in only
one inscription, [166A], is hard to determine. According to the Shuowen,
ni was the name for rice that grows naturally, without being planted (
今年落,來年自生,謂之,“Rice, that this year falls to the ground and
next year grows naturally, is called ni"). Yu Xingwu (1996:1436) and Qiu
Xigui (1989:17) rely upon this definition to understand oracle-bone ni in
the sense of "plant rice." This, however, requires some stretching of the
Shuowen definition, which particularly specified that rice called ni had not
been planted. That the Yupian E, a multi-author, multi-period work,
although it is more distant in time from the Shang, defines ni as "wheat"
(xiao mai/) introduces a further element of doubt (for these definitions
see ZWDZ:10448, no. 25546). Given these uncertainties, I simply treat the
word as "grain (?)."328

329
qi Z (Y1283.2-85.2). Sōran, no. 0037, lists a variety of meanings that schol-
ars have assigned to the oracle-bone character. These range from qi Z,
"stop, finish" (as in Heji 22650, 22655); to qi Z, "to reach to, down to"
(as in [322A] and Yingcang 1923); to qi, "seek, request" (as in Heji 9395
and 22913; cf. Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 3326; Qiu Xigui 2000:290-91; Shen Pei
2002a; Takashima 2002:133, n. 36). In some cases, however, such as [236], I
find the meaning hard to determine.
qi (Y1080.2-81.2; Sōran, no. 0577; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2815). Qi had a
sense of tentative expectancy that gave it the force of a conditional in cer-
tain contexts: some times "perhaps," some times "may" and "ought." Thus
[97AB], "... there may (qi) be something ominous /... there
will be nothing ominous" (cf. Takashima 1996:9, 15). The verb expresses
"might" or "may" or "it shall be going to" (HYDZ:133, 356). Shaughnessy
(1996a) translated qi as "expects" in the Shang bone inscriptions.37 In the
Li diviner group inscriptions—with scapulas rather than plastrons, and
favoring a form of divination in which they treated a single topic rather
than the positive-negative in complementary fashion (Keightley 1988:373-
77)-it served as "will, intent" or "assertive," used with controllable activ-
ity (Takashima, comment of 24 May 2000).
There has probably been more discussion than agreement on the role
of qi in the oracle-bone inscriptions; see, e.g., Serruys 1986:204-22;
Takashima 1994 (which resumes much of the recent scholarship); 1996;
2010:II:714; Keightley 1988:373-77; 1995; Nivison 1996; Campbell 2004:22,
n. 12. I believe that, in many of the Bin charge pairs of Period I, as Serruys
(1974:25-33) had noted, the qi is frequently associated with the less desir-
able of the two alternatives, thus serving to weaken the undesired outcome,
as in [16A], [93A], etc. Building on previous scholarship-e.g., Yu Xingwu
1979:427-31 (see too 1996:2807-10); Takashima 1984:68; 1996; JGWJ:295-
96-I treat qi, as it appears in the concluding apodosis of a charge, as a
preverbal modal particle of tentative expectancy (cf. Takashima 1988a:124,
128, "perhaps, perchance"), which leads me to translate the verb as a sub-
junctive, as in [132B] and in:
[323A]己卯卜殻貞: 不其雨
Crack-making on jimao (day 16), Que divined: "It ought (qi)
not to rain."
37. And as a dating criteria: "several of these poems (Mao nos. 269, 271, 272, 273) use the
modal auxiliary qi, familiar from the dedications of Western Zhou bronze inscriptions
where it is used with the prayerful meaning 'may, would that' (e.g., zi sun qi yong bao yong
, 'may sons and grandsons eternally treasure and use [this vessel]')" (Shaugh-
nessy 1997:178).
Crack-making on jimao, Que divined: "It will rain." (Prog-
nostication:) His Majesty read the cracks (and said): "If (qi) it
rains, it will be (wei) on a ren day." (Verification:) On renwu
(day 19), it really did rain. (I. Bin) (Heji 902f)
There are numerous other examples in this book-either for rain or
against: [104], [122AB], [123AB], [165EF], [175AB], [189C], [199AB], [200A-
C], [205], [250], [252], [253], [313AB]. I believe that when i was used in the
introductory protasis (as in [94A-F] and [313AB]), or when it was used in
a prognostication (as in [165FG]), that it should be understood as a fore-
runner of the full verb qi, "expect, wait for" (GSR, no. 952k), and having
the force of a conditional: thus, "expecting that"- →"it may be that" → "if
(or when) it be that" "if (or when)." The two uses are clearly related. As
Malmqvist proposed, the qi in later Zhou texts-like the word in Shang
oracle-bone inscriptions-"is a marker of modality."
→
In English, modality can be expressed in a great variety of lexical ways.
It may be expressed by nouns such chance, hope, intention...; by adjec-
tives such as possible, likely, necessary ; by main verbs...
..., and by the
modal verbs shall, should, will, would, can, could, may, might, must and
ought. (Malmqvist 1982:368-69)
A significant number of charge pairs, as Takashima and Serruys were
the first to explicate, were not presented in neutral opposition. 38 When they
appear as paired charges, I characterize charges as "strong," if they do not
contain the modal particle qi in their predicate, and "weak," if they
do. When charge pairs were "unbalanced" in this way, with one member
being strong and the other weak, it is evident that the weak charge was, in
virtually every case, the one whose consequences the king or his diviners
found the less desirable or favorable. Thus, in [132AB], the diviner Que
asserted in a strong, desirable charge, "We will receive millet harvest." He
formulated the weak, undesirable charge, with less force, "We might not
(qi) receive millet harvest."
In [323A], ], which I translate as "It may (qi) not rain," may have
been, "It cannot be expected to rain"; this was in contrast to the strong,
matching member of the charge pair, [323B] F, "It will rain," in which
uncertainty or mere expectation were not involved. Serruys, it should be
noted, consistently translates qi as a full verb, "anticipate" (1986:204-05
and passim); his translation of [132B] ##±***, for example, reads,
"We should not anticipate a (good) harvest" (1986:214, no. 87). See too
Lefeuvre 1985:192–94; Djamouri 1988, esp. 373-425.
38. Takashima 1973:267-305; Serruys 1974:25, 58. See too Keightley 1978:51, n. 124, 66, n.
41, 80, n. 89; 1982:303-04,308;1991:34-40;1997a:49-51.330
Working for His Majesty

3311
When qi appears in the protasis (identified by the first verb in a com-
pound or complex sentence), the expectant environment it establishes per-
mits us to translate that clause as a subordinate one. Thus, in a charge like
[251], we have
"(His Majesty expects [gi] to hunt =) If His
Majesty hunts, going and coming back there will be no disasters"; and in a
prognostication like Heji 14002, we have
"(Expecting it to be
[gi] =) If it be on a ding day that she gives birth, it will be good." As Kwok-
ching Chow (1982:159-70) and Takashima (1988:673-75; 1990:39-40) have
pointed out, it is the presence of two verbs-"hunts" and "going and com-
ing back" in the first case, "be on a ding day" and "be good" in the sec-
ond- and not the qi itself, that indicates a subordinate construction (as in
[61], where I translate the first clause conditionally even though no qi is
present). The qi, in other words, was not an obligatory marker of subordi-
nation, but it served, in my view, to reinforce the conditional expectation
on many occasions.
qin. I read it as qin, following JGWD:808; Y757.2; Sōran, no. 0908. It is
some kind of chamber or apartment, presumably in the palace.
Qin, qin (Y1091.1-95.2; Sōran, no. 1668; see too no. 1667; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 2824) was a name, as in [8B] and [22]-[25]. Qin seems to have
been the name of a hereditary group, descended from the royal house and
perhaps originally associated with hunting, whose members were gener-
als of the Shang army; see Shima 1958:464-66; Satō 1962:15-16; the discus-
sion in the commentary to Jimbun 344, n. 1; Y1082.2-87.1. The verb qin was
"catch," "capture," "bag," or "net" (e.g., Fiskesjö 2001:120): [194B], [202A],
[204], [231], [254], [256], [257].
There is no certainty about the reading of this name, partly because the
graph seems to have left no descendant in the Shuowen (e.g., JGWZ:2578-
79; Qiu Xigui 2000:330-31). The Shang graph consists of a net on a pole (?)
with a figure, which might be either a renor a bit above it (see, e.g.,
Keightley 1978:67, n. 47). The uncertainty extends to the related graph (and
word?) (Sōran, no. 1667), which some scholars read as bi, "a handnet
for catching birds" (e.g., GSR, no. 407; Takashima 1973:104-05; 2010:I:594
for Bingbian 379.3-4; Schuessler 1987:31) and others read as qin (=),
"bird, animal, catch" (e.g., S404.1; Gao Ming 1980:8; Y1082.2). Some schol-
ars simply give a modern transcription of and provide no phonetic
information at all (e.g., Chen Mengjia 1956:507). If oracle-bone is part of
the same word family as oracle-bone, and if may be read as anteced-
ent to qin, “catch, capture," then one has grounds for reading the name
of the Shang person as Qin; this is the way that a fair number of Chinese
scholars, in conversation, do pronounce it. If, on the contrary, one takes the
element as bi E, there are grounds for taking the oracle-bone form as bi
(see, e.g., Jimbun 344, n. 1; Takashima 1973:104; HYDZ:25, etc.).
qu X (Y232.2-36.1). The subject of much inconclusive discussion (JGWZ:
0933-37; JGWD:292-93; Sōran, no. 0366; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0681), it is
generally felt that this word had two main meanings: first as a verb of
sacrifice (a burnt offering [?]) (e.g., Heji 21113); second as a verb meaning
"to take, requisition," as in [16C], [107A], Heji 23721, and 26901. See, e.g.,
Shima 1958:306; Jimbun: no. 1, n. 8; no. 158, n. 1; JGWZ:937; Yao Xiaosui
1985:111-12; Qiu Xigui 2000:187. Tsung-tung Chang (1970:171, n. 1) takes
it as a symbolic offering of the left ear; cf. Serruys (1974:54), who translates
"make ear offerings."
ren: "man, men, persons" (Y1.1-10.2; Sōran, no. 0986, etc.; see too zhong-
ren). One of the commonest terms in the oracle inscriptions, but not
in connection with formalized ritual activities (Magoshi 1996). See p. 50.

For ri as a ritual term, see Itō 1956:22; Qu Wanli (commentary to Jiakao
398), who notes the existence of "day-cult" (ri), "night-cult" (xi), and
"yi-day cult" (yi ri); Qiu Xigui 1980a:21-22; Lian Shaoming 1989:54,
62; He Cheng 1994:72-83; Chang Yuzhi 1998:239-47. I believe that ri meant
"offer the day cult of the ancestor whose gan temple name was the gan of
that day." Since ri could mean both "day" and "sun," it is likely that such
daytime cult had a solar dimension.
san (?): "to separate, disperse" (ZWDZ:6145, no. 13570; Y514.1). Qiu
Xigui (1980:169; 1989:25), following an original insight of Yu Xingwu,
reads the Shang graph in question, as do most of the entries at Sōran, no.
0438, as referring to the cutting of vegetation to clear the land. I find this
reasonable; see the translations offered for [193AB]-[196A] and [201C].
But other readings have been proposed. Zhang Bingquan, for instance,
transcribed the graph in question as the place-name Chu (commentary
to Bingbian 286.1 [= Heji 10910f]; cf. Soran, no. 0757), a reading followed
by Serruys 1981:332. Takashima (1988:671) and Takashima (2010:I:495 for
Bingbian 286.1), by contrast, had read the word as ye , "field" (cf. Sōran,
nos. 0053, 1601). Qiu Xigui (1989:25-26) also includes the graph entered at
Sōran, no. 0439, as a fuller form used to write the same word; this is pos-
sible, and I have so treated it in my translations, but some of the scholars
cited there treat it as proper name, others as a method of hunting.
sang : "to lose," or sang zhong, "to lose zhong" (Y520.1-21.1; Sōran,
no. 0140; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 1447; Shen Jianhua 2004:11). See too the
Glossary entry for zhi zhong. Chen Mengjia (1956:607-08), Sun Miao
(1987:552-54), Peng Bangjiong (1990:37), and others had argued that this
phrase referred to loss of population by flight. He noted that just like
charges such as T, "Fan will not be (the one) to lose sheep"
(Qianbian 8.11.4 = Heji 20676), and X, "(We) might lose pigs" (Yicun
594-Heji 21233), sang zhong probably refers to the zhong escaping from the
king's control. A similar argument had been advanced by Satō Taketoshi
1954:17. But I can find no other inscription that refers to a loss of livestock,
either through roaming or enemy capture. I believe that the yang and
shi in these two inscriptions might well have referred to the Duo Yang
, "Many Sheep(-officers)," and Duo Shi X, "Many Pig(-officers),"
of the royal administration. If the putative Duo Yang (the title appears
isolation only once, on the fragmentary Heji 11183) were military figures
(there are no records of the Duo Shi), it is reasonable that such divinations
would have referred to battle casualties. I also find it unlikely that offi-
cers of fairly privileged status, including the zhong, would have sought
to flee the king's control. See Keightley 1969:88-89; Qiu Xigui 1983a:17;
1992a:325; Lian Shaoming 1995a:108.
in
San Zu 三族. See Wu Zu Shu 五族戍 in Glossary.
shang (?): "afflict" (Y203.2; Sōran, no. 0681). It is a picture of an arrow
lodged in the side of a man. It is another kind of word that meant "disas-

sheng : "to present, offer" (when used as a verb) (Y1236.2-38.1; Sōran,
nos. 1628, 5478, etc.). Taiping Chang (1981:274-77) gives "make ascend."
Takashima (1988-89:37-38) gives "makes the offering to." Lefeuvre (1976-
78:60) notes that sheng frequently referred to the presentation of decapi-
tated (or dismembered) victims (fa (*). The ritual was first divined in the
late Chu-group inscriptions and became more important after that (Zhu
Fenghan 1990:7). It is often thought that the oracle-bone graph (Sōran,
no. 5478) was antecedent to the sheng sacrifice recorded in Yi li "Jin
li
(e.g., Hu Houxuan 1973:54). Since that sacrifice was addressed
to Nature Powers, while the sheng of the oracle bones was an activity
addressed to ancestors (as in Heji 34047) (see Chen Bangfu, quoted
at JGWZ:4109), the filiation is unclear. It is evident, however, that, as a
verb, sheng had the general sense of "raise, elevate, present" in many early
texts. As a noun, sheng referred to a ritual location; on this usage, see Zhu
Fenghan 1990:7-8.
shi
(Y1125.2-30.1; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2933; Takashima 2010:II:41-42).
For the view that the oracle-bone word could be read as shi E, li E, or shi
, which were originally the same word, see Sōran, no. 0004. That in cer-
tain contexts like [69], involving the setting up of officers across the land-
scape, one might also be read as shi (, "envoy" (or as shi , "emissary"
in [305A-F]), see Qiu Xigui 1983:3. See too Hu Houxuan 1985.
shi (?): "consort" (Y95.2-100.2; Sōran, no. 1242; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 0225; Takashima 2010:II:238). There is general agreement that the ora-
cle-bone graph was used by the ritualists of Periods II and V to iden-
tify the consorts of the main-line kings, but the graph seems to have left
no identifiable descendant. For a summary of some of the speculations
that have been proposed, see, e.g., JGWZ:1191-95; Akatsuka 1977:379-82;
Yu Xingwu 1996:241-55; see too other entries at Sōran, no. 1242. That the
word was equivalent to qie, "secondary wife," and mu , "Mother,"
has been well demonstrated by Zhang Zhenglang (1948, quoted at JGWZ:
1183-91); see too Chen Mengjia 1956:379; Vandermeersch 1977:281-82;334

Chang Yuzhi 1987:15-16. Chen Hanping (1989:58) notes that, on the basis
of chou being used as a loan for ju in Shi, Mao no. 220, the oracle-

or qiu, which appear in the Shi in the sense of "mate, companion";
Karlgren (1964, no. 709), however, rejects the loan, originally proposed
by Zheng Xuan, as "out of the question." The Y editors (Y95.2) offer
both shuang and shi as modern transcriptions for oracle-bone, but
use the shuang in the body of their text. I follow Chen Mengjia (1956:379)

shi : "fertilize" (Y13.1; Sōran, no. 1011), as in [169]. The oracle-bone

Shi: "Ancestor" (Y398.2-408.2; Sōran, no. 0009, etc.; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
1118). In oracle-bone usage, the word appears to have been a term, per-
haps a synecdoche, for a tablet or altar stand used for "Ancestor." See the
scholarship cited at Keightley 1978:17, n. 71; for the view that oracle-bone
shi should be understood as zhu E, the ancestral tablet, see Tang Lan and
Chen Mengjia, cited by Yang Shengnan 1985:3. I capitalize the word to
indicate that Shi referred to the Ancestors in their ritual embodiment. For
the various types of Shi designated in the inscriptions-Da Shi ("The
Greater Ancestors"), Yuan Shi ("The Primary Ancestors"), Shang Shi
E("The Superior Ancestors"; a single instance, on Heji 102), Zhong Shi
("The Middle Ancestors"), etc.-see Chao Fulin 1989.
shou .The Shang graph (Y1215.2-19.1; Sōran, nos. 0538-39; Yu
Xingwu 1996, no. 3128) was used to write the two homophones shou
"to receive," and shou, "to confer" (see, e.g., JGWZ:1443-49; JGWD:454-
55; Zhao Cheng 1989:324; Qiu Xigui 2000:189, 252). As the Shuowen defini-
tion,, "shou means to give mutually," indicates, the early word
represented by involved a sense of exchange. Qiu Xigui (1980b:77) has
proposed that the central element, placed between two hands, represented
the object that was handed back and forth. The two shou words should be
taken as what Herforth (1984; 1990), following the work of other linguists,
has referred to as "converse verbs," defined as "pairs of verbs whose par-
ticipants 'switch places."" Cf. Takashima, who uses the term antiphrasis,
"the use of a word in a sense opposite to its 'basic' meaning" (1980:92);
he also refers to the "amphibolous nature" of such words "which are fre-
quently found as verbs grammatically manifesting an active/passive rela-
tionship. This is a question of aspect; depending on which base-point the
action is seen from" (1988-89: 26-27). Takashima (1990:19) also speaks of
such verbs as introvertive [*] and extrovertive []. Following Herforth's
analysis, one may speak of shou and shou as trivalent converses,
involving an entity (harvest, assistance, etc.), a goal (the receiver, like the
king), and a source (the donor, like Di).
The diviners presumably distinguished the two words in their mantic
formulas, and yet they would have understood their converse nature; har-
vest or assistance, for example, might have been understood, in our mod-
ern terms, as both received from, and conferred by, some Power. There
are, to be sure, numerous cases in which the extrovert nature of the shou
is required by the grammatical structure of the charge. It is evident from
the inscriptions that Di was indeed the Power who awarded assistance in
battle (see sixteen cases of the formula (= ), "Di confers assis-
tance on us," at Y420.2-21.1). Such conferrals of assistance could also be
made by the ancestors, as in:
[326A]貞:禱于上甲授我又
Divined: "(We) should pray to (dao) Shang Jia (P1), for he
will confer (shou) assistance on us."
[326B] 勿禱于上甲不我其[授又]
"(We) should not pray to Shang Jia, for he may (qi) not
[confer assistance on us]." (I. Bin) (Heji 1171f)
It is also evident from Heji 9731 (Y232.1), which contains the phrase
..., “Di... confers harvest on us," that Di could award harvest.
So could the Nature Powers, as in [146]-[148]. And so could the ancestors
(see the additional charges transcribed at Ji Xiaojun 1991:37-38), either
directly or through their ability to intercede with Di, as in:
[327A] 于祖乙禱王受年
"(If) to Zu Yi (K12) he prays (dao), His Majesty will receive
harvest."
[327B] 于大乙禱王受年
"(If) to Da Jia (K3) he prays, His Majesty will receive har-
vest." (III-IV. Nameless-Diviner) (*Heji 28274)
0
I believe, accordingly, that Di was believed to confer both harvests and
assistance; when his presence is explicit, accordingly, I transcribe the336

Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
337
oracle-bone form as . Furthermore, in the absence of other linguistic
clues (see below), it also seems likely that even when Di was not men-
tioned-in formulas like 受ㄓ年(or受又年) (as at Y535.1-2) and 受ㄓ又
(=) (as at Y1217.2)—his benevolent participation (or that of some other
Power) was implicitly understood. I nevertheless translate such texts lit-
erally, still taking the as "receive" rather than "confer." In some cases,
indeed, it is impossible to determine which meaning—if they were sepa-
rable was dominant. The X in the predicate of [61], for example,
which I have translated as "(they) will receive abundant assistance," could
equally well be taken as "(some Power) will confer abundant
assistance (on us)."
shou you nian: “receive (confer?) abundant harvest"
(Y515.1-2; Sōran, no. 0539). HYDZ:185: "will we harvest a good crop";
Takashima (2010:I:109 for Bingbian 9): "Would that (we) harvest an abun-
dant (millet) crop!" I originally understood this you (written in the
later inscriptions; Keightley 1978:216, no. 4.3) as having an attributive,
pronominal function meaning "this" or "in this case" (Keightley 1978:78,
n. 85). I now incline, however, to the view that you meant "abundant";
see, e.g., Nivison 1977:7; Takashima 1980:91, 94, 104; 2010:1:108-09; Serruys
1986:232-37. Serruys cites analogous Zhou passages (see too Schuessler
1987:770), to which I would add as supplementary evidence, Shi, Mao no.
298, para. 3: 1, "the years will be abundant," and the explanation in
Guliang zhuan, Huan 3, commenting on the Chunqiu sentence
有年(Legge, tr., 1872:42, “There was a good year″):五谷皆熟為有年,
"When the five grains all ripen it constitutes an abundant harvest" (Yan
Yiping 1973:3a; cf. Malmqvist [1971:99] who also translates you nian as "a
good year”). One reason for rejecting the attributive function of you must
be its apparent redundancy. Redundancy, to be sure, is in the eye of the
beholder, but it is hard to see why the Shang diviners would have consis-
tently reiterated “in this case" when it was evident, from the very divina-
tion charge itself, which case was involved. Serruys (1986:237) makes a
similar point. The endurance of the phrasing suggests that the you had
strong, positive value, i.e., "abundant."
One curious distribution, involving the participation of Di, requires
comment. When the benefactor was not Di, we do indeed find the formula
X, "receive abundant assistance" (as in [61] and Heji 6098, 36181,
36511). But when Di was the benefactor, we only find X, "Di con-
fers assistance on us" (as in the inscriptions at Y420.2-21.1); we never find
*, *"Di confers abundant assistance." The same distribution can
be found in the harvest divinations. We find 4, "receive abundant
harvest" (as in [132C] and the inscriptions at Y535.1-2) when Di's role was
not recorded; but when it was recorded, we have ..., "Di...
confers harvest on us," as in Heji 9731 (Y232.1) cited above. We never find
*, *"Di confers abundant harvest." I do not know the signifi-
cance of this "avoidance" of you when Di was involved; conceivably,
the Shang might have found it presumptuous to ask Di for "abundance"
in any matter.
shou you you X(=): "receive abundant assistance" (Y1215.2; Sōran,
no. 0539). See the earlier entries for shou and shou you nian E.
Takashima (2010:I:123) gives "receive abundant (divine) assistance (Bing-

shu/Shu : "to guard; Guard, Guards" (Y897.2-99.1; Sōran, no. 1505;
Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2411). See Chen Mengjia 1956:515; JGWZ:81; Qiu
Xigui 2000:55. The oracle-bone graph, as Guo Moruo noted (commentary
to Cuibian 1155), is close in form to fat, "attack, dismember" (see the
Glossary entry for fa; cf. Qiu Xigui 2000:55); whereas fa depicted a dag-
ger-axe blade touching the human body, shu showed the human stand-
ing guard under the weapon. The activities of the Guards, who appear
in [28AB] and [29AB], are discussed by Chou Hong-hsiang 1968:312-21;
Akatsuka 1977:664; Zhang Yongshan 1982:216-18; Luo Kun 1991:416-17.
Kolb (1991:53) treated the Shu as the "commanders of the border hunters"
(Grenzjägerkommandanten). Barnard (1963:219) translated Shu in an early
Western Zhou bronze inscription as "Garrison-Governor."
si : "wild buffalo." Although oracle-bone (Y628.1; Sōran, no. 1149; Yu
Xingwu 1996, nos. 1651, 1708) has frequently been taken to mean "rhinoc-
eros"
(e.g., HYDZ:653, 654, 656), Lei Huanzhang (1984; Lefeuvre 1990-91)
who argues, on the basis of graph form, paleontological analysis, and the
numbers caught in Shang hunts (1 wild buffalo in [256], 11 wild buffalo in
[202A]), that the animal in question was a species of wild water buffalo.
This conclusion was generally accepted by the scholars who heard his
original 1982 paper (see Falkenhausen, ed., 1986:51-52). See too Fiskesjö
2001:95, n. 230; Liu Li, Yang Dongya, and Chen Xingcan 2006.
si : "ritual cycle" (Y688.1-89.1; Sōran, no. 0018; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
1846; Takashima 2010:II:719). It is generally agreed-see the entries at
Sōran, no. 0014-that si meant "ritual cycle." By Period V, numbered si
were recorded as part of the date in the postface to certain inscriptions,
with the ritual cycle now approximating the length of the solar year
(Keightley 2000:50-53):338

It may be that by extension, si also had the meaning of "perform ritual,
offer cult," particularly in the case of harvest divinations, such as [173AB]
and [174AB]. It appears to have had this verbal sense in the Guoyu
“Zhouyu shang 周語上”:服者祭,侯服者... 日祭,月祀,“Those of
the Royal Domain perform the ji sacrifice, those in the Domain of the
Lords, the si sacrifice. . . . The ji sacrifice is performed daily, the si monthly"
(Wei Zhao, ed., 1919-36:10; tr. based on Hart 1973:259). This understand-
ing would be supported by the argument of Qiu Xigui (1999), who builds
upon the original insight of Gao Quxun (1949), to argue that the post-
face formula (and its variants) in the Huang diviner group inscriptions
of Period V, and also in Shang bronze inscriptions, that is usually read as
*(wei) wang ershi si *()=+E, "(It was) His Majesty's twentieth ritual
cycle" (as I had taken it in translating Heji 37867 at Keightley 2000:52),
should instead be read as (wei) wang yue si (), "(It was when) His
Majesty said, 'Perform the ritual."" Thus:
[329] 癸丑卜泳貞: 王旬亡禍. 在六月甲寅配翌報甲. 王曰祀

Jia (P1).40 His Majesty said: "Perform the ritual (si)." (V.
Huang) (Heji 37867)

Qiu's argument, which I find plausible, depends upon challenging the
reading of the oracle-bone graph form U, which has usually been taken
as modern, "twenty," taking it instead to be a variety of oracle-bone,
modern, "say." As he notes, the graph for "twenty" in oracle-bone and
bronze scripts, and all the way down to Zhanguo times, was written with
the graph "open" on top; unlike the that appears in these postface for-
mulas, "twenty" was not "closed."
sui (Y917.2; Sōran, no. 0161):
(1) verb, "cut." Most scholars agree that, in ritual contexts, the Shang
word refers to the cutting or chopping up of sacrificial victims. See,
e.g., Britton 1940:60; Chen Mengjia 1956:71; Qu Wanli (commentary to
Jiakao 103); Akatsuka 1977:374; Itō 1990; Lian Shaoming 2007. Takashima
(1984:33, 58-63) has argued on linguistic grounds that the oracle-bone
graph is best read as gui, "harm, wound" (JGWJ:238, 317, agrees), but
with an earlier meaning of "cut"; he sees its usage in Shang cult inscrip-
tions as both verbal ("to cut") and nominal ("that which has been cut"-as
in Heji 34047).
(2) noun, "season." When used as a time word, the meaning of sui was
flexible. In the phrase lai sui, "the coming sui," it referred to a period
beyond the present ten-day week but within the present moon. In the
phase jin sui, "the present sui," which was used in contexts of agri-
culture (as in [124A], [157AB], [159A]) and hunting, it had the sense of the
harvest season, which might cover half a year (Chen Mengjia 1956:225-26,
who refers to two growing seasons; Liu Zhao 1986:230-31; cf. JGWJ:266,
which argues that sui referred to a summer or autumn harvest season).
tian: "field" (Y211.2-12.1; 801.2-16.2; Sōran, no. 1602; Yinxu 701). See
Chen Weizhan 1989; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2189. In hunting contexts it
meant, as a verb, "to take to the field, to hunt" (see the discussion at Qiu
Xigui 1989:22, 26; Fiskesjö 2001:106-13); e.g., [194A], [201A], [203A-C],
[246], [250]-[254], [258] (see too Li Xueqin and Peng Yushang 1996:360-
63). In agricultural contexts, it meant, as a verb, "to cultivate the fields, to
work the field, to till." That tian could also refer to agricultural fields, and340
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
341
thus to farming (see Fiskesjö 2001:105), is demonstrated by tian charges
that end with the incantation (4), "(we) will receive (abundant) har-
vest" or, "(we) will receive grain." See, e.g., [155]: "Calling out to
cultivate the fields (tian) at Y, (We) will receive harvest." As Qiu Xigui
(1989:26) notes, oracle-bone references to mou tian, "the field(s) of
X place," denotes agricultural fields (as in [169], [184], [196B]; cf. Fiskesjö
2001:108). Qiu Xigui (1983:5-7) sees the Tian as "field officers" in cer-
tain situations). See too the discussion of farming and hunting in chapters
12 and 14.
=
Tun (Y1266.2-69.2; Sōran, no. 0040; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 3275). It
mainly appears in the marginal notations of scapulas and carapaces, not
plastrons. It refers to a pair of right and left scapulas or the pair of right
and left sections into which the carapace was divided. Keightley 1978:16,
n. 65; cf. p. 14, n. 56. See too Akatsuka 1977:107-08; Ding Su 1980:111;
Wang Guimin 1989:87-89. A possible exception is [286], where Tun may
appear as a name.
Wang E: "king, His Majesty" (Y1245.1-61.1; Sōran, no. 0033; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 3246; Gao Ming 2001:78-83). (Qi Wenxin [1985] argues that wang,
in a few cases, also refers to the head of non-Shang polities.) I prefer "His
Majesty," for the following reason. Never, in the oracle-bone inscriptions,
are the living name and the title, wang, attached. King Da Yi (K1), Wu Ding
(K21), and all the rest were only probably so named after their deaths. We
never know their names when alive. They were always the wang, "His
Majesty" (see Harbsmeier 2004:4).
Etymological studies of the word wang have not yet led to convincing
new conclusions about the original meaning of this title. Various theories
have been advanced, from Xu Shen's paranomastic definition, E, F
E, "a king is the one to whom the world turns," to modern views
that the graph was a picture of a phallus, or that the basic meaning was
"axe," i.e., the weapon that the king used to rectify and conquer the realm
and that thus symbolized his authority (see p. 3); see, e.g., JGWZ:113-27;
JWGL:204-34; JGWJ:227. Ammassari (1990:67-68; 1991:88-97) provides a
summary of various views. The most plausible view seems to be based
partly on the graph which, in its earliest form A (later I and, by the
end of the dynasty, E), appears to depict a standing man, viewed from
the front, with his arms and legs spread apart. For general discussions
see Tōdō (1969:413-15), who argues that wang belongs to a word fam-
ily with the basic meaning of "extend, enlarge"; hence, the ruler was the
great or large man. See too Hopkins 1940:361; Katō 1970:2480; Shirakawa
1969–74:1:80–85. Qi Wenxin (1991) has argued, partly from ethnographic
analogies, that the graph depicted a Big Man, a chief; she has noted that
high-status figures were depicted frontally in the oracle-bone script while
low-status figures were depicted from the side. I believe that a graphic
and phonetic relationship linked E (oracle-bone form A) and 1 (oracle-
bone form), and that the king was possibly "the big man who goes"
(Keightley 1983:553). In religious terms, he was "going" into the ances-
tral hierarchy, both by his divinatory and his sacrificial communications.
The verb wang E did have ritual meaning (e.g., Yu Xingwu 1979:154; Qiu
Xigui 1980a:21). Li Yan (1970a:12) and Kwok-ching Chow (1982:301-04,
n. 7), in fact, discuss several of the relevant inscriptions, and translate the
wang E as a verb, to "wang-sacrifice" (including Xucun 2.187 = Heji 39549).
Such an interpretation, with its implications of a journey to the ances-
tors, would support those who discern a significant shamanistic quality in
Shang kingship (e.g., Chen Mengjia 1936; K. C. Chang 1983:48-55; 1992;
1994; 1999:51, 52, 63-64; also Mair 1990, Tong Enzheng 2002. For a contrary
view, see Keightley 1983a; 1998:798-823; Puett 2002:33-40). I would note,
however, that such inscriptions were rare and that their rarity makes them
hard to interpret with assurance. As Kwok-ching Chow notes (1982:303),
"There is not sufficient material to determine whether this ritual-sacrifice
wang E is a method of sacrificing animals or of approaching the spir-
its," and, following a suggestion of Takashima, he notes that I might
have been causative-"to cause to go," and thus "to send" sacrifices to the
ancestors. I do not, for example, believe that EFT (III. He; Heji
27867) meant, *"If His Majesty goes to the sun, he will not encounter rain."
It is evident that terrestrial travel was presumably involved; the meaning
was "If His Majesty goes to perform the day ritual (or performs the wang
ritual to the sun), he will not encounter rain or get wet."
In secular terms, in any event, the king was "the one who goes" in the
sense that he was continually travelling to the various areas of his domain,
as the inscriptions-which use the verb wang 1-amply attest (see, e.g.,
[250], [251], etc.) (Keightley 1979-80:26-27; 1983:551-54; Lewis 2006:137).
Wang was indeed used as a loan for wang 1 in Shijing (Karlgren 1964,
no. 933).
Wang Cheng (Y106.2-08.2; Sōran, nos. 1035, 0719). Scholars evidently
take it as "Cheng of Wang," place-name plus personal-name, or a squad
of scouts or spies (Shirakawa 1972:165). This works nicely for: "On yimao
(day 52) we send out the scouts" ([303AB]); "On dingsi (day 54) we send
the zhong" ([212AB]), with the same diviner, Que ; it was likely part of
the same campaign.
wang zu E: "royal lineage"; see zu .342
