=
wei : "surround, encircle, roam around" (Y309.2-12.2; Sōran, no.
3289). Many scholars (e.g., Chen Weizhan 1981:233; JGWD:147-48; Yu
Xingwu 1996:807, 808) have understood to be a fuller, original form of
oracle-bone, hence zheng IE, "march to regulate" (see Glossary entry
for zheng IE). For versions of the form in Shi É (large characters) and
Shi (small characters) inscriptions (numerous examples at Y310.1-2), see
Li Xueqin and Peng Yushang 1996:62, 67, 73, tables 4, 5, and 7. Yu Xingwu
(1996:808) cites a few cases in which the Shang king was the subject of
the charge, as in (Heji 33023, 33024), "When it comes to
xinsi (day 18), His Majesty will roam around (?) the Shaofang" (Li 2). The
similar and contemporary charge,
X, “If His Majesty marches
to regulate the Shaofang, he will receive assistance" (Heji 33022; Li 1),
supports the view that in these cases at least-the inscriptions of the Li
1-2 diviner group-the engravers used the two graphs, and, inter-
changeably, with no semantic distinction intended.
More generally, however, distinctions in usage do suggest that two dif-
ferent words may have been involved. Fang enemy states were always
the object of zheng E, as the Shang, the subject of the charge, marched to
regulate them (as in Heji 33022 in the previous paragraph); but the Fang,
apart from the few cases cited above, were nearly always the subject of
(e.g., [217A] and [322A]), as they acted upon the Shang object. The pro-
posal that be read as wei (accepted, e.g., by Kolb 1991:74) appears
to fit most of the contexts in which it appears. Presumably, however, the
Fang would not always have been "besieging" the Shang but would have
been encroaching on the Shang domain, roaming around the various
Shang settlements. When, as was frequently the case (e.g., [322A], Heji
137b, 584, 6067, 6068f, 6352, 6677, 6778f, at Y310.1), the was followed
by the locative and a place-name (PN), the translation "surround at
PN" (in which, if the PN were the direct object of the verb, the yu would
be superfluous) works less well than "roam around at PN," with the yu
indicating the location of the activity (see Takashima 2001:10 on the loca-
tive yu). In those cases, accordingly-as in [322A] @F#¤¥4=8,
"The Tufang have been roaming around in our eastern borders and have
seized two settlements"-I tentatively translate as "roam around."
When the yu is absent ([217A] and Yingcang 1133f), I translate the as
"surround." (I would note that a number of scholars treat a still more com-
plex form of the graph, with two "feet" both inside the enclosure as
well as below it, as wei ; see Sōran, no. 3290). And no doubt, from the
Shang point of view, the presence of enemy forces within their domains
would have suggested that settlements might have been "besieged."
wei : "protect" (Y862.1-63.2; Sōran, no. 0232; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2300).
Scholarly opinion is divided on the meaning and transcription of the
oracle-bone graph involved. Seven of the scholars whose opinions are tab-
ulated at Sōran, no. 0160, read it as wei; eighteen read it as bu, "to go." I
tentatively adopt the wei reading (as in [108]), largely on graphic grounds.
It is evident that the word, in certain contexts, had a ritual dimension, the
"protecting ritual (?)" (as in Heji 916b, 3482f, 6354b, etc.). It also formed
part of the title Duo Wei, the "Many Protectors (?)" (Y862.1).
wei : a non-modal, explanatory copula (Y649.1-2; Sōran, no. 0489). The
Shang engravers used a graph that we would now read as zhui to write
the classical copula wei, or. A comprehensive study is Takashima
1990 (see, in particular, his conclusions on pp. 63-66). I believe that, in
charges, wei was used in the present tense: "X is equivalent to Y," "X is Y,"
"X means Y"; it was not used to refer to future events (*"X will be equiva-
lent to Y," "X will be Y," *"X will mean Y"). Thus, we never find *
"there may be a bad omen."
For the argument that wei as a copula functions in such contexts “not
simply as equating the subject and complement but also as showing 'rea-
son' which in English may be expressed by some such phrases as 'due to,'
'because of,'" see Takashima 1973:243, 394, n. 34. As he notes (1990:28–32),
wei served an explanatory function, as opposed to the other copula, hui
(or ) (q.v.), which did not.
As the inscriptions assembled at MZ 396.1 indicate, the normal dis-
tribution of positive and negative copulas in Period I charge pairs was
either (1a) 隹禍 vs. (1b) 不隹禍 (common) or (za) 其ㄓ禍 vs. (2b) 亡禍.
I suggest that (1a) meant "is ominous" vs. (1b) "is not ominous"; these
usages involved neutral requests for information rather than attempts to
shape the future. Compare Takashima (1988-89:50; 1989:136), who notes
that such charges have “equal informational value"; see, e.g., [311AB],
[313AB]. In these cases, the diviner did not make one charge stronger than
the other (on this distinction, see page 329). I take the second form, with its
weaker positive charge, in the same way: (2a) "may have something omi-
nous (about it)" vs. (2b) "does not have something ominous (about it)." In
this view, the verb of existence, you , "have, there is, there are," could
apply to both present and future; wei could apply only to the present.
wu : “fog, foggy” (Y659.1; Sōran, no. 0968; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 1762—a
picture of "mouth" inverted [gou ] over a "bird" [wei #]). Context indi-
cates that the oracle-bone graph referred to some atmospheric condition
that was associated with, but distinct from, rain, sun, or clearing. Scholars
have proposed (JGWJ:191, 371): "cloudy" (Ikeda 1964 [commentary to Hou-
bian 2.25.6]; Wen Shaofeng and Yuan Tingdong 1983:131, 153-55]); "over-
cast" (Serruys 1974:32). I have adopted "foggy," partly because a significant344
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
345
number of scholars favors it, partly because archaeologists familiar with
the Yinxu site tell me that fog is indeed experienced there today.
Wu Zu Shu : "Guards of the Five Warbands" (Y987.1); see too the
Glossary entries for shu and zu . The phrase Wu Zu Shu-which
Kolb (1991:44) translates as "the border hunters of the five lineages" (die
5-Lineagen Grenzjäger)-appears on only a small number of bones: [84],
[85F], and Heji 28054, all from the Nameless-Diviner group of Period
II-IV (see p. 102). Matsumaru (1970:67) provides evidence that the officers
were tribal groups not individuals. The Wu Zu Shu does not, I believe,
despite the transcriptions offered by MZ and Heshi, appear on Heji 28053;
I agree with the readings provided at Ikeda 1964 for Houbian 2.42.6, which
take the putative shu 戍as fa伐. The MZ reading of *...令五族戍羌方,
"... who orders the Five Warbands to guard the Qiangfang," does not,
I think, make as much sense as the earlier readings,...,
"... who orders the Five Warbands to attack the Qiangfang." But given
the graphic similarity of oracle-bone fa and shu (Sōran, nos. 1007, 1505), it
is hard to resolve the issue with certainty.
The San Zu (Y986.2-87.1), "the Three Warbands," (Heji 6438, 32815,
34134-35) were probably similar to the Wu Zu F, all being troops of the
king's guard (Fan Yuzhou 1991:214). It probably, on one occasion, referred
to a unit being commanded by the Horse(-chariot officers):
[332A】一族合
"It should be the One Warband that (we?) order."
[332B] 三族馬令
0
"It should be the Three Warbands (Wu Zu Shu) that the
Horse(-chariot officers) (ma) order." (I-II. Li) (Heji 34136)
Inscriptions such as these confirm the military role of the zu (see too [224]).
xi: "alarming news." The identification of oracle-bone xi (Y1075.2-78.2;
Sōran, no. 0996) is purely graphic and purely a matter of conventional con-
venience; it carries no semantic weight; the modern word xi, "hand-
some, play, amusement," has no filiation to the Shang word. JGWD:1464
suggests jian, "calamity." As Mickel (1976:173) has noted, the Shang
graph, which was used mainly in Period I, appears to have fallen into
disuse before the end of the dynasty. Similar considerations apply to the
transcriptions offered by JGWJ:283 (cf. Mickel 1976:173). For the functional
rendering "alarming news," which I used in Keightley 1978 (44, n. 80),
see Chow Tse-tsung 1968:168, n. 65; Mickel 1976:174-78. As Kaizuka notes
(commentary to Jimbun 337), among the various kinds of "harm," xi was
particularly linked to external invasions; we see this usage in [322AB] and
Heji 24146. It also appeared, as Mickel noted, in contexts associated with
illness and calamities (see the Glossary entry for jiu).
xiang : "specifically" (Y217.1; Sōran, no. 0472). This oracle-bone
form (and variants)-composed with yang #, "sheep," and two mu E,
"eyes"-has been given a variety of modern readings, ranging from yang
*(JGWZ:1315) to xiang (Zhang Bingquan, commentary to Bingbian 98,
pp. 142-44) to mo (Zhang Zhenglang 1983a:20). I also tentatively take
the oracle-bone graph (Y219.2; Sōran, no. 0115) as similar in meaning.
As a number of scholars have pointed out-see the opinions of Kaizuka,
Itō, and Takashima cited at Sōran, no. 0472; see, too, Lian Shaoming 1981;
Zhu Qixiang 1999:39-40-the word, when it followed the prohibitive wu
, acted as a particle of intensification. There are, however, two diametri-
cally opposed views about the meaning and function of the phrase ".
Zhang Zhenglang (1983a) reads as mo (defined by Shuowen as
TE, "eyes not being straight"), and suggests that it had the same sound
and meaning as mie, with the sense of "small, petty." He concludes that
had negative connotations and that was a double negative that
had the meaning of "do not overlook," "do not stop (from)," thus serving
to emphasize and reinforce in a positive way the actions that followed the
phrase. A similar view was proposed to me by David Nivison (conversa-
tion of 10 March 1978), suggesting that should be read as its cognate,
yang 1, "hesitate" (e.g., GSR, no. 732f). The phrase wu xiang, accord-
ingly, would mean "without fail, unfailingly." As we shall see next, I find
this view of the phrase's function difficult to accept.
The alternative view has been proposed by Takashima (1973:391;
1990:44, 81, n. 29), who suggested that oracular be understood as
xiang, "specifically > absolutely" (cf. Takashima 2010:II:726). Serruys
(1981:351, for Bingbian 197.4 = [335B]) offered a similar translation, giving
"do not at all cost" for . My understanding of the illocutionary force
of the phrase agrees with that of Takashima and differs, to some degree,
however, from that of Serruys, since I believe it did not imply absolute
prohibition, but rather served, by prohibiting too narrow a definition of
the action proposed, to allow other possibilities to be considered.
I believe that, whatever the precise identity of the word represented
by, the second view is correct because it provides translations that, in
accordance with Wu Ding's practice, indicate the two charges in a pair
were offering choices. If Zhang Zhenglang's understanding is followed,
we are faced with a series of charge pairs in which the diviner "said the
same thing twice," but was more emphatic the second time. This seems346
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
347
"
less plausible. For example, the charges of [335AB] were written as a pair,
on either side of the plastron spine. Zhang Zhenglang (who cites this
inscription, as Zhuihe 173, at 1983a:20) would presumably translate the
start of the charges as [335A] **ZTZ..., "On the coming yihai, in
making a you-offering to Xia Yi...," and [335B], *£Z££TZ..
"It should definitely be yihai when we make a you-offering to Xia Yi....
I find this translation less plausible than the one offered in my text, "It
should be on the coming yihai...." and "It should not specifically be on
yihai...," in which the two charges are understood as having offered a
choice of action. Wang Tao (1993:103-07) offers "immediately," "rashly,"
"with anxiety," etc., for xiang, but cautions that "my translation... is a
preliminary experiment."
The implausibility of the "reinforcement" interpretation is further sug-
gested by other charge pairs (for which I first give the translation I prefer),
as in [154AB] and:
[333A]甲午卜爭貞:翌乙未用羌. 用之日霧
Crack-making on jiawu (day 31), Zheng divined: "On the
next day, yiwei (day 32), (We) should sacrifice (yong) Qiang
(captives)." (Verification:) (We) sacrificed them (but) on that
day it was foggy (wu).
【333B] 甲午卜爭真:翌乙未勿够用羌
Crack-making on jiawu (day 31), Zheng divined: "On the
next day, yiwei, (We) should not specifically (xiang) sacrifice
Qiang (captives)." (I. Bin) (Heji 456f)41
I find it more plausible that the Shang diviners offered genuine choices
in such charges. The view that the diviner was simply reiterating the
previous charge, but with more force-so that [333A], *"We should sac-
rifice Qiang captives" was simply reinforced by [333B], "We should not
overlook sacrificing Qiang captives," or [154A], *"If we plant millet...,"
was simply reinforced by [154B], *"We should not overlook planting mil-
let...."―seems less likely, particularly given the Shang proclivity under
Wu Ding for balancing positive and negative charge pairs that pre-
sented the divinee with a real choice (Keightley 1988:373-78; 1999b:243).
I believe in conclusion that oracle-bone served to specify or delimit
(thus the reading, with Takashima, of xiang *); when prohibited by the
wu, the resulting phrase served to expand the limits of action, hav-
ing the sense of "we should not delimit, we should not specifically do
thus-and-so."
41. See the discussion at Zhu Qixiang 1999a:17-18 and 144-45 (for Bingbian 106).
Xia Shang FE: "The Lower and Upper (Ancestors from Xia Yi FZ [K12]
to Shang Jia [P1])" (Y397.2-98.1). HYDZ:131 gives "(the spirits of)
Below and Above"; see too Zhu Qixiang 1999a:9-10 (front). As Hu Hou-
xuan (1944d) has shown, Zu YiZ (K12) was also referred to as Xia Yi F
Z (see too Keightley 1978:205). Cao Dingyun (1990:37) cites three inscrip-
tions that show that the ancestors "from Shang Jia to Xia Yi" formed a
ritual unit for receiving sacrifices: Bingbian 45 (= Heji 270f), Yibian 5338
(= Bingbian 328 = Heji 419f), and Yibian 7579 (= Heji 241b). Zu Yi may well
have been given the additional ritual title of Xia Yi, "Lower Yi," because he
served as the last king of the Primary Ancestors (Yuan Shi ; see Glos-
sary entry), the ritual unit that started with Shang Jia, the "Upper Jia," and
ended with the ancestor Zu Yi Z. That he was also known, primarily to
the Li-Nameless Transitional Diviner group (Period II-III), as Gaozu Yi
EZ, "the High Ancestor Yi" (e.g., Heji 22959, 32445-56; see too Keightley
1978:205 and the citations at p. 208, n. m), may also have been due to the
same reason, to elevate the ancestral figure. Two of the charges on a large
scapula fragment illustrate the pivotal role of Xia Yi:

(ji zong) to Shang Jia (P1)...."

Working for His Majesty
As they moved up the hierarchy, the Shang ritualists referred to the
twelfth king as Zu Yi ([334C]); as they moved down, they referred to him
as Xiao Yi ([334F]).
Xia Shi : "The Lower Ancestors" (Y397.1-98.1). See Cao Dingyun 1993
and [94A-C*]. See too the Glossary entry for "Xia Shang FE" and "Yuan
Shi 元示”
xie
(Y263.2-66.2; Sōran, no. 1613). I believe with a number of scholars
that the oracle-bone graph, as it appears in [143], should be read as xie
in the sense of "mutual help, cooperation." See, e.g., Chen Mengjia
1956:537; Shima 1958:342; Rao Zongyi 1959:95-96; Taiping Chang 1981:315-
17; Zhang Yongshan 1982:203; Zhu Qixiang 1999a:92-93; the appropriate
entries at Sōran, no. 1613; Liu Huan 2002:118-20. Thus the phrase xie tian
meant "work together in the fields." Qiu Xigui (1989:34), moreover,
holds that xie tian referred to a grand turning up of the soil in preparation
for the next year's cultivation; it was a ritual concerned with agriculture,
probably performed by the king in the spring (Peng Bangjiong 1990:37).
For another view, see the gloss to Heji 1, which raises the possibility that
the xie in [143] was the name of a seasonal wind. The xie of xie tian, in any
event, should probably not be confused with the Shang xie ritual, writ-
ten with the same graph. None of the charges about this ritual (Y264.2-
66.2), from Period II on, mention the zhong or ren or end with the incanta-
tion "we will receive harvest." Presumably the oracles had changed; the
zhong and ren were now so standard that, it is possible, the king no longer
divined about them.
xing: "to inspect" (Y211.2-13.1; Sōran, no. 0466; HYDZ:336, etc.). See Lu
Liancheng 1996:114; Qi Wenxin and Wang Guimin 1998:71. See also the
discussion at [196]. Fiskesjö (2001:108) notes that "since there is never any
game 'captured' on such tian [] 'inspections,' tian probably here refers
to physical 'fields.""
xue: "instruct, learn" (Y1242.2-43.1; Sōran, no. 0453; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 3232). Both Serruys (1986:213) and Takashima (2010:I:124 for Bingbian
22.5-6) give "instruct." Yang Shengnan (1982:381) takes xue in the sense of
"to train," including Bingbian 22.5–6 = [212AB*].
Ya : the Ya title (Hayashi 1968:70-71; Zhu Fenghan 1983:55-56; Y1115.1-
17.1; Sōran, no. 1662; Barnard 1986:144-50; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2898; Aihe
Wang 2000:39-46, 170). The title Duo Ya E, "The Many Ya," is analo-
gous to other Shang titles such as Duo Chen E, "The Many Servitors"
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
349
(Y228.1), Duo Ma ([106]), “The Many Horse-(chariot) Officers" (see
Glossary for ma), and Duo She, "The Many Archers," ([89], [238]).
The parallel usage would suggest that the Ya would have been Ya-officers.
Chen Mengjia (1956:508-11) concluded, on the basis of both Shang inscrip-
tions and transmitted texts, that the Ya were military officers; they were
certainly involved in warfare or related expeditionary activities (as in
[120], [121], [236]). It has also been proposed, however, that the Ya title
distinguished the ranked members of the Shang lineage and was also used
by those who were related to that lineage by marriage (Liu Jie, as
quoted at JWGL 14:7864-65; JGWD:1523-24). Here one may think of analo-
gies with the Duo Sheng E, the term by which the Shang king referred
to his “Many Progeny," the many descendants of the royal lineage (Qiu
Xigui 1983a:13; Keightley 1999:49). Confronted by the patrimonial nature
of Shang administration (see ch. 1, n. 30), I conclude that a designation
like Ya Qin ([120], [242A], [281]) probably meant “the Qin who held
Ya status" (see Guo Moruo's commentary to Cuibian 1178 = [120]), and I
translate it, ambiguously, as "the Ya Qin." The Ya Qin emblem, con-
sisting of the Qin graph inside a Ya cartouche, appears on at least three
Shang bronzes. See Chen Mengjia 1956:510–11; Zhang Bingquan's com-
mentary on Bingbian 10; Shima 1958:461-62; 1958a; Satō 1962:15-16;
Watson 1962:71-72; Kaizuka 1967:245-47; Kwang-chih Chang 1970:310;
Chao Lin 1972:74-76; Niu Jipu 1993. It is thought that the cartouche used
in such emblems, like the Ya title itself, had an honorific, military, and
probably religious, significance; see, e.g., Shima 1958:510–11; Kaizuka
1967:244; the opinions quoted at JWGL 14:7850-65; Yin Zhiyi 1977; Cao
Dingyun 1980:143-44; Tsung-tung Chang 1986-87:10 (who proposes the
translation of “viceroy"); Allan 1991:89-98; Xiaoneng Yang 2000:94 (Yang
Xiaoneng 2008:175).
Yang : "the Mountain Power" (Y467.1-73.2). In its full form, the oracle-bone
graph was composed of the "sheep" and "mountain" || elements.
Many scholars (see the entries at Sōran, no. 0512) transcribe oracle-bone
as "Gao" or "Yue ." Itō, for example (1961:271), takes the Yue spirit
to be that of Songshan, Mount Song, the sacred peak west-southwest
of Zhengzhou; see too Tsung-tung Chang 1970:184-91; Kleeman 1994:226-
27; Zheng Jiexiang 1994:44-45; HYDZ:451, etc., "(to) the Yue mountain
spirit.” I, however, follow the contextual analysis of Akatsuka (1977:92-
105), which led him to transcribe the graph as #, which he reads as Xiang
(a reading followed at Itō and Takashima 1996:1:57, 63). Zhang Bingquan
and Takashima (2010:1:686) read the graph Yang (e.g., Bingbian 494.6-7,
etc.). For some of the interpretive issues involved, see Keightley 1982:268,
n. 5, 281; see too Serruys 1974:89-90; 1982:482-87; Ikeda 1980:30-31;
Pulleyblank 1983:420–21; 1996:24, n. 10; Boltz 2001:174, n. 15.350

yi : "epidemic, pestilence" (Y71.2-72.1; Sōran, no. 0407). Rao Zongyi
1959:115-16; Ikeda 1964 for Houbian 2.26.18; Tsung-tung Chang 1970:257,

yi : "settlement" (Y116.2-20.2; Sōran, no. 0801; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
0305). See the scholarship cited by Wheatley 1971:62, 100, n. 193; K. C.

=
yong : "to sacrifice" (Y1307.1-14.1; Sōran, no. 0458; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
3338). For yong as a verb used to express the "using," i.e., killing, of vic-
tims, human or animal, see, e.g., Hu Houxuan 1939a:471; Chen Mengjia
1956:327; Qu Wanli (commentary to Jiakao 433); Jin Xiangheng 1973;
JGWJ:238, 363. The usage was standard in Eastern Zhou; see, e.g., the entry
for Chunqiu, Xi 19, para. 3 (641 B.C.), which is even reminiscent, with its
dating formula, of a divination charge: 7, "On jiyou
(day 46), the people of Zhu seized the viscount of Ceng, and used him (as
a human sacrifice)" (Legge, tr., 1872:176, revised; see also index, p. 913).
you : "fault, calamitous fault" (Y1332.2; Sōran, no. 1676; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 3353; see too the Glossary entry for huo ). Apart from general
agreement that the oracle-bone graph should be read as you (JGWZ:4229),
there has been little discussion of the precise meaning and function of this
disaster word. JGWJ:282 offers the standard, circular definition: you was
a term for good and bad fortune "like huo and zai ." HYDZ:13, 14,
etc., suggests "mishap." (Mickel 1976 does not discuss the word because
it was used rarely in Period I.) The incantatory formula wang you t
was recorded at the end of numerous Period II and V charges about cult.
In all those cases, such as [289A], [310], and Heji 35490, 35599, 35630, in
which the king was specified as the subject of the charge, I treat him as the
implied subject of the predicate: "He will have no fault." The calamity-
be it drought, enemy attack, sickness, or some other untoward happen-
ing-was a calamity, both in itself and as a sign of blame and fault on the
part of humans below; hence "chastisement," or at least a sense of penalty,
was perhaps involved. You clearly has the sense of "blame, censure, fault-
finding," and even "guilt," in Zhou texts; see GSR, no. 996; Schuessler
1987:766. Lefeuvre (Faguo, pp. 345-46) offers "a harm,' indicating that it
is probably connected with a ritual fault." Some commentators equate it
with jiu (see Glossary entry), a character with similar meanings (e.g.,
Qu Wanli's commentary to Jiakao 17).
you Œ, X: “is, have, offer” (Y1321.1–32.2; 338.1–45.2; Sōran, nos. 0839,
0350; Yu Xingwu 1996, nos. 0905, 3350). Takashima (1980:81) sees the you
as having "the existential verb meaning 'there is"" ([34]) and "as a con-
junctive particle meaning 'also, and' used to connect numbers." The basic
meaning of this word appears to have been "the right hand; to have (in the
right hand), there exists (in the right hand); to honor, esteem" (Takashima
1980:91); Takashima has argued that when read with the qusheng it
meant "to cause to have" and thus "to offer." Kwok-ching Chow (1982:37-
40) has further suggested the existence of two antiphrastic (fanxun )
word groups, one (including 4) meaning “to possess," the other meaning
"to release, let go." In cult contexts, accordingly, 4 might have meant "to
release," thus "to offer," to the spirits.
In contexts of cult, I translate you as "to offer, make offering." See
Takashima 1979-80:52-53; 1980:84, 88, 92, 94; Taiping Chang 1981:338-
47; Serruys (1986:227-37) translates you as "sacrifice." As JGWJ:231 notes,
some scholars (e.g., Jin Xiangheng 1967:5a, 12a; Takashima 1979-80:52-
53; the Y editors) read the word as you \\, “offer, sacrifice, encourage (to
have), encourage to drink." Takashima (1980:84) documents the philologi-
cal connection among 4, X, and , and suggests that the association
between the verb of sacrifice and animal victims (see too Lefeuvre, Faguo,
p. 294) may account for the presence of the "meat" element-rou -
in the Zhou form, composed of the "right hand" and "meat." Huang
Xiquan (1981) has also argued for a graphic link between the shape of the
and the horns of the cows offered in cult. In contexts of Shang cult (and
assistance; see below), was replaced by X in the last half of Period II
(Keightley 1978:216, no. 4.2; Qiu Xigui 1979:123; cf. Takashima 1980a:83). I
treat you as a full verb, not an auxiliary. Thus, Z, "(We) make offer-
ing to Zu Yi" (Heji 1549 and many similar cases at Y1395.2).
There are reasons for thinking that you was non-specific, referring to
offerings in general; thus, we find charges like, “In mak-
ing offering to Bao, it should be (hui) on xinmao that we perform the you-
cutting sacrifice (you)" (Heji 25038). When you appeared with other full
verbs of cult, it was often listed first, thus serving to introduce what fol-
lowed, as in E£&£Z★★ª, “If His Majesty makes an
offering (you) to Da Yi, Da Ding, and Da Jia, it should be that he presents
(sheng) the cut-up victims (sui) to the ancestor (?)" (Heji 27149; I suspect
that the may have been an engraver's error for Я, "eighth moon";
see the next inscription);升歲于唐亡害.十二月,““If we make an offer-
ing and present the cut-up victims to Tang (K1), there will be no harm
(hai). In the twelfth moon" (Heji 27150).352
Working for His Majesty
When used in the incantatory predicate of non-cult charges, you (4,
later X) as an adjective had the sense of "abundant"; on this reading, see
the Glossary entry for shou you nian, "receive abundant harvest."
When used as a noun in such environments, i.e., as the second you in for-
mulas like shou you you, 受又(受又又,受又=), the you stood for 祐,
thus, "receive abundant assistance (from the Powers)" ([33], [47],
[50], [52A], [67], etc.).
you (a modern graphic invention for oracle-bone ): "the you-cutting
sacrifice" (Y1042.1-60.2; Sōran, no. 1713; Zhu Fenghan 2002). Follow-
ing Takashima (1984-85:251; 1988:680, n. 23; 2002:118-19), I now accept
the graphic, contextual, and grammatical analysis of Kwok-ching Chow
(1982:319-23; cf. pp. 341-46)-pursuing an original insight of Kaizuka
(commentary to Jimbun 83 and 2265)-who concludes that oracle-bone
(Y1042.1) did not, as it is usually thought to have done (e.g., JGWB, no.
1709; Britton 1940:22; Keightley 1978:75; JGWD:1601; Yu Xingwu 1996, no.
1317; HYDZ:59, 200, etc.), refer to an ale libation or libation sacrifice, i.e.,
jiu . (Knechtges 1997:237; 2002:768; H. T. Huang 2000:149-50, 156-57;
cf. Anderson [1988:120-21], who translates it as "ale"; Bagley [2004:209]
translates as "beer.") Chow, following the lead of Guo Moruo, speculated
that the word might have been you, "to pile up wood to burn," hence
"sacrifice." Takashima's suggestion (1988:680, n. 23) that was etymo-
logically related to diao, "cut, injure"--so that he translates as "per-
form the you-cutting sacrifice of victims” (1988-89:20; 1989:156, n. 48)-is
supported by charges like Heji 16223f, ++, "(We) cleave
(zhuo) eight sheep and you-cut thirty cows."
Strongly attached to the "ale," interpretation, I resisted accepting
the views of Chow and Takashima for a long time. Certainly, there were
a lot of "ale" vessels (Kwang-chih Chang 1968:207; 1986:312, 314, 363;
Hayashi 1984:61-67, 123-77; YXFX:267-300; H. T. Huang 2000:153; and,
indeed, back to the Neolithic (McGovern et al. 2005). 43 "The most com-
mon bronze vessel types at Yinxu are gu [] drinking goblets and jue []
pouring cups, normally found as a pair," and he, jia, and jue that
"all warmed wine" (Thorp 2006:194, 198). But the inscriptions contain no
reference to gu or he; jue is only a name of a vessel type (Sōran, no. 0661);
and jia and jue contain no reference jiu (Y1065.2–66.1; Sōran, nos. 0661,
43. "Not only is alcohol a mild antiseptic, but the process of brewing alcoholic bever-
ages often required that the liquid be boiled or subjected to similarly sterilizing treatments.
'It's possible that people who drank fermented beverages tended to live longer and repro-
duce more than did their teetotaling peers, Dr. [Patrick] McGovern said- an archaeological
chemist at the University of Pennsylvania-'which may partly explain why people have a
proclivity to drink alcohol"" (Angier 2007).

1627; cf. Lian Shaoming 1996:172-75). (See Childs-Johnson 1987 for the use
of the jue.) There are eleven inscriptions which contain zuo li F, "make
sweet wine (?)" (Y1240.1; Sōran, no. 0613); they similarly contain no refer-
ence jiu酒.
A number of inscriptions persuaded me to "the you-cutting sacrifice."
In addition to [300] "On the coming yihai (day 12), in performing a you-
cutting sacrifice to Zu Yi (K12), (we) will offer ten dismembered victims
and split open ten penned sheep," we also have:
[335]乙卯卜貞:來乙亥下乙十伐ㄓ五卯十宰. 二旬ㄓ一日乙

【335B] 勿隹乙亥下乙十伐五卯十宰四乙
"It should not specifically (xiang) be (wei) on yihai, that in
making a you-cutting sacrifice to Xia Yi, (we) offer fifteen
dismembered victims and (you) split open ten penned
sheep, four (?)...." (I. Bin) (*Heji 903f)
The you-cutting sacrifice was followed with the fa- "dismember" and
mao- "split open" rituals. And, if read correctly, the charge and notation
on Heji 32202 (I. Shi-Nameless Transitional),十伐.茲用.卯二牢, will be
"(We) offer ten dismembered victims (fa). This was used. We split open
(mao) two penned cows (lao)." Such evidence also indicates that although
the fa, mao, sui, you, and zhuo proceedings and offerings (see
the Glossary entries) involved some form of cutting up victims, they were
distinct in time.

However one construes the final - of [336] and [337], it does appear to
record that, in the first case, "one cow" was offered in the sui -"cutting
sacrifice," and that, in the second case, "one cow" was offered in the you
-"cutting sacrifice." The parallel provides one more piece of evidence to
indicate that you did indeed involve some form of cutting rather than the
offering of ale.
A third inscription may also be valuable:
[338] 勿呼雀酢于河五十牛

"(We) should not call upon (hu) Que to perform you-
cutting (you) sacrifice to the He (River Power) (He) of fifty
cows." (I. Bin) (Heji 1140)
It would not appear that the jiu "ale" sacrifice-rather than the you
-cutting sacrifice-was especially well suited to the offering in sacrifice
of fifty cows.
Lefeuvre (1997:322-23; Lei Huanzhang 1990), who resumes some of
the scholarship, including the arguments of Takashima and Chow, makes
the additional suggestion that rather than understanding the graph as
a xiangxing character it might be viewed as a xingsheng form,
composed with and a phonetic, and that it accordingly would have
been read yong, not you. This, however, has been rejected by Takashima
(2000:389-90), who, in his review of Lefeuvre 1997, resists the phonetic
linkage that Lefeuvre proposes, noting that "out of eighteen characters
with the element"-which he would read as shan (sc. or ), the
Shuowen "has no examples in which is designated as phonetic."
Archaeologically, the Neolithic and Shang remains of ale
are best explained as a mixed fermented beverage composed of rice,
honey, and a fruit, possibly with barley and a plant resin (or herb) as addi-
tives.... A beverage in which rice was the principal ingredient accords
with the archaeobotanical finding thus far. (McGovern et al. 2005:262)
This is not to deny the Shang served ale (or beer) in their sacrifices: [57],
[58], [60], [299], e.g., Zhu Qixiang 1999a:115-16; Ma Chengyuan 2003:156-
258, who describes twenty-three different types of alcohol-bearing vessels;
Falkenhausen 2006:49; Thorp 2006:194-95, 198, 200; Allan 2007:485-86,
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
355
who notes that "alcohol has been used in religious ceremonies throughout
the world." But the inscriptions do not bear markedly on the issue, perhaps
because the use of jiu, "ale," was so standard. Lu Liancheng (1996:144–
46; cf. Zhang Deshui 1994) resumes the evidence. In fact, in the five inscrip-
tions he cites-Heji 301, 22557, 26914, 32536, Tunnan 2276-the word you
does not appear. In Heji 1076 we find, "Use mixed aro-
matic ale to conduct a large-scale exorcism ritual (yu) directed to Zu Yi"
(JGWD:562; HYDZ:320, 399); cf. the inscriptions for chang (Y1096.1-98.2
[the word you 酝 appears just once, Heji 27349, but the meaning is prob-
lematic]; Sōran, no. 0660). In five more inscriptions (Y1041.2-42.1) we find
the word yin X, "to drink"; the word you does not appear.
yu:"progenitor, progenitress" (Y185.2; Sōran, no. 1698; Yu Xingwu
1996, nos. 0086, 0087, 0461, 0544; Takashima 2010:II:587-89). Oracle-bone
was a picture of a woman giving birth to a child; see, e.g., Wang Guo-
wei, quoted at Ōshima 1964:87; JGWZ:4325; Gao Ming 1980:119; Qiu Xigui
1998; 2000:190. Many scholars (e.g., Chen Mengjia 1956:488-89; Wang
Guowei, Guo Moruo, and Ye Yusen, quoted at JGWZ:4326, 4327, and 2859,
respectively; Li Xiaoding at JGWZ:2860, 4332; Tsung-tung Chang 1970:99,
n. 1), however, have taken oracle-bone as a loan for hou, "after, last,
descendant, queen." I find this improbable on phonetic grounds. As Guo
Moruo (cited at JGWZ:4327) has argued, in fact, hou as it was used in
Shu and Shi passages was always applied to the early rulers and kings, not
to their later descendants. This led him to propose that the earliest mean-
ing of hou (which he traced back to the era of matriarchal society) could
not have been "after"; the word must instead have referred to a powerful
progenitress, from which came the later usage of "queen."
There is no certainty, in any event, that, in Shang times, oracle-bone
had the sense of hou; it may well be, as Li Xiaoding (JGWZ:4331) con-
cluded, that the use of oracle-bone hou as the word for "after" and as a
loan for hou, was a later development. I reject Wang Guowei's argument
that Z(Y1424.1-25.1) should be read as Hou Zu Yi Z, "the last
Ancestor Yi," a reference to Wu Yi (K25) (cited at JGWZ:4326-27)-which
would thus prove that meant hou, "last" -for three interrelated rea-
sons. First, it flies in the face of other scholarly opinion that links the title
in question to Xiao Yi (K20) (Keightley 1978:206, 208, n. ab). If the phrase
did refer to Xiao Yi, he was not the last Yi king-Wu Yi (K26) and Di Yi
(K28) were still to come; so the word in question is unlikely to have meant
"last." Second, all the inscriptions in which the phrase appears (Y1419.1)
have been dated to Periods III to IV (Yang Yuyan 2005; the Heji and Y edi-
tors date to III); some of them, accordingly, cannot have referred to the Wu
Yi of Period IV. Third, the claim is inaccurate; even if the title had referred356
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
357
to Wu Yi, he still would not have been the last Yi king, for he was followed
by Di Yi (K28).
In light of these considerations, I believe that oracle-bone should
be read as yu . As a verb (rare), it meant "to give birth, procreate," as
in: 今五月 [婦]好毓 [王固曰: □]其嘉,“(In) the present fifth moon...
[Lady] Hao will give birth (yu)...." (Prognostication:) ["His Majesty read
the cracks and said: '... '] it might be good" (Heji 14123; Y187.1); see too
Heji 14021f, 1412 (Y187.1), and two V divinations, Heji 38243 and 38244
(Y195.1), where a more complex form of the graph was used. For discus-
sion, see Öshima 1964:86; Vandermeersch 1977:267. As a noun, yu meant
"Progenitor" or "Progenitress," as in the title Duo Yu : TAL
ET, "(We) pray for (dao) harvest to (the ancestors) from Shang Jia
down to the Many Progenitors (Duo Yu )" (Heji 10111; many exam-
ples at Y185.2-86.2). Yu in these contexts was male, I believe, because har-
vest-seeking rituals were not usually performed to female spirits (see, e.g.,
the prayers for crops and harvest at Y523.1-24.2, 530.1-31.1, offered to
such figures as the River, Nao, Wang Hai, Shang Jia, Shi Ren, Da Yi, Zu Yi,
etc.). I suspect, therefore, that the temple names like Yu Zu YiZ, con-
sidered earlier, meant "Progenitor Ancestor Yi." For female ascendants
we find such temple names as Yu Bi Gui, "Progenitress Ancestress
Gui" (Heji 1249; Y1446.2) and Yu Bi Xin, "Progenitress Ancestress
Xin" (Heji 27456f; Y139.4; for three more occurrences of the temple name
Yu Bi, see the three fragmentary inscriptions, Heji 23326, Tunnan 447, and
Yingcang 2269, at Y186.2); Chen Mengjia (1956:488–89) provides examples.
For all these reasons, I do not believe that oracle-bone was used for hou
, "after, last" or "queen."
yu: "to ward off, protect against (enemies)," hence "to perform an
exorcism ritual (on sombody's behalf or to invoke the aid of a certain
Power)" (Y143.2-56.1; Sōran nos. 0207, 0251; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0351;
Zhang Yujin 2002; Xia Hanyi 2005:82-92; Smith 2008:163, n. 19). The yu
ritual was frequently associated with the offering of sacrifices to ward
off disasters such as illness (see, e.g., commentaries to Jiabian 62.2, 909.2,
and 2121.8; Bingbian 32.1). The mechanism is documented and discussed
at Keightley 2001a:162–63. See too Hayashi 1968:51; Tsung-tung Chang
1970:51, n. 1, and 51-63; 1986-87:3; Taiping Chang 1981:293-97; Takashima
1988:678; 2002:129, n. 23; 2010:II:730; Ding Su 1993:246-48; Zhang Yujin
2003. HYDZ gives "conduct the exorcism sacrifice" (p. 24) and "direct the
exorcism ritual" (p. 452); Takashima (2010:II:72, n. 13; cf. II:730) reads the
graph yu as "drives away [= confronts and pushes away] the evil spirits";
cf. Takashima (2000:387), who treats zi yu as "this [divination] was
lustrated."
Yuan Shi : "The Primary (or First) Ancestors" (Y399.2-400.1; see Shi
,"Ancestor"). I disagree with Chen Mengjia (1956:460), Ikeda (1964, com-
mentary to Houbian 1.19.7), and Hu Houxuan (1972:6, n. 63), who concluded
that Yuan Shi referred only to Shang Jia, i.e., "the Primary Ancestor." The
phrase liu Yuan Shi L, "the six Primary Ancestors," indicates that
Yuan Shi could refer to more than one ancestor. See, e.g., the fragmentary
Heji 14830, which refers to an offering of five (penned animals?) "to the
six Primary Ancestors," which presumably referred to Shang Jia (P1), Bao
Yi (P2), Bao Bing (P3), Bao Ding (P4), Shi Ren (P5), and Shi Gui (P6) (Cao
Dingyun 1990:36); the same collective title, liu Yuan Shi, also appears in Heji
14829. Kaizuka (commentary to Jimbun 716, n. 3, pp. 282-84) was closer to
the truth when he argued that the opposition between the Yuan Shi and Er
Shi, "Secondary Ancestors," paralleled that between the Da Shi X,
"The Greater Ancestors," and Xiao Shi, "The Lesser Ancestors." Chao
Fulin (1989:160) argues for the equivalence of the Da Shi and Yuan Shi; Cao
Dingyun (1990:37), however, has argued that the Yuan Shi and Er Shi do
not correspond to the Da Shi and Xiao Shi, and that the title of Yuan Shi,
"Primary Ancestors," referred to the more senior ancestors, both dynastic
and pre-dynastic, that included both the main and collateral lines, start-
ing with Shang Jia and ending with Zu Yi (Xia Yi, K12); Er Shi, "Second-
ary [or Next] Ancestors" (Y400.1), thus referred, in Cao's view, to the later
kings who came after Zu Yi. Developing the insights of Zhang Zhenglang
(1979:70) and Li Xueqin (1980:39; 1981:29-30), Cao Dingyun assesses the
three formulas in the ritual inscriptions: (1) TZ, "(the ances-
tors) from Shang Jia to Xia Yi" (Heji 270; see too Heji 271 and 419f); (2)
甲元示,“The Primary Ancestors from Shang Jia (on),” as in ㄓ自上甲元示
=+==+, "(We) offer (to) the Primary Ancestors from Shang Jia (P1)
(on), three cows, (we offer to) the Secondary Ancestors, two cows" (Heji
25025); and (3) + "from the Twenty Ancestors from Shang Jia
(on)" (Heji 34120).44 For the assumptions required to find twenty ancestors
who start with Shang Jia (P1) and stop with Xia Yi (K12), see the Glossary
entry for “Ershi Shi 二十示”

2869). I follow Zhang Zhenglang 1985 in reading oracle-bone (Y111.1)
44. Cao Dingyun (1990:34, 40, 41, 43, 44, 45) concludes that Heji 34120 (which he discusses

and (Y35.1) as yun, which he takes to be a euphemism for death: yan
cang, "to secrete, hide away," or mai , "to bury." Other scholars
have read as si E, "die, death" (Y111.1), expressing the wish that par-
ticular members of the elite not die; yi, "kill" (Y35.2), etc. See also the
Glossary entry for zang, "to bury."
zai /: "harm" (Y903.2-05.1; Sōran, no. 1508; Yu Xingwu 1996, nos. 1316,
2417, 2419). This common verb-written with a dagger-axe blade and
a zai phonetic-referred to harm inflicted in battle, as in [91]. Shuowen
defined zai as "to wound, injure" (zai, shang ye). I translate zai
as "harm." The word has also been interpreted as zai ", "disasters" (as
at Y903.2, 905.1; other forms at Y486.1, 496.1); the two words, although
not part of the same xiesheng series, were probably closely related
in meaning. As Li Xiaoding (JGWZ:3778) and S580.1 have noted, and
could be used interchangeably; Li also suggests that was used for
"disasters" that involved weapons. A study of the word, in both bone and
bronze inscriptions, is Zhang Zhenglang 1981:133–34. Zai, as Zhang notes,
should not be confused with oracle-bone * (see bo in the Glossary).
zang : “to bury" (?). A variety of graphs have been so identified (Sōran,
nos. 0787, 0788, 0859, 1015, 3974, 4103). The oracle-bone graph (Y1184.2),
which depicts a human body lying on a bed within an enclosure, presum-
ably depicted the placing of a corpse in a coffin, hence a burial. Thus, Hu
Houxuan (1944a:7b, discussing Xubian 5.4.3 (= [274]), speculates that it was
the character zang; Kaizuka (commentary to Jimbun 451) agrees. I so take
the word in [273]. For some discussion of the word, see, e.g., Ikeda 1964,
commentary to Houbian 2.22.9; Akatsuka 1977:255; JGWD:461-62. See also
the Glossary entry for yun: "(secrete, hide away, to bury =) die, death."
zhai : "reside, settle" (Y765.1; Sōran, no. 0897; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2062;
Liu Huan 2005a). There has been little discussion of this word (JGWZ:2437;
JGWJ:216, 342), but its consistent use in Zhou texts in the sense of "dwell-
ing" or "dwell" supports the view that it had a similar meaning in Shang.
The parallel between [126B], on the one hand, and Shu, "Shao Gao,"
para. 2, TE, "The Grand Protector came in the morning to
考
Luo and divined about settling (there)," and Shi, Mao no. 244, para. 7,
:, "The one who examined the cracks was His Majesty:
Where (we) reside should be Haojing," on the other, is fairly clear. There
was a long tradition of divining about where a ruler should reside.
zhao: "initiate, deploy" (Y882.2-83.1; Sōran, no. 4892; Yu Xingwu 1996,
nos. 2397-99). It is generally agreed that the oracle-bone word that modern
scholars generally transcribe as zhao had the sense of "initiate or begin."
In military contexts such as [222], I translate it as "deploy." In contexts of
illness, it appears to refer to the "initiating" of the illness by some Power,
as in:

zhe (Y507.1-09.2; Sōran, no. 3190; see too no. 3988). For this reading of
oracle-bone, see Liu Zhao 1986. It was not, he believes, a time word in of so
its own right, but was attached to time words as a suffix. Many scholars I should be
interpret it as chun #, "spring," but its occurence in first, third, fourth, distributed
fifth, seventh, eleventh, twelfth, and thirteenth moons (Y507.1-09.2) ren-
more widely
der such a reading impossible (see too Shirakawa 1972:165). I accordingly
translate a phrase such as jin zhe as "in the present season" (cf. Song
Huaqiang 2006, who opts for zhao, “morning"). Another graph for
spring may appear in [86] (Y514.2–15.2; Sōran, no. 0053; cf. Zhu Qixiang
1999a:4), but the graph is problematic (see, e.g., Ding Su 1980:113-15). The
graph appears in the second moon (Heji 37852), tenth moon (Heji 18), and
eleventh moon (Heji 6073), which is not encouraging.
zhen : "to divine" (Sōran, no. 0455; Wang Yuxin and Yang Shengnan,
eds., 1999:277-80). It is generally accepted that oracle-bone A, when used
in divination prefaces, served as the divinatory verb zhen (frequently
given the modern romanization of zheng; see GSR, no. 834g; Li Ji and Wan
Jiabao 1970:39-42, 105-07). The meaning of that word has been much
debated, particularly because it bears on the question of whether or not
the Shang diviners couched their charges as interrogative sentences, as
early scholars of the inscriptions supposed and many still do (e.g., Brit-
ton 1940:10, 18, 21, 33, etc.; Dong Zuobin 1945; JGWJ:312; Qu Wanli, Jiakao;
Chang Tsung-tung 1970 [who translates both the bufand zhen as "fra-
gen," question]; Vandermeersch 1980; Chang Yuzhi 1987; Zheng Jiexiang
1994; Zhu Qixiang 1999a; Zhang Shichao 2002, etc.), or as declaratives, as
I do in this book (Keightley 1972; Serruys 1974:21-27; Itō and Takashima
1996:I:xi; Djamouri [1999:15, 20] gives "soumit à l'oracle [la prédiction360
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
361
361
suivante]"). For the latest, exhaustive summary of the evidence, which I
shall not resume here, see Qiu Xigui 1988a and 1989a (with its accompa-
nying discussion; see too the critical response of Takashima 1988-89:45-
49; 1989:97-133). Without laboring the point, I would note that divinatory
zhen belonged to a word family whose core meaning was zheng IE,
"regulate, rectify," or ding E, "determine, settle, fix," related presumably
to ding (the object presumably depicted, in simplified form, by oracle-
bone graph ); see, e.g., Keightley 1972:9-13 (summarized at 1989:138-
39); critical summary at Takashima 1988-89:25-35; 1989:38-68. Takashima
(1987:408-09) provides Li Fang-kuei's phonetic reconstructions for
these and related words; as he notes, H, "cauldron," may have referred
to a "stable object or vessel," thus "settled." (Boltz 1990 offers a different
reconstruction.) He Cheng (1992) offers a different explanation: the ding
was not a verb meaning "to divine" but was a tripod used in sacrifices
after the divination. Takashima (1988-89:35-42; 1989:68-92) further evalu-
ates the views of Serruys, Nivison, and Lefeuvre about zhen and related
words. As a result, I agree that the divination process did indeed test the
diviners' propositions-the translation for zhen offered by Serruys (e.g.,
1974), Takashima (e.g., 1973; 1988-89; 2010), and Kolb (1992:22, passim:
"Test der Proposition"). Takashima (2006:17-19; HYDZ, passim) gives
"tested (the following proposition against the numen of the bone)." The
Shang diviner may well have been "regulating, determining, fixing, or
attesting" his charges, but I see no way at present to decide the matter; my
own translation, "to divine," is merely functional. Zhen referred to some
divinatory, ritual act, but I cannot yet tell its precise nature. For zhen in
Yijing, see Shaughnessy 1983:124-33; Kunst 1985:200-211.
zhen: "to move, tremble, shock" (Y442.1-2; Sōran, no. 0196; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 1166). See Tongzuan 603 (Guo Moruo's commentary): Li Xueqin
1958:68; JGWD:195.
zheng E: "march to regulate" (Y304.1-09.2; Sōran, no. 0163; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 0821; see too the Glossary entry for wei .) HYDZ:209, 291, 312
give "conduct a punitive expedition against." The oracle-bone graph,
as used in military contexts, is generally interpreted as zheng (IE, "to go on
campaign against," as in Heji 6098, 36485, 36487, and 36511 (see, e.g., Chen
Weizhan 1981:231; Wang Hui 1981:80). S582.1 lists parallel inscriptions
that indicate the interchangeability of zheng IE and wang (E, "to go," and
of zheng IE and chu H, "to go out (on campaign)." (Wang Hui [1981:80]
also notes that zheng IE in Shijing, Mao no. 21, has been understood as
xing 1, "to go, walk.") The root etymon (see, e.g., GSR, nos. 833j, 8330)
suggests that the Shang verb would have had a strong moral overtone,
"to rectify, regulate, correct"; this appears to be supported by oracle-bone
usage. With few exceptions, the word was reserved for campaigns in
which the king was the protagonist. He and his dependents, for example,
might fa \\, “attack," the Gongfang or the Tufang (as in [61]); his officers
might bo*, "strike," the enemy ([90], [118], [119]); but only the king could
zheng E them ([50] and Y304.1-05.2). To convey this sense of moral and
political rectification that was reserved for the king's military expeditions,
I translate zheng as "march to regulate."
The Shang also used zheng in the phrase zheng yue EA, the "regulating
moon," another name for the "first moon" (yi yue) with which they
started their luni-solar calendar ([103]; Keightley 2000:43).
Some of the other contexts in which oracle-bone was used, however,
are not easy to translate with certainty since it is possible that the Shang
used the same graph to write both the words for zheng IE = (IE and zu Ē
(see too JGWD:146, 148; Yu Xingwu 1996:808), and even the word for pi E,
the last two words both meaning "foot."
[340]
辛未卜古貞:黍年ㄓ足雨
Making cracks on xinwei (day 8), Gu divined: "For the millet
crop there will be (you) sufficient rain." (I. Bin) (Heji 10137)
This is the view of Wang Hui (1981:79), who argues that the oracle-bone
pictograph depicting the leg and foot (S88.1; Y313.2; Sōran, nos. 3243-45)
was replaced by the form; that in the oracle bones zu and zheng
E can only be distinguished on the basis of context (Chen Weizhan 1981
reaches similar conclusions); and that it was only in the bronze inscrip-
tions of the Zhou that the two words were written with different forms,
and that even then some confusion is still evident. Uncertainty still attends
the few instances in which oracle-bone I may have been a way of writ-
ing zu E, "foot" (as proposed, e.g., by Chen Weizhan 1981:232-33; Wang
Hui 1981:79; Yu Xingwu 1996:808), but the examples are rare. Sometimes,
in this view, it meant "sufficient, enough," as in Qianbian 1.50.1 (= Heji
10139): 貞: 帝令雨弗其足年/帝令雨足年, which Chen Weizhan takes to
mean something like "The rain which Di orders might not/will be suffi-
cient for the harvest" (cf. the translation at Schuessler 1987:870). But other
translations are possible: "If Di orders it to rain, we might not/will (receive)
a regular (good) harvest" (the translation proposed by the Oslo Discus-
sion Group). Given the numerous divinations about "receiving abundant
harvest" (shou you nian, ; see e.g. [132C], [133A], [154], [164], etc.,
and Heji 787, 9503, 9504, 9507, 9552, 9572, etc.), a divination about "suffi-
cient" harvest might seem uncharacteristically understated. See another
example, the fragmentary Jiabian 1640 (= Heji 28106; Period III), in which
can be read as ji zu E, "sick foot," the reading also proposed by362
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
363
the Jiabian kaoshi. But for a contrary view, see Lefeuvre (1971:59, n. 108),
who proposes instead that E in such contexts signified something favor-
able, so that Jiabian 1640 meant only "the malady will have favorable out-
come," with no reason for assuming that the foot was involved. (I am not
fully persuaded by his argument, partly because charges about sickness-
in which the pictographic form depicting the leg and foot appeared, as in
Heji 775b and 13693-were no longer divined after Period I.)
zheng
: "present, raise" (Y366.2-69.1; Sōran, no. 0611; Yu Xingwu 1996,
no. 1032). Oracular, a picture of two hands raising a dou (or gui )
vessel, has been understood in three related ways. First, Yang Shuda (cited
at JGWZ:1764)-who cited tempting parallels in classical texts-argued
that was deng, read as zheng, "levy, raise"; but on this identifica-
tion see JGWZ:465-69, 1673-77; Serruys 1974:113, n. 59. Second, oracle-
bone has been understood as an early form of yong (?), meaning
"cooked food" in Zhou texts; in the context of Shang mobilization, how-
ever, it is thought to have had the sense of "to present" (Tang Lan, cited
at JGWZ:1763). Third, the graph has been read as an early form of zheng
, "steam; to rise as steam; to advance, bring forward" (rejected by Shima
1958:310) and thus zheng, "to present meat in sacrifice" (Luo Zhenyu,
cited at JGWZ:1673; GSR, no. 896h, k). Following the Y editors, I adopt this
third transcription, . See [12AB], [138], and [139].
For other discussions on the use of this word in contexts of labor con-
scription, see, e.g., Dong Zuobin 1945:II:9:39a; Chen Mengjia 1956:606;
Shima 1958:387-88, 473; Kaizuka's commentary to Jimbun 671; JGWZ:779-
82; Akatsuka 1977:605-06, n. 22; JGWJ:247, 318-19.
zhi: "seize, manacle, shackle" (Sōran, nos. 1255, 1256), as in [213]. I
believe that the two oracle-bone forms Y1000.2 and Y1005.2 should both
be read in the sense of zhi. See the instances of equivalent usage at S583.4
and in Sōran. See too Qiu Xigui 2000:190; Takashima 2010:I:67-71, II:732.
zhi zhong : "lose zhong" (Y666.1-67.1; Sōran, no. 0492; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 1780; Shen Pei 2002). Two related phrases are involved in the
divinations about the loss of zhong (and, to a lesser extent, of ren):
sang zhong (see the Glossary entry for sang) and zhi zhong (Yang
Shengnan 1982a:388-89). I translate both as "to lose zhong." I had origi-
nally argued, following Chen Mengjia (1956:609) that zhi zhong referred
to the "marshalling" of the zhong (Keightley 1969:80-84). Various other
meanings have been proposed: Zhang Zhenglang (1973:113), who pro-
posed that the word I now read as zhi meant di , "take, bring, or lead";
Xiao Nan (1981a:129), who takes zhi in the sense of zhengji, "collect,
assemble"; cf. Takashima (1988:663, n. 7), who, following JGWZ:1257-63,
suggests "deploy." I now believe this must be wrong, partly because the
distinction between strong and weak charges (p. 329) reveals that the king
did not want to zhi his zhong. In the positive formulations, for example
such as [85G], [92], and [93A], the presence of the weakening qi in the
phrases, and, "we might lose zhong," indicates that the king
did not want to lose zhong; that no qi appears in any of the negative for-
mulations 不喪眾,弗雉工眾,不雉眾,“We will not lose (the royal) zhong”
(e.g., [25], [85A-F], [90], [115]), confirms the point.
I am satisfied, based on the usage of the word in the bone inscriptions,
that it had an injurious meaning. This is also the way that Ishida (1964:68),
Lin Zutai (1978:234), Yang Baocheng and Yang Xizhang (1983:34, n. 7), and
Kolb (1991:44, 91, who cites additional scholarship) take it, as do Jin Xiang-
heng, Yu Xingwu, Zhang Yongshan, and Xu Zhongshu (see the entries at
Sōran, no. 0492).
It should be noted that the term wang zhong E, the "royal zhong,"
only appears in inscriptions in which the divination was about their zhi-
loss, as in [85A-G] and Heji 26881-85; this suggests that the verb may have
had a particular meaning that distinguished it from the more "plebeian"
word for "loss," sang . Vandermeersch (1980:II:181), in fact, suggests
that the "bird" element in the zhi #graph might be linked to the leg-
end about Jian Dik, the Shang progenitress, swallowing the egg of a
black bird; as he notes, the Shang also often wrote the hai of Wang Hai
Ewith a bird element (Heji 22152, 24975, 30447-48, 34293-45; Tunnan
1116; see Chen Zhi 1999:142-44). This is possible.
zhong (Y69.1-71.1; Sōran, no. 1033; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 0079; Schuessler
2006:623; Yinxu 116, 553; Takashima 2010:II:113-17.). Chao Lin (1982:14)
translates zhong as "the Multitudes," but I do not believe it had that sense
in the Shang. As discusssed in chapter 9 (p. 97), the numbers of zhong
recorded in the bone inscriptions were relatively small, and the term was
evidently used to refer to the king's permanent service corps. The logo-
graph for zhong, moreover, as it appears on the Shang bones was ;
it depicted three men (ren) )-rarely, two men, as in [212AB] (see Y69.1-
71.1)-a further indication that the numbers may not have been large. The
Guoyu 國語,“Zhouyu shang 周語上,” contains the passage 人三為眾...,
"three people constitute a crowd..." (Wei Zhao, ed., 1929:12; tr. based on
Hart 1973:263). (Sima Qian also repeats the anecdote in his "Zhou benji
"[Nienhauser, ed., 1994:70].) The fact that the speaker could draw
such a conclusion suggests that zhong could still be so understood in the
Eastern Zhou, and even perhaps in the Han. This again supports the view
that the zhong of Shang need not have been particularly numerous.364
Working for His Majesty
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
365
For a discussion of the Shang graph, see, e.g., GSR, no. 1010; Satō
1954:10; the entries at Sōran, no. 1033. Dong Zuobin (1948:11) saw "three
men under a piece of territory" but this misunderstands the graphic evi-
dence. Two main versions of the graph appear on the bones: and m.
The first, which does depict the sun above, was the earliest and more com-
mon. The abbreviated form (in which the was replaced by a ☐ form-
which Dong evidently took as depicting a piece of territory) appears in
the Li inscriptions of Period I-II: see, e.g., Heji 31971, 31969-70, 31972-
85, 31987-92, 31994, 31995-32007. The zhong in the contemporary inscrip-
tions, also dated to Li, was also written with the element: Heji 31986 and
31993. (See too the examples at JGWB:353 and Gao Ming 1980:23, and the
discussions at Ishida 1964: 64, 65, and Xu Jinxiong 1984:127.) The readi-
ness to simplify the "sun" element in this way, in fact, may call into ques-
tion the argument that the graph for zhong represented men under the
emblem, perhaps totemic, of the Shang sun (see Guo Moruo 1952:9; Ding
Shan 1956:38).
That the oracle-bone graph for zhong could also be written with just
two, rather than three, men under the sun (Sōran, no. 1033; eight instances
at S25.1, including, perhaps, [212AB]), suggests first, that, contrary to the
view that "the three men represented 'many"" (e.g., Guo Moruo 1952:9),
that was not the Shang intent. It also suggests that the Shang might have
felt a certain graphic (as well as phonetic; see the archaic reconstructions
offered at Schuessler 1987:101, 847) similarity between the words for zhong
and cong (=), "to follow." Zhong, accordingly, might be translated as
"followers." Other possibilities, such as "company, companions, retinue,
retainers, throng, troop," also suggest themselves (cf. J. L. Wei 2005:31).
I prefer, at present, however, to leave the term untranslated. The Shang
kings thought of their labor dependents as zhong; so may we.
zhongren: "the men of the zhong." Scholarly opinion has generally
taken zhongren as a compound and not as two independent terms. See,
e.g., Zhao Xiyuan 1956; 1982; Li Yan 1970a:2; Zhang Zhenglang 1973:109;
Vandermeersch 1980:176-82 ("la masse des gens"); Xiao Nan 1981a:129;
Yang Baocheng and Yang Xishang 1983:32; Peng Bangjiong 1990:36; Wang
Yuxin and Yang Shengnan, eds., 1999:478-81. Kolb (1991:27-29; also 1992:
51-54) provides a useful summary and discussion of the scholarly opin-
ions. Chen Mengjia (1956:610) concluded that, while the Shang king and
the tribal statelets (zubang) had their zhong and ren, the term zhongren
appeared only in connection with the king. Chen Mengjia, in any event,
concluded that the difference between the zhong and zhongren was small.
For my earlier view, to which I no longer subscribe, that zhongren should
be read as two independent terms, zhong and ren, see Keightley 1969:70-73.
For why I now believe that zhongren was probably a compound, see the
discussion in chapter 4.
zhu : “pray, prayer” (Y120.2–24.1; Sōran, no. 0018; Takashima 2010:II:315-
16). Taiping Chang (1981:367-68) gives "the invoking ceremony." See Lian
Shaoming (2005) for "dispelling disaster." The Shang frequently divined
about offering prayers to their ancestors, as in [209], [335AB], and
辛,“(We) pray to Ancestor Xin (K13)”(Heji 787); 翌丁亥上甲祝用,
"... on the next dinghai (day 24), it should be (hui) the prayer to Shang Jia
(P1) that (we) use" (Heji 8093); and other inscriptions at Y120.2-21.2. Zhou
usage-as in Shangshu, "Jin teng," para. 5, ..., "The recorder
then put on tablets the prayer that said..."-supports the view that zhu
involved some sort of written entreaty to the Powers.
The Shang graph has been understood as a picture of a man kneeling
in prayer before an ancestral tablet (shi T) (JGWZ:83-84; JGWJ:73, 234-35).
Zhu may also, by extension, have meant someone who prays, "the invoca-
tor" (as in [286]), although in that case, Guo Moruo, in his commentary to
Tongzuan 581, thought that Zhu was a personal name.
zhuo : "cleave" (Y1079.1-80.2; Sōran, no. 5085). I had originally read ora-
cle-bone as tou=(?) (Keightley 1978: 43, n. 79, which provides
a summary of the previous scholarship). On the basis of further study
(and stimulated in part by Takashima 1979-80:54, n. 19), I now find zhuo
(part of the same word family; GSR, no. 1235) a more satisfactory read-
ing; see Yu Xingwu (quoted at JGWZ:4068); Chow Kwok-ching 1982:210,
321; cf. Zhou Guozheng 1983:245; Serruys 1986:248, transcription for no.
113. The argument of Yan Yiping (1980a:13-24) that should be read as
yan, "misty," leads, in my view, to "incoherent" results (see Keightley
19978:44-51).
When used in contexts of sacrifice (Y1079.2-80.1), this verb zhuo
meant "to cut, chop in pieces, cleave," as in [292]. Kwok-ching Chow
(1982:210) regards zhuo as a type B verb (defined under you on page 262),
representing a specific method for sacrificing victims.
When used as a time word in verifications (Y1079.1-2), the verb referred
to that night-time period when one ganzhi day "cleaved" to the next, as in
Heji 6057, 6834, and 12908. (Takashima [1990:30], however, translates
Z as "(about the time) jiazi day passes over and on to the yichou
day"; or "to cut, cleave" (Takashima 2010:I:92, II:733). Zheng Huisheng
(1987) reads the word as jian, but his article only demonstrates that zhuo
had a jian-like function, serving to "separate" ganzhi days (and portions
of victims). See too Qiu Xigui 1993 (cf. 2000:363), who determines that the
word had the sense of xiangzai E, i.e., "inclining to," its function where366
Working for His Majesty
it links the night of one day to the ganzhi of the next. See also HYDZ:84,
"During the transitional period from" one ganzhi to the next; Zhao Cheng
2006:959-65.
zi yong (Y1225.2): "This was used." As Huang Tianshu has argued
(1988:264-70), this notation was used by the Nameless (royal)- and Li-
group diviners. It served as a reading of the crack. The negative notation,
bu yong, "This was not used," was also sometimes recorded (cases at
Hu Houxuan 1939a:480). On occasion, the notation was engraved to the
right or left of the crack, as in:
[341]
其雨. 大吉.茲用.允大雨
(Charge:) "It may (qi) rain." [Engraved to the right of the
crack:] (Prognostication:) Greatly auspicious. (Notation:) This
was used (zi yong). (Verification:) "There really was a great
rain." (III-IV. Nameless-Diviner) (Heji 30149)
乙
The notation was also appended to the end of the charge itself, as in Tun-
nan 726.
It is generally agreed that zi yong was used in auspicious environments
(see Keightley 1978:119), but two puzzling inscriptions raise the possibil-
ity that the "use" may have been unrelated to the auspiciousness of the
crack. Thus, Tunnan 676 appears to contain the notation T, "Exten-
sively auspicious; this was not used," and Tunnan 139 reads
TH, "(We) offer a cutting sacrifice (sui) to Ancestor Xin (K13)...' Auspi-
cious; this was not used" (Huang Tianshu 1988:269 discusses these two).
It is not certain, however, that in these two cases the and the Twere
part of the same inscription unit.
zu (Y986.1-87.1; Sōran, no. 0828; Yu Xingwu 1996, no. 2559). K. C. Chang
(1976:73)-cf. Chen Enlin (1985:171, 178, 180–81), basing himself upon
Chen Mengjia (1956:496-97)-suggested that the zu of Shang, as found in
the oracle-bone inscriptions, was "an elementary unit of kin for the joint
performance of many activities," particularly those involving military
activities. The members of the wang zu E, "royal lineage," were cer-
tainly sent on military expeditions, as in [26], and a significant number of
the scholars cited at Sōran, no. 0828, posit a military role for the zu. I ten-
tatively translate the Shang term zu as "lineage," on the assumption that
kinship ties played a role. That the term also had the sense of "warband"
should also be borne in mind (see San Zu under Wu Zu Shu ). For
a further introduction to the issues and scholarship, see Kwang-chih
Chang 1980:163-66; Keightley 1982:311-12; 1999b:270-71; Yang Sheng-
nan 1982a:382-87; Liu Zhao 1989:76-79; Kolb 1991:67-71; Shen Changyun
Appendix 2: Glossary of Shang Terms and Phrases
3
1998; Shao Wangping 2000:211-12. I would like to propose, tentatively, the
word for "kindred" as a translation for zu. This refers principally to the
"relationship by blood or descent." But it refers also to "allied in nature,
character, or properties; possessing similar qualities or features" (OED:
702-03). I suspect that some of the organization of Shang society, particu-
larly among the artisans, shared this feature: linked in a common preoc-
cupation, but not necessarily linked by ties of blood.
zu : "Ancestor" (Y1426.1-32.2; 1433.2-34.1; Sōran, no. 1618; Yu Xingwu
1996, no. 3627). The term may also have had the sense of "grandfather," but
the fact that it was routinely used in the temple names of kings who were
several generations senior to the reigning king indicates that the sense
of "grandfather" has to be understood in a generic rather than a genera-
tional sense. (See too Goody [1962:403; cf. 376] among the Lodagaa of West
Africa.) Thus Zu Yi (K12), "Ancestor Yi" or "Grandfather Yi," would, in
fact, have been Wu Ding's great-great-grandfather; Zu Jia (K23), "Ances-
tor Jia" or "Grandfather Jia," would, in fact, have been Di Xin's great-
great-great-great-grandfather (see fig. 1, "The Royal Genealogy"). It is for
this reason that I do not translate the title but simply refer to kings such as
Zu Yi and Zu Jia with their Chinese titles. By contrast, I do translate titles
such as Fu Ding T as "Father Ding," on the grounds that Fu was used
only to refer to the males of the previous generation-the "father," bio-
logical or classificatory; zu, by contrast, meant more than the generation
of the "grandfather."Bibliography A
